### [1] [PoQ-Judge: A Multi-Architecture Evaluation Framework for Cost-Aware Proof-of-Quality in Decentralized LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11196)
*Arther Tian,Alex Ding,Frank Chen,Simon Wu,Aaron Chan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PoQ-Judge trains lightweight, reference-free judge models for decentralized LLM inference quality scoring. Using two-stage training on UltraFeedback and in-domain data, the best model (DeBERTa-based) achieves 0.747 Pearson correlation, matching reference-based evaluators. Cascade evaluation cuts cost by 72.7% with minimal quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decentralized LLM inference networks require a Proof of Quality (PoQ) mechanism to assess query-output quality without ground-truth references, as reference-based evaluation is impractical in decentralized settings. Existing evaluators either rely on references or are too heavyweight for on-chain/lightweight deployment.

Method: Three architectures are studied under a quality-cost tradeoff: TextCNN (lightweight), MiniLM cross-encoder (mid-range), and DeBERTa (high-capacity). A two-stage training pipeline is used — first on the general-purpose UltraFeedback dataset, then fine-tuned on GPT-labeled in-domain data. A cascade evaluation mechanism routes simpler queries to cheaper models for cost savings. Online calibration identifies dominant quality dimensions (e.g., semantic quality).

Result: The best PoQ-Judge model reaches 0.747 Pearson correlation with a ground-truth proxy on held-out test data, surpassing prior reference-based evaluators. As a reference-free component in composite scoring, it achieves 0.645 Pearson correlation, matching the best single reference-based evaluator without needing reference answers. Cascade evaluation reduces cost by 72.7% with only modest quality degradation. Performance is substantially stronger on QA than on summarization tasks.

Conclusion: Reference-free quality evaluation is viable for decentralized PoQ, with the DeBERTa-based PoQ-Judge delivering strong correlation comparable to reference-based methods. The cascade strategy significantly lowers operational cost. The QA-vs-summarization performance gap indicates that proxy quality (GPT labels) remains the primary bottleneck, especially for complex generative tasks like summarization.

Abstract: Decentralized LLM inference networks need lightweight, reference-free quality evaluation for Proof of Quality (PoQ). We present PoQ-Judge, a framework that trains dedicated judge models to score query-output pairs without ground-truth references. We study three architectures across the quality-cost tradeoff: a TextCNN judge, a MiniLM cross-encoder, and a DeBERTa judge. Using two-stage training on UltraFeedback plus GPT-labeled in-domain data, the best model reaches 0.747 Pearson correlation with the ground-truth proxy on a held-out test set, outperforming reference-based evaluators from prior work. As a reference-free component in composite scoring, it achieves 0.645 Pearson correlation, matching the best single reference-based evaluator while removing the need for reference answers. We also show that online calibration identifies semantic quality as the dominant dimension and that cascade evaluation reduces cost by 72.7 percent with only modest quality loss. Results are much stronger on QA than summarization, pointing to proxy quality as the main remaining limitation.

</details>


### [2] [The Structural Attention Tax: How Retrieval Format Hijacks In-Context Learning Independent of Content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11198)
*Yuqi Zhang,Di Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RAG systems suffer from a "structural attention tax" where knowledge graph triples capture 2-3x more attention per token than semantically equivalent natural language, compressing demonstration attention by up to 42%. The authors decompose attention into semantic and structural components, revealing two orthogonal optimization axes: retrieval quality and format-driven attention capture. Empirically, source-task alignment dominates over gating strategies, and five mitigation strategies are proposed, with format flattening showing validated improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG research focuses on the semantic relevance of injected content but overlooks how the format/structure of that content independently distorts LLM attention distributions, potentially degrading in-context learning (ICL) performance regardless of content quality.

Method: The authors formalize the structural attention tax phenomenon, develop a mathematical framework decomposing attention scores into semantic and structural components (Eq. 2), derive a compression bound (Proposition 1) linking token-level format bias to demonstration attention loss, and empirically test across two model families (Mistral-7B, LLaMA-3-8B) and three QA benchmarks (HotpotQA, etc.) using both KG-structured and natural-language retrieval. Five structure-aware mitigation strategies are derived: structural dispersal (S1), format flattening (S3), and training-time regularization, among others.

Result: KG triples capture attention at ô(KG) ≈ 0.70 vs. ô(neutral) ≈ 0.25 per token, compressing demonstration attention by up to 42% regardless of relevance. Source-task alignment dominates: task-matched BM25 retrieval achieves 58-62% on HotpotQA vs. ConceptNet's 25-27% (>30 pp gap), dwarfing gating strategies (≤2 pp). Format flattening (S3) is validated by both accuracy and attention-level evidence, while structural dispersal (S1) yields mixed results.

Conclusion: Format-driven attention capture is a significant, independent factor in RAG performance degradation. The semantic-structural decomposition reveals two orthogonal optimization axes: improving retrieval quality (semantic) and reducing format-driven attention diversion (structural). Format flattening emerges as a practical, zero-cost mitigation, but format-level interventions remain challenging, warranting further research.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems inject external knowledge to improve LLM outputs, yet the format of injected content -- distinct from its semantic relevance -- can independently distort the model's attention distribution. We identify and formalise a phenomenon we term the structural attention tax: knowledge graph (KG) triples, due to their relational delimiters and repeated slot patterns, capture 2-3x more attention per token than semantically equivalent natural-language text ($\hat{o}$(KG) $\approx$ 0.70 vs. $\hat{o}$(neutral) $\approx$ 0.25), compressing demonstration attention by up to 42% -- regardless of whether the triples are relevant or noise. We develop a formal framework decomposing attention scores into semantic and structural components (Eq. 2), derive a compression bound (Proposition 1) connecting token-level format bias to demonstration attention loss, and show that the structural term governs how much attention is diverted while the semantic term governs whether this helps or hurts. This decoupling reveals two orthogonal axes for improving retrieval-augmented ICL: optimising retrieval quality (semantic axis) and reducing format-driven attention capture (structural axis). Empirically, across two model families (Mistral-7B, LLaMA-3-8B) and three QA benchmarks, we observe that source-task alignment dominates: task-matched BM25 retrieval achieves 58-62% on HotpotQA vs. ConceptNet's 25-27%, a >30 pp gap that dwarfs all gating strategies ($\leq$2 pp). We derive five structure-aware mitigation strategies from the framework, ranging from zero-cost prompt modifications to training-time regularisation; format flattening (S3) is validated by both accuracy and attention-level evidence from a verbalized-triple control, while structural dispersal (S1) yields mixed results that illuminate the challenges of format-level intervention.

</details>


### [3] [NightFeats @ MMU-RAGent NeurIPS 2025: A Context-Optimized Multi-Agent RAG System for the Text-to-Text Track](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11199)
*Quentin Fever,Naziha Aslam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: NightFeats is a multi-agent RAG system that won Best Dynamic Evaluation at NeurIPS 2025's MMU-RAGent competition. It decomposes knowledge synthesis into retrieval, curation, and composition phases with explicit handoff contracts, outperforming proprietary baselines like Claude-SonnetV2 and Nova-Pro on human-aligned evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rather than optimizing for benchmark metrics, the authors aim to design a principled, transparent RAG pipeline where architectural decisions are guided by verifiable evidence grounding and human preference alignment. The work is motivated by Agentic Context Engineering (ACE), emphasizing explicit intermediate representations and structured agent coordination.

Method: The system employs a three-phase architecture: (1) retrieval with temporal-semantic reranking, (2) curation with bounded contradiction reconciliation, and (3) composition with citation-preserving generation. Each phase is governed by explicit intermediate representations and handoff contracts between agents, ensuring architectural transparency throughout the pipeline.

Result: NightFeats surpassed proprietary baselines including Claude-SonnetV2 and Nova-Pro on both LLM-as-a-Judge and Human Likert evaluations in the text-to-text track, earning the Best Dynamic Evaluation award at the NeurIPS 2025 MMU-RAGent competition.

Conclusion: Architectural transparency and verifiable evidence grounding align more closely with human preferences than systems narrowly optimized for automatic similarity metrics, validating the principled multi-agent RAG design over black-box proprietary approaches.

Abstract: We present NightFeats, a structured multi-agent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system submitted to the MMU-RAGent competition at NeurIPS 2025, where it was awarded Best Dynamic Evaluation in the text-to-text track. Rather than targeting benchmark maximization, this work proposes a principled pipeline that decomposes knowledge synthesis into three coordinated phases: retrieval, curation, and composition, each governed by explicit intermediate representations and handoff contracts. Inspired by Agentic Context Engineering (ACE), the system introduces temporal-semantic reranking, bounded contradiction reconciliation, and citation-preserving composition as core architectural primitives. Competition results show that NightFeats surpasses proprietary baselines including Claude-SonnetV2 and Nova-Pro on LLM-as-a-Judge and Human Likert evaluations, confirming that architectural transparency and verifiable evidence grounding are better aligned with human preferences than systems optimizing narrowly for automatic similarity metrics.

</details>


### [4] [Detecting AI-Generated Content on Social Media with Multi-modal Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11200)
*Chenyang Yang,Shen Yan,Yibo Yang,Litao Hu,Yuchen Liu,Yuan Zeng,Hanchao Yu,Yinan Zhu,Sumedha Singla,Brian Vanover,Huijun Qian,Zihao Wang,Fujun Liu,Aashu Singh,Jianyu Wang,Xuewen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A practical pipeline for detecting AI-generated content on social media using a continuously updated multi-modal dataset and a compact vision-language model that provides both detection and explanation, achieving state-of-the-art performance and positive real-world deployment results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AIGC detection methods suffer from poor generalization to new generative models, over-reliance on single modalities, and lack of interpretability, making them inadequate for dynamic, real-world social media environments where photorealistic AI-generated images and videos are increasingly used for spam, misinformation, manipulation, and fraud.

Method: The authors propose a pipeline that: (1) continuously curates diverse, multi-modal social media data to keep pace with evolving generative models; (2) trains a compact vision-language model capable of both detecting AI-generated content and generating interpretable explanations for its decisions.

Result: The model achieves state-of-the-art detection performance on public benchmarks and demonstrates robust detection and explanation capabilities on internal social media datasets spanning multiple platforms. Post recommendation deployment led to positive downstream impacts on user engagement.

Conclusion: Effective, interpretable AIGC detection in dynamic, real-world social media environments is feasible when combining continuous multi-modal data curation with compact vision-language model training.

Abstract: Generative AI has enabled the creation of photorealistic images and videos that are increasingly disseminated on social media, often used for spam, misinformation, manipulation, and fraud. Existing AI-generated content (AIGC) detection methods face challenges including poor generalization to new generation models, reliance on single modalities, and lack of interpretable explanations. We present our pipeline that mitigates these issues by continuously curating diverse multi-modal social media data and training a compact vision-language model for detection and explanation. Our model achieves state-of-the-art detection performance on public benchmarks and demonstrates robust detection and explanation capabilities on internal social media datasets across multiple platforms. We deployed our model for post recommendation on social media platforms and observed positive downstream impacts on user engagement, demonstrating that it is feasible to perform effective AIGC detection in dynamic, real-world social media environments.

</details>


### [5] [One Jailbreak, Many Tongues: Learning Language-Insensitive Intention Representations for Multilingual Jailbreak Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11202)
*Shuyu Jiang,Kaiyu Xu,Xingshu Chen,Hao Ren,Rui Tang,Yi Zhang,Tianwei Zhang,Hongwei Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MLJailDe is a multilingual jailbreak detection framework that uses back-translation data augmentation, relative-distance constraints, and imbalance-aware classification to achieve robust cross-lingual detection of harmful prompts, reaching 98.5% F1 on seen languages and 97.1% on unseen languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM safety training and jailbreak defenses are concentrated in dominant languages, creating exploitable safety gaps for multilingual users. Current defenses suffer from scarce aligned multilingual supervision and cross-lingual representation dispersion due to language variation.

Method: MLJailDe combines three components: (1) a multilingual back-translation data augmentation algorithm producing a dataset of 2,232 benign and 1,239 jailbreak samples across 11 languages; (2) relative-distance constraints to reduce cross-lingual representation dispersion and cluster jailbreak prompts with similar intent; and (3) an imbalance-aware classification objective to handle class imbalance and learn reliable multilingual decision boundaries.

Result: MLJailDe outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across multiple languages, achieving an F1 score of 98.5% on seen languages and an average F1 score of 97.1% on unseen languages.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates strong multilingual robustness and cross-lingual generalization for jailbreak detection, effectively addressing the safety gap in non-dominant languages.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in applications for global multilingual users, yet safety training remains concentrated in dominant languages and has not progressed in parallel with multilingual capability, creating exploitable gaps for jailbreak attacks. Current jailbreak defenses are largely developed and evaluated in dominant languages, and their effectiveness is limited by the scarcity of aligned multilingual supervision and representations dispersion caused by language variation. To address this issue, we propose MLJailDe, a multilingual jailbreak detection framework designed to improve both multilingual robustness and cross-lingual generalization. MLJailDe first introduces a multilingual back-translation data augmentation algorithm to construct a semantically consistent and functionally effective dataset spanning 11 languages, consisting of 2,232 benign and 1,239 jailbreak samples. On this basis, MLJailDe employs relative-distance constraints to reduce cross-lingual representation dispersion and encourage jailbreak prompts with similar intent to form consistent clusters across languages, while an imbalance-aware classification objective is further used to alleviate class imbalance and learn more reliable multilingual decision boundaries. Experimental results show that MLJailDe outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across multiple languages, achieving an F1 score of 98.5\%, and obtains an average F1 score of 97.1\% on unseen languages, demonstrating strong effectiveness and cross-lingual generalization.

</details>


### [6] [LatticeBridge: Rare-Event Sequential Inference for Faithful Structured Sequence Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11203)
*Faruk Alpay,Bugra Kilictas*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LatticeBridge addresses structured sequence generation as a rare-event inference problem, using twisted SMC with instance-compiled automata to improve constraint satisfaction over standard decoding methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard decoding methods (greedy, beam search, ancestral sampling) fail to reliably generate outputs that satisfy multiple input-derived constraints simultaneously, because high-probability fluent continuations often miss required anchors. This is framed as a rare-event sequential inference problem.

Method: LatticeBridge proposes a twisted Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) decoder that combines: (1) a compact prefix language model as proposal, (2) instance-compiled surface automata for constraint representation (without manually curated lexical classes), (3) multilevel splitting with resampling, and (4) a source-support proposal term derived from instance-provided phrases. Constraints are compiled per input instance.

Result: On 2,610 validation tasks across CommonGen, E2E NLG, and WikiBio, the particle decoder improves exact anchor satisfaction and mean anchor coverage over greedy, beam-filtered, and best-of-k ancestral baselines under a shared proposal model. Evaluation includes required-anchor coverage, source coverage, source-intrusion diagnostics, overlap, runtime, and particle statistics.

Conclusion: The benchmark characterizes the faithfulness-overlap-latency frontier under a fixed proposal model, showing that SMC-based inference with instance-compiled constraints offers a principled way to navigate the trade-off between constraint satisfaction and generation quality.

Abstract: Structured sequence generation often requires a model to satisfy several input-derived constraints in a single output. Standard decoding methods may assign high probability to fluent continuations while placing low mass on continuations that realize all required anchors jointly. We study this regime as a rare-event sequential inference problem. LatticeBridge combines a compact prefix language model, instance-compiled surface automata, and a twisted sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) decoder with resampling, multilevel splitting, and a source-support proposal term derived from instance-provided phrases. The constraint representation is compiled from each input instance and does not rely on manually curated lexical classes. On 2,610 attainable validation tasks spanning CommonGen, E2E NLG, and WikiBio, the particle decoder improves exact anchor satisfaction and mean anchor coverage over greedy, beam-filtered, and best-of-k ancestral baselines under a shared proposal model. Since exact anchor satisfaction alone does not rule out unsupported attribute substitutions, the evaluation reports required-anchor coverage, source coverage, source-intrusion diagnostics, overlap, runtime, and particle statistics jointly. The benchmark characterizes the faithfulness-overlap-latency frontier under a fixed proposal model.

</details>


### [7] [Benchmarking Large Language Models for Safety Data Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11204)
*Jonas Grill,Thomas Bayer,Sören Berlinger*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Benchmarks four LLMs (Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Llama 3.1-70B) on SDS data extraction across three prompting strategies. Text-based pipelines outperform multimodal ones; Gemini 1.5 Pro + Chain-of-Thought achieves best accuracy (84%), but no model reaches the 90% threshold needed for reliable industrial deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Structured information extraction from Safety Data Sheets (SDS) is critical for industrial safety but is hindered by heterogeneous document formats and the inadequacy of traditional rule-based methods. The study aims to determine whether state-of-the-art LLMs can serve as a viable automated alternative.

Method: Systematic benchmarking of four LLMs (Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Llama 3.1-70B) using both text-based and multimodal processing pipelines across three prompting strategies: zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought (CoT). Evaluation covered accuracy, latency, and cost across 50,000+ extracted data fields.

Result: Text-based extraction consistently outperformed multimodal processing across all metrics. Gemini 1.5 Pro with CoT prompting achieved the highest accuracy (84%), followed by GPT-4o (81%) and Claude 3.7 Sonnet (79%). No model reached the 90% accuracy threshold required for reliable real-world deployment.

Conclusion: General-purpose LLMs are not yet robust enough for unsupervised industrial SDS extraction. However, performance is promising and suggests strong potential with task-specific fine-tuning, domain-adapted training, model calibration, and Human-in-the-Loop verification for safety-critical reliability.

Abstract: Accurate extraction of structured information from Safety Data Sheets (SDS) remains challenging in industrial safety due to heterogeneous document formats and the limitations of traditional rule-based methods. This study benchmarks state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) for automated SDS data extraction, comparing text-based and multimodal processing pipelines. We systematically evaluate four models: Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, and Llama 3.1-70B, across three prompting strategies: zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought. The evaluation framework assessed accuracy, latency, and cost across more than 50,000 extracted data fields. Results show that text-based extraction consistently outperforms multimodal processing across all metrics. Gemini 1.5 Pro combined with a Chain-of-Thought prompt achieved the highest accuracy (84%), outperforming GPT-4o (81%) and Claude 3.7 Sonnet (79%). However, no model surpassed the 90% accuracy threshold commonly required for reliable real-world deployment. These findings indicate that general-purpose LLMs are not yet robust enough for unsupervised industrial use, though performance suggests strong potential with task-specific fine-tuning. Future research should focus on domain-adapted training, model calibration, and the integration of Human-in-the-Loop verification to ensure safety-critical reliability.

</details>


### [8] [Compatibility-Aware Dynamic Fine-Tuning for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11206)
*Yucheng Zhou,Junwei Sheng,Qianning Wang,Jianbing Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CADFT extends Dynamic Fine-Tuning (DFT) by adding sample-level variance control via a compatibility signal from model likelihoods, suppressing high-variance gradients from mismatched demonstrations, and includes a rewriting strategy for persistently incompatible samples, yielding improved stability, generalization, and RL initialization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) exhibits optimization instability and poor generalization due to pathological gradient scaling. While DFT addresses this at the token level, it naively treats all demonstrations as equally suitable, ignoring the heterogeneity of large-scale instruction data where demonstration-policy mismatch causes high-variance sample-level updates.

Method: CADFT derives a dynamic, policy-dependent compatibility signal from model likelihoods to modulate supervised updates, suppressing high-variance gradients from incompatible demonstrations. It also introduces a delayed, low-frequency compatibility-guided rewriting strategy that transforms persistently incompatible demonstrations into learnable targets. The approach is framed as a variance-controlled estimator generalizing DFT's token-level stabilization to the sample level.

Result: Extensive experiments show that CADFT achieves improved training stability, better generalization, and superior cold-start reinforcement learning initialization compared to baselines, all while remaining fully supervised and independent of explicit reward models.

Conclusion: CADFT effectively extends DFT's token-level gradient correction to the sample level, addressing demonstration-policy mismatch through compatibility-aware modulation and rewriting. This principled variance-controlled approach enables more stable and generalizable LLM alignment within a purely supervised framework.

Abstract: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is the predominant paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs), yet it suffers from optimization instability and limited generalization. Recent work attributes this issue to pathological gradient scaling and proposes Dynamic Fine-Tuning (DFT) to correct it at the token level. However, DFT assumes all demonstrations are equally suitable learning targets, an assumption violated by the strong heterogeneity of large-scale instruction data, where demonstration-policy mismatch induces high-variance updates at the sample level. We introduce Compatibility-Aware Dynamic Fine-Tuning (CADFT), a principled extension of DFT that controls sample-level optimization variance. CADFT derives a dynamic, policy-dependent compatibility signal from model likelihoods to modulate supervised updates, suppressing high-variance gradients from incompatible demonstrations. We further propose a delayed, low-frequency compatibility-guided rewriting strategy to transform persistently incompatible demonstrations into learnable targets. We show that CADFT can be interpreted as a variance-controlled estimator that generalizes token-level stabilization in DFT to the sample level. Extensive experiments demonstrate improved stability, generalization, and cold-start reinforcement learning initialization, while remaining fully supervised and independent of explicit reward modeling.

</details>


### [9] [BioDivergence: A Benchmark and Evaluation Framework for Hidden Contextual Contradictions in Biomedical Abstracts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11208)
*Elias Hossain,Sanjeda Sara Jennifer,Sabera Akter Bushra,Niloofar Yousefi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BioDivergence introduces a framework for distinguishing context-dependent biomedical claim divergence from true contradictions, featuring a six-class conflict taxonomy, a 13-axis ontology, and structured outputs. The accompanying silver benchmark (11,865 pairs) reveals that article-disjoint evaluation reduces memorization artifacts, with Mistral-7B-Instruct achieving 0.5523 accuracy on the primary test set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NLI and scientific claim-verification benchmarks flatten nuanced biomedical disagreements into coarse entailment/contradiction/neutral labels, ignoring that many apparent conflicts are context-dependent (e.g., differing cohorts, protocols, disease subtypes). This obscures genuine contradictions and inflates performance through article-level memorization rather than true reasoning.

Method: The authors construct BioDivergence, comprising: (1) a six-class conflict taxonomy; (2) a 13-axis divergence ontology capturing contextual confounders (cohort, geography, assay, etc.); (3) four structured outputs per claim pair — conflict type, divergence axes, dominant confounder, and reconciliation explanation. They release BioDivergence-Silver-v1.0 (11,865 claim pairs across five biomedical domains) in two variants: an article-disjoint split preventing train-test leakage, and a legacy deduplicated version for comparison.

Result: Under the article-disjoint setting, the fine-tuned reference model drops approximately 12 points compared to the legacy split, demonstrating that prior approaches inflated performance via memorization. Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 attains 0.5523 accuracy and 0.3894 contextual-F1 on the 842-example primary test set, indicating substantial room for improvement on this more challenging, faithful evaluation.

Conclusion: BioDivergence provides a more ecologically valid framework for biomedical claim verification by explicitly modeling contextual divergence. The article-disjoint split is critical for separating genuine task learning from superficial pattern memorization, and current models — including fine-tuned LLMs — still struggle on this benchmark, highlighting a meaningful research gap.

Abstract: Biomedical findings often seem to conflict across studies, but many of these differences are context-dependent rather than true contradictions. Variations in cohort, geography, assay protocol, disease subtype, and clinical setting can make both claims locally valid. Existing NLI and scientific claim-verification benchmarks reduce such cases to entailment, contradiction, or neutral, failing to capture the contextual structure behind divergence. To address this, we introduce BioDivergence, an evaluation framework with a six-class conflict taxonomy, a 13-axis divergence ontology, and four structured outputs per claim pair: conflict type, divergence axes, dominant confounder, and reconciliation explanation. We release BioDivergence-Silver-v1.0, an article-disjoint silver benchmark of 11,865 claim pairs across five biomedical domains, alongside a legacy deduplicated variant for comparison. Results show notable ranking differences between the two variants, with the fine-tuned reference model dropping about 12 points under the article-disjoint setting, while Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 achieves 0.5523 accuracy and 0.3894 contextual-F1 on the 842-example primary test set. BioDivergence offers a more faithful way to distinguish contextual divergence from direct contradiction and to separate article-level memorization from genuine task learning.

</details>


### [10] [ProcessThinker: Enhancing Multi-modal Large Language Models Reasoning via Rollout-based Process Reward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11209)
*Jingpei Wu,Xiao Han,Weixiang Shen,Boer Zhang,Zifeng Ding,Volker Tresp*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ProcessThinker introduces a post-training pipeline that provides dense, step-level process rewards for multimodal reasoning without training an explicit process reward model. It rewrites reasoning traces into step-tagged formats, then uses rollout-based empirical success rates as step rewards during GRPO, improving logical consistency across four video reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR/GRPO approaches for multimodal reasoning rely on sparse outcome-only rewards, making it hard to distinguish whether errors stem from early trajectory flaws or late mistakes. Training a PRM for step-level supervision requires costly annotations. ProcessThinker addresses this gap by providing step-level rewards without needing a separately trained PRM.

Method: The pipeline has two stages: (1) Cold-start supervised fine-tuning on reasoning traces rewritten into a step-tagged format. (2) GRPO with a standard format reward plus a rollout-based process reward: for each intermediate step, multiple continuations are sampled and the empirical success rate (final-answer verification) serves as the step reward. This provides dense credit assignment favoring steps that reliably lead to correct conclusions.

Result: ProcessThinker consistently improves over the baseline Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct model across four challenging video benchmarks: Video-MMMU, MMVU, VideoMathQA, and LongVideoBench.

Conclusion: Dense step-level process rewards derived from rollout-based empirical success rates, without training an explicit PRM, effectively improve multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning by reducing inconsistent or self-contradictory progress across steps.

Abstract: Visual question answering increasingly requires multi-step reasoning. Recent post-training with reinforcement learning under verifiable rewards (RLVR) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) can improve multimodal reasoning, but most approaches rely on sparse outcome-only rewards. As a result, they struggle to tell whether an incorrect answer comes from a small mistake late in the reasoning or from an unhelpful trajectory from the start. A common solution is to train a process reward model (PRM) for step-level supervision, but this typically requires large-scale high-quality chain-of-thought annotations and additional training cost. We propose ProcessThinker, a practical post-training pipeline that provides step-level process rewards without training an explicit PRM. ProcessThinker first rewrites reasoning traces into a step-tagged format for cold-start supervised fine-tuning, then applies GRPO with a standard format reward and our rollout-based process reward. Concretely, for each intermediate step, we sample multiple continuations from that step and use the empirical success rate (final-answer verification) as the step reward. This gives dense credit assignment and encourages reasoning steps that more reliably support a correct conclusion, helping reduce inconsistent or self-contradictory progress across steps -- a key issue in logical reasoning. Across four challenging video benchmarks (Video-MMMU, MMVU, VideoMathQA, and LongVideoBench), ProcessThinker consistently improves over the baseline model Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct

</details>


### [11] [T2MM: An LLM Supported Architecture For Inquiry-Based Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11210)
*John Kos,Rudra Singh,Ashok Goel*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: T2MM is an LLM-supported architecture that generates interactive, user-adjustable models (not static images) within the VERA ecology modeling software, outperforming baseline code-generation approaches on all success metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model construction in science learning requires both visualization and interactivity. Current LLM-integrated educational tools lack the visual interactivity needed for certain learning contexts, particularly inquiry-based modeling environments where learners must dynamically adjust models.

Method: The authors propose Text to Multimodal Model (T2MM), an architecture integrated into the VERA (Virtual Experimental Research Assistant) ecology-based modeling platform. T2MM interprets natural language learner requests, considers the current state of the learner's model, and generates interactive, manipulable models rather than static images. Technical evaluation uses a custom procedurally generated dataset of natural language modeling requests paired with target models, comparing T2MM against a baseline LLM-supported full code generation architecture.

Result: T2MM outperforms the baseline full code generation architecture across all measured success metrics on the procedurally generated evaluation dataset.

Conclusion: The paper contributes both a concrete integration of LLMs into inquiry-based learning modeling tools and a generalizable architecture for building interactive multimodal LLM tools. T2MM demonstrates that context-aware, interactive model generation is technically feasible and superior to static or code-only generation approaches.

Abstract: Model Construction is a foundational practice in science learning that relies on visualization and interactivity. Large Language Models, increasingly augmented with multimodal capabilities, have been integrated in education contexts to support learning. However, these tools lack visual interactivity that is required by some learning contexts. We introduce Text to Multimodal Model (T2MM), a robust, dynamic LLM supported architecture that assists in model construction within the open inquiry ecology-based modeling software Virtual Experimental Research Assistant (VERA). T2MM accounts for the current context of the learner's model and creates interactive models, rather than static images, enabling the model to remain responsive to manual adjustment. To measure technical feasibility, we evaluate T2MM through a custom procedurally generated dataset of natural language learner modeling requests and target models within the VERA system. T2MM outperforms a baseline model generation architecture implemented through LLM-supported full code generation, common in the literature, across all measured success metrics. Our contribution not only outlines LLM integration into a inquiry-based learning modeling tool, but also describes a possible architecture through which more interactive multimodal LLM tools can be created.

</details>


### [12] [Calibration Drift Under Reasoning: How Chain-of-Thought Budgets Induce Overconfidence in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11211)
*Prakul Sunil Hiremath,Harshit R. Hiremath*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Longer chain-of-thought reasoning can cause LLMs to become systematically overconfident on incorrect answers—a phenomenon termed Calibration Drift Under Reasoning (CDUR). The paper introduces a Hypothesis Lock-In model to explain this and proposes CABStop, a calibration-aware stopping rule.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While CoT reasoning improves LLM accuracy, its effect on uncertainty calibration is poorly understood. Safe deployment requires well-calibrated confidence estimates, and the authors investigate whether more reasoning always improves calibration.

Method: The authors define a reasoning budget B and analyze ECE(B) theoretically, proposing a Hypothesis Lock-In model based on autoregressive generation. They empirically evaluate Llama-3.1-8B and Llama-3.3-70B on 47 reasoning-trap questions across four reasoning budgets and three seeds (1,368 API calls). They also introduce CABStop, a stopping rule that halts reasoning when confidence diverges from an auxiliary accuracy estimate.

Result: The 8B model exhibits non-monotonic calibration: ECE first decreases (reasoning corrects errors), then increases (longer reasoning produces internally consistent but incorrect explanations). The 70B results were inconclusive for budget-dependent effects.

Conclusion: Increasing reasoning depth does not always improve reliability. Calibration should be actively monitored, and stopping rules like CABStop can help mitigate overconfidence drift during extended reasoning.

Abstract: The ability of large language models (LLMs) to express calibrated uncertainty is important for safe deployment. Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is widely used to improve accuracy and reliability, but its effect on calibration is not fully understood. We show that this picture is incomplete: in some settings, increasing the reasoning budget beyond a task-specific threshold can cause models to become systematically overconfident, assigning high confidence to incorrect answers. We call this phenomenon Calibration Drift Under Reasoning (CDUR) and study it both theoretically and empirically.
  We define reasoning budget B and analyze conditions under which Expected Calibration Error ECE(B) follows a non-monotonic pattern: it first decreases as reasoning corrects errors, then increases as longer reasoning produces internally consistent but incorrect explanations. We propose a Hypothesis Lock-In model based on autoregressive generation to explain this behavior.
  We evaluate Llama-3.1-8B and Llama-3.3-70B on 47 reasoning-trap questions across four reasoning budgets and three seeds (1,368 API calls; 574 valid responses). The 8B model shows non-monotonic calibration behavior, while results for the 70B model are limited to baseline evaluation and are inconclusive for budget-dependent effects.
  We introduce CABStop, a calibration-aware stopping rule that halts reasoning when confidence diverges from an auxiliary accuracy estimate. These results suggest that increasing reasoning depth does not always improve reliability and should be monitored carefully.

</details>


### [13] [EverydayGPT: Confidence-Gated Routing for Efficient and Safe Hybrid GPT-RAG Conversational QA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11212)
*Jaspreet Singh Nahal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EverydayGPT introduces Confidence-Gated Routing (CGR), a mechanism that selectively invokes a GPT model only when retrieval confidence is low, achieving 120x latency reduction on 85% of queries with modest quality gains over unconditional RAG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RAG pipelines waste computation by routing every query through retrieval and generation, even when retrieval alone is sufficient. This incurs unnecessary latency and risks propagating low-quality context to the generator. The authors seek a resource-efficient routing strategy that balances speed and answer quality.

Method: The system uses a 205M-parameter GPT trained from scratch on 10B tokens of FineWeb-Edu. The CGR mechanism formalizes routing as a joint policy over two signals: retrieval distance (similarity-based) and extraction adequacy. Queries are resolved via fast RAG extraction (~45ms) when confidence is high; only low-confidence queries trigger the full GPT pathway (~5.9s).

Result: CGR avoids the costly GPT pathway for 85% of queries. On a 500-question in-domain benchmark, the system achieves F1=0.226±0.004 vs. GPT-only (0.171) and unconditional RAG (0.210). Mean latency is reduced 6.3x. A grounding audit found no unsupported claims, and the system explicitly states its scope limitations.

Conclusion: The work is positioned as a study of routing strategies under resource constraints rather than a state-of-the-art claim. The modest but consistent quality gains combined with substantial efficiency improvements demonstrate that confidence-gated routing is a viable approach for lightweight conversational QA systems.

Abstract: Standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines route every query through retrieval and generation unconditionally, incurring unnecessary computation and propagating low-quality context to the generator. We introduce EverydayGPT, a lightweight conversational QA system built around a Confidence-Gated Routing (CGR) mechanism that formalises the routing decision as a joint policy over retrieval distance and extraction adequacy. The backbone is a 205M-parameter GPT trained from scratch on 10B tokens of FineWeb-Edu. CGR avoids invoking the costly GPT pathway (~5.9s) for 85 percent of queries by resolving them via fast RAG extraction (~45 ms), yielding over 120x latency reduction on the majority of queries while maintaining answer quality. On a 500-question in-domain benchmark, the system achieves F1 = 0.226 +/- 0.004 compared to 0.171 for GPT-only and 0.210 for unconditional RAG. Gains over strong baselines are modest but consistent, while efficiency improvements are substantial (6.3x mean latency reduction). A structured grounding audit finds no unsupported claims in the sampled set, with explicit scope limitations. We position this work as a study of routing strategies under resource constraints rather than a claim of state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [14] [Beyond Compaction: Structured Context Eviction for Long-Horizon Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11213)
*Andrew Semenov,Svyatoslav Dorofeev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CWL is a context-management scheme that uses typed, dependency-linked episodes and a deterministic eviction policy to give LLM agents an effectively unbounded working horizon. It preserves active reasoning context while evicting action episodes whose effects are already persisted, outperforming both summarization-based and recency-based methods on long-horizon tasks (89 tasks, 80M tokens) with no accuracy degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents face a fundamental tension: long-horizon tasks accumulate history that exceeds context windows, but existing solutions — summarization (lossy, destroys causal structure, costly, hallucination-prone) and recency truncation (semantically blind) — each have critical flaws. There is a need for a context-management scheme that can maintain effectively unbounded working memory without degrading agent performance.

Method: CWL introduces three components: (1) an annotation protocol where the agent marks its trajectory as typed, dependency-linked episodes during work; (2) an episode graph that captures semantic and causal dependencies among these typed episodes; and (3) a deterministic, LLM-free eviction policy that, when a token budget is exceeded, evicts content in priority order — aggressively removing action episodes whose effects are already persisted in the environment while preserving user turns and active exploratory context. A token-accounting loop enforces the budget near a stable ceiling.

Result: In evaluation, a single agent session using CWL completed 89 sequential tasks spanning 80 million tokens with no measurable degradation in task accuracy compared to per-task isolated sessions, demonstrating that CWL maintains performance even at extreme scale.

Conclusion: CWL provides a practical, semantics-aware alternative to summarization and recency truncation for context management, enabling LLM agents to operate over unbounded horizons without the performance degradation typical of large prompts or the information loss of existing compaction methods.

Abstract: We present Context Window Lifecycle (CWL), a context-management scheme that gives long-horizon LLM agents an effectively unbounded working horizon. As a session accumulates history, CWL keeps the context within budget through graduated, semantically-aware eviction: the agent annotates its trajectory as typed, dependency-linked episodes as work proceeds, and a deterministic, LLM-free policy evicts content in priority order within that structure when a token budget is exceeded. CWL preserves user turns and the exploratory context the agent is actively reasoning over, while aggressively shedding action episodes whose effects are already persisted in the environment, keeping active context near a stable ceiling that also avoids the performance degradation associated with very large prompts.
  Compared to summarization-based compaction, CWL avoids four well-known limitations: unpredictable lossiness, destruction of causal structure, blocking model cost, and compression-induced hallucination. Compared to recency truncation, CWL is semantically aware: it drops the oldest-and-most-recoverable content according to the dependency graph rather than oldest-in-time regardless of relevance. We describe the annotation protocol, the episode graph, the eviction policy, and the token-accounting loop, and evaluate CWL on long-horizon agentic benchmarks: a single agent session completing 89 sequential tasks across 80 million tokens with no measurable degradation in task accuracy relative to per-task isolated sessions

</details>


### [15] [Afrispeech Semantics: Evaluating Audio Semantic Reasoning in Spoken Language Models Across Domains and Accents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11219)
*Chibuzor Okocha,Christan Grant*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Audio language models lack rigorous evaluation for semantic reasoning beyond transcription. This paper introduces five tasks—entailment, consistency, plausibility, accent drift, and accent restraint—to assess how well ALMs reason over spoken audio, revealing critical limitations related to accent variation, domain shift, and semantic over-inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ALM benchmarks focus narrowly on transcription, captioning, retrieval, and QA accuracy, neglecting deeper semantic reasoning capabilities. The effects of accent variation, domain shift, and semantic over-inference on audio reasoning are poorly understood, creating a gap in evaluating whether models can reliably use spoken audio as primary evidence for inference.

Method: The authors propose a five-task evaluation framework: (1) entailment—determining whether a textual hypothesis is inferred, contradicted, or undetermined by audio; (2) consistency—assessing alignment between statements and spoken content; (3) plausibility—judging whether claims are plausible given the discourse; (4) accent drift—measuring prediction stability across accent variations; and (5) accent restraint—evaluating appropriate constraint of predictions despite accent differences. These tasks collectively test semantic and paralinguistic reasoning with spoken audio as the primary evidence.

Result: The evaluation reveals critical limitations in current ALMs' audio reasoning abilities, particularly regarding robustness to accent variation, susceptibility to domain shift, and tendencies toward semantic over-inference where models draw conclusions unsupported by the audio evidence.

Conclusion: Current ALM evaluation practices are insufficient for capturing semantic reasoning competence. The proposed task suite exposes significant weaknesses and provides guidance for developing more robust, equitable ALM design and assessment methodologies that account for accent diversity and reasoning fidelity.

Abstract: Audio language models (ALMs) are increasingly used for speech-based understanding, yet their ability to perform semantic reasoning beyond transcription, Text-to-Audio Retrieval, Captioning, and Question-Answering accuracy remains insufficiently benchmarked. In particular, the effects of accent variation, domain shift, and semantic over-inference on audio reasoning are poorly understood. We evaluate audio language models across five semantic and paralinguistic reasoning tasks: entailment, consistency, plausibility, accent drift, and accent restraint. Collectively, these tasks assess a model's ability to reason over spoken audio as the primary evidence source, including whether a textual hypothesis can be inferred, contradicted, or left undetermined by the audio, whether statements align or conflict with spoken content, whether claims are plausible given the discourse, and whether model predictions remain stable or appropriately constrained across accent variation. These findings highlight critical limitations in current audio reasoning evaluations and hope to provide guidance for more robust and equitable ALM design and assessment

</details>


### [16] [LifeSentence: Language models can encode human life course trajectories from longitudinal panel data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11220)
*Samuel Liu,Muchen Xi,William Yeoh,Joshua J. Jackson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LifeSentence is a 24B-parameter language model instruction-tuned on structured life-event records from ~65,000 individuals in the German Socio-Economic Panel. It outperforms classical and deep learning baselines across 18 prediction, robustness, and reasoning tasks, achieves 91.2% Kendall's tau in chronological reconstruction, and recovers social stratification patterns without explicit supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional statistical approaches for forecasting human life outcomes have limited accuracy because they discard the sequential structure of life courses. Modern transformer-based methods require large-scale training data that most longitudinal panel studies lack. The authors aim to bridge this gap by leveraging pretrained LLM knowledge to work effectively with modestly sized panel data.

Method: The authors introduce LifeSentence, which represents each life event as a structured natural-language sentence and instruction-tunes a pretrained 24B-parameter language model on data from ~65,000 individuals in the German Socio-Economic Panel (SOEP). The model is evaluated across an 18-task taxonomy covering prediction, robustness, and reasoning. Training data is roughly 45 times smaller than prior transformer-based approaches.

Result: LifeSentence outperforms both classical and deep learning baselines across all task families, with a threefold improvement in joint event-and-timing prediction over the best baselines. It achieves 91.2% Kendall's tau when reconstructing chronological order from timestamp-stripped event sequences. Without explicit supervision, the model recovers documented social stratification patterns (education premium, gender wage gap, motherhood penalty). The natural-language interface enables counterfactual queries linking early-life histories to late-life endpoints.

Conclusion: LifeSentence demonstrates that instruction-tuning a large pretrained language model on structured life-event sequences enables effective life-course reasoning with relatively small panel datasets. It serves as both a predictive tool for individual life outcomes and a probe for counterfactual exploration of human biographies, opening new research avenues in social science and health forecasting.

Abstract: Forecasting human life outcomes is important to gain insights into how individuals attain long and healthy lives. Conventional statistical approaches yield limited accuracy, potentially due to discarding the sequential structure of the life course. Modern methods such as transformer architectures require large scale training data that most longitudinal panel studies lack. Here we introduce LifeSentence, a model for life-course reasoning that bridges large language models with longitudinal panel data. By representing each life event as a structured natural-language record and instruction-tuning a pretrained 24-billion-parameter language model across an 18-task evaluation taxonomy spanning prediction, robustness and reasoning, LifeSentence supplements panel data with distributional knowledge already encoded during pretraining. Trained on approximately 65,000 individuals from the German Socio-Economic Panel - roughly 45 times fewer than prior transformer-based approaches - LifeSentence outperforms classical and deep learning baselines across all task families, achieving a threefold improvement in joint event-and-timing prediction from best baselines and 91.2% Kendall's tau when reconstructing chronological order from timestamp-stripped event sets. Without explicit supervision, the model recovers documented patterns of social stratification, including the education premium, the gender wage gap and the motherhood penalty, from discrete event sequences alone. A natural-language interface further enables qualitatively new research queries, such as connecting an early-life history to a specified late-life endpoint, establishing LifeSentence as both a predictive tool and a probe for counterfactual exploration of human biographies.

</details>


### [17] [When More Documents Hurt RAG: Mitigating Vector Search Dilution with Domain-Scoped, Model-Agnostic Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11350)
*Nabaraj Subedi,Ahmed Abdelaty,Shivanand Venkanna Sheshappanavar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Scaling RAG to large document collections causes "vector search dilution," where top-k retrieval returns contextually incorrect results. The authors propose MASDR-RAG, showing that domain scoping via organizational metadata is the key fix, improving retrieval metrics significantly. They also identify a precision-faithfulness paradox in multi-agent orchestration and recommend scoping first with single synthesis, reserving multi-agent setups for truly multi-domain corpora.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) performance degrades substantially when scaled to large, heterogeneous document collections. Dense similarity loses discriminative power, and top-k retrieval returns semantically similar but contextually incorrect chunks—a phenomenon the authors term "vector search dilution." This was empirically observed in a real-world deployment where scaling from 54 to 1,128 documents caused accuracy to drop from 75% to below 40%, even with hybrid dense+sparse retrieval.

Method: The authors propose MASDR-RAG (Multi-Agent Scoped Domain Retrieval for RAG), which leverages organizational metadata to perform domain scoping prior to retrieval. They evaluate the approach on 200 expert-validated queries across five LLM backbones, six corpora, and two index stacks. The study systematically investigates both domain scoping and multi-agent orchestration configurations to isolate their individual and combined effects on retrieval and generation quality.

Result: Domain scoping using organizational metadata significantly improved P@10 from 0.77 to 0.86 (p < 0.05). The investigation into multi-agent orchestration revealed high configuration dependence, manifesting as a "precision-faithfulness paradox" where different setups trade off between these two qualities in conflicting ways.

Conclusion: The practical recommendation is to scope first using domain metadata, then perform a single synthesis call. Full multi-agent orchestration should be reserved for genuinely multi-domain corpora paired with native-tool-call-capable LLM backbones. Code and data will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation degrades when scaled to large, heterogeneous document collections, where dense similarity loses discriminative power, and top-k retrieval increasingly returns semantically similar but contextually incorrect chunks. We refer to this failure mode as vector search dilution. Even when using hybrid dense+sparse retrieval, we observed this firsthand in a deployed Wyoming Department of Transportation corpus, where scaling from 54 to 1,128 documents (88,907 chunks) reduced accuracy from 75% to below 40%. To address this dilution, we propose MASDR-RAG ( Multi-Agent Scoped Domain Retrieval for RAG) and evaluate it on 200 expert-validated queries across five LLM backbones, six corpora, and two index stacks. Our results indicate that domain scoping using organizational metadata is the key fix, significantly improving P@10 from 0.77 to 0.86 ($p < 0.05$). Furthermore, our investigation of multi-agent orchestration revealed that a high degree of configuration dependence results --creating what we call the precision-faithfulness paradox. Based on these varied outcomes, our practical recommendation is simple: scope first, then perform a single synthesis call, reserving full multi-agent orchestration for genuinely multi-domain corpora paired with native-tool-call backbones. Code and Data will be made public upon acceptance.

</details>


### [18] [A Geometric Profile of Semantic Information in Text: Frame-Conditional Uniqueness and a Trade-Off Triangle for Scalar Summaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11222)
*Dmitriy Kompaneets*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper develops a geometric framework to measure semantic content from sentence embeddings, axiomatizing a scalar measure, proposing a three-coordinate semantic profile (novelty, breadth, integration), and proving a no-go theorem showing no single scalar can satisfy three desirable properties simultaneously. Two practical scalars are offered, validated across synthetic and real-world texts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shannon information theory is indifferent to meaning, and pairwise metrics like BERTScore require comparing two texts. There is a gap: no principled, intrinsic measure of how much semantic content a single text carries. The authors aim to fill this gap with a geometrically grounded framework.

Method: The framework proceeds in three parts: (1) Within a fixed embedding space and baseline, six axioms uniquely determine a scalar semantic content measure (up to scale), yielding a frame-conditional uniqueness theorem. (2) A richer three-coordinate profile is proposed — novelty (displacement from generic discourse baseline), breadth (diversity of distinct ideas via clustering), and integration (connectedness among clusters) — along with a minimal unit called the semantic quantum. (3) A no-go theorem proves no scalar summary can simultaneously satisfy analytic stability, ordinal robustness across scales, and cross-representation comparability. Two scalars, S_minmax and S_rank, are derived occupying different trade-off corners. Breadth is further connected to the log-determinant of a determinantal point process via a variational result.

Result: Validation on 23 synthetic categories, 5 Project Gutenberg novels, and 3 embedding models confirms the trade-off. The rank-normalized configuration passes 25/28 ordinal checks as point estimates (21/28 after Benjamini-Hochberg correction), outperforming seven baselines. Breadth correlates with the log-determinant of a DPP at Spearman ρ=0.985 over 507 Gutenberg chapters.

Conclusion: The geometric framework provides a principled, intrinsic measure of semantic content, with the three-coordinate profile offering richer characterization than any single scalar. The no-go theorem delineates fundamental trade-offs, and the rank-normalized scalar emerges as the recommended practical configuration, validated across diverse texts and embedding models.

Abstract: How much meaning does a text carry? Shannon's theory measures uncertainty over symbols and is intentionally indifferent to meaning, while pairwise metrics such as BERTScore compare two texts rather than characterizing one. We develop a geometric framework that measures semantic content from the structure of a text's sentence embeddings.
  The framework has three parts. First, within a fixed embedding and baseline, six natural axioms uniquely determine a scalar measure up to scale, a frame-conditional uniqueness theorem. The resulting scalar is empirically too coarse, motivating a richer representation. Second, we propose a three-coordinate semantic profile capturing novelty (displacement from generic discourse), breadth (diversity of distinct ideas), and integration (connectedness among them), together with a discrete minimal unit (the semantic quantum) whose resolution is fixed by a clustering threshold $τ$. Third, we prove a no-go theorem: no scalar summary of the profile can simultaneously satisfy analytic stability under paraphrase and concatenation, ordinal robustness across text scales, and cross-representation comparability. We exhibit two practical scalars, $S_{\mathrm{minmax}}$ and $S_{\mathrm{rank}}$, each occupying a distinct corner of this trade-off triangle.
  Validation across 23 synthetic categories, 5 Project Gutenberg novels, and 3 embedding models confirms the trade-off. The recommended rank-normalized configuration passes 25 of 28 ordinal checks as point estimates (21 of 28 after Benjamini-Hochberg correction), outperforming seven baselines including unigram entropy and a BERTScore-based novelty signal. A separate variational result connects the breadth coordinate to the log-determinant of a determinantal point process (Spearman $ρ= 0.985$ over 507 Gutenberg chapters), giving an optimization-theoretic foundation for breadth.

</details>


### [19] [Every Act Has Its Price: Compressed Moral Composition in Frontier LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11232)
*Weijia Zhang,Ruiqi Chen,Yunze Xiao,Weihao Xuan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces Moral Trolley Arena, a two-stage ELO benchmark measuring how LLMs compose multiple moral signals. Findings show composite moral judgments are compressed (not additive), with intensity anchoring, bounded foundation-specific residuals, and convergent preference surfaces across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM moral benchmarks only measure isolated moral preferences (which act, value, or foundation a model prefers), failing to capture how models combine multiple moral signals within a single judgment — a requirement for realistic moral reasoning.

Method: A two-stage blind ELO benchmark: (1) Single-scene arena calibrates individual moral acts across five Moral Foundations Theory foundations using a 229-scenario corpus; (2) Composite arena combines calibrated acts into two-act moral items over a controlled intensity grid and measures resulting composite preferences. Ten frontier models are evaluated.

Result: Composite judgments are largely predicted by component act strength but with consistent compression (not simple additivity). Additional findings include non-additive intensity anchoring, bounded foundation-specific residuals after controlling for components, and highly convergent composite preference surfaces across providers.

Conclusion: Moral audits of LLMs should measure composition rules for integrating moral evidence, not merely rankings over isolated acts. The field needs to move beyond single-signal moral benchmarking to capture how models trade off and combine competing moral considerations.

Abstract: Existing LLM moral benchmarks usually ask which isolated moral act, value, or foundation a model prefers. This is useful but incomplete. Realistic judgments often require a model to combine several moral signals within the same option. We introduce **Moral Trolley Arena**, a two-stage blind ELO benchmark for measuring how LLMs compose moral evidence. The single-scene arena first calibrates individual moral acts from a 229-scenario corpus across five Moral Foundations Theory foundations; the composite arena then combines calibrated acts into two-act moral items over a controlled intensity grid and measures the resulting composite preferences. Across ten frontier models, composite judgments are largely predicted by component act strength, but the relation is consistently compressed rather than simply additive. Models also show non-additive intensity anchoring, bounded foundation-specific residuals after component control, and highly convergent composite preference surfaces across providers. These results suggest that moral audits should measure composition rules for moral evidence, not only rankings over isolated acts.

</details>


### [20] [uva-irlab-conv at SemEval-2026 Task 8: Multi-Turn RAG with Learned Sparse Retrieval and Listwise Reranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11945)
*Simon Lupart,Kidist Amde Mekonnen,Zahra Abbasiantaeb,Mohammad Aliannejadi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The authors propose a multi-turn RAG pipeline for SemEval-2026 Task 8 that uses learned sparse retrieval with LLM-based query rewriting, reranking, and generation, emphasizing cross-domain robustness and handling of unanswerable queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-turn conversational question answering across diverse domains (finance, cloud docs, government, Wikipedia) poses challenges in effectively integrating conversational history and handling unanswerable queries where evidence is insufficient. Existing approaches may lack robustness when applied across heterogeneous domains.

Method: A multi-step retrieval-augmented generation pipeline: (1) learned sparse retrieval as the primary retrieval method for cross-domain generalization; (2) LLM-based conversational query rewriting conditioned on full dialogue history; (3) pointwise and listwise reranking using LLMs with long-context capabilities; (4) final response generation also conditioned on the complete conversational context.

Result: The paper is a task description/systems report and does not present quantitative experimental results. It describes participation in SemEval-2026 Task 8 and the proposed system architecture.

Conclusion: The multi-step design effectively integrates conversational context throughout retrieval and generation, improving robustness across domains while addressing the challenge of unanswerable queries.

Abstract: This report describes our participation in SemEval-2026 Task 8 on multi-turn retrieval and question answering. The task evaluates conversational systems across four domains (finance, cloud documentation, government, Wikipedia), and includes unanswerable queries where the available collection does not contain sufficient evidence to produce a complete response. We propose a multi-turn retrieval-augmented generation pipeline that combines learned sparse retrieval with LLM-based reranking and generation. Using sparse retrieval as the primary retrieval method, we leverage its strong generalization across domains. In addition, we make use of the long-context capabilities of LLMs for conversational query rewriting, pointwise and listwise reranking, and generating the final response, each conditioned on the full conversational history. This multi-step design enables effective integration of conversational context throughout retrieval and generation, improving robustness across domains.

</details>


### [21] [Energy-Efficient On-Device RAG on a Mobile NPU: System Design and Benchmark on Snapdragon X Elite](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11257)
*Zhiyuan Cheng,Longying Lai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: First end-to-end on-device RAG pipeline running all neural stages (embedding, reranking, LLM generation) on a Qualcomm Hexagon NPU, demonstrating 4× lower latency and 4× less energy vs. CPU with no answer quality regression, enabling practical green edge intelligence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG pipelines are compute-intensive and running them on-device for privacy, latency, and offline use is hindered by the high energy cost of CPU inference. NPU acceleration offers a potential solution but had not been demonstrated for complete end-to-end RAG pipelines.

Method: The authors implemented an end-to-end RAG pipeline running embedding, reranking, and LLM generation entirely on the Qualcomm Hexagon NPU of the Snapdragon X Elite SoC. Profiling was conducted on a Dell XPS 13 laptop, comparing NPU-accelerated RAG against CPU and OpenCL/Adreno GPU baselines on both indexing and query workloads (120-query Wikipedia-passage benchmark). Answer quality was evaluated using GPT-4.1 as an LLM-as-judge on a 1–10 rubric.

Result: Indexing: NPU achieves 9.1× higher embedding throughput and 12.3× less system energy vs. CPU. Query workload: 18.1× faster LLM prefilling, 4.0× lower end-to-end query latency, and 4.0× less system energy vs. CPU. GPU baseline is 1.7× slower than CPU and uses 6.5× more energy than NPU. Answer quality: NPU (mean 9.32) on par with CPU (8.95) and GPU (9.03); 86.7% of queries scored identically across all three backends.

Conclusion: The Hexagon NPU enables practical, energy-efficient on-device RAG without quality regression. This represents a sustainable path toward green edge intelligence, expected to generalize to comparable mobile NPUs (Apple Neural Engine, Intel NPU, MediaTek APU) as their software stacks mature.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines are compute-intensive, combining embedding, retrieval, reranking, and large language model (LLM) generation. Running them entirely on-device benefits privacy, latency, and offline use, but the energy cost of CPU inference is a major barrier. We present what is, to our knowledge, the first end-to-end RAG pipeline that runs all neural stages -- embedding, reranking, and LLM generation -- on the Qualcomm Hexagon NPU of the Snapdragon X Elite. Profiling on a Dell XPS 13 laptop, we compare NPU-accelerated RAG against CPU and OpenCL/Adreno GPU baselines on indexing and query workloads. On indexing, the NPU achieves 9.1x higher embedding throughput and 12.3x less system energy. On a 120-query Wikipedia-passage benchmark, it delivers 18.1x faster LLM prefilling, 4.0x lower end-to-end query latency, and 4.0x less system energy than the CPU baseline; the same workload on the integrated GPU is 1.7x slower than CPU and uses 6.5x more energy than the NPU. A GPT-4.1 LLM-as-judge evaluation finds NPU answer quality on par with CPU and GPU within evaluator noise (mean 9.32 vs. 8.95 vs. 9.03 on a 1-10 rubric), with 86.7% of queries scoring identically across all three backends. On the Snapdragon X Elite / Hexagon class of laptop SoC, the NPU thus enables practical, energy-efficient on-device RAG without quality regression -- a sustainable path toward green edge intelligence that we expect to generalize to comparable mobile NPUs (Apple Neural Engine, Intel NPU, MediaTek APU) as their software stacks mature.

</details>


### [22] [Doc-to-Atom: Learning to Compile and Compose Memory Atoms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12400)
*Xingjian Diao,Wenbo Li,Yashas Malur Saidutta,Avinash Amballa,Lazar Valkov,Srinivas Chappidi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Doc2Atom decomposes documents into semantically typed "knowledge atoms" (micro-LoRA adapters with retrieval keys), then uses a query router to assemble query-specific adapters at inference time, outperforming monolithic adapter approaches while reducing memory costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monolithic adapter approaches like Doc-to-LoRA suffer from irrelevant-query interference, limited compositional recall, and poor scalability for long-document reasoning. A more fine-grained, composable parametric memory is needed to selectively retrieve and combine only the relevant knowledge per query.

Method: Doc2Atom decomposes each document into semantically typed knowledge atoms, each compiled into an independent micro-LoRA adapter paired with a provenance retrieval key. At inference, a lightweight query router selects and assembles relevant atoms into a query-specific adapter, which is injected into a frozen base LLM. The entire system is trained end-to-end via a multi-objective distillation framework.

Result: Experiments on six diverse QA benchmarks show Doc2Atom outperforms Doc-to-LoRA baselines while reducing the memory cost of document internalization.

Conclusion: Compositional parametric memory via typed knowledge atoms with query-driven routing is an effective and efficient strategy for context distillation, improving both performance and scalability over monolithic adapter methods.

Abstract: Long input sequences are central to document understanding and multi-step reasoning in Large Language Models, yet the quadratic cost of attention makes inference both memory-intensive and slow. Context distillation mitigates this by compressing contextual information into model parameters, and recent work such as Doc-to-LoRA amortizes context distillation into a single forward pass that generates one LoRA adapter per document. However, producing a single monolithic adapter for all queries leads to irrelevant-query interference, limited compositional recall, and poor scalability to long-document reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose Doc-to-Atom (Doc2Atom), a compositional parametric memory framework that decomposes each document into semantically typed knowledge atoms. Each atom is compiled into an independent micro-LoRA adapter and a provenance retrieval key. At inference time, a lightweight query router selects and assembles only the relevant atoms into a query-specific adapter, which is then injected into a frozen base model. The entire system is trained end-to-end through a multi-objective distillation framework. Experiments on six diverse QA benchmarks demonstrate that Doc2Atom outperforms Doc-to-LoRA baselines while reducing the memory cost of document internalization.

</details>


### [23] [Schützen: Evaluating LLM Safety in Bulgarian and German Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11316)
*Kiril Georgiev,Yuxia Wang,Dimitar Iliyanov Dimitrov,Preslav Nakov,Ivan Koychev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Schützen, a German-Bulgarian safety evaluation dataset for assessing LLM safety across a high-resource and a low-resource language within shared European sociocultural contexts. Experiments reveal significant cross-language safety disparities, underscoring the need for region-specific safety benchmarks beyond English and Chinese.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM safety evaluation datasets are heavily English- and Chinese-centric, neglecting languages that share regional legal, ethical, and sociocultural frameworks. This gap is critical for responsible LLM deployment in European contexts like Germany and Bulgaria, where cross-lingual safety behavior remains poorly understood.

Method: The authors construct Schützen, a bilingual German-Bulgarian dataset for safety evaluation, covering both refusal (answerability under risk) and harmfulness dimensions. They benchmark multilingual and language-specific LLMs on this dataset to measure cross-language safety alignment and identify performance disparities.

Result: Experiments reveal pronounced cross-language differences in safety behavior: models exhibit inconsistent refusal patterns and harmfulness across German and Bulgarian, even when sharing regional sociocultural contexts. Language-specific and multilingual models both show significant safety gaps for the low-resource language (Bulgarian).

Conclusion: The findings highlight the necessity of tailored, region-specific safety evaluation resources. Off-the-shelf multilingual models cannot be assumed to transfer safety alignment equitably across languages, particularly for lower-resource ones, necessitating dedicated datasets like Schützen for responsible deployment.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed across professional domains, bringing hard-to-predict risks, including the generation of harmful or disrespectful content. Although substantial progress has been made in developing safety evaluation datasets, existing resources remain overwhelmingly English- and Chinese-centric. This limitation is particularly pronounced when evaluating languages that operate within shared sociocultural, legal, and ethical contexts. To address this gap, we introduce Schützen: a German--Bulgarian safety dataset designed to assess model answerability under risk, covering both a low-resource language (Bulgarian) and a high-resource language (German). Experiments with multilingual and language-specific LLMs reveal pronounced cross-language differences in safety behavior, highlighting the necessity of tailored, region-specific evaluation resources to support the responsible deployment of LLMs in Germany and Bulgaria. Datasets and code are available at https://github.com/xnlp-lab/Schutzen. Warning: this paper contains examples that may be offensive, harmful, or biased.

</details>


### [24] [The Dynamics of Human and AI-Generated Language: How Semantics Fluctuates across Different Timescales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11371)
*Han-Jen Chang,Yasir Çatal,Angelika Wolman,Agustín Ibáñez,David Smith,I-Wen Su,Kai-Yuan Cheng,Georg Northoff*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a semantic-timescale analysis pipeline that converts timestamped word-level transcripts into semantic time-series, then uses autocorrelation-window (ACW) metrics to quantify how generic versus specific content is temporally organized. Applied to human, TTS, and LLM-generated speech, longer ACW-0 segments correlate with generic vocabulary and shorter ACW-0 with specific words — effects abolished by shuffling, confirming non-trivial temporal structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
