Motivation: There is a lack of simple, interpretable time-series features that capture how generic versus specific semantic content is distributed over time in spoken narratives, and no established metrics for comparing temporal semantic organization between human and AI-generated speech.

Method: The pipeline (1) transforms timestamped word-level transcripts into two semantic time-series: WordNet-based semantic specificity (word depth) and SBERT-based contextual similarity; (2) computes temporal dependence via autocorrelation-window measures (ACW-0 and related metrics); (3) compares original speech against multiple shuffled controls that selectively randomize lexical identity, temporal order, and word duration; (4) evaluates across human-read autobiographical narratives, TTS readings, and LLM-generated texts rendered with TTS.

Result: Segments with longer ACW-0 tend to contain more generic vocabulary, while segments with shorter ACW-0 are enriched in more specific words. These associations are strongly attenuated or abolished when word order and timing are randomized, indicating ACW captures non-trivial temporal organization beyond static lexical distributions.

Conclusion: ACW-based semantic timescale measures are a useful and interpretable family of features for analyzing and comparing the temporal structure of semantic content in both human and AI-generated speech.

Abstract: Spoken language, whether produced by humans or large language models (LLM), unfolds over time with varying semantic content. However, we still lack simple, interpretable time-series features that capture how generic versus specific content is distributed over time, and that can be used to compare human and AI-generated speech. We introduce a semantic-timescale analysis pipeline that turns word-level transcripts with timestamps into semantic time-series. For each spoken narrative, we compute (i) semantic specificity using WordNet-based word depth and (ii) contextual similarity using SBERT embeddings and quantify their temporal dependence using autocorrelation-window measures (ACW-0 and related metrics). We then compare original speech to multiple shuffled controls that selectively disrupt lexical identity, temporal order, and word duration. Across human-read autobiographical narratives, TTS readings, and LLM-generated texts rendered with TTS, we find that segments with longer ACW-0 in the semantic time-series tend to contain more generic vocabulary, whereas segments with shorter ACW-0 are enriched in more specific words. These associations are strongly attenuated or abolished when word order and timing are randomized, indicating that ACW-based measures capture non-trivial temporal organization of semantic content beyond static lexical distributions. Our results suggest that ACW-based semantic timescales are a useful family of features for analyzing and comparing the temporal structure of human and AI-generated speech.

</details>


### [25] [When Probing Accuracy Saturates, Fragility Resolves: A Complementary Metric for LLM Pre-Training Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11375)
*Orion Reblitz-Richardson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Probe accuracy saturates early in training, making it blind to ongoing representational changes. The authors propose "fragility" — the noise level at which probe accuracy collapses — as a complementary metric that captures margin of separability and redundancy, revealing evolving structure in moral representations, layer-depth gradients, and fine-tuning fingerprints that accuracy alone misses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard linear probing declares a property "encoded" once classifier accuracy on hidden states is high, but this accuracy plateaus within the first few thousand training steps, rendering most of pretraining invisible. The authors seek a complementary metric that remains sensitive to representational change even after accuracy saturates.

Method: Introduce "fragility": for each layer, add isotropic Gaussian noise of varying magnitudes to activations and measure the noise level at which probe accuracy drops below a threshold. Fragility captures both the classification margin and representational redundancy. Applied across open-checkpoint LMs, tracking both accuracy and fragility over training, and testing transfer of compositional moral encoding across construction types with no shared contrast tokens.

Result: Fragility recovers structure where accuracy is flat: (1) Moralized representations follow a lexical→compositional gradient (lexical moral detection emerges first, compositional encoding later); (2) a layer-depth robustness gradient develops monotonically across training; (3) matched fine-tuning corpora with identical probe accuracy yield distinct fragility fingerprints, showing data curation shapes robustness without affecting accuracy.

Conclusion: Fragility reveals that representations continue evolving long after probe accuracy saturates. In every comparison tested, where probing accuracy returns a flat answer, fragility returns a structured one, establishing it as a valuable complementary diagnostic for interpretability research.

Abstract: Standard linear probing declares a property "encoded" when a classifier on hidden states achieves high accuracy. The protocol works well on a snapshot but breaks across pre-training: probe accuracy saturates within the first few thousand steps, leaving most of training invisible to the instrument. We introduce fragility, a complementary per-layer metric defined as the activation-noise level at which probe accuracy collapses. Fragility is sensitive to both the margin of separability and the redundancy of representation, both of which keep evolving long after accuracy plateaus. Applied to open-checkpoint language models, fragility recovers structure that accuracy alone cannot see. Moralized representations emerge along a lexical $\to$ compositional gradient: lexical moral detection first, compositional moral encoding later. Because probe accuracy on its own tracks how lexically separable a dataset is, we establish the compositional encoding directly, by showing it transfers across construction types that share no contrast tokens. A layer-depth robustness gradient develops monotonically across training while accuracy stays flat. And matched fine-tuning corpora that produce identical probing accuracy leave distinct fragility fingerprints, showing that data curation reshapes probe robustness without changing probe accuracy. In every comparison we test, where probing accuracy returns a flat answer, fragility returns a structured one.

</details>


### [26] [Overcoming State Inertia in Full-Duplex Spoken Language Models via Activation Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11386)
*Cheng-Kuang Chang,Kai-Wei Chang,Alexander H. Liu,James Glass*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FD-SLMs have distinct internal predictive states for listening vs. speaking, but state transitions lag during user interruptions ("state inertia"), causing comprehension failures. The authors introduce the Zero-Buffer Benchmark to measure this and propose activation steering with a perception vector to mitigate the problem, achieving significant gains without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full-duplex spoken language models coordinate simultaneous listening and speaking, but the internal mechanisms governing this coordination are underexplored. Understanding and improving how these models handle abrupt user interruptions is critical for natural spoken interaction.

Method: The authors analyze hidden representations of FD-SLMs, identifying stream-specific predictive patterns (generative vs. perceptive states). They introduce the Zero-Buffer Benchmark (ZBB) to evaluate immediate interruption comprehension using response correctness and Initial-Word Occurrence Rate (IWOR). To mitigate the discovered state inertia, they employ activation steering with a perception vector—a training-free intervention that nudges the model toward the perceptive state during interruptions.

Result: FD-SLMs exhibit delayed internal transitions (state inertia) when users interrupt, causing them to miss the beginning of incoming speech. The proposed Zero-Buffer Benchmark effectively diagnoses this failure mode. Activation steering with a perception vector substantially improves interruption handling across multiple state-of-the-art FD-SLMs; on PersonaPlex, correctness improves from 28% to 45% and IWOR from 40% to 72%.

Conclusion: State inertia is a key failure mode in FD-SLMs during user interruptions. Activation steering offers an effective, lightweight, training-free solution to realign the model's predictive focus, significantly enhancing real-time interruption comprehension and enabling more robust full-duplex spoken dialogue.

Abstract: Full-duplex spoken language models (FD-SLMs) enable seamless speech interaction by allowing models to listen and speak simultaneously, yet the internal mechanism by which they coordinate listening and speaking remains underexplored. We analyze the predictive behavior encoded in FD-SLM hidden representations and find that they exhibit stream-specific predictive patterns: during listening, they preferentially predict the incoming user stream, whereas during speaking, they preferentially predict the model output stream. Building on this observation, we show that FD-SLMs dynamically modulate their internal predictive focus between two states: a generative state aligned with model output generation and a perceptive state aligned with incoming user input. However, this modulation can lag behind abrupt changes in conversational context. During user interruptions, the model remains transiently biased toward the generative state before transitioning into the perceptive state, causing it to miss the beginning of the incoming input. We term this delayed internal transition state inertia. To quantify its downstream impact, we introduce the Zero-Buffer Benchmark (ZBB), a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating immediate interruption comprehension when user speech begins abruptly. We evaluate this setting using response correctness and initial-word occurrence rate (IWOR). Finally, we mitigate state inertia through activation steering with a perception vector, a training-free intervention with little additional computational overhead. Across multiple state-of-the-art FD-SLMs, activation steering substantially improves interruption handling; for example, on PersonaPlex, it improves correctness from 28% to 45% and IWOR from 40% to 72% without any fine-tuning.

</details>


### [27] [Small Experiments, Cheaper Decisions: A Case Study in Staged Promotion for Micro-Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11387)
*Felipe Chavarro Polania*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents an auditable staged-promotion protocol for micro-pretraining that uses fixed rules to screen configurations across increasing budget stages (2 min → 12 hours), demonstrating that a bridge reference condition can be reliably promoted through cost-effective gates while maintaining auditability and controlling experimental costs (169.2 GPU-hours total for the full protocol).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Short pretraining runs risk over-promoting configurations that perform well only at small budgets, creating false positives in hyperparameter screening. The authors aim to develop a transparent, rule-bound staged-promotion framework that balances cost efficiency against the risk of prematurely discarding promising configurations, while maintaining full auditability of all promotion decisions.

Method: A fixed micro-pretraining runner is evaluated on two heterogeneous host blocks (Windows A100 and Linux L40S). Starting from 12 prior-screened configurations, a staged-promotion protocol uses frozen decision rules at budgets of 2, 5, 10, 60 minutes, and 12 hours. The protocol includes a "Staged Factorial Screening bridge reference" condition that is tracked across stages. Promotion gates use two frozen criteria: a 0.010 val_bpb near-equivalence rule and a 0.020 mean-gap rule. Seed ranges differ across stages, making ranking changes operational promotion evidence rather than within-seed learning curves. Cost accounting includes both executed GPU-hours and counterfactual costs for unrun continuations.

Result: Early-stage rankings (5- and 10-minute) are host-sensitive and unstable. The eventual 12-hour top-ranked condition is not the mean-best at the replicated 10-minute gate. The bridge reference condition ranks first in all four 60-minute host-seed cells at the replicated 60-minute gate and retains first rank in all four 12-hour host-seed cells. A greedy comparator fails the 0.010 val_bpb near-equivalence rule, and a d8/ar48 sentinel fails the 0.020 mean-gap rule. Total executed cost is 169.2 GPU-hours; continuing all four 60-minute candidates would cost 192 GPU-hours, and continuing all nine replicated 10-minute candidates would cost 432 GPU-hours.

Conclusion: The work is framed as a bounded cost-allocation finding, not a claim of global optimality or superiority over adaptive methods. The protocol demonstrates that staged promotion with frozen rules and a bridge reference condition can produce auditable, cost-controlled screening decisions in heterogeneous compute environments, though the unstable early-stage rankings validate the need for multi-stage confirmation rather than single-gate selection.

Abstract: Short pretraining runs can reduce experimental cost, but they can also over-promote configurations that only look strong at tiny budgets. We study an auditable staged-promotion protocol for a fixed micro-pretraining runner on two heterogeneous host blocks: Windows A100 and Linux L40S. Starting from twelve prior-screened configurations, we use staged budgets of 2 minutes, 5 minutes, 10 minutes, 60 minutes, and 12 hours, with frozen promotion rules before expensive continuations.
  The early screens are intentionally treated as unstable: the 5- and 10-minute rankings are host-sensitive, and the eventual 12-hour top-ranked condition is not the mean-best condition at the replicated 10-minute gate. Because seed ranges differ across stages, these changes are operational promotion evidence, not within-seed curves. A replicated 60-minute gate keeps the Staged Factorial Screening bridge reference in the promoted set, where it ranks first in all four 60-minute host-seed cells. In the final 12-hour confirmation package, the bridge condition ranks first in all four host-seed cells across two seeds; the greedy comparator does not meet the frozen 0.010 val_bpb near-equivalence rule; and the cheaper d8/ar48 (depth-8, aspect-48) sentinel does not meet the frozen 0.020 mean-gap rule.
  The executed 12-hour branch spends 144 GPU-hours, and the full staged protocol records 169.2 training GPU-hours including screening stages. Continuing all four 60-minute candidates would spend 192 GPU-hours, while continuing all nine replicated 10-minute candidates would spend 432 GPU-hours. The latter numbers are accounting counterfactuals for unrun continuations, not evidence that skipped candidates could not have overtaken the reference. The result is a bounded cost-allocation finding, not a claim of global optimality, capacity-normalized superiority, or superiority over adaptive hyperparameter optimization methods.

</details>


### [28] [Scenario-based Probing and Steering Cultural Values in Large Language Models--Extended Version](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11399)
*Trung Duc Anh Dang,Tung Kieu,Sarah Masud*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework to probe and steer latent cultural values in LLMs using the Inglehart-Welzel axes. By converting survey questions into behavioral dilemmas and extracting token probabilities, the authors measure implicit values and apply activation steering to shift model behavior. They find that steering one cultural dimension can inadvertently shift another — a phenomenon called latent entanglement — which mirrors real-world WVS correlations and constrains axis-independent alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often reflect homogenized cultural values from training data. Existing cultural alignment evaluations using direct survey-style questions elicit neutral or safety-aligned responses, failing to capture underlying model preferences. The authors aim to develop a method that probes deeper, latent cultural representations and enables fine-grained steering without retraining.

Method: The framework translates WVS social value questions into scenario-based behavioral dilemmas to extract token-level probabilities as implicit value measures. Activation steering is applied along the two Inglehart-Welzel axes (Traditional vs. Secular-rational, Survival vs. Self-expression), optionally combined with country-conditioned prompting. Experiments span three open-source LLMs and four target cultures, assessing steerability, cross-dimensional shifts, and general task performance preservation.

Result: Substantial variation in steerability was found across models and cultures. A key discovery is latent entanglement: interventions along one cultural dimension induce shifts along the other, mirroring correlations found in human WVS data. This entanglement persists across activation-only, prompt-only, and hybrid steering approaches, constraining the ability to achieve fully axis-independent alignment. General task performance remains largely preserved despite these interventions.

Conclusion: Latent cultural values in LLMs can be probed and steered using activation interventions on behavioral dilemmas, but the inherent entanglement between cultural dimensions — reflecting real-world value correlations — poses a fundamental constraint on achieving fully independent cultural alignment without retraining.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are deployed across cultural contexts but often reflect homogenized values inherited from training data. Evaluations of cultural alignment typically rely on direct prompting with survey-style questions, which frequently elicit neutral or safety-aligned responses and fail to capture underlying model preferences. We propose a framework for probing and steering latent cultural representations in LLMs along the two Inglehart--Welzel axes of the World Values Survey (WVS). By translating social value questions into scenario-based behavioral dilemmas, we extract token-level probabilities to measure implicit values and apply activation steering, optionally combined with country-conditioned prompting, to shift model behavior without retraining. Across three open-source LLMs and four target cultures, we find substantial variation in steerability and identify latent entanglement, where interventions along one cultural dimension induce shifts along another. This coupling mirrors correlations in human WVS data and persists across activation, prompt, and hybrid steering. It constrains axis-independent alignment, though general task performance is largely preserved.

</details>


### [29] [Context-Aware Multimodal Claim Verification in Spoken Dialogues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11420)
*Chaewan Chun,Delvin Ce Zhang,Dongwon Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MAD2 is a benchmark for spoken misinformation verification using 1,000 multi-turn dialogues (~10h audio, 3,368 check-worthy claims). The study shows dialogue context improves verification, preceding context alone suffices for live moderation, and conversational structure matters more than misinformation framing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Millions consume spoken claims daily via podcasts and streams, where misinformation is constructed conversationally — through framing, reinforcement, or lack of challenge across turns. Existing fact-checking research focuses almost exclusively on isolated text, leaving multi-turn spoken dialogue verification critically under-studied.

Method: The authors introduce MAD2 (Multi-turn Audio Dialogues), a benchmark of 1,000 two-speaker dialogues containing 3,368 check-worthy claims (~10 hours of audio). They propose a calibrated multimodal fusion approach combining a context-aware audio encoder with a dialogue-aware text model (likely a transformer-based architecture). The study systematically evaluates how varying amounts of dialogue context (preceding turns, full context, none) and modality (audio, transcript, fused) affect verification performance across different scenario types.

Result: (1) Adding dialogue context consistently improves claim verification, but gains are scenario-dependent. (2) Using only preceding context often matches offline (full-context) performance, supporting real-time/live moderation applications. (3) Audio modality contributes most when transcript-based models are destabilized by additional context. (4) Overall, conversational structure has a greater impact on verification accuracy than how misinformation is specifically framed within dialogue.

Conclusion: Spoken misinformation verification requires modeling conversational structure, not just isolated claims. The MAD2 benchmark demonstrates that dialogue context — particularly preceding turns — is critical, and multimodal fusion can leverage audio signals especially when transcripts alone become unreliable with added context. This has practical implications for building live fact-checking systems for podcasts and streams.

Abstract: Every day, millions absorb claims from podcasts and streams that no fact-checker ever sees. Spoken misinformation is built through conversation, where credibility comes not from facts alone but from how claims are framed, reinforced, or left unchallenged across turns. Yet fact-checking has focused on isolated text, leaving dialogue audio under-studied. We introduce MAD2, a new Multi-turn Audio Dialogues benchmark for spoken claim verification, containing 1,000 two-speaker dialogues with 3,368 check-worthy claims and approximately 10 hours of audio, and propose calibrated multimodal fusion of a context-aware audio encoder and a dialogue-aware text model. Across settings, adding dialogue context improves verification, but the gains depend on scenario type. Using only preceding context often matches offline performance, supporting live-moderation settings, and audio contributes most when transcript-based models are destabilized by additional context. Overall, conversational structure matters more for verification than misinformation framing.

</details>


### [30] [SOMA-SQL: Resolving Multi-Source Ambiguity in NL-to-SQL via Synthetic Log and Execution Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11424)
*Sai Ashish Somayajula,Marianne Menglin Liu,Chuan Lei,Fjona Parllaku,Daniel Garcia,Rongguang Wang,Syed Fahad Allam Shah,Ankan Bansal,Sujeeth Bharadwaj,Tao Sheng,Sujith Ravi,Dan Roth*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SOMA-SQL autonomously resolves ambiguity in NL2SQL by generating synthetic query logs for schema grounding, then using ambiguity-driven probing queries to disambiguate and repair SQL — achieving up to 16.7% accuracy gains without human intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NL2SQL systems fail in real-world settings due to ambiguity across user questions, database schemas, and model interpretations. Existing solutions either require human clarification (which doesn't scale) or treat ambiguity as a schema representation problem (which doesn't resolve it autonomously). There is a need for an automatic, scalable approach to ambiguity discovery and resolution.

Method: SOMA-SQL operates in two stages: (1) It constructs synthetic query logs to ground schema interpretation and guide candidate SQL generation. (2) It executes targeted probing queries against the database, driven by a structured ambiguity taxonomy and disagreements among candidate SQLs, to produce disambiguation evidence used for final SQL selection and repair. This active approach works without human-in-the-loop and generalizes across unseen schemas and query distributions.

Result: On six public benchmarks, SOMA-SQL improves execution accuracy by 13.0% on average over state-of-the-art baselines, with gains up to 16.7% specifically on ambiguous questions.

Conclusion: Autonomous ambiguity resolution via synthetic query logs and targeted probing is an effective, generalizable strategy for improving NL2SQL robustness in real-world settings with underspecified queries and large, ambiguous schemas.

Abstract: Natural language interfaces to databases aim to translate user questions into executable SQL, yet remain brittle in real-world settings where questions are underspecified and schemas are large and ambiguous. Ambiguity across user questions, database schemas, and model interpretations are central failure modes in NL2SQL, leading to misaligned intent, incorrect schema grounding, and erroneous SQL generation. Existing approaches rely on human clarification or treat ambiguity as a schema representation problem, but these do not scale nor resolve ambiguity autonomously. We propose SOMA-SQL to automatically resolve ambiguity via targeted synthetic query log and ambiguity-driven probing. SOMA-SQL constructs synthetic query log to ground schema interpretation and guide candidate SQL generation; it then executes targeted probing queries, driven by a structured ambiguity taxonomy and candidate disagreements, to produce disambiguation evidence for final SQL selection and repair. This active approach to ambiguity discovery and resolution generalizes across unseen schemas and query distributions without human-in-the-loop. Experiments on six public benchmarks demonstrate that SOMA-SQL improves execution accuracy by 13.0% on average over state-of-the-art baselines, with gains of up to 16.7% on ambiguous questions.

</details>


### [31] [Agent Skill Evaluation and Evolution: Frameworks and Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11435)
*Kexin Ding,Yang Zhou,Can Jin,Feng Tong,Mu Zhou,Dimitris N. Metaxas*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This survey examines the paradigm shift from isolated agent skill creation to automated, evaluation-driven skill evolution. It categorizes skill evolution into four paradigms (execution feedback, trajectory distillation, compression, and reinforcement learning), analyzes six skill-centric benchmark categories, and identifies open challenges for building generalizable, efficient, and verifiably safe skill ecosystems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As agent skill libraries scale, rigorous evaluation becomes critical for ensuring utility, quality, and safety in real-world applications. The field is shifting from merely creating skills in isolation toward automated, evaluation-driven evolution of skills, yet no systematic survey has mapped this emerging landscape.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic survey organizing the field along two axes: (1) four paradigms of skill evolution — execution feedback, trajectory distillation, compression, and reinforcement learning — showing how each improves skill utility and reliability; and (2) six categories of skill-centric benchmarks, analyzed for structural gaps, trade-offs, and metric richness.

Result: The survey identifies structural gaps in benchmark coverage, highlights trade-offs across evaluation approaches, and reveals limited metric richness in existing benchmarks. It also delineates how each evolution paradigm contributes distinctly to improving agent skills.

Conclusion: The paper concludes by outlining open research directions for building skill ecosystems that are generalizable, efficient, and verifiably safe, calling for more comprehensive benchmarks and integrated evolution-evaluation pipelines.

Abstract: The growth of agent skills has transformed how agentic systems are built, evaluated, and deployed. As skill libraries continue to scale, rigorous evaluation becomes critical to ensuring their utility, quality, and safety in real-world applications. Consequently, the field is undergoing an emerging paradigm shift from isolated skill creation to automated, evaluation-driven skill evolution. In this survey, we systematically examine the landscape of skill evolution and evaluation beyond foundational skill creation. We categorize evolution into four distinct paradigms, spanning execution feedback, trajectory distillation, compression, and reinforcement learning, showing how each element contributes to improving skill utility and reliability. We also provide an analysis of six skill-centric benchmark categories, identifying structural gaps in benchmark coverage, trade-offs, and metric richness to advance skill research. Finally, we identify open directions for building skill ecosystems that are generalizable, efficient, and verifiably safe. The project URL is https://github.com/Cassie07/AgentSkill_Survey

</details>


### [32] [AI Coding Agents Can Reproduce Social Science Findings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11447)
*Meysam Alizadeh,Mohsen Mosleh,Fabrizio Gilardi,Atoosa Kasirzadeh,Joshua Tucker*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SocSci-Repro-Bench is a 221-task benchmark evaluating AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex) on reproducing social science findings. Claude Code substantially outperforms Codex, both exceed prior benchmarks. Performance is not primarily driven by memorization; providing paper PDFs modestly helps but introduces bias; subtle prompt framing can nudge agents toward confirmatory specification search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for evaluating AI agents' ability to reproduce scientific findings are either too small or conflate agent performance with flaws in reproduction materials (e.g., non-executable code). There is a need for a systematic, well-controlled evaluation across social sciences that isolates agents' true reproduction capacity.

Method: The authors construct SocSci-Repro-Bench, a benchmark of 221 reproduction tasks across 4 disciplines and 13 substantive domains, drawn from studies that are either fully reproducible or demonstrably non-reproducible due to missing data. Two frontier coding agents (Claude Code and Codex) are evaluated on reproduction success. Additional experiments test: (1) reasoning about underlying research questions, (2) memorization effects, (3) impact of providing original paper PDFs, and (4) susceptibility to confirmatory specification search via prompt framing.

Result: Both agents reproduce a large share of social science findings, with Claude Code substantially outperforming Codex. Reproduction rates considerably exceed prior general-purpose LLM benchmarks. Agents perform strongly on research-question identification tasks. Results are not primarily driven by memorization. Providing the original paper PDF modestly improves performance but introduces bias on non-reproducible tasks. Subtle prompt framing can nudge agents toward confirmatory specification search.

Conclusion: Frontier coding agents can serve as reliable executors of computational workflows in social science reproduction, but careful benchmarking and prompt design are essential as AI systems take on larger roles in scientific production. The findings highlight both the promise of AI-assisted reproducibility and risks of bias introduced through prompt design or supplementary materials.

Abstract: Recent anecdotal evidence suggests that AI coding agents can reproduce published findings when provided with original data and code; yet systematic evaluation across social sciences remains limited. Existing evaluation benchmarks are insufficient, either small or conflate agent performance with problems in the reproduction materials themselves, such as code that fails to execute correctly. Here we introduce SocSci-Repro-Bench, a benchmark of 221 tasks spanning four disciplines and 13 substantive domains, constructed from studies whose results are either fully reproducible with available materials or demonstrably non-reproducible due to missing data, allowing us to isolate agents' reproduction capacity. Evaluating two frontier coding agents, Claude Code and Codex, we find that both can reproduce a large share of social science findings, with Claude Code substantially outperforming Codex. These reproduction rates considerably exceed those previously reported for general-purpose LLM-based agents on comparable reproducibility benchmarks. Both agents also perform strongly on a reasoning task requiring identification of underlying research questions, and additional analyses suggest that results are not primarily driven by memorization. Providing the original paper PDF alongside replication materials modestly improves performance but introduces bias on tasks where reproduction is impossible. We also show that agents can be nudged toward confirmatory specification search through subtle prompt framing. Together, these findings suggest that at least some frontier coding agents can serve as reliable executors of computational workflows while underscoring the need for careful benchmarking and prompt design as AI systems assume larger roles in scientific production.

</details>


### [33] [AI Coding Agents in Social Science: Methodologically Diverse, Empirically Consistent, Interpretively Vulnerable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11456)
*Meysam Alizadeh,Fabrizio Gilardi,Mohsen Mosleh,Enkelejda Kasneci*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper empirically tests whether LLM-based agents threaten methodological diversity or amplify analytic flexibility in scientific research. Using Claude Code and Codex against a many-analysts human baseline, the authors find that AI agents rival or exceed human methodological diversity at the design layer but are vulnerable to bias at the verdict layer, where prompts can flip final conclusions without shifting coefficient estimates — the locus of AI bias is interpretation, not estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle two opposing concerns about LLM-based agents in scientific analysis: (1) that they may homogenize methodological diversity, and (2) that they may amplify analytic flexibility enabling motivated reasoning. The authors argue these map onto two separable layers — the design layer (methodological choices) and the verdict layer (decision rules mapping estimates to claims) — and seek to test both empirically.

Method: Twenty independent executions of Claude Code and Codex were run on a prominent immigration and social-policy dataset, benchmarked against a many-analysts human baseline. Two experimental interventions were applied: a prompt-induced anti-immigration researcher prior (targeting the design layer) and an explicit confirmatory prompt (targeting the verdict layer). Specification counts, effect estimates, model similarity, and final verdicts were compared across agents and humans.

Result: At the design layer, Codex matched human methodological diversity; Claude Code produced ~3× more specifications. Both agents' effect estimates remained broadly aligned with the human consensus, and no agent model exactly matched any human model. The anti-immigration prior reorganized methodological decisions but, unlike for biased humans, did not shift aggregate estimates or verdicts. At the verdict layer, a confirmatory prompt flipped Claude Code's support from 10% to 90% while leaving coefficient distributions essentially unchanged — operating through rule omission, not rule softening.

Conclusion: AI agents can match or exceed human methodological diversity at the design layer while remaining acutely vulnerable at the verdict layer. The primary locus of AI bias is not in estimation but in interpretation — agents change what they conclude from the same estimates rather than massaging estimates to fit a conclusion. This asymmetry has significant implications for the responsible deployment of LLM-based agents in scientific research.

Abstract: The deployment of LLM-based agents in scientific analysis raises opposing concerns: that agents may reduce methodological diversity, or that they may amplify the analytic flexibility through which researchers reach motivated conclusions. We argue these worries target two empirically separable layers: a design layer of methodological choices, and a verdict layer in which a decision rule maps estimates to a substantive claim. We test both by running 20 independent executions of Claude Code and Codex on a prominent immigration and social-policy against a many-analysts human baseline. At the design layer, Codex matches human methodological diversity and Claude Code produces nearly three times as many specifications; both agents' effect estimates remain broadly aligned with the human consensus, and no agent model exactly matches any human model. A prompt-induced anti-immigration researcher prior reorganizes each agent's methodological decisions but, unlike for biased human analysts in the same data, does not shift aggregate estimates or final verdicts; nor do agents reroute along the methodological axes humans use to bias their estimates. At the verdict layer, an explicit confirmatory prompt flips Claude Code's verdicts from 10% to 90% support while leaving its coefficient distribution essentially unchanged, operating through rule omission rather than rule softening. AI agents can rival or exceed human methodological diversity at the design layer while remaining vulnerable at the verdict layer. In our setting, the locus of AI bias is not estimation but interpretation.

</details>


### [34] [APEX: Automated Prompt Engineering eXpert with Dynamic Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11459)
*Fei Wang,Si Si,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Inderjit S. Dhillon*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: APEX is a prompt optimization framework that dynamically stratifies evaluation data into Easy, Hard, and Mixed tiers, prioritizing the Mixed tier to identify high-leverage subsets for efficient mutation and candidate ranking, achieving superior results over static baselines under fixed evaluation budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evolutionary prompt optimization methods treat the development dataset as a static benchmark, wasting compute on uninformative data. There is a need for data-efficient prompt optimization that selectively uses the most informative examples.

Method: APEX dynamically stratifies the dataset into Easy, Hard, and Mixed tiers based on the optimization lineage (historical performance trajectories). It prioritizes the Mixed tier to extract two subsets: the addressable frontier (for generating informative prompt mutations) and the rank-sensitive frontier (for distinguishing candidate quality during selection). Evaluation uses a fixed budget of 5,000 LLM calls across IFBench, SimpleQA Verified, and FACTS Grounding, tested on Gemini 2.5 Flash and Gemma 3 27B.

Result: Under a fixed 5,000-call budget, APEX outperforms the initial prompt by an average of 11.2% on Gemini 2.5 Flash and 6.8% on Gemma 3 27B, demonstrating consistent improvements over static data usage baselines.

Conclusion: A data-centric approach—dynamically selecting which examples to use during optimization—is key to efficient and effective prompt engineering. APEX's tiered stratification and frontier-based data selection significantly improve optimization outcomes under constrained compute budgets.

Abstract: Large Language Models are highly sensitive to prompt formulation, necessitating automatic prompt optimization to unlock their full potential. While evolutionary algorithms have emerged as the dominant paradigm, they suffer from a critical bottleneck: data efficiency. Current methods treat the development dataset as a static benchmark, wasting significant compute budget on uninformative data. In this work, we introduce APEX (Automatic Prompt Engineering eXpert), a novel framework that optimizes the data usage alongside the prompt search. APEX dynamically stratifies the dataset into Easy, Hard, and Mixed tiers based on the optimization lineage. By prioritizing the Mixed tier, which identifies the data where the LLM has mixed performance, we identify two high-leverage subsets: the addressable frontier for generating informative mutations and the rank-sensitive frontier for distinguishing candidate quality. We evaluate APEX across three diverse benchmarks: IFBench, SimpleQA Verified, and FACTS Grounding. Under a fixed budget of 5,000 evaluation calls, due to its data efficiency, APEX outperforms the initial prompt by an average of 11.2% on Gemini 2.5 Flash and 6.8% on Gemma 3 27B, demonstrating that a data-centric approach is key to efficient and effective prompt optimization.

</details>


### [35] [The Periodic Table of LLM Reasoning: A Structured Survey of Reasoning Paradigms, Methods, and Failure Modes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11470)
*Avinash Anand,Mahisha Ramesh,Avni Mittal,Ashutosh Kumar,Erik Cambria,Zhengkui Wang,Timothy Liu,Aik Beng Ng,Simon See,Rajiv Ratn Shah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A systematic survey of 300+ papers examining how reasoning capabilities emerge in LLMs and where they fail, introducing a taxonomy covering nine reasoning paradigms, analyzing methodological trends, synthesizing failure modes, and identifying future directions such as meta-reasoning and multimodal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite strong NLP performance, LLMs exhibit inconsistent reasoning behavior sensitive to prompting, task design, and model scale. The rapidly expanding literature lacks a unified organization, motivating this systematic survey to map capabilities, limitations, and emerging directions in LLM reasoning.

Method: Systematic analysis of over 300 recent papers from arXiv, Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar, Papers with Code, and the ACL Anthology. The authors construct a structured taxonomy, analyze methodological trends across prompting methods, architectures, training objectives, reward modeling, and evaluation benchmarks, and synthesize recurring failure modes.

Result: A structured taxonomy encompassing nine reasoning paradigms: Chain-of-Thought, multi-hop, mathematical, commonsense, visual/temporal, code/algorithmic, retrieval-augmented, tool-augmented/agentic, and RL-based reasoning. Recurring failure modes identified include reasoning hallucinations, brittle multi-step inference, weak causal abstraction, and poor cross-domain generalization.

Conclusion: The survey provides a unified view of LLM reasoning capabilities and limitations, serving as a reference for developing more robust, interpretable, and generalizable reasoning systems. Emerging directions include meta-reasoning, self-evolving frameworks, multimodal reasoning, and socially grounded reasoning.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across natural language processing tasks, yet reliable reasoning remains an open challenge. Although modern LLMs show progress in structured inference, multi-step problem solving, and contextual understanding, their reasoning behavior is often inconsistent and sensitive to prompting strategies, task design, and model scale. This survey provides a systematic analysis of more than 300 recent papers from arXiv, Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar, Papers with Code, and the ACL Anthology to examine how reasoning capabilities emerge in LLMs and where they fail. We make three main contributions. First, we introduce a structured taxonomy of LLM reasoning research, covering Chain-of-Thought reasoning, multi-hop reasoning, mathematical reasoning, common sense reasoning, visual and temporal reasoning, code and algorithmic reasoning, retrieval-augmented reasoning, tool-augmented and agentic reasoning, and reinforcement learning-based reasoning. Second, we analyze methodological trends across these paradigms, including prompting methods, model architectures, training objectives, reward modeling, and evaluation benchmarks. Third, we synthesize recurring limitations and failure modes, such as reasoning hallucinations, brittle multi-step inference, weak causal abstraction, and poor cross-domain generalization. By organizing a rapidly expanding literature, this survey offers a unified view of the current capabilities and limitations of reasoning in LLMs. We also identify emerging research directions, including meta-reasoning, self-evolving reasoning frameworks, multimodal reasoning, and socially grounded reasoning. Overall, this work aims to serve as a reference for developing more robust, interpretable, and generalizable reasoning systems in future language models.

</details>


### [36] [Hubs or Fringes: Pretraining Data Selection via Web Graph Centrality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11499)
*Vedant Badoni,Danqi Chen,Xinyi Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: WebGraphMix uses web graph centrality scores to balance central vs. peripheral documents in pretraining data mixtures, improving model performance from 39.8% (uniform) to 41.4% (1:1 mix) and 43.8% when combined with quality classifiers, all without requiring model training or labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data selection methods for pretraining rely on auxiliary classifiers or mixture optimization, which introduce computational overhead and dependence on labeled data. The authors aim to leverage the web graph's topological structure as a lightweight, orthogonal signal for curating pretraining data.

Method: WebGraphMix computes structural centrality scores over the Common Crawl host-level web graph. Documents from central hosts (hypothesized to provide reusable abstractions) and peripheral hosts (hypothesized to provide specialized, long-tail knowledge) are mixed at varying ratios. The framework requires no model training, no labeled data, and no downstream supervision. Experiments are conducted at 400M and 1B parameter scales (8B and 28B tokens) using the DataComp-LM pipeline, with 23 evaluation tasks.

Result: A 1:1 mixture of central and peripheral documents achieves 41.4% average across tasks vs. 39.8% for uniform sampling. Combining structural centrality scores with document-level quality classifiers further boosts performance to 43.8%, demonstrating that web graph topology provides complementary, orthogonal information to content-based selection methods.

Conclusion: Web graph topology is a meaningful and largely orthogonal axis for pretraining data curation. Structural centrality scores offer a lightweight, scalable alternative to classifier-based methods and capture complementary capabilities from different web regions.

Abstract: The performance of modern language models depends critically on pretraining data composition. Yet existing data selection methods rely on auxiliary classifiers for document scoring or mixture optimization, adding computational overhead and dependence on labeled data. We propose WebGraphMix, a lightweight data selection framework that computes structural centrality scores over the Common Crawl host-level web graph and uses them to vary the proportion of central versus peripheral documents in the pretraining mixture. We hypothesize that central hosts expose models to reusable abstractions, while peripheral hosts encode specialized, long-tail knowledge. WebGraphMix computes centrality scores efficiently at web scale, requiring no model training, labeled data, or downstream supervision. We integrate WebGraphMix into the DataComp-LM pipeline and train models at 400M and 1B parameter scales with 8B and 28B tokens respectively, evaluating on 23 tasks ranging from factual knowledge to symbolic reasoning. Our experiments show that central and peripheral web regions encode complementary capabilities. Mixture combining both at a ratio of 1:1 achieves 41.4% on average, compared to 39.8% for uniform sampling. Combining structural scores with document-level quality classifier scores further improves performance to 43.8%. These findings demonstrate that web graph topology is a meaningful axis for pretraining data curation, capturing information that is largely orthogonal to existing content-based approaches.

</details>


### [37] [When Roleplaying, Do Models Believe What They Say?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11502)
*Benjamin Sturgeon,David Africa,Sid Black*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether persona-based role-playing in LLMs merely changes outputs or fundamentally alters internal truth representations. Using linear truth probes, the authors find that role-playing historical personas suppresses era-believed falsehoods less than era-false ones, but both remain classified as false internally. In contrast, Emergent Misalignment (EM) shifts false claims substantially toward the "true" region of probe space, with greater resistance to challenge and downstream reasoning use. Role-play and EM thus lie on a spectrum of belief internalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent work suggests persona adoption is fundamental to LLM operation. However, it remains unclear whether adopting a persona merely changes surface-level outputs or genuinely alters the model's internal representations of truth. The authors aim to disentangle these two possibilities, especially given safety implications if models can internalize false beliefs through role-playing or fine-tuning.

Method: The authors apply linear truth probes to LLMs (Qwen 2.5 14B, Qwen 3 8B, Llama 3.3 70B) role-playing historical personas with beliefs diverging from modern consensus. They construct two categories of false claims: *era-believed* (false claims the persona would likely endorse) and *era-false* (topic-matched false claims they would not endorse). Persona induction is tested via prompting, in-context learning, and supervised fine-tuning. They then compare how these conditions affect probe-space representations against models exhibiting Emergent Misalignment, measuring resistance to challenge and downstream reasoning use.

Result: Under role-play, era-believed statements are suppressed less than equally false alternatives, yet both remain classified as false by probes — indicating a dissociation between output and internal representation. In contrast, EM-trained models' false claims move substantially toward the true region of probe space, are defended under challenge ~50% of the time (vs. ~1/6 for role-play), and are used in downstream reasoning. Role-play thus changes surface behavior with minimal representational shift, while EM meaningfully shifts internal representations without fully marking false claims as true.

Conclusion: Role-playing and Emergent Misalignment represent points on a spectrum of belief internalization. Role-play primarily alters what a model says with little change to internal truth representations, whereas EM shifts internal representations of false claims toward truth without full internalization. This distinction has significant implications for AI safety, suggesting that surface-level persona adoption is less concerning than phenomena that genuinely perturb internal belief representations.

Abstract: Language models can state that "the Earth orbits the Sun" and, when role-playing Aristotle, assert the opposite. Recent work argues that persona adoption is fundamental to how language models operate, with models constantly selecting the most appropriate persona for a given context. Does such role-playing merely change the model's outputs, or does it also affect what the model internally represents as truthful? We study this question with linear truth probes, applying them to LLMs role-playing historical personas whose likely beliefs differ from modern consensus. For each persona, we compare false claims the persona would likely have endorsed (*era-believed*) with topic-matched false claims they would not have endorsed (*era-false*). Across prompting, in-context learning, and supervised fine-tuning, persona induction suppresses era-believed statements less than equally false alternatives, yet they remain classified as false overall. Role-play therefore shifts what these models say more than what they internally represent as true. We contrast this with models trained on harmful advice that exhibit Emergent Misalignment (EM). Across three model families (Qwen 2.5 14B, Qwen 3 8B, and Llama 3.3 70B), their false claims move substantially toward the true region of probe space, are defended under challenge roughly half the time versus about a sixth for role-play, and are used in downstream reasoning. Role-play and Emergent Misalignment thus are points on a spectrum of belief internalization, where role-play changes what a model says with little representational change, while Emergent Misalignment shifts the internal representation of false claims without fully marking them as true.

</details>


### [38] [SAGE: Answer-Conditioned Uncertainty Targets for Verbal Uncertainty Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11512)
*Kaiwen Shi,Zheyuan Zhang,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes SAGE, a group-level uncertainty target that uses answer-conditioned entropy geometry, and GUPO, a training framework that aligns verbal uncertainty expressions with actual model behavior, improving calibration across reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often express uncertainty in natural language that does not reflect their actual sampled behavior. Current approaches to verbal uncertainty alignment treat it as a distributional calibration problem, but existing group-level targets fail to provide useful training signals due to poor handling of categorical, numeric, and symbolic answer distinctions.

Method: The authors introduce SAGE (Semantic-Answer Guided Entropy), which constructs an answer-conditioned uncertainty geometry over sampled responses, preserving categorical, numeric, and symbolic distinctions while providing smooth, scale-preserving calibration. This target is applied via GUPO (Group-Uncertainty Preference Optimization), a training framework that supervises only the verbal uncertainty expression channel rather than the full response.

Result: Experiments across factual, mathematical, and multiple-choice reasoning tasks demonstrate improved uncertainty ranking, lower calibration error, and reduced overconfidence compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: SAGE and GUPO together provide an effective solution for aligning verbal uncertainty expressions with actual model behavior, addressing a key limitation in LLM reliability and trustworthiness.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly express uncertainty through natural-language statements, yet these expressions often fail to reflect the model's sampled behavior. We study verbal uncertainty alignment as a distributional calibration problem: the appropriate uncertainty target for a prompt should be estimated from repeated model outputs rather than from an isolated response. However, group rollouts alone are insufficient, since the resulting target must provide a useful training signal. Existing targets only partially satisfy this requirement. We propose SAGE, Semantic-Answer Guided Entropy, a group-level uncertainty target that constructs an answer-conditioned uncertainty geometry over sampled responses. SAGE preserves categorical, numeric, and symbolic answer distinctions while maintaining a smooth and scale-preserving calibration signal. We further apply this target through Group-Uncertainty Preference Optimization, or GUPO, an uncertainty-channel training framework that supervises verbal uncertainty expressions rather than the full response. Experiments across factual, mathematical, and multiple-choice reasoning tasks show improved uncertainty ranking, lower calibration error, and reduced overconfidence.

</details>


### [39] [ISE: An Execution-Grounded Recipe for Multi-Turn OS-Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11520)
*Siyuan Luo,Nairong Zheng,Lin Zhou,Tiankuo Yao,Shengyou Yuan,Haojia Yu,Cong Pang,Jiapeng Luo,Lewei Lu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ISE introduces a three-stage synthesis paradigm (Intent → Simulate → Execute) to generate grounded OS agent training data. Stage 1 creates ~44K unique structured intents via a 4D framework; Stage 2 produces ~23K multi-turn trajectories averaging 68 dialogue turns using a role-locked user simulator; Stage 3 executes tool calls in live OS environments for authentic failure-recovery dynamics. Fine-tuning on the resulting ISETrace dataset boosts ClawEval pass@1 from 19.3 to 37.7 with Qwen3-8B, surpassing GPT-4o and Qwen3-32B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing datasets lack three essential properties simultaneously: structured user intents, multi-turn task delegation, and grounded tool execution with real environmental feedback. This gap prevents training capable OS agents that can handle realistic, complex, interactive scenarios.

Method: The ISE paradigm: (1) Stage 1 — Intent Construction: generates ~50K structured intents using a 4D framework (Persona × Domain × Task × Complexity), deduplicated to 43,956 unique intents achieving a Vendi Score of 61.57 (mpnet-base-v2 embeddings, cosine kernel, q=1); (2) Stage 2 — Multi-turn Simulation: a role-locked user simulator drives interaction grounded in prior execution outcomes, yielding 23,132 trajectories averaging 8.12 user turns and 68.24 total dialogue turns; (3) Stage 3 — Live Execution: every tool call runs in an isolated OS workspace, producing genuine failure-recovery dynamics. The resulting dataset (ISETrace) is used to fine-tune Qwen3-8B, evaluated on ClawEval with a standard protocol.

Result: Fine-tuning Qwen3-8B on ISETrace improves ClawEval pass@1 from 19.3 to 37.7. This outperforms zero-shot GPT-4o and the 4× larger Qwen3-32B base model. Ablation on Stage 2 shows multi-turn simulation accounts for a large portion of the performance gain.

Conclusion: ISE provides an effective, scalable synthesis pipeline for generating high-fidelity OS agent training data. Multi-turn, execution-grounded simulation is critical for agent performance, and the resulting dataset enables small models (8B) to surpass much larger models and strong closed-source baselines. Code and dataset are open-sourced.

Abstract: Training capable OS agents requires data that simultaneously captures structured user intents, multi-turn task delegation, and grounded tool execution--properties absent from existing datasets. We propose ISE (Intent -> Simulate -> Execute), a three-stage synthesis paradigm that addresses these gaps jointly. Stage 1 constructs roughly 50000 structured intents via a 4D framework (Persona x Domain x Task x Complexity); after deduplication the pool contains 43956 unique intents and attains a Vendi Score of 61.57 over the entire pool on mpnet-base-v2 embeddings (cosine kernel, q=1). Stage 2 drives multi-turn user-agent interaction through a role-locked user simulator that grounds each user turn in actual execution outcomes, producing 23132 complete trajectories averaging 8.12 user turns and 68.24 total dialogue turns. Stage 3 runs every tool call inside a live, isolated OS workspace, generating authentic failure-recovery dynamics instead of simulated responses. Fine-tuning on ISETrace improves ClawEval pass@1 from 19.3 to 37.7 using Qwen3-8B on agent tool-use tasks with a standard protocol. This result outperforms zero-shot GPT-4o and the larger Qwen3-32B base model which is four times bigger. An ablation on Stage 2 proves multi-turn simulation brings a large portion of the performance gain. We release all source code and dataset at https://github.com/Valiere01/ISE-Trace.

</details>


### [40] [Measuring language complexity from hierarchical reuse of recurring patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11531)
*Junyi Zhou,Rui Liu,Pengyu Liu,Yu Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the "ladderpath index," an algorithmic information theory-based measure of language complexity that counts minimal steps to reconstruct a sequence via hierarchical reuse of repeated substructures. Applied to 21 parallel corpora, it reveals approximate invariance across languages (supporting the equi-complexity hypothesis) and trade-offs between character inventory size and corpus length, with automatically discovered substructures overlapping with genuine morphological units.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a principled, representation-independent measure of linguistic complexity grounded in algorithmic information theory that can empirically test the equi-complexity hypothesis (all languages are roughly equally complex) and the trade-off hypothesis (complexity is conserved and redistributed across linguistic levels).

Method: The ladderpath index quantifies the minimum number of steps to reconstruct a sequence by hierarchically reusing repeated substrings. This is applied to 21 parallel corpora from the Parallel Universal Dependencies dataset, analyzed both in native orthographic form and mapped to a unified binary representation to ensure representation-independence.

Result: (1) The ladderpath index is approximately invariant across languages, varying far less than corpus length; invariance strengthens under unified binary representation. (2) Trade-offs emerge between character inventory size and corpus length, and between vocabulary-level and corpus-level reconstruction complexity. (3) Automatically identified reusable substructures overlap significantly with actual words and morphological components, despite no linguistic input being provided.

Conclusion: The ladderpath approach provides empirical evidence for both the equi-complexity and trade-off hypotheses. The hierarchical reuse mechanism parallels cognitive chunking theories, grounding these linguistic universals in shared human cognitive architecture — specifically, the compression of linguistic input into nested, reusable units under common memory and processing constraints.

Abstract: We introduce the ladderpath index as a measure of language complexity grounded in algorithmic information theory. It counts the minimum steps needed to reconstruct a sequence through hierarchical reuse of repeated substructures, capturing an exactly computable but constrained form of algorithmic compressibility related to, but distinct from, Kolmogorov complexity. We apply the ladderpath approach to 21 parallel corpora from the Parallel Universal Dependencies dataset. The ladderpath index is approximately invariant across the languages, and varies much less than the corpus length. This is more pronounced when all corpora are mapped to a unified binary representation, providing evidence for the equi-complexity hypothesis from a representation-independent perspective. We also observe trade-offs between character inventory size and corpus length, and between vocabulary-level and corpus-level reconstruction complexity, supporting the trade-off hypothesis that total complexity is conserved and redistributed across linguistic levels. The reusable substructures identified by the ladderpath approach, without any linguistic input, overlap with words and morphological components attested in the natural vocabulary. The hierarchical reuse captured by the ladderpath approach parallels the chunking mechanisms proposed in cognitive science, where the human cognitive system compresses linguistic input into nested, reusable units under shared memory and processing constraints. This connection between cognitive chunking and the ladderpath approach provides a new interpretation for the equi-complexity and trade-off hypotheses, grounding both in the shared cognitive architecture that underlies language processing across human languages.

</details>


### [41] [Pretrained self-supervised speech models can recognize unseen consonants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11542)
*Chihiro Taguchi,Éric Le Ferrand,Hirosi Nakagawa,Hitomi Ono,Kanji Kato,Emily Prud'hommeaux,David Chiang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Fine-tuned self-supervised speech models (Wav2Vec2, HuBERT) on two Khoisan languages reveal that click consonants are recognized more accurately than non-clicks, demonstrating that self-supervised pretraining generalizes effectively to rare, typologically uncommon phonemes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pretrained ASR models are trained predominantly on high-resource languages, raising concerns about their ability to handle typologically rare speech sounds like click consonants found in Khoisan languages. The authors investigate whether these models can recognize clicks as accurately as more common speech sounds.

Method: The authors fine-tune two pretrained self-supervised speech models — Wav2Vec2 and HuBERT — on data from two click-rich Khoisan languages: G|ui and West !Xoon. They then compare recognition accuracy between click and non-click consonants.

Result: Fine-tuned models consistently recognize click consonants more accurately than non-click consonants across both languages and model architectures.

Conclusion: Self-supervised pretraining enables robust generalization across diverse human speech sounds, including rare, typologically uncommon phonemes like click consonants, despite their underrepresentation in training data.

Abstract: Modern pretrained self-supervised automatic speech recognition models are trained on large-scale audio data to encode speech into contextualized representations. However, their training data are heavily skewed toward high-resource languages with little data from low-resource languages, raising concerns about the potential underrepresentation of typologically uncommon speech sounds such as click consonants primarily found in Khoisan languages. This leads to our central research question: Can these models recognize click consonants as accurately as other speech sounds? To address this question, we fine-tune and compare pretrained self-supervised speech models (Wav2Vec2 and HuBERT) on data from two click-rich Khoisan languages (G|ui and West !Xoon). Our results reveal that the fine-tuned models consistently recognize clicks more accurately than non-clicks, suggesting that self-supervision enables generalization across human speech sounds including rare phonemes.

</details>


### [42] [Teaching Diffusion to Speculate Left-to-Right](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11552)
*Lexington Whalen,Yuki Ito,Ryo Sakamoto*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Three training-time interventions — token positional weighting, first-error focal loss, and a chain loss term — are introduced to bridge the gap between bidirectional block-diffusion drafters and left-to-right autoregressive target models in speculative decoding, improving accepted draft length by 21–76% without additional inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Block-diffusion drafters generate tokens bidirectionally (symmetric training), but autoregressive target models verify left-to-right (asymmetric verification), creating a mismatch between training objective and verification reward that limits accepted draft length and overall efficiency.

Method: Three orthogonal, additive training-time interventions: (1) token positional weighting that emphasizes earlier tokens in the block; (2) a first-error focal loss that specifically targets the position where the accepted prefix breaks; (3) a chain loss term providing a differentiable surrogate for expected accepted length. These compose additively and are orthogonal to test-time mechanisms like multi-draft self-selection.

Result: Across 4 target models and 6 benchmarks (reasoning, code, dialogue), the three interventions raise accepted draft length by 21–76% per benchmark over a position-uniform baseline, with no additional forward passes and no changes to the inference pipeline or rejection-sampling exactness guarantee.

Conclusion: The proposed training-time interventions effectively narrow the train–test asymmetry gap in block-diffusion speculative decoding, yielding substantial efficiency gains while preserving the exactness contract and remaining compatible with complementary test-time alignment strategies.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve remarkable performance across a wide range of tasks, but their autoregressive decoding process incurs substantial inference costs due to inherently sequential token generation. Speculative decoding addresses this bottleneck by employing a lightweight draft model to propose multiple future tokens that are subsequently verified in parallel by a larger target model. Recent work has demonstrated that diffusion language models are well suited for this setting, as they can generate entire blocks of draft tokens in parallel and thereby alleviate the sequential constraints of autoregressive drafting. A subtlety of this regime is that block-diffusion drafters generate tokens bidirectionally within a block, whereas verification is performed by an autoregressive target model that evaluates tokens in a strictly left-to-right manner, leaving a gap between the symmetric training-time objective and the asymmetric verification-time reward. In this work, we offer an empirical analysis of three training-time interventions that narrow this gap: token positional weighting, a first-error focal loss that targets the position that breaks the accepted prefix within each block, and a chain loss term that substitutes a differentiable surrogate for the expected accepted length. The three interventions act along orthogonal axes (position, block-conditional first error, joint prefix) and compose additively; they are likewise orthogonal to test-time alignment mechanisms such as multi-draft self-selection, with which they can in principle be combined. Across four target models and six reasoning, code, and dialogue benchmarks, the three interventions raise accepted draft length by 21-76% per benchmark over a position-uniform baseline, without adding additional forward passes and without changing the inference pipeline or the rejection-sampling exactness contract.

</details>


### [43] [From Architecture to Output: Structural Origins of Hallucination in Large Language Models and the Amplifying Role of Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.07537)
*Md. Rejaul Korim Sadi,Toufiqur Rahman Tasin,Golam Mostofa Naeem*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Hallucination in LLMs is traced to three architectural mechanisms—self-attention's co-occurrence learning, MLE training without factual constraints, and autoregressive decoding's exposure bias—each mapped to specific hallucination types, with dataset pathologies amplifying but not causing them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination taxonomies classify outputs descriptively (intrinsic/extrinsic, faithfulness/factuality) but fail to identify the internal mechanisms that produce hallucination. The paper aims to bridge this gap by providing a mechanistic, architectural account of why hallucination occurs.

Method: The authors conduct a structural analysis decomposing LLM hallucination into three interacting architectural decisions: (1) self-attention relying on co-occurrence statistics rather than semantic grounding, (2) maximum likelihood estimation optimizing next-token probability without truth constraints, and (3) autoregressive decoding with irreversible left-to-right commitment under exposure bias. Each mechanism is mapped to specific output categories in the Alansari and Luqman taxonomy, and dataset pathologies are analyzed as amplifiers rather than root causes.

Result: Three mappings were established: self-attention → intrinsic hallucination (entity confusion, fact misattribution, semantic drift); MLE training → extrinsic hallucination (statistically plausible but factually wrong outputs); autoregressive decoding → logical inconsistency (cascading errors from a single wrong token). Dataset pathologies (long-tail deficiencies, training bias, synthetic pollution) were shown to exploit these mechanisms rather than independently cause hallucination.

Conclusion: Hallucination is a compound failure system arising from interacting architectural design choices, not merely a data problem. Output-type-only taxonomies have a diagnostic limitation: they describe what hallucination looks like but not why it occurs. This mechanistic understanding points toward inference-layer mitigation strategies rather than purely data-centric fixes.

Abstract: Large language models hallucinate--producing fluent, confident, factually wrong outputs--with a consistency that persists across generations and scales. Existing taxonomies classify hallucination by output type, distinguishing intrinsic from extrinsic failures and faithfulness from factuality divergence. These frameworks are descriptively rigorous but do not identify which internal mechanism produced a given instance. This paper analyses hallucination as a structural consequence of three architectural decisions that together form a compound failure system. Self-attention's co-occurrence learning substitutes statistical proximity for semantic meaning and produces entity confusion, fact misattribution, and semantic drift. The maximum likelihood estimation training objective optimises next-token probability without factual constraint, rewarding statistically plausible outputs regardless of their truth value. Autoregressive decoding's permanent left-to-right commitment under exposure bias ensures that a single wrong token cascades forward through the entire output sequence without revision. Dataset pathologies--long-tail deficiencies, training bias, and synthetic pollution--amplify these vulnerabilities but do not independently cause them. We make three contributions. First, we map each mechanism to a specific output category in the Alansari and Luqman taxonomy, locating intrinsic hallucination in self-attention, extrinsic hallucination in MLE, and logical inconsistency in autoregressive decoding. Second, we show that each commonly cited dataset pathology exploits one of these mechanisms rather than originating hallucination independently. Third, we identify the diagnostic limitation of output-type-only classification and contrast it with inference-layer mitigation approaches.

</details>


### [44] [When is Your LLM Steerable?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11599)
*Chenrui Fan,Yize Cheng,Ming Li,Soheil Feizi,Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ASTEER is a testbed of 1.4M steered generations across 150 concepts, used to show that early hidden-state features (comparing pre-/post-steering activations across layers and tokens) can predict steering outcomes (under-steer, succeed, over-steer) with ~0.7 macro-F1 via a GBDT classifier, enabling efficient steering-strength search without full autoregressive rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation steering is a lightweight inference-time control method, but its success is highly sensitive to prompt, concept, model, and steering configuration. Determining the viable steering regime typically demands expensive grid searches and full-generation evaluation. The authors aim to predict steerability from early internal states to avoid this cost.

Method: The authors introduce ASTEER, a dataset of 1.4M steered generations covering 150 concepts with binary success/failure labels. They extract features comparing hidden states before and after steering across layers and early token positions, capturing how steering effects propagate. A Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT) classifier is trained on these early-decoding features to predict whether steering will under-steer, succeed, or over-steer. The predictor is then used to guide steering strength search, dramatically reducing decoding cost.

Result: The GBDT classifier achieves ~0.7 macro-F1 on unseen concepts, showing that early hidden states contain substantial structured information about eventual steering efficacy. Using the predictor as a guide for steering-strength search yields near-optimal performance at a fraction of the full decoding cost.

Conclusion: Early internal states encode reliable signals about steering outcomes, enabling efficient prediction without full rollouts. This opens the door to more adaptive, cost-effective activation steering pipelines.

Abstract: Activation steering offers a lightweight approach to control language models' behavior at inference time, but whether it succeeds or fails heavily depends on the prompt, concept, model, and steering configuration. Finding the regime and boundaries of successful steering typically requires expensive grid searches and post-hoc evaluation of full autoregressive rollouts. In this work, we investigate whether steerability can be predicted from the model's internal states at the beginning of the generation process, e.g., after generating the first few tokens, and how to leverage such a predictor to improve steering success rate. To this end, we first introduce ASTEER, a testbed including 1.4M steered generations, spanning 150 concepts with each steering success/failure labeled. Leveraging this testbed, we analyze the model's early decoding dynamics by extracting features that compare hidden states before and after steering across layers and initial decoding steps. These features help us understand how steering's effects propagate along layers and token positions, which provide key information for steerability prediction. We then train a Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT) classifier on these features to predict whether an intervention will under-steer, succeed, or over-steer without requiring full rollout. Our predictor achieves around 0.7 macro-F1 score on unseen concepts, demonstrating that early hidden states encode substantial, structured information about eventual steering efficacy. We further leverage this steerability predictor as guidance for steering strength searching, achieving near-optimal performance with a small fraction of decoding cost.

</details>


### [45] [Multi-Agent Reasoning with Adaptive Worker Allocation for Stance Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11609)
*Meysam Sabbaghan,Arman Zareian Jahromi,Doina Caragea*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-agent framework for stance detection that uses a Manager-Worker architecture with adaptive worker allocation, shifting aggregation from label voting to reasoning synthesis. Evaluated on three benchmark datasets, it achieves strong gains on implicit/context-dependent stance cases (86.07 Macro-F1 on COVID-19, 82.90 on SemEval-2016) while remaining competitive on explicit stance datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-pass LLM prompting for stance detection is brittle when multiple interpretations are plausible. Existing aggregation strategies (majority voting, self-consistency) improve robustness by combining labels but discard the intermediate reasoning needed to resolve conflicting interpretations — a critical gap when stance is implicit, indirect, or rhetorically framed in short-form texts.

Method: A Manager-Worker multi-agent architecture where: (1) the Manager adaptively allocates a variable number of Worker agents based on input complexity; (2) each Worker analyzes the input from a distinct perspective and produces reasoning-only explanations without emitting stance labels; (3) the Manager synthesizes these explanations to produce the final prediction. This shifts aggregation from label-level voting to reasoning-level synthesis.

Result: The framework achieved 86.07 Macro-F1 on COVID-19 Stance and 82.90 on SemEval-2016, with the largest gains concentrated on implicit and context-dependent stance cases.
