Performance remained competitive on more explicit datasets like P-Stance. Evaluations used Llama, Mistral, and Gemini as backbone LLMs.

Conclusion: Adaptive reasoning-level aggregation is most beneficial when stance cannot be reliably inferred from surface cues alone, suggesting that synthesizing diverse intermediate reasoning paths is a more effective aggregation paradigm than label-level voting for nuanced stance detection tasks.

Abstract: Stance detection requires identifying an author's position toward a target, often from short-form texts where stance is implicit, indirect, or rhetorically framed. Although large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on this task, single-pass prompting can be brittle when multiple interpretations are plausible. Existing aggregation strategies, such as majority voting or self-consistency, improve robustness by combining labels, but they discard the intermediate reasoning needed to resolve conflicting interpretations.
  We introduce a multi-agent reasoning framework with adaptive worker allocation for stance detection that shifts aggregation from label-level voting to reasoning-level synthesis. The framework employs a Manager-Worker architecture in which a Manager adaptively allocates a variable number of Worker agents based on input complexity. Each Worker analyzes the input from a distinct perspective and produces a reasoning-only explanation without emitting a stance label; the Manager then synthesizes these explanations to produce the final prediction.
  We evaluate the proposed framework on SemEval-2016, P-Stance, and COVID-19 Stance using Llama, Mistral, and Gemini. Results show that the framework yields the largest gains on implicit and context-dependent stance cases, achieving 86.07 Macro-F1 on COVID-19 and 82.90 on SemEval-2016, while remaining competitive on more explicit stance datasets such as P-Stance. These findings suggest that adaptive reasoning-level aggregation is most beneficial when stance cannot be reliably inferred from surface cues alone.

</details>


### [46] [Evaluating Bias in Phoneme-Based Automatic Speech Recognition Systems: An Analysis of IPA Transcription Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11639)
*Catherine Bao,Maneesha Rani Saha,Neal Patwari*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates demographic biases (race, gender, accent, age, ethnicity) in two open-source phoneme-based ASR systems—WhisperIPA and ZIPA—that produce IPA transcriptions. Using both standard Phoneme Error Rate (PER) and a novel Soft PER metric that tolerates linguistically similar substitutions, the study reveals persistent performance disparities across demographic groups and languages, informing more inclusive ASR development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most ASR bias research has focused on grapheme-based systems, neglecting phoneme-based models that generate IPA representations. As ASR shifts toward multilingual and low-resource language support, IPA-based layers become critical language-agnostic foundations. Understanding demographic biases in these systems is essential for building equitable, linguistically robust ASR.

Method: The authors evaluate WhisperIPA and ZIPA on multilingual speech corpora and demographically annotated English corpora. Performance is measured by comparing model-generated IPA transcriptions against grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) references using standard PER and a proposed Soft PER metric that accounts for linguistically similar phoneme substitutions. Analysis is stratified across languages and demographic dimensions: gender, accent, ethnicity, and age.

Result: Persistent performance disparities were found across demographic groups and languages, even after allowing for acceptable phonemic variation via the Soft PER metric, indicating that bias in phoneme-based ASR is not merely an artifact of strict evaluation criteria.

Conclusion: Phoneme-based ASR systems exhibit demographic biases comparable to those documented in grapheme-based systems. The findings highlight the need for more inclusive training data and model design, and the proposed Soft PER metric offers a more linguistically informed evaluation framework. Code and data will be released publicly.

Abstract: The popularization of automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems has increased exploration of the demographic biases related to race, age, gender, and accent, often formed from imbalanced training data. Most of these studies focused on standard grapheme-based ASR systems with comparatively little emphasis on phoneme-based systems, such as models that produce International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) representations. As ASR systems shift toward multilingual support and low-resource language modeling, IPA-based layers serve as a critical, language-agnostic foundation. In this study, we evaluate the performance of two state-of-the-art open-source ASR systems, WhisperIPA and ZIPA, that generate IPA transcriptions across diverse accents and language sources. Our evaluation includes existing multilingual speech corpora and demographically annotated English-language corpora. We measure model performance by comparing model-generated IPA transcriptions against grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) systems using both standard phoneme error rate (PER) and a proposed Soft PER metric that tolerates linguistically similar phoneme substitutions. Our analysis examines how performance varies across languages and demographic groups such as gender, accent, ethnicity, and age, revealing persistent disparities even after accounting for acceptable phonemic variation. These findings provide insight into potential sources of bias and inform the development of more inclusive and linguistically robust phoneme-based ASR systems. Our code and data will be made publicly available to the community.

</details>


### [47] [Improving Cross-Format Robustness in Language Models with Multi-Format Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11643)
*June M. Liu,Shaomian Zheng,He Cao,Dingnan Jin,Qing Cui,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Training LLMs with diverse answer formats on just ~30% of the dataset improves cross-format robustness as much as full-format training. Format diversity, not extra supervision, is the key driver.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit format sensitivity: a question answered correctly in one format (e.g., MCQ) may be answered incorrectly in a semantically equivalent format. The authors aim to understand and mitigate this brittleness through multi-format training.

Method: The authors define cross-format robustness as consistency of correct answers across equivalent formats. They propose FormatMix, which expands only a subset of training items into multiple formats using either random or targeted selection. Experiments compare full-format training, MCQ-only supervision, and FormatMix across GLM4 and Llama-3.1 model families.

Result: Multi-format supervision consistently improves both task performance and cross-format robustness. MCQ-only supervision provides little benefit and can reduce robustness. Expanding only ~30% of the training set recovers most gains from full-format training across model families and sizes.

Conclusion: Format diversity, rather than additional supervision volume, is the primary driver of robustness. Lightweight multi-format augmentation (FormatMix) is a practical, model-agnostic method to reduce format sensitivity without altering the base model.

Abstract: Large language models often remain sensitive to answer format: a question solved correctly in one form may fail in another semantically equivalent form. To study this gap, we define cross-format robustness as the extent to which a model answers the same underlying question consistently across formats. We then compare full-format training with FormatMix, which expands only a subset of training items into multiple equivalent formats using either random or targeted selection. Across GLM4 and Llama-3.1, multi-format supervision consistently improves both task performance and cross-format robustness, whereas Multiple-choice question (MCQ)-only supervision alone brings little benefit and can even reduce robustness. We further find that expanding only about 30% of the training set into multiple formats often recovers most of the gain from full-format training, and this effect appears across the model families and sizes we study. These results suggest that format diversity, rather than additional supervision alone, is the key driver of robustness. That lightweight multi-format augmentation is a practical way to make LLMs less sensitive to answer format without changing the base model.

</details>


### [48] [Can AI Reason Like an Urban Planner? Benchmarking Large Language Models Against Professional Judgment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11678)
*Yijie Deng,He Zhu,Wen Wang,Junyou Su,Minxin Chen,Wenjia Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: UPBench evaluates 25 LLMs on planning knowledge using a 4×5 matrix (pillars × cognitive levels), revealing a non-monotonic cognitive curve where models excel at higher-order analysis but struggle with factual recall and integrative judgment due to institutional, jurisdictional, and temporal context dependence. Four epistemic failure modes are identified: regulatory hallucination, conceptual conflation, wickedness paralysis, and phronetic deficit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing use of LLMs in planning practice lacks a systematic framework to assess whether AI can reason with the contextual sensitivity, value awareness, and institutional literacy central to planning expertise, creating a need to delineate which professional knowledge forms are replicable by AI versus those requiring human judgment.

Method: Development of Urban Planning Bench (UPBench), a domain-specific evaluation framework structured as a 4×5 matrix crossing four knowledge pillars with five cognitive levels adapted from Bloom's revised taxonomy. Twenty-five LLMs were assessed through automated scoring combined with expert review to evaluate planning reasoning capabilities.

Result: A non-monotonic cognitive curve emerges: LLMs perform better on higher-order analytical tasks than on factual recall and integrative judgment, revealing that ostensibly lower-order planning knowledge is deeply conditioned by institutional, jurisdictional, and temporal context. Four epistemic diagnostics characterize model failures: regulatory hallucination, conceptual conflation, wickedness paralysis, and phronetic deficit.

Conclusion: LLMs are suitable for differential delegation — assisting with cross-disciplinary synthesis, literature review, scenario generation, and preliminary policy analysis — but remain unreliable for jurisdiction-specific regulation, normative conflict resolution, and context-sensitive procedures. Agencies should mandate verification for AI-assisted regulatory analysis, and planning education should strengthen institutional literacy, normative judgment, and contextual sensitivity.

Abstract: Problem, Research Strategy, and Findings: The rise of large language models (LLMs) raises a key question for urban planning: which forms of professional planning knowledge can AI replicate, and which still require human judgment? Although AI tools are increasingly used in planning practice, there is still no systematic framework for testing whether they can reason with the contextual sensitivity, value awareness, and institutional literacy central to planning expertise. This paper introduces Urban Planning Bench (UPBench), a domain-specific evaluation framework that assesses LLM reasoning through a 4x5 matrix of four knowledge pillars and five cognitive levels adapted from Bloom's revised taxonomy. Evaluating 25 LLMs with automated scoring and expert review, we find a non-monotonic cognitive curve: models perform better on higher-order analytical tasks than on factual recall and integrative judgment. This suggests that planning knowledge often treated as lower-order is deeply shaped by institutional, jurisdictional, and temporal context, making it hard for LLMs to generalize. We summarize these limits as four epistemic diagnostics: regulatory hallucination, conceptual conflation, wickedness paralysis, and phronetic deficit.
  Takeaway for Practice: The findings support differential delegation in planning. LLMs can assist with cross-disciplinary synthesis, literature review, scenario generation, and preliminary policy analysis. However, they remain unreliable for jurisdiction-specific regulation, normative conflict resolution, and context-sensitive procedure. Agencies should require verification for AI-assisted regulatory analysis, while planning education should emphasize institutional literacy, normative judgment, and contextual sensitivity.

</details>


### [49] [UR-BERT: Scaling Text Encoders for Massively Multilingual TTS Through Universal Romanization and Speech Token Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11681)
*Sangmin Lee,Eekgyun Ahn,Woongjib Choi,Hong-Goo Kang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: UR-BERT is a Romanized transcription-based TTS encoder that scales to 495 languages by unifying writing systems into a shared Romanization representation, augmented with a speech token prediction objective for improved phonetic fidelity and text-speech alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional G2P-based TTS encoders are limited to ~100 languages due to the scarcity of reliable G2P resources. The authors aim to break this language barrier by leveraging Romanization as a universal intermediate representation, enabling massively multilingual TTS at unprecedented scale.

Method: UR-BERT unifies diverse writing systems into Romanized transcriptions, bypassing the need for language-specific G2P modules. A speech token prediction objective is introduced during training, where the encoder predicts discretized speech tokens derived from a self-supervised speech model, encouraging the learning of speech-aware phonetic representations in a data-efficient manner.

Result: TTS systems using UR-BERT consistently outperform recent text encoder baselines across diverse languages and resource conditions, and exhibit strong generalization to unseen languages.

Conclusion: Romanization-based text encoding combined with speech token prediction enables scalable, high-quality multilingual TTS across 495 languages, substantially expanding the language coverage beyond traditional G2P-dependent methods.

Abstract: We propose UR-BERT, a Romanized transcription-based text-to-speech (TTS) encoder for massively multilingual TTS systems. Conventional grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P)-based approaches are limited to around 100 languages due to the availability of reliable G2P resources. In contrast, UR-BERT scales to 495 languages by unifying diverse writing systems into a shared Romanization representation. To further enhance phonetic fidelity and text-speech alignment, we introduce a speech token prediction objective during training, which encourages the encoder to learn speech-aware phonetic representations in a data-efficient manner. Experiments show that TTS systems built on UR-BERT consistently outperform recent text encoder baselines across a wide range of languages and resource conditions, and demonstrate strong generalization to unseen languages.

</details>


### [50] [Layer-Isolated Evaluation: Gating the Deterministic Scaffold of a Production LLM Agent with a No-LLM, Regression-Locked Test Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11686)
*Sawyer Zhang,Alexander Wang,Sophie Lei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Layer-isolated evaluation decomposes an LLM agent into a taxonomy of layers, each tested by deterministic, no-LLM assertion slices. Experiments show that aggregate pass-rates mask regressions (moving only -1.7 to -5.9 pp), while the affected slice craters (-25 to -91 pp), demonstrating that per-slice baseline-locked gates localize regressions that aggregate metrics conceal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end task-success evaluation of LLM agents provides only an aggregate pass-rate, which indicates regression occurred but not where. This lack of localization makes debugging costly and slow. The authors aim to replace opaque aggregate metrics with fine-grained, layer-specific evaluation that pinpoints the exact component responsible for a regression.

Method: The authors decompose a production ordering agent into a fixed taxonomy of layers: ontology, intent, routing, decomposition, escalation, safety, memory, and cross-cutting envelope/defense. Each layer is tested by its own deterministic assertion slice in a "pure" mode that uses no LLM. The suite contains 238 cases across 23 slices (225 run in 2.39s, ~10ms/case) and executes in CI on every change against a locked per-slice baseline. Validation employs controlled regression injection, degrading one layer at a time across seven non-safety layers. The approach is replicated on a second, structurally different tenant (Starbucks SG).

Result: Aggregate masking is the key finding: for six local regressions, aggregate pass-rate drops only -1.7 to -5.9 pp, while the corresponding slice craters -25 to -91 pp. Damage remains isolated — the injected layer's slice is the single worst-hit in 5 of 7 cases and top-3 in all 7 (mean rank 1.29 of 19). The localization effect replicates on the Starbucks SG tenant: all seven matching slices crater, confirming it is not a single-catalog artifact.

Conclusion: The authors position layer-isolated evaluation as a concrete, deterministic instantiation of EDDOps' component-level evaluation prescription, with CheckList as an ancestor and as the deterministic mirror of whole-workflow stochastic mutation testing. Contributions are: (a) a fully decomposed, sub-second, no-LLM per-layer harness for a production agent; (b) a coverage-honesty test-adequacy criterion that refuses to score an unexercised layer; and (c) regression-injection evidence that per-slice baseline-locked gates localize regressions aggregate metrics mask.

Abstract: End-to-end task-success is the dominant way to evaluate LLM agents, but one aggregate number tells you that an agent regressed, not where. We present layer-isolated evaluation: a deployed ordering agent is decomposed into a fixed taxonomy of layers (ontology, intent, routing, decomposition, escalation, safety, memory, and cross-cutting envelope/defense), each exercised by its own assertion slice in a deterministic, no-LLM "pure" mode. The pure suite (238 cases across 23 slices; 225 run in 2.39 s, ~10 ms/case) runs in CI on every change against a locked per-slice baseline. We validate by controlled regression injection, degrading one layer at a time across seven non-safety layers. The effect we did not design in is masking: the aggregate pass-rate barely moves (-1.7 to -5.9 pp for six local regressions), while the matching slice craters (-25 to -91 pp). A layer's slice reacting to its own fault is partly by construction; the measured results are (i) the aggregate masking and (ii) that damage stays off the other slices: the injected layer's slice is the single worst-hit in 5 of 7 cases and top-3 in 7 of 7 (mean rank 1.29 of 19). Localization replicates on a second, structurally different tenant (Starbucks SG): all seven matching slices crater, so it is not a single-catalog artifact. We position it as a concrete, deterministic instantiation of the component-level evaluation EDDOps prescribes but leaves unimplemented, with CheckList as ancestor and as the deterministic mirror image of whole-workflow stochastic mutation testing. Our contributions: (a) a fully decomposed, sub-second, no-LLM per-layer harness for a production agent, (b) a coverage-honesty test-adequacy criterion that refuses to score an unexercised layer, and (c) the regression-injection demonstration that per-slice baseline-locked gates localize regressions an aggregate metric masks.

</details>


### [51] [Goal-Autopilot: A Verifiable Anti-Fabrication Firewall for Unattended Long-Horizon Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11688)
*Youwang Deng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Autopilot introduces an execution model that structurally eliminates fabricated success claims in LLM agents by externalizing state into a gated finite-state machine, proving a No-False-Success theorem. Across 3,150 test cells, fabrication dropped to 0.95% versus 8.10–25.05% for baselines, with SWE-bench Lite fabrication falling from 33.7% to 0.67%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents operating without human supervision tend to fabricate success — confidently reporting task completion they never actually verified. This makes them untrustworthy for unattended long-horizon autonomy. The authors argue honesty (bounded claims at termination) should be treated as a distinct, first-class metric separate from capability.

Method: Autopilot externalizes all working state into a durable, gated finite-state machine advanced by a scheduler one stateless tick at a time. A hard floor forbids any terminal "done" claim unless the corresponding falsifiable gate actually executed and passed. The model is rehydrated each tick with only the state machine, yielding constant per-step context cost. The authors prove a No-False-Success theorem: under gate soundness, floor enforcement, and plan coverage, termination implies the goal holds. Worst-case behavior degrades to an honest stall, never a fabricated success.

Result: Evaluated on a 3,150-cell corpus (70 tasks × 3 systems × 3 models × 5 seeds, including 50 SWE-bench Lite tasks across 11 OSS repos): Autopilot fabricated success on 0.95% of cells [95% CI 0.38–1.62] vs. Reflexion (8.10%) and StateFlow (25.05%). On SWE-bench Lite, Autopilot's fabrication rate was 0.67% vs. StateFlow's 33.7% (paired difference of −33.07 pp). Notably, all ten Autopilot fabrications came from the strongest model; two weaker models never fabricated across 700 paired cells, indicating the mechanism — not model strength — drives honesty.

Conclusion: The firewall trades coverage for honesty by design. An honest stall is recoverable (via human intervention or retry), whereas a confidently wrong output shipped downstream is not. The gate mechanism, not model quality, is the key enabler of trustworthy unattended autonomy.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents are not trusted to run unattended: with no human watching, they confidently report success they never verified. We treat honesty -- bounding what an agent may claim at termination -- as a first-class metric for unattended autonomy, distinct from capability. We present Autopilot, an execution model that makes silent fabricated success structurally impossible rather than merely rarer. Autopilot externalizes all working state into a durable, gated finite-state machine that a scheduler advances one stateless tick at a time; a hard floor forbids any terminal "done" claim whose falsifiable gate did not actually execute and pass. We prove a No-False-Success theorem -- under gate soundness, floor enforcement, and plan coverage, termination implies the goal holds -- whose only trust points are empirically measurable, and show the worst case degrades to an honest stall, never a fabricated success. Because each tick rehydrates only the state machine, per-step context cost is constant in the horizon. Across a 3,150-cell paired corpus (70 tasks $\times$ 3 systems $\times$ 3 models $\times$ 5 seeds, including 50 SWE-bench Lite tasks across 11 OSS repos), Autopilot fabricates on 0.95% of cells [95% CI 0.38--1.62] while Reflexion and StateFlow baselines fabricate on 8.10% [6.48--9.81] and 25.05% [22.48--27.62] respectively. The headline contrast lives in the hard regime: on SWE-bench Lite, the firewall reduces fabrication from 33.7% (StateFlow) to 0.67%, a paired difference of $-33.07$ pp [95% CI $-36.53, -29.73$]. The mechanism is the gate, not the model: all ten Autopilot fabrications come from the strongest model, while two weaker mid-tier models never fabricate across 700 paired cells. The firewall trades coverage for honesty by design -- an honest stall is recoverable; a confident wrong output shipped downstream is not.

</details>


### [52] [Substrate Asymmetry in User-Side Memory: A Diagnostic Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11712)
*Youwang Deng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: User memory in LLMs decomposes into three orthogonal axes (behavioral consistency, factual presence, factual absence) that are in tension: per-user LoRA excels at style/voice while RAG excels at knowing when to abstain. This trade-off is causal (same attention layers drive both effects oppositely), worsens under RLHF alignment, and routing between substrates is best solved by question classification rather than calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard practice of evaluating LLM user-side memory as a single "personalization" score masks critical failure modes. The authors argue that memory is not monolithic — a system can be good at one aspect (e.g., mimicking user style) while failing at another (e.g., knowing when a fact about the user is absent). This motivates factorizing memory into orthogonal axes to diagnose and mitigate trade-offs between parametric memory (LoRA) and retrieval-based memory (RAG).

Method: The authors compare per-user gamma-LoRA (a LoRA adapter trained on each user's conversation history) against BGE-large dense top-K retrieval on a controlled 50-user synthetic corpus and the real-data LaMP-3 benchmark. They conduct causal probing by zeroing LoRA weights in specific attention layers (21-35), perform a 9-condition mitigation sweep on real data diagnosing instruction-following collapse, and evaluate substrate-selection routing using both logit-based routers and a 110M DistilBERT question classifier.

Result: Key findings: (1) gamma-LoRA wins behavioral consistency while RAG wins factual absence; (2) attention layers 21-35 causally drive both effects in opposite directions (zeroing LoRA weights raises absence TPR by +33pp but drops presence TPR by 20pp); (3) on RLHF-tuned Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, parametric memory's behavioral advantage collapses while its absence-calibration deficit widens (an "alignment tax"); (4) on LaMP-3, gamma-LoRA underperforms a majority baseline due to instruction-following collapse, fixable via eval-time logit masking; (5) a 110M DistilBERT classifier on question text alone beats all logit-based routers for substrate selection.

Conclusion: User memory is inherently multi-dimensional, and no single memory substrate (parametric or retrieval) wins all axes. The diagnostic framework reveals fundamental trade-offs that are exacerbated, not resolved, by RLHF alignment. Substrate routing should be treated as a question classification problem rather than a calibration problem. The paper contributes a diagnostic methodology, empirical negatives on real data, alignment-tax replication, and the routing-as-classification insight.

Abstract: User-side memory in LLMs is typically scored as a single "personalization" capability: given a user's history, is the output more user-aware? We show this aggregate metric hides opposite-direction failures. Memory factorises into at least three orthogonal axes -- behavioral consistency (style, voice), factual presence (recall facts in history), and factual absence (abstain when a fact is absent) -- and no single substrate wins all three. Comparing per-user gamma-LoRA (a small LoRA adapter trained on each user's history; gamma denotes per-user, not per-task) against BGE-large dense top-K retrieval on a controlled 50-user synthetic corpus and a real-data probe (LaMP-3), we find gamma-LoRA decisively wins behavioral style while RAG decisively wins factual absence -- and the same query-projection cells in attention layers 21-35 causally load-bear both effects in opposite directions (zeroing those LoRA weights raises absence-probe TPR by +33 pp and drops presence-probe TPR by 20 pp). On the more heavily RLHF-tuned Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct the asymmetry strengthens, not heals: parametric memory's behavioral advantage collapses while its absence-calibration deficit against retrieval widens -- an alignment tax on parametric user-memory. On real-data LaMP-3, gamma-LoRA underperforms a majority baseline; a 9-condition mitigation sweep diagnoses this as instruction-following collapse, not substrate failure (a 9x2 cross-product shows the eval-time {1..5} logit mask drives main_acc to >=0.995 on every recipe), and the best training-time fix replicates bit-identically on Llama. Finally, substrate-selection routing is question-classification, not calibration: a 110M DistilBERT on the question text alone beats every logit-based router. We contribute the diagnostic framework, the diagnosed real-data negative, the alignment-tax replication, and the routing-as-classification finding.

</details>


### [53] [Hey Chat, Can You Teach Me? Structuring Socratic Dialogue for Human Learning in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11744)
*Sidney Tio,Arunesh Sinha,Pradeep Varakantham*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLMs struggle with extended tutoring because they must simultaneously sequence curriculum, conduct Socratic dialogue, and infer student knowledge. The authors separate these tasks: a prerequisite knowledge graph structures the curriculum, a lightweight PPO policy decides which node to teach next and how long to spend, while the LLM handles Socratic exchange. This system outperforms heuristic baselines, frontier models, and Socratic specialists on mastery rate and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based learning interactions are unstructured chats with no prior student model. Scaling models alone fails to close the gap between informal chat and effective tutoring over extended sessions, which requires simultaneous curriculum sequencing, Socratic dialogue, and knowledge state inference — a triple demand that overwhelms even frontier and education-tuned LLMs.

Method: The system constructs a prerequisite knowledge graph (subtopics as nodes, dependencies as edges). Tutoring is framed as a sequential decision problem: which node to teach next and how many dialogue turns to allocate. A lightweight PPO policy handles this sequencing decision, while the LLM conducts Socratic dialogue at the chosen node and returns a progress signal. The policy is trained via reinforcement learning.

Result: The PPO-paired tutor outperforms heuristic baselines, frontier general-purpose models, and a Socratic-dialogue-specialized model on both the rate of students reaching full curriculum mastery and the number of turns required. These gains are attributed to explicit curriculum structure rather than model scaling.

Conclusion: Explicit curriculum structure, implemented via a prerequisite knowledge graph and RL-based sequencing policy, delivers tutoring improvements that scaling the underlying LLM does not. Separating curriculum planning from dialogue generation is key to effective extended tutoring sessions.

Abstract: Large language models are now widely used for everyday learning, but the underlying interactions are typically unstructured chats rather than following a curriculum. Unlike formal online learning systems, these interactions carry no prior record of the student, so any estimate of what the student already knows must be inferred from the dialogue itself. We show that this gap is not closed by scaling models alone. Frontier and education-tuned LLMs perform poorly when asked to tutor a student over an extended session, because doing so requires three things at once. The tutor must sequence a curriculum, conduct Socratic dialogue, and infer the student's knowledge state from that dialogue. We propose separating these responsibilities. Given a student query, our system constructs a prerequisite knowledge graph in which subtopics are nodes and dependencies are edges, and frames tutoring as deciding which node to teach next and how many dialogue turns to spend on it before moving on. A lightweight PPO policy handles this sequencing decision, while an LLM conducts the Socratic exchange at the chosen node and returns a signal of student progress. Across held-out STEM and non-STEM topics, our PPO-paired tutor outperforms heuristic baselines, frontier general-purpose models, and a model specialised for Socratic dialogue: on both the rate at which students reach full curriculum mastery and the number of turns required. Explicit curriculum structure delivers gains that scaling the underlying model does not.

</details>


### [54] [Automated Creativity Evaluation of Language Models Across Open-Ended Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11762)
*Min Sen Tan,Zachary Kit Chun Choy,Syed Ali Redha Alsagoff,Nadya Yuki Wangsajaya,Mohor Banerjee,Swaagat Bikash Saikia,Alvin Chan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a domain-agnostic, automated framework for evaluating LLM creativity across open-ended tasks, separating measurement from the task itself. Divergent creativity is measured via semantic entropy (validated against human judgments), while convergent creativity uses a retrieval-based multi-agent judge. Validated across problem-solving, research ideation, and creative writing, the framework reliably captures novelty, diversity, and task fulfilment, revealing how model properties affect creative performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing creativity metrics for LLMs are tightly coupled to specific tasks, embedding domain assumptions that limit scalability and generality. There is a need for systematic, scalable, and task-agnostic methods to evaluate creativity across diverse open-ended tasks.

Method: Divergent creativity is quantified using semantic entropy — a reference-free, robust metric capturing novelty and diversity, validated against human annotations, LLM-based novelty judgments, and baseline diversity measures. Convergent creativity is assessed through a novel retrieval-based multi-agent judge framework, providing context-sensitive evaluation of task fulfilment with over 60% improved efficiency. The framework is validated in three domains: problem-solving (MacGyver), research ideation (HypoGen), and creative writing (BookMIA), using a broad suite of LLMs.

Result: The framework reliably captures key creativity facets — novelty, diversity, and task fulfilment. Empirical results reveal how model properties (size, temperature, recency, and reasoning capabilities) impact creative performance across the three tested domains.

Conclusion: The work establishes a reproducible and generalizable standard for automated LLM creativity evaluation, enabling scalable benchmarking and accelerating progress in creative AI.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in language understanding, reasoning, and generation, sparking growing interest in their creative potential. Realizing this potential requires systematic and scalable methods for evaluating creativity across diverse tasks. However, most existing creativity metrics are tightly coupled to specific tasks, embedding domain assumptions into the evaluation process, and limiting scalability and generality. To address this gap, we introduce an automated, domain-agnostic framework for quantifying LLM creativity across open-ended tasks. Our approach separates the measurement apparatus from the creative task itself, enabling scalable, task-agnostic assessment. Divergent creativity is measured using semantic entropy, a reference-free and robust metric for novelty and diversity, validated against human annotations, LLM-based novelty judgments and baseline diversity measures. Convergent creativity is assessed via a novel retrieval-based multi-agent judge framework that delivers context-sensitive evaluation of task fulfilment with over 60% improved efficiency. We validate our framework in three qualitatively distinct domains: problem-solving (MacGyver), research ideation (HypoGen), and creative writing (BookMIA), using a broad suite of LLMs. Empirical results show that our framework reliably captures key facets of creativity, including novelty, diversity, and task fulfilment, and reveal how model properties, such as size, temperature, recency, and reasoning, impact creative performance. Our work establishes a reproducible and generalizable standard for automated LLM creativity evaluation, paving the way for scalable benchmarking and accelerating progress in creative AI.

</details>


### [55] [Lius: Translation Model Based Instructional Lingustic Using Continual Instruction Tuning In Kupang Malay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11786)
*Joanito Agili Lopo,Yunita Sari,Guntur Budi Herwanto*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A fine-tuning method called Continual Instruction Tuning (CIT) is proposed for low-resource neural machine translation, leveraging bilingual dictionary features. The resulting model, Lius, outperforms standard instruction-tuned LLMs by 4–6 points and NMT/multilingual LLMs by 10–13 points on Kupang Malay translation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs suffer performance degradation on low-resource languages due to scarcity of large-scale parallel corpora. The authors aim to mitigate this reliance by exploiting readily available bilingual lexical resources and a novel iterative training paradigm.

Method: The approach has two core components: (1) instruction design leveraging explicit lexical and semantic features extracted from a bilingual dictionary for Kupang Malay, and (2) Continual Instruction Tuning (CIT), an iterative instruction-based training paradigm that progressively refines the model's translation capability over multiple rounds of fine-tuning.

Result: The model Lius outperforms standard instruction-tuned models by 4–6 points and surpasses both Neural Machine Translation (NMT) baselines and Multilingual LLM models by 10–13 points across multiple evaluation metrics on Kupang Malay translation.

Conclusion: Leveraging bilingual dictionary features combined with iterative instruction tuning substantially reduces dependence on large-scale parallel data, offering a viable pathway for adapting LLMs to low-resource language translation.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer new potential for translation tasks but often experience performance degradation when handling low-resource languages. To address this limitation, we propose an approach for fine-tuning LLMs on a low-resource language, Kupang Malay. Our approach involves designing a set of instructions by leveraging explicit lexical and semantic features from a bilingual dictionary, and introducing Continual Instruction Tuning (CIT), a training paradigm that enables iterative instruction-based training. Experimental results demonstrate that our model, named Lius, yields notable improvements over standard instruction-tuned models by outperforming 4-6 points, and surpassing both Neural Machine Translation (NMT) and Multilingual LLM models by 10-13 points on several evaluation metrics. These findings highlight the potential of our approach to mitigate the reliance on large-scale parallel data in low-resource language translation.

</details>


### [56] [External Experience Serving in Production LLM Systems: A Deployment-Oriented Study of Quality-Cost Trade-offs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11806)
*Lin Sun,Heming Zhang,Xiangzheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper frames external experience serving in LLM production systems as a quality-cost trade-off problem. Using a real moderation task with tool-use and GPQA as contrast tasks, it compares no-experience, random, global, and retrieval-based injection strategies. Results show that selective retrieval outperforms unconditional global injection when experience is case-dependent, and that retrieval quality — not just quantity — drives gains. The cost-benefit profile varies significantly across different output regimes, suggesting external experience should be treated as a selective, cost-aware decision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Production LLM systems accumulate reusable operational experience, but there is no systematic understanding of how different strategies for injecting external experience trade off task quality against online serving costs (prompt burden, latency, serving pressure). The paper addresses this gap by treating experience serving as a deployment-oriented optimization problem.

Method: The authors evaluate four strategies — no-experience baseline, random experience control, global prompt injection (unconditional), and retrieval-based selective injection — across three task regimes: a real production moderation setting, tool-use, and GPQA. They measure both task quality and serving cost (e.g., prompt length, latency, compute), analyzing how the quality-cost operating curve shifts under each strategy.

Result: Selective retrieval provides a stronger operating point than global injection when experience is case-dependent. Retrieval quality (not merely increasing Top-K) drives benefit. The same serving policy exhibits substantially different cost-benefit profiles across short-output (moderation) versus decode-heavy (GPQA, tool-use) regimes. External experience pays off only when the serving interface and task-specific cost structure jointly make quality gains worth the online cost.

Conclusion: External experience should be treated as a selective, cost-aware serving decision rather than a universal add-on. Deployment decisions must account for both the serving interface and the task-specific cost structure to determine whether experience injection is justified.

Abstract: Production LLM systems accumulate reusable operational experience, but the practical deployment issue is not merely whether such experience can help. It is how different serving strategies trade off quality against online cost under realistic constraints. Injecting external experience can improve task quality, yet it also increases prompt burden, latency, and serving pressure. We study \textit{external experience serving} as a deployment-oriented quality-cost trade-off problem. We evaluate this question in a real production moderation setting, with tool-use and GPQA as supporting contrast tasks that expose different output-cost regimes. We compare no-experience baselines, random experience controls, global prompt injection, and retrieval-based selective injection, and analyze both task quality and serving cost. The results show that, once experience becomes case-dependent, selective retrieval provides a stronger operating point than unconditional global injection. They further show that retrieval quality matters more than simply increasing Top-$K$, and that the same serving policy can exhibit substantially different cost-benefit profiles across short-output and decode-heavy regimes. These findings suggest that external experience is best treated as a selective, cost-aware serving decision rather than as a universal add-on. Overall, in the settings studied here, external experience pays off only when both the serving interface and the task-specific cost structure make its quality gains worth the online cost.

</details>


### [57] [WorldReasoner: Evaluating Whether Language Model Agents Forecast Events with Valid Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11816)
*Yizhou Chi,Eric Chamoun,Zifeng Ding,Andreas Vlachos*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: WorldReasoner is an evaluation framework for event forecasting that tests language-model agents on three axes — outcome accuracy, evidence quality, and causal reasoning — by restricting agents to temporally valid (pre-forecast-date) evidence and comparing their predictions, cited sources, and causal graphs against post-resolution hindsight references. The benchmark contains 345 resolved tasks from 14,141 articles with 8,087 extracted events, and experiments show that temporally valid retrieval drives accuracy while causal graph construction improves key-event recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing forecasting evaluations focus solely on final-answer accuracy, which fails to distinguish genuine forecasting from memorization, fabricated evidence, or unsupported causal narratives. There is a need for a framework that enforces temporal validity — ensuring agents only access information available before a forecast date — and that evaluates not just what agents predict, but how they reason and what evidence they cite.

Method: WorldReasoner consists of: (1) an agentic construction pipeline that generates forecasting questions, collects time-stamped evidence, and builds hindsight causal event graphs at scale; (2) a temporally-gated evaluation protocol where agents receive a resolved question, a simulated forecast date, and access only to pre-date evidence; (3) a three-axis scoring rubric measuring outcome quality (probability vs. resolution), evidence quality (faithfulness and relevance of cited sources), and reasoning quality (causal graph alignment with post-resolution hindsight graphs). The benchmark yields 345 tasks from 14,141 articles covering 8,087 extracted events, tested across six controlled agent settings.

Result: Temporally valid retrieval emerged as the strongest driver of outcome accuracy. Causal graph construction improved key-event recovery in agent reasoning. Correct graph-enabled forecasts were more strongly grounded in key events and relevant sources. However, agents consistently struggled to convert grounded evidence into well-calibrated probabilities, revealing a gap between evidence retrieval and probabilistic judgment.

Conclusion: WorldReasoner demonstrates that multi-axis evaluation of forecasting — encompassing outcome, evidence, and causal reasoning under temporal validity constraints — provides a more rigorous and diagnostic assessment of agent capabilities than accuracy alone. The framework highlights specific failure modes, particularly in probability calibration, that future forecasting agents must address.

Abstract: Forecasting real-world events requires language-model agents to reason under uncertainty from incomplete, time-bounded information. Yet evaluating whether agents genuinely forecast requires more than final-answer accuracy: a model may be correct by recalling memorized training facts, citing fabricated evidence, or producing an unsupported causal story. We present WorldReasoner, an evaluation framework for temporally valid event forecasting. Each task gives an agent a resolved forecasting question, a simulated forecast date, and access only to evidence available before that date; after resolution, the framework scores the submitted probability, cited evidence, and optional causal event graph. WorldReasoner reports three complementary axes: outcome quality against resolved answers, evidence quality over cited sources, and reasoning quality against post-resolution hindsight graphs. The benchmark is built by an agentic construction pipeline that generates forecasting questions, collects time-stamped evidence, and builds hindsight reference graphs at scale, yielding 345 resolved tasks derived from 14,141 articles with graphs covering 8,087 extracted events. Across six controlled agent settings, temporally valid retrieval is the strongest driver of outcome accuracy; causal graph construction improves key-event recovery; and correct graph-enabled forecasts are more strongly grounded in key events and relevant sources, yet agents still struggle to convert grounded evidence into calibrated probabilities.

</details>


### [58] [I Understand How You Feel: Enhancing Deeper Emotional Support Through Multilingual Emotional Validation in Dialogue System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11875)
*Zi Haur Pang,Yahui Fu,Koji Inoue,Tatsuya Kawahara*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces emotional validation in dialogue systems, decomposing it into three subtasks (response identification, timing detection, response generation), releases multilingual corpora (M-EDESConv and M-TESC), proposes a novel architecture (MEGUMI) for timing detection that outperforms baselines, and benchmarks LLMs revealing that emotional understanding remains a key limitation despite fluent generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emotional validation — explicitly acknowledging a user's feelings as understandable — has proven therapeutic benefits but has been largely overlooked in computational dialogue research. The authors aim to formalize this underexplored problem and provide resources and models to advance multilingual emotional validation in dialogue systems.

Method: 1) Corpus creation: M-EDESConv (120k English-Japanese multilingual corpus with hybrid manual/automatic annotation) and M-TESC (multilingual spoken-dialogue test set). 2) Validation timing detection: MEGUMI architecture that fuses frozen XLM-RoBERTa semantic representations with language-specific emotion encoders using cross-modal attention and gated fusion. 3) Benchmarking: EmoValidBench evaluates GPT-4.1 Nano and Llama-3.1 8B on validating response generation.

Result: MEGUMI achieves superior performance on both M-EDESConv and M-TESC datasets in both objective metrics and subjective evaluations. GPT-4.1 Nano and Llama-3.1 8B generate contextually similar and diverse validating responses, but their emotional understanding remains insufficient, representing a major area for improvement.

Conclusion: Emotional validation is a promising direction for dialogue systems with therapeutic applications. While the proposed MEGUMI model effectively handles timing detection and the released multilingual corpora enable further research, current LLMs still fall short in genuine emotional understanding, highlighting a critical gap for future work.

Abstract: Emotional validation - explicitly acknowledging that a user's feelings make sense - has proven therapeutic value but has received little computational attention. Emotional validation in dialogue systems can be decomposed into (i) validating response identification, (ii) validation timing detection, and (iii) validating response generation. To support research on all three subtasks, we release M-EDESConv, a 120k English-Japanese multilingual corpus created through hybrid manual and automatic annotation, and M-TESC, a multilingual spoken-dialogue test set. For timing detection, we propose MEGUMI, a Multilingual Emotion-aware Gated Unit for Mutual Integration, that fuses frozen XLM-RoBERTa semantics with language-specific emotion encoders via cross-modal attention and gated fusion. MEGUMI shows superior performance on both the M-EDESConv and M-TESC datasets, both objectively and subjectively. Finally, our EmoValidBench benchmarks of GPT-4.1 Nano and Llama-3.1 8B indicate that current LLMs generate contextually similar and diverse validating responses, but emotional understanding remains a major area for improvement. Project page: https://github.com/zihaurpang/Multilingual-Emotional-Validation

</details>


### [59] [Notes2Skills: From Lab Notebooks to Certainty-Aware Scientific Agent Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11897)
*Shi Liu,Jiayao Chen,Chengwei Qin,Yanqing Hu,Jufan Zhang,Linyi Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Notes2Skills is a two-stage framework that extracts verifiable skills from lab notebooks while preserving author certainty, preventing AI agents from conflating uncertain observations with confirmed conclusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lab notes contain intermingled validated observations, tentative judgments, and experimental next steps. Prior AI work on scientific text ignores informal lab notebooks. Conflating these signals causes AI agents to mistake uncertain judgments for confirmed conclusions or executable actions.

Method: A two-stage framework (Notes2Skills) that parses lab notebooks into verifiable agent skills. The first stage extracts structured skill representations; the second stage applies certainty preservation to distinguish firm instructions from uncertain notes. Evaluated across seven conditions and three wet-lab sessions.

Result: Notes2Skills was the only configuration across all conditions and sessions that neither mistakenly treated uncertain notes as firm instructions nor discarded firm ones. Certainty preservation proved essential for reliable skill extraction.

Conclusion: Certainty preservation is the critical missing component between lab notebooks and reliable AI agent skills, enabling safer AI co-scientist systems that respect the nuanced, evolving nature of scientific reasoning in laboratory records.

Abstract: Scientific discovery workflows usually contain and rely heavily on lab notes, where researchers record observations, interpret uncertain results, and plan follow-up experiments. Such informative lab notes preserve evolving scientific reasoning and author uncertainty, rather than polished final results exhibited in publications, providing a valuable opportunity for AI to engage in scientific exploration at a more comprehensive and deeper level. However, most prior work on scientific text focuses on papers, protocols, or structured databases, leaving informal laboratory notes underexplored as inputs to AI agents for science. This gap matters because lab notes often intermingle validated observations, tentative judgments, and possible experimental next steps within the same passage. If these signals are conflated, an AI agent may mistake uncertain scientific judgments for confirmed conclusions or executable actions. To this end, we present Notes2Skills, a two-stage framework for turning lab notebooks into verifiable skills for scientific AI agents while preserving the author's certainty. Across seven conditions and three wet-lab sessions, Notes2Skills is the only configuration that neither mistakes uncertain notes for firm instructions nor discards firm ones. We show that certainty preservation is the missing piece between lab notebooks and reliable agent skills, opening a path toward safer AI co-scientist systems.

</details>


### [60] [GraspLLM: Towards Zero-Shot Generalization on Text-Attributed Graphs with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11898)
*Hengyi Feng,Zeang Sheng,Meiyi Qiang,Meiyi Qiang,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GraspLLM is a framework that enhances cross-dataset and cross-task generalization on Text-Attributed Graphs by combining motif-aware contrastive learning for structural pattern extraction with LLM-based semantic understanding, using optimal contextual subgraph extraction and alignment to the LLM token space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods integrating LLMs into TAGs struggle to generalize across diverse graphs and tasks, and their ability to capture transferable graph structural patterns remains limited. There is a need for a framework that can achieve strong zero-shot cross-dataset and cross-task performance.

Method: GraspLLM uses three key components: (1) a frozen general embedding model to unify node texts across graphs in a shared semantic space; (2) motif-aware contrastive learning over multiple motif-induced adjacency matrices to extract dataset-agnostic structural information; and (3) an optimal contextual subgraph extraction module combined with an alignment projector that maps these subgraphs into the LLM token space for downstream task processing.

Result: Extensive experiments on TAG benchmark datasets across diverse domains show that GraspLLM consistently outperforms previous LLM-based TAG methods, with particularly strong performance in zero-shot settings, demonstrating superior cross-dataset and cross-task generalizability.

Conclusion: GraspLLM effectively combines graph structural comprehension with LLM semantic capabilities, achieving state-of-the-art generalization on TAGs. The framework's strong zero-shot performance validates the effectiveness of motif-aware structural learning and optimal contextual subgraph alignment.

Abstract: Research on Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) has gained significant attention recently due to its broad applications across various real-world data scenarios, such as citation networks, e-commerce platforms, social media, and web pages. Inspired by the remarkable semantic understanding ability of Large Language Models (LLMs), there have been numerous attempts to integrate LLMs into TAGs. However, existing methods still struggle to generalize across diverse graphs and tasks, and their ability to capture transferable graph structural patterns remains limited. To address this, we introduce the GraspLLM, a framework that combines Graph structural comprehension with semantic understanding prowess of LLMs to enhance the cross-dataset and cross-task generalizability. Specifically, we represent node texts from different graphs in a unified semantic space with a frozen general embedding model, on top of which we perform motif-aware contrastive learning across multiple motif-induced adjacency matrices to extract dataset-agnostic structural information. Then, with our proposed optimal contextual subgraph, we extract the most contextually relevant subgraph for each target node and align these subgraphs to the token space of LLM via an alignment projector. Extensive experiments on TAG benchmark datasets spanning diverse domains reveal that GraspLLM consistently outperforms previous LLM-based methods for TAGs, especially in zero-shot scenarios, highlighting its strong generalizability across different datasets and tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/Heinz217/GraspLLM.

</details>


### [61] [When Does Language Matter? Multilingual Instructions Reveal Step-wise Language Sensitivity in Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11906)
*Xuan Dong,Zhe Han,Tianhao Niu,Qingfu Zhu,Wanxiang Che*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: First systematic multilingual evaluation of VLA models reveals severe performance drops (30-50%) under non-English instructions on LIBERO. Language sensitivity is step-wise non-uniform: certain steps dominate failure while others are language-agnostic. A step-wise inference-time intervention aligning representations by language sensitivity substantially recovers performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models excel at language-conditioned manipulation, but their robustness to linguistic variation (especially non-English languages) is poorly understood, limiting deployment in multilingual real-world settings.

Method: Translate LIBERO benchmark into ten languages; conduct fine-grained, step-level analysis of task execution trajectories to identify language-dependent vs. language-agnostic steps; propose a step-wise inference-time intervention that aligns representations according to per-step language sensitivity.

Result: Non-English instructions cause 30-50% success rate degradation. Language influence is highly non-uniform across steps: certain steps dominate failure, others are robust. The proposed step-wise intervention substantially improves performance under linguistic variation.

Conclusion: Language robustness in VLA models is fundamentally a step-wise control problem. Temporally structured, step-level analysis and intervention are critical for building reliable multilingual embodied agents.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong performance in language-conditioned robotic manipulation, yet their robustness to linguistic variation remains poorly understood. In this work, we present the first systematic multilingual evaluation of VLA models by translating the LIBERO benchmark into ten languages, revealing severe performance degradation under non-English instructions, with success rates dropping by 30-50%. Through fine-grained analysis of task executions, we find that language influence is highly non-uniform across steps: certain steps exhibit strong language dependence and dominate overall task failure, while others are largely language-agnostic. Based on this insight, we propose a step-wise inference-time intervention that aligns representations according to step language sensitivity, substantially improving performance under linguistic variation. Our results indicate that language robustness in VLA models is fundamentally a step-wise control problem, highlighting the importance of temporally structured analysis for reliable embodied agents.

</details>


### [62] [An Ontology-Guided Multi-Anchor Graph Retrieval Framework for Traffic Legal Liability Determination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11910)
*Xu Li,Shuqi Tian,Xun Han,Kuncheng Zhao,Xinyi Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: To address the multi-dimensional retrieval bottleneck in traffic law liability determination, the authors propose OMAGR, an ontology-guided framework that decomposes queries into ontology-aligned anchors for parallel graph retrieval across legal dimensions, and validate it on a newly created TrafficLaw-QA benchmark dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) methods for traffic law liability determination rely on single-axis architectures that compress complex, multi-dimensional legal queries into a single retrieval pathway. This causes interdependent statutory provisions across different legal dimensions to be systematically overlooked, creating a "multi-dimensional retrieval bottleneck" that undermines accurate liability assignment.

Method: The authors propose OMAGR (Ontology-guided Multi-Anchor Graph Retrieval), which: (1) decomposes complex legal queries into ontology-aligned anchors corresponding to distinct legal dimensions; (2) executes parallel graph retrieval independently along each dimension; and (3) fuses the independently retrieved results. They also created TrafficLaw-QA, an expert-validated benchmark with 200 questions and 527 statutory provisions.

Result: TrafficOmni-RAG (the instantiated system) outperformed baselines on Context Precision and Faithfulness metrics when evaluated on the TrafficLaw-QA dataset, demonstrating that parallel multi-anchor retrieval effectively resolves the multi-dimensional retrieval bottleneck.

Conclusion: Parallel multi-anchor retrieval guided by legal ontologies is an effective approach for overcoming the limitations of single-axis RAG architectures in multi-dimensional legal reasoning tasks. The framework offers a promising direction for advancing traffic law liability determination research and related legal AI applications.

Abstract: Traffic law liability determination is critical for assigning legal penalties, requiring the simultaneous identification of interdependent statutory provisions across multiple legal dimensions. However, existing retrieval-augmented generation methods suffer from a multi-dimensional retrieval bottleneck: single axis architectures compress complex legal queries into a single pathway, causing interdependent statutory dimensions to be overlooked. To address this, we propose OMAGR, an ontology-guided framework that decomposes queries into ontology-aligned anchors and executes parallel graph retrieval across each dimension, ensuring independent retrieval across dimensions before fusion. To evaluate the proposed method, we created the TrafficLaw-QA dataset, an expert-validated benchmark dataset containing 200 questions and 527 legal provisions. Results show that TrafficOmni-RAG outperforms baselines on Context Precision and Faithfulness metrics. The findings demonstrate that parallel multi-anchor retrieval effectively resolves the multi-dimensional retrieval bottleneck, offering a promising direction for traffic law liability determination research.

</details>


### [63] [Toward Generalist Autonomous Research via Hypothesis-Tree Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11926)
*Jiajie Jin,Yuyang Hu,Kai Qiu,Qi Dai,Chong Luo,Guanting Dong,Xiaoxi Li,Tong Zhao,Xiaolong Ma,Gongrui Zhang,Zhirong Wu,Bei Liu,Zhengyuan Yang,Linjie Li,Lijuan Wang,Hongjin Qian,Yutao Zhu,Zhicheng Dou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Arbor is a framework for autonomous AI research that uses a hierarchical tree structure (Hypothesis Tree Refinement) to enable cumulative, long-horizon scientific iteration. A coordinator manages global strategy while short-lived executors test hypotheses in isolation. Across six real research tasks, Arbor outperforms Codex and Claude Code by 2.5x average relative gain, and achieves 86.36% Any Medal on MLE-Bench Lite with GPT-5.5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous AI research agents treat experimentation as a sequence of independent, local attempts, failing to carry lessons, evidence, and strategic insights across time. This limits their ability to engage in the cumulative, iterative loop of exploration, experimentation, and abstraction that characterizes genuine scientific progress. The authors aim to bridge this gap by enabling an agent to conduct long-horizon autonomous research with persistent memory and strategic refinement.

Method: The authors introduce Arbor, composed of three key components: (1) a long-lived coordinator that manages global research strategy; (2) short-lived executors that implement and test individual hypotheses in isolated worktrees; and (3) Hypothesis Tree Refinement (HTR), a persistent tree structure that links hypotheses, artifacts, evidence, and distilled insights across time. Results from executors are fed back into the tree, propagating reusable lessons, refining the search frontier, and admitting verified improvements. The framework is evaluated under Autonomous Optimization (AO), where an agent iteratively improves an initial artifact without human step-level supervision.

Result: Arbor achieved the best held-out result on all six real-world research tasks spanning model training, harness engineering, and data synthesis. It attained more than 2.5x the average relative held-out gain compared to Codex and Claude Code under identical task interfaces and resource budgets. On MLE-Bench Lite, Arbor with GPT-5.5 reached 86.36% Any Medal, the strongest result reported in the comparison.

Conclusion: Arbor demonstrates that structuring autonomous research as a cumulative, tree-mediated process — rather than a sequence of independent attempts — yields substantial gains in long-horizon research performance. The persistent Hypothesis Tree Refinement mechanism enables effective strategy propagation and evidence accumulation across time, setting a new state-of-the-art for autonomous research agents.

Abstract: Scientific progress depends on a repeated loop of exploration, experimentation, and abstraction. Researchers test candidate directions, interpret the evidence, and carry the resulting lessons into later attempts. We study how an AI agent can run this loop autonomously over long horizons. We introduce Arbor, a general framework for autonomous research that combines a long-lived coordinator, short-lived executors, and Hypothesis Tree Refinement (HTR), a persistent tree that links hypotheses, artifacts, evidence, and distilled insights across time. The coordinator manages global research strategy over the tree, while executors implement and test individual hypotheses in isolated worktrees. As results return, Arbor updates the tree, propagates reusable lessons, refines the search frontier, and admits verified improvements. This design turns autonomous research from a sequence of local attempts into a cumulative process in which strategy, execution, and evidence are carried across time. We evaluate Arbor under Autonomous Optimization (AO), an operational setting where an agent improves an initial research artifact through iterative experimentation without step-level human supervision. Across six real research tasks in model training, harness engineering, and data synthesis, Arbor achieves the best held-out result on all six tasks, attaining more than 2.5x the average relative held-out gain of Codex and Claude Code under the same task interface and resource budget. On MLE-Bench Lite, Arbor reaches 86.36% Any Medal with GPT-5.5, the strongest result in our comparison.

</details>


### [64] [Semantic Grading of Written Answers in Low-Resource Language Bangla Using a Fine-Tuned Lightweight Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11931)
*Meherun Farzana,Aniket Joarder,Mahmudul Hasan,Md. Mosaddek Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A bilingual Bangla-English automatic answer grading system is developed for low-resource educational settings, using a QLoRA-tuned Qwen3-8B model that prioritizes semantic correctness over lexical overlap, achieving strong human agreement (rho=0.936) and leakage-resistant feedback (RoRa=0.819).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bangla is underserved in educational NLP. Remote regions lack qualified teachers, forcing manual grading that is slow and inconsistent. Automatic assessment is challenging because semantically equivalent answers can differ lexically, and existing systems struggle to evaluate meaning rather than surface form.

Method: The authors fine-tune a lightweight language model (Qwen3-8B) using QLoRA for efficient adaptation. The model takes a (question, reference answer, student answer) triplet as input and outputs both a numeric score and context-grounded feedback. A synthetic bilingual (Bangla-English) dataset is constructed for controlled training and evaluation. Performance is benchmarked against both proprietary and open-source LLMs under a unified protocol.

Result: The QLoRA-tuned Qwen3-8B achieves a RoRa score of 0.819 in synthetic evaluation (indicating leakage-resistant feedback) and demonstrates the strongest correlation with human evaluators (Spearman's rho = 0.936, MAE = 0.725) in a dedicated human study.

Conclusion: Semantic-over-lexical approaches using fine-tuned lightweight models are viable for low-resource educational NLP. The system provides reliable, scalable automatic grading with context-grounded feedback suitable for real classroom deployment in Bangla-speaking regions.

Abstract: Bangla is among the world's most widely spoken languages, yet it remains underserved in educational NLP research. In many remote and rural regions, access to qualified subject teachers is limited, and written answers are consequently graded largely by hand, restricting timely and consistent feedback. Automatic assessment is challenging because semantically correct responses can vary substantially in surface form. We present a bilingual (Bangla-English) evaluation system designed for low-resource educational settings that prioritizes semantic correctness over lexical overlap. Our approach fine-tunes a lightweight language model to grade each response using the question, reference answer, and student answer, producing a numeric score and concise, context-grounded feedback suitable for classroom deployment. We also construct a synthetic bilingual dataset to enable controlled training and evaluation. Across proprietary and open-source LLMs evaluated under a unified protocol, our QLoRA-tuned Qwen3-8B confirms consistent improvement by producing the most leakage-resistant feedback (RoRa = 0.819) in synthetic evaluation and the strongest agreement with human scores (rho = 0.936, MAE = 0.725) in a dedicated human study.

</details>


### [65] [Decoding Multimodal Cues: Unveiling the Implicit Meaning Behind Hateful Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11953)
*Junyu Lu,Deyi Ji,Liqun Liu,Xiaokun Zhang,Youlin Wu,Roy Ka-Wei Lee,Peng Shu,Huan Yu,Jie Jiang,Bo Xu,Liang Yang,Hongfei Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses explainable hateful video detection by introducing two annotated datasets (Ex-HateMM, Ex-ImpliHateVid) and proposing the IARE framework, which combines multimodal chain-of-thought for evidence enrichment with Direct Preference Optimization for reasoning alignment, achieving SOTA performance with accurate rationales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hateful video detection methods are limited to binary classification without providing contextual rationales, which undermines model explainability and the understanding of implicit meanings behind detection decisions.

Method: (1) Introduction of two datasets—Ex-HateMM and Ex-ImpliHateVid—with fine-grained multimodal harmful element annotations and contextual rationales. (2) Proposal of the IARE framework: an Information Augmentation phase using multimodal chain-of-thought to integrate harmful elements and enrich rationale evidence, and a Reasoning Enhancement phase using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to steer the model toward correct reasoning paths and away from incorrect ones.

Result: Extensive experiments on both datasets demonstrate that IARE achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to multiple baselines and generates accurate contextual rationales.

Conclusion: The IARE framework effectively enables explainable hateful video detection, advancing beyond binary classification by producing coherent, evidence-grounded rationales that enhance both content understanding and decision explainability.

Abstract: Hateful videos have become prevalent on online platforms, highlighting an urgent need for effective detection. However, existing studies primarily focus on binary classification and fail to provide contextual rationales that reveal the implicit meanings behind these judgments, significantly undermining model explainability. To fill this gap, we aim to achieve explainable hateful video detection, enabling models to provide contextual rationales that integrate relevant evidence and logical reasoning alongside decisions. This approach can comprehensively enhance the understanding of video content and the explainability of the decision-making process. We first introduce two datasets, Ex-HateMM and Ex-ImpliHateVid, for explainable hateful video detection. Each dataset provides fine-grained annotations of multimodal harmful elements, along with contextual rationales. We then propose an Information Augmentation and Reasoning Enhancement (IARE) framework designed for explainable detection. The framework employs an information augmentation phase that leverages the multimodal chain-of-thought to integrate harmful elements, thereby enriching rationale evidence. Additionally, IARE incorporates a reasoning enhancement phase, in which Direct Preference Optimization guides the model toward correct reasoning paths and away from incorrect ones, thereby improving the logical coherence of its justifications. We conduct extensive experiments on the two datasets, comparing multiple baselines with our proposed IARE framework. The results demonstrate that IARE achieves state-of-the-art performance while also generating accurate rationales.

</details>


### [66] [Agreement in Representation Space for Open-Ended Self-Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12003)
*Paula Ontalvilla,Gorka Azkune,Aitor Ormazabal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Embedding-Based Agreement (EBA), a training-free method that frames self-consistency as a geometric property of the representation space rather than exact matching. By clustering sampled generations in embedding space, EBA provides a robust consistency signal for open-ended tasks like code generation and summarization, consistently outperforming random selection and showing that centrally located generations are more reliable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional self-consistency relies on exact matching, which limits its applicability to categorical-output tasks. The authors aim to extend self-consistency to open-ended generation tasks (code synthesis, summarization) where outputs are syntactically diverse but semantically equivalent, requiring a similarity-based rather than identity-based notion of agreement.

Method: The authors propose Embedding-Based Agreement (EBA), a training-free approach that: (1) samples multiple outputs from an LLM, (2) embeds them in a representation space, and (3) clusters the embeddings to estimate agreement. The most consistent answer is selected from the largest or most central cluster. Experiments span mathematical reasoning, code generation, and summarization across multiple model families and embedding spaces (including native hidden representations).

Result: EBA consistently outperforms random selection and exhibits more stable scaling behavior than LLM-based evaluation or uncertainty estimation approaches. The agreement signals remain stable across different model families and embedding spaces. A key finding is that generations near central regions of representation space are strongly correlated with higher quality, while peripheral generations are substantially less accurate.

Conclusion: Self-consistency should be understood as a property of the geometric organization of sampled generations in representation space, not as exact symbolic overlap. This geometric perspective enables principled extension of self-consistency to open-ended generation tasks.

Abstract: Self-consistency improves LLM reasoning by sampling multiple outputs and selecting the most consistent answer, but existing formulations largely rely on exact matching and therefore remain limited to tasks with categorical outputs. In this work, we study self-consistency in open-ended generation tasks such as code synthesis and text summarization. We hypothesize that consistency can be understood as a geometric property of the generation space, where semantically compatible generations concentrate in similar regions of representation space. To study this hypothesis, we introduce Embedding-Based Agreement (EBA), a simple training-free operationalization that estimates agreement by clustering sampled generations in embedding space. Through experiments on mathematical reasoning, code generation, and summarization, we show that agreement in representation space provides a robust and scalable signal of self-consistency for open-ended tasks. In particular, EBA consistently outperforms random selection and exhibits more stable scaling behavior than recent selection approaches based on LLM evaluation or uncertainty estimation. We further show that these agreement signals remain stable across model families and embedding spaces, even with native hidden representations. Finally, our analysis shows that the geometric location occupied by sampled generations is strongly correlated with generation quality: generations concentrated near central regions of representation space tend to correspond to more reliable outputs, whereas peripheral generations are substantially less accurate. Overall, our findings support viewing self-consistency as a property of the geometric organization of sampled generations rather than exact symbolic overlap.

</details>


### [67] [StanceNakba Shared Task: Actor and Topic-Aware Stance Detection in Public Discourse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12068)
*Kholoud K. Aldous,Md Rafiul Biswas,Mabrouka Bessghaier,Shimaa Ibrahim,Kais Attia,Wajdi Zaghouani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Presents StanceNakba 2026, a shared task on stance detection in Palestinian-Israeli conflict discourse with two subtasks: English actor-level stance detection (Pro-Palestine/Pro-Israel/Neutral) and Arabic cross-topic stance detection. Transformer-based models achieved strong results (Macro F1 up to 0.9620), though cross-topic generalization and neutral class prediction remain challenging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
