Motivation: To advance stance detection research in the highly polarized domain of the Palestinian-Israeli conflict, addressing the need for nuanced, multilingual (English and Arabic) stance classification across different granularities — actor-level and cross-topic — while providing a standardized benchmark for the NLP community.

Method: The shared task provides an annotated dataset of 2,606 social media posts across two subtasks: Subtask A (actor-level stance detection in English: Pro-Palestine, Pro-Israel, Neutral) and Subtask B (cross-topic stance detection in Arabic: Favor, Against, Neither across two topics — normalization with Israel and refugee presence in Jordan). Participating teams (7 in Subtask A, 6 in Subtask B) primarily employed fine-tuned transformer-based models (MARBERT, AraBERT, DeBERTa-v3 variants) with cross-validation, ensemble methods, and topic-conditioned architectures.

Result: Best Macro F1 scores: 0.9620 on Subtask A and 0.8724 on Subtask B. Transformer-based approaches proved highly effective for conflict-domain stance detection, with a notable performance gap between actor-level English classification and cross-topic Arabic classification.

Conclusion: Transformer-based models are highly effective for stance detection in conflict discourse. However, persistent challenges remain in cross-topic generalization and neutral class prediction, particularly in the more complex Arabic cross-topic setting, indicating areas for future improvement.

Abstract: We present StanceNakba 2026, a shared task on stance detection in polarized social media discourse related to the Palestinian-Israeli conflict, organized as part of Nakba-NLP 2026 at LREC-COLING 2026. The task introduces two subtasks: Subtask A (Actor-Level Stance Detection), which classifies English social media posts as Pro-Palestine, Pro-Israel, or Neutral; and Subtask B (Cross-Topic Stance Detection), which identifies Favor, Against, or Neither stances in Arabic posts toward two conflict-related topics, normalization with Israel and refugee presence in Jordan. The task is grounded in an annotated dataset of 2,606 social media posts. A total of 7 teams participated in Subtask A and 6 teams in Subtask B. Participating systems primarily fine-tuned Arabic and multilingual transformer-based models, including MARBERT, AraBERT, and DeBERTa-v3 variants, with several teams employing cross-validation, ensemble methods, and topic-conditioned architectures. The best-performing systems achieved a Macro F1 of 0.9620 on Subtask A and 0.8724 on Subtask B, demonstrating that transformer-based approaches are highly effective for conflict-domain stance detection while highlighting persistent challenges in cross-topic generalization and neutral class prediction.

</details>


### [68] [FORT-Searcher: Synthesizing Shortcut-Resistant Search Tasks for Training Deep Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12087)
*Jia Deng,Yimeng Chen,Xiaoqing Xiang,Ziyang Zeng,Shuo Tang,Wayne Xin Zhao,Feng Chang,Chuan Hao,Yuan Wei,Ran Tao,Bryan Dai,Ji-Rong Wen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies that existing deep search training data synthesis methods focus on structural graph complexity but fail to prevent shortcut solutions, where agents find answers without performing genuine deep search. The authors formalize a shortcut-aware difficulty framework, introduce FORT for generating shortcut-resistant training data, and demonstrate that models trained on FORT data outperform comparable open-source search agents on deep search benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing synthesis methods for training deep search agents increase apparent difficulty through graph enrichment, but structural complexity does not guarantee realized search difficulty — agents can collapse the intended search process via cheaper identifying routes (shortcuts). There is no formal framework to analyze and mitigate these shortcut risks in search agent training data.

Method: The authors (1) formalize a shortcut-aware difficulty framework identifying four shortcut risks: evidence co-coverage, single-clue selectivity, exposed constants, and prior-knowledge binding; (2) diagnose realized effects using trajectory signatures (solving cost, answer hit time, prior-shortcut rate); (3) introduce FORT, a synthesis framework that controls shortcut risks across four stages: entity selection, evidence graph construction, question formulation, and adversarial refinement; (4) train FORT-Searcher via SFT on resulting trajectories.

Result: FORT-generated data induces longer pre-answer search trajectories and fewer shortcut patterns compared to existing open-source deep search datasets. FORT-Searcher (SFT-only) achieves the best overall performance among comparable-size open-source search agents on challenging deep search benchmarks.

Conclusion: Structural complexity alone is insufficient for training deep search agents; controlling shortcut risks is essential. The FORT framework effectively generates shortcut-resistant training data, and models trained on such data demonstrate superior deep search capabilities without needing reinforcement learning or other post-training techniques.

Abstract: Training deep search agents requires verifiable questions whose answers remain unavailable until sufficient evidence has been acquired through search. Existing synthesis methods often increase apparent difficulty by enriching graph structures, but structural complexity alone does not guarantee realized search difficulty: the intended search process can collapse through a cheaper identifying route. We formalize this gap with a shortcut-aware difficulty framework and identify four actionable shortcut risks: evidence co-coverage, single-clue selectivity, exposed constants, and prior-knowledge binding. To diagnose their realized effects, we use trajectory signatures including solving cost, answer hit time, and prior-shortcut rate. Guided by this framework, we introduce FORT, a Framework of Shortcut-Resistant Training-Data Synthesis. FORT constructs shortcut-resistant training data by controlling shortcut risks across entity selection, evidence graph construction, question formulation, and adversarial refinement. Experiments show that FORT induces longer pre-answer search and fewer shortcut patterns than existing open-source deep search datasets. Using the resulting trajectories, we train FORT-Searcher with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) only, and it achieves the best overall performance among comparable-size open-source search agents on challenging deep search benchmarks. Relevant resources will be made available at https://github.com/RUCAIBox/FORT-Searcher.

</details>


### [69] [Debiasing Without Protected Attributes: Latent Concept Erasure from Textual Profiles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12088)
*Shun Shao,Zheng Zhao,Anna Korhonen,Yftah Ziser,Shay B. Cohen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes H-SAL, a post-hoc debiasing method that uses self-description text as an implicit signal instead of explicit protected attributes (e.g., gender, race). It introduces a Stack Exchange-based fairness benchmark and finds that implicit self-description often matches or outperforms explicit-label debiasing across encoder and decoder-only LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real-world NLP deployments, protected attributes like gender or race are often unavailable due to privacy, legal, or missing-data constraints. Yet models can still infer these attributes from textual cues. The core question is whether effective debiasing can be achieved without direct access to sensitive attribute labels.

Method: The authors propose H-SAL, a post-hoc concept and attribute erasure technique that leverages self-description text (e.g., "I am a woman") as an implicit debiasing signal rather than explicit protected attribute labels. They also construct a multi-domain fairness benchmark using Stack Exchange data for helpfulness prediction, incorporating both explicit and implicit signals to compare standard debiasing (with protected labels) against debiasing without sensitive attribute access.

Result: Across both encoder-only and decoder-only language models, implicit self-description-based debiasing (H-SAL) often matches or surpasses explicit-label-based debiasing in reducing bias while maintaining task performance.

Conclusion: Debiasing can be effective without direct access to protected attributes when self-description text serves as an implicit signal. This broadens the scope of representation-level fairness research and provides a realistic benchmark for studying debiasing under practical data constraints.

Abstract: Most fairness research in NLP assumes direct access to protected attributes such as gender, race, or nationality. In practice, however, such information is often unavailable due to privacy constraints, missing metadata, or legal restrictions, even though models may infer it from indirect textual cues. This raises a key question: can debiasing succeed without direct access to sensitive attributes? We propose H-SAL, which performs post-hoc concept and attribute erasure using self-description text as an implicit debiasing signal. To support this setting, we introduce a multi-domain Stack Exchange-based fairness benchmark for helpfulness prediction that includes both explicit and implicit signals, enabling comparison between standard debiasing with protected labels and debiasing without access to sensitive information. Across encoder and decoder-only language models, we find that implicit self-description often matches or outperforms explicit-label-based debiasing. Our results broaden representation-level fairness research and provide a new benchmark for studying debiasing under realistic data constraints.

</details>


### [70] [Augmenting Molecular Language Models with Local $n$-gram Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12113)
*Xinni Zhang,Zijing Liu,He Cao,Yu Li,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MolGram introduces a conditional n-gram memory module that injects local chemical pattern embeddings into Transformer-based molecular language models via hash lookups, overcoming the SMILES tokenization locality gap. It consistently outperforms baselines — including those with 3× more parameters — across molecule generation, forward reaction prediction, and retrosynthesis tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard character-level tokenization of SMILES strings fragments chemically meaningful motifs (e.g., functional groups, rings), creating a "locality gap." Transformers must then waste capacity re-learning these local syntactic patterns across positions, undermining their ability to model long-range molecular dependencies critical for chemical tasks.

Method: MolGram integrates a conditional n-gram memory module into molecular Transformer LMs. Local SMILES string patterns are mapped to learned embeddings via scalable hash lookups. These regional context embeddings are then dynamically injected into the model's hidden states, providing an explicit inductive bias for local chemical syntax without modifying the standard tokenizer.

Result: MolGram achieves consistent performance improvements across three tasks: unconditional molecule generation, forward reaction prediction, and single-step retrosynthesis. Notably, it outperforms baseline models with 3× more parameters, demonstrating the parameter efficiency of the explicit local pattern memory approach.

Conclusion: Explicit local pattern memory, implemented via a conditional n-gram module, serves as a highly efficient inductive bias for molecular language models. MolGram closes the SMILES locality gap without tokenizer modifications and achieves strong results with substantially fewer parameters, validating the approach.

Abstract: Transformer-based language models for SMILES strings suffer from a locality gap: standard character-level tokenization fragments chemically meaningful motifs, forcing models to repeatedly learn local syntax at the expense of long-range dependencies. To address this without disrupting standard tokenizers, we propose MolGram, which integrates a conditional $n$-gram memory module into molecular language models. MolGram maps local string patterns to learned embeddings via scalable hash lookups and dynamically injects this regional context into hidden states. Evaluations across three tasks, including unconditional molecule generation, forward reaction prediction, and single-step retrosynthesis, show that MolGram consistently improves performance. Crucially, our analyses demonstrate that MolGram outperforms baselines with 3$\times$ more parameters, establishing explicit local pattern memory as a highly efficient inductive bias.

</details>


### [71] [Detecting Sensitive Personal Information in Japanese Pre-Training Corpora for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12114)
*Rei Minamoto,Yusuke Oda,Daisuke Kawahara*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents the first study on detecting special care-required personal information (SCPI) — sensitive data defined under Japan's Act on the Protection of Personal Information — in Japanese text corpora. The authors construct an SCPI dataset using LLM-based annotation and train classifiers to automatically detect such sensitive content, demonstrating effective identification while highlighting the challenges unique to Japanese.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale pre-training corpora for LLMs often contain sensitive personal information, necessitating detection and filtering to comply with privacy regulations and prevent data leakage. While research in English and other languages exists, work on sensitive personal information detection in Japanese is notably limited. Japan's APPI defines specific categories of "special care-required personal information," but no prior methods exist for automatically detecting SCPI in Japanese text corpora.

Method: The authors (1) construct a dataset of Japanese text annotated for SCPI categories using LLM-based annotation, and (2) train machine learning models as classifiers to rapidly detect SCPI in text. The annotation is guided by the legal definitions of SCPI under Japan's APPI.

Result: The resulting SCPI classifier demonstrates effective identification of information related to SCPI categories defined under Japanese privacy law. The study also surfaces challenges inherent to accurate SCPI detection in Japanese text.

Conclusion: This is the first study to address SCPI detection specifically for Japanese text corpora. The work underscores both the feasibility of automated SCPI filtering and the ongoing challenges that must be addressed for reliable, regulation-compliant detection in Japanese NLP pipelines.

Abstract: Sensitive personal information can appear in large-scale pre-training corpora for large language models (LLMs). Detecting and filtering such information is therefore essential to ensure compliance with privacy regulations and prevent unintended information leakage. However, in contrast to English and other languages, research into sensitive personal information has been limited in the Japanese language. In this study, we focus on sensitive personal data defined as special care-required personal information (SCPI) under Japan's Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI). We construct an SCPI dataset using LLM-based annotation and train machine learning models to rapidly detect SCPI in text. As a result, our SCPI classifier can effectively identify information related to SCPI. This study is the first to explore SCPI detection in Japanese text corpora, highlighting the challenges of accurate detection.

</details>


### [72] [Soft-Prompt Tuning for Fair and Efficient LLM Benchmark Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12117)
*Selen Erkan,Bastian Boll,Kristian Kersting,Björn Deiseroth,Letitia Parcalabescu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Soft-prompt tuning with only 10 vectors (~0.0006% of parameters) efficiently adapts base LLMs to benchmark formats in ~80 steps, revealing underlying knowledge that standard prompting misses and enabling fairer, architecture-agnostic model comparison without full post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard benchmark evaluation unfairly penalizes base LLMs that possess correct knowledge but lack the format-following ability typically acquired during post-training. Existing prompting methods (zero-shot, few-shot) confound knowledge accuracy with format compliance, making it difficult to fairly compare base models trained with different recipes or to predict downstream post-trained model quality.

Method: The authors propose soft-prompt tuning for evaluation: optimizing only 10 soft-prompt vectors (~0.0006% of a 7B model's parameters) on a small number of samples (~640) to adapt models to specific benchmark output formats. They also introduce metrics that disentangle format-following accuracy from knowledge accuracy, enabling separate quantification of each capability. The approach is architecture-agnostic and requires no full post-training.

Result: Evaluated across 7 models and 7 datasets, soft-prompt tuning saturates format-following within 80 steps (~640 samples). It significantly outperforms zero- and few-shot prompting at surfacing base model knowledge. Even post-trained models benefit from soft-prompting for format compliance. Critically, soft-prompted base model performance predicts post-trained model rankings more reliably than zero- and few-shot baselines, providing a low-cost proxy for downstream quality.

Conclusion: Soft-prompt tuning is an efficient, fair, and cost-effective evaluation protocol that decouples format-following from knowledge assessment, enabling more accurate benchmarking of base LLMs and reliable early-stage identification of optimal pre-training strategies without expensive full post-training.

Abstract: Benchmark scores often misrepresent a large language model's (LLM's) knowledge, because they rely, e.g., on the model's ability to follow specific formatting requirements. This especially penalizes base models that may know the correct answers but lack the ability -- typically introduced in post-training -- to structure them as instructed. To overcome this, we propose soft-prompt tuning, an efficient, fair, and architecture-agnostic model evaluation. By optimizing only 10 soft-prompt vectors (roughly 0.0006% parameters for a 7B model) over a short tuning period, we adapt models to specific benchmark formats, closing gaps in format-following and ensuring that underlying knowledge is accurately reflected in benchmark scores. This allows one to fairly compare different base models -- trained with various pre-training recipes -- on benchmarks without the need for full post-training. We evaluated soft-prompt tuning across 7 models and 7 datasets. The results show that (a) soft-prompt tuning saturates format-following within 80 steps (~640 samples) making it highly efficient, (b) soft-prompt tuning significantly outperforms zero- and few-shot prompting, surfacing base model knowledge that standard prompting misses, that (c) even post-trained models can benefit from soft-prompts to maximize format compliance, and that (d) soft-prompted base model performance predicts post-trained model rankings more reliably than zero- and few-shot baselines, offering a low-cost proxy for downstream model quality. Our contributions include (1) metrics which disentangle format-following and knowledge accuracy, (2) a fairer benchmarking protocol of LLM knowledge, and (3) a cost- and memory-effective recipe to identify optimal pre-training strategies early in LLM development.

</details>


### [73] [A Controlled Study of Decoding-Time Truthfulness Methods on Instruction-Tuned LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12160)
*Ao Sun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CHAIR is a supervised hallucination detection framework that extracts compact statistical features (max, min, mean, std, slope) from token logits across all layers of an LLM, achieving strong zero-shot detection accuracy on TruthfulQA and MMLU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucination remains a critical challenge in LLMs, and existing detection methods often rely on external knowledge or complex architectures. The authors aim to leverage the rich internal representations already present in LLMs—specifically per-layer token logits—to build a simple, generalizable, and overfitting-resistant hallucination detector.

Method: CHAIR extracts a compact feature set (maximum, minimum, mean, standard deviation, and slope) from the logit values of each token across all transformer layers. These features are then fed into a supervised classifier trained to distinguish hallucinated from factual outputs. The approach avoids raw logit memorization by summarizing distributional properties per token.

Result: Experiments on TruthfulQA and MMLU show that CHAIR significantly improves hallucination detection accuracy, with particularly strong performance in zero-shot settings, demonstrating robustness and cross-dataset generalizability.

Conclusion: CHAIR provides a practical, lightweight solution for hallucination detection and suggests that internal logit representations hold untapped potential for designing better decoding strategies to reduce hallucinations and improve factual coherence in LLMs.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce CHAIR (Classifier of Hallucination As ImproveR), a supervised framework for detecting hallucinations by analyzing internal logits from each layer of every token. Our method extracts a compact set of features such as maximum, minimum, mean, standard deviation, and slope-from the token logits across all layers, enabling effective hallucination detection without overfitting. Experiments on TruthfulQA and MMLU datasets demonstrate that CHAIR significantly improves detection accuracy, particularly in zero-shot scenarios, showcasing its robustness and generalizability. Beyond hallucination detection, CHAIR highlights the potential of using internal representations for designing advanced decoding strategies. By leveraging patterns in logits, we suggest that more sophisticated models and adaptive decoding methods could further reduce hallucinations and enhance text completion quality. CHAIR not only offers a practical solution for detecting hallucinations but also lays the groundwork for exploring richer representations in LLMs to improve their factuality and coherence.

</details>


### [74] [A Resource for Enthymeme Detection in Controversial Political Discourse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12186)
*Martial Pastor,Nelleke Oostdijk*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A dataset of 1,482 political tweets annotated by five annotators for enthymemes and argument structure, designed to study label variation. The paper proposes annotation guidelines based on Walton's argumentation schemes, demonstrates that models trained on annotator disagreement outperform those trained on majority-vote labels, and advocates embracing structural openness in enthymeme annotation rather than eliminating disagreement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enthymeme annotation is notoriously subjective, yet past resources tend to eliminate annotator disagreement rather than study it. The authors argue this obscures the sources of variation and prevents investigation into whether disagreement can benefit model performance. They aim to create a resource that explicitly captures and analyzes label variation in enthymeme annotation.

Method: The authors annotated 1,482 tweets from politically controversial discourse using five annotators. Annotation guidelines were grounded in Walton's argumentation schemes, providing structured constraints while preserving interpretive flexibility. They conducted a complexity analysis of the annotation task to identify sources of high cognitive load and inconsistent annotation. Preliminary modeling experiments compared training on annotator disagreement versus hard majority-vote labels.

Result: Models trained on annotator disagreement signals outperformed those trained on hard majority-vote labels. The complexity analysis revealed specific annotation steps imposing high cognitive load, which correlate with sources of annotator inconsistency.

Conclusion: Structural openness in enthymeme definitions and annotation guidelines enables productive study of variation in subjective inferential processes. Future resources and NLP applications concerned with human inference should embrace rather than eliminate annotator disagreement.

Abstract: Enthymemes, arguments with unstated premises or conclusions, are pervasive in persuasive discourse, yet their annotation remains notoriously subjective. We present a resource of 1,482 tweets from politically controversial discourse, annotated by five annotators for the presence of enthymemes and their argument structure, designed to study label variation. We first revisit the definition of enthymemes and propose annotation guidelines anchored in Walton's argumentation schemes, offering a structured and constrained approach that nonetheless preserves room for the interpretive nature of the task. This contrasts with past resources, which tend to eliminate disagreement, obscuring its sources and preventing investigation of its potential benefits for model performance. We further propose a complexity analysis of the task, identifying where annotation imposes high cognitive load and may give rise to inconsistent annotation. Our preliminary experiments show that models trained on annotator disagreement outperform models trained on hard majority-vote labels. We close by reflecting on how structural openness in enthymeme definitions and guidelines enables the study of variation in subjective inferential processes for future resources and downstream NLP applications concerned with human inference.

</details>


### [75] [Agentic Environment Engineering for Large Language Models: A Survey of Environment Modeling, Synthesis, Evaluation, and Application](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12191)
*Jiachun Li,Zhuoran Jin,Tianyi Men,Yupu Hao,Kejian Zhu,Lingshuai Wang,Dongqi Huang,Longxiang Wang,Shengjia Hua,Lu Wang,Jinshan Gao,Hongbang Yuan,Ruilin Xu,Kang Liu,Jun Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a systematic survey of agentic environments for LLM-based agents, organizing the field through the lens of an "environment engineering lifecycle" covering environment modeling, synthesis, evaluation, and application. It introduces a taxonomy of eight attributes and eight domains, two paradigms for automated environment synthesis (symbolic and neural), and characterizes agent-environment co-evolution through four agent evolution pathways and three environment evolution paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the critical role that environments play as interactive systems for LLM-based agents — driving continual model capability evolution across diverse scenarios — the field lacks a systematic categorization and deep analysis of existing work on agentic environments.

Method: The authors conduct a comprehensive systematic literature survey. They organize the analysis around the environment engineering lifecycle: (1) modeling — categorizing environments across eight attributes and eight domains with development path analysis; (2) synthesis — introducing symbolic and neural synthesis paradigms with corresponding evaluation methods; (3) application — analyzing agent-environment co-evolution through four agent evolution perspectives (memory-centric experience, orchestration-centric workflow, trajectory-centric offline, exploration-centric online) and three environment evolution paradigms (neural-driven, difficulty-driven, scaling-driven).

Result: The survey provides: (1) a structured taxonomy of agentic environments spanning eight attributes and eight domains; (2) identification of two dominant automated environment synthesis paradigms (symbolic and neural); (3) a characterization of agent-environment co-evolution covering four complementary agent evolution pathways and three distinct environment evolution paradigms; (4) identification of future directions including Environment-as-a-Service, Multi-agent Environments, and Neural-Symbolic Environments.

Conclusion: Agentic environments are fundamental infrastructure for LLM-based agent development, and systematically engineering them through the proposed lifecycle framework can accelerate agent capability advancement. The authors highlight that future progress lies in service-oriented environment delivery, multi-agent settings, and hybrid neural-symbolic approaches.

Abstract: Environments serve as interactive systems for large language model (LLM) based agents across diverse scenarios and play a crucial role in driving the continual evolution of model capabilities. Despite this importance, existing work lacks a systematic categorization and deep analysis. This paper systematically studies current researches on agentic environments from the perspective of the environment engineering lifecycle, covering their modeling, synthesis, evaluation and application. Specifically, the paper first introduces representative environments from the perspectives of eight attributes and eight domains, providing detailed analyses of their development paths and highlighting their core capabilities. Second, for automated environment synthesis, two paradigms are introduced, such as symbolic synthesis and neural synthesis. This paper also shows different environment evaluation methods in each paradigm. Thirdly, the corresponding environment applications from the perspective of agent-environment co-evolution are discussed. In specific, the paper characterizes the primary pathways for agent evolution in dynamic environments from four complementary perspectives: memory-centric experience evolution, orchestration-centric workflow evolution, trajectory-centric offline evolution, and exploration-centric online evolution. And three paradigms of environment evolution are identified, namely neural-driven, difficulty-driven, and scaling-driven approaches. At last, several promising future directions are discussed, including Environment-as-a-Service, Multi-agent Environments, and Neural-Symbolic Environments.

</details>


### [76] [Adaptive Multi-Resolution Procedural Knowledge Compression for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12203)
*Changyue Wang,Weihang Su,Qingyao Ai,Yichen Tang,Runzhong Qiao,Xuancheng Li,Min Zhang,Yiqun Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SKIM is an adaptive multi-resolution soft token compression framework that compresses procedural skill prompts for LLMs to 30–60% of their original length while maintaining task performance, addressing the limitations of existing compression methods designed for factual rather than procedural knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reusable natural language skills (procedural knowledge) are repeatedly invoked in LLM workflows, but including their full text in every context incurs high prefill cost and latency. Existing text compression methods target factual knowledge in documents and fail to preserve logical dependencies among workflows and tool protocols, making them insufficient for skill compression. There is a need for a method that (1) preserves logical dependencies, (2) enables lightweight offline compression for frequently updated skills, and (3) adapts to varying skill complexities.

Method: SKIM is an adaptive multi-resolution soft token compression framework. It creates a variable number of soft tokens per skill based on the skill's complexity, enabling more tokens for complex skills and fewer for simpler ones. These soft tokens are learned offline and injected in place of the full skill text during inference, reducing prefill cost while preserving procedural fidelity.

Result: Experiments show SKIM compresses skills to 30–60% of their original token length and outperforms existing compression methods in preserving task performance.

Conclusion: SKIM demonstrates that adaptive, multi-resolution soft token compression tailored to procedural knowledge can significantly reduce LLM inference costs for skill-based workflows without sacrificing effectiveness. The authors have released their code to facilitate further research.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are widely used to tackle complex tasks with autonomous workflows. Recently, reusable natural language skills have emerged as a popular paradigm to inject procedural knowledge into LLM applications. Since popular skills are often invoked repeatedly, placing their full text in every context significantly increases prefill cost and latency. While text compression techniques have the potential to solve this problem, most existing methods are designed to compress factual knowledge in documents instead of procedural knowledge, making them insufficient for skill compression. In this paper, we argue that an effective skill compression method should: 1) preserve logical dependencies among workflows and tool protocols, 2) enable lightweight, offline compression for frequently updated community skills, and 3) be adaptable to varying complexities across skills. To address this, we present SKIM (SKIll coMpression), an adaptive multi-resolution soft token compression framework for procedural skills. Depending on the complexity of each skill, SKIM creates different numbers of soft tokens that not only improve the efficiency of LLM inference, but also preserve the effectiveness of skill usage. Experiments indicate that SKIM compresses skills to 30 to 60 percent of their original token length while preserving task performance better than existing compression methods.We have released our code at https://github.com/bebr2/SKIM .

</details>


### [77] [Can News Predict the Market? Limits of Zero-Shot Financial NLP and the Role of Explainable AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12210)
*Ali M Karaoglu,Shreyank N Gowda*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether zero-shot NLP models can predict short-term stock movements from financial news and finds they consistently fail to outperform simple baselines, particularly for negative movements. However, the authors' explainability framework can reliably distinguish trustworthy from unreliable predictions, suggesting value in transparency-focused decision-support systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advances in LLMs, it remains unresolved whether financial news can reliably predict short-term stock movements. The authors aim to test whether zero-shot models can extract actionable trading signals without domain-specific training, while also addressing the critical need for transparency in high-stakes financial applications.

Method: A structured zero-shot pipeline combining (1) zero-shot Natural Language Inference (NLI) to classify news sentiment without fine-tuning, (2) temporal aggregation that explicitly models recency and event-dependent impact horizons, and (3) a multi-layered explainability framework producing token-level, article-level, and aggregate evidence with natural language rationales.

Result: Zero-shot approaches consistently fail to outperform simple baselines across multiple models and prediction horizons, with notably weak performance on negative movements. However, the explainability signals reliably distinguish between trustworthy and unreliable predictions, offering practical utility despite limited predictive accuracy.

Conclusion: The results highlight fundamental limitations of zero-shot financial NLP for short-term price prediction, suggesting deeper structural barriers in mapping news sentiment to price dynamics. The authors advocate shifting toward decision-support systems that prioritize transparency and uncertainty awareness over raw predictive accuracy.

Abstract: Can financial news reliably predict short-term stock movements? Despite advances in large language models, this question remains unresolved. We revisit this problem using a zero-shot natural language processing framework, investigating whether models can extract actionable signals from financial news without domain-specific training. We design a structured pipeline that combines zero-shot natural language inference with temporal aggregation, explicitly modelling recency and event-dependent impact horizons when integrating information across articles. To address the need for transparency in high-stakes settings, we introduce a multi-layered explainability framework that links predictions to token-level, article-level, and aggregate evidence, and produces grounded natural language rationales. Across multiple models and prediction horizons, we find that zero-shot approaches consistently fail to outperform simple baselines, with particularly weak performance on negative movements, suggesting deeper structural limitations in mapping news sentiment to short-term price dynamics. However, explainability signals reliably distinguish between trustworthy and unreliable predictions, offering practical value even when accuracy is limited. These findings highlight the limits of zero-shot financial NLP and motivate a shift toward decision-support systems that prioritise transparency and uncertainty awareness. Code: https://github.com/alimert05/zero-shot-stock-xai

</details>


### [78] [On The Effectiveness-Fluency Trade-Off In LLM Conditioning: A Systematic Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12234)
*Iuri Macocco,Pau Rodríguez,Arno Blaas,Luca Zappella,Marco Baroni,Xavier Suau*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper systematically evaluates LLM conditioning methods across injection and removal scenarios, revealing a critical fluency-steerability trade-off in activation steering, a previously overlooked interaction with instruction tuning, and that cheap textual metrics correlate well with LLM-as-judge scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of LLM conditioning methods narrowly focuses on effectiveness at injecting/removing target concepts while neglecting generation quality. A comprehensive understanding of the trade-offs involved across different methods remains missing.

Method: The authors systematically benchmark a range of conditioning methods (activation steering, prompting, supervised fine-tuning) across both concept injection and removal scenarios. They evaluate using both automated textual metrics and LLM-as-judge scores, analyzing fluency, steerability, and their interaction with model training paradigms (base vs. instruction-tuned).

Result: Key findings: (1) Efficient steering methods achieve conditioning at a steep cost to fluency. (2) Activation steering is far less effective on instruction-tuned models than base models—a previously overlooked interaction. (3) Prompting and SFT are viable for concept injection but underperform on concept removal. (4) Cheap textual metrics strongly correlate with costly LLM-as-judge scores.

Conclusion: There exists a fundamental trade-off between conditioning effectiveness and generation quality. Method selection should depend on the specific goal (injection vs. removal) and model type. Simple textual metrics can serve as reliable, cost-effective evaluation proxies for conditioning methods.

Abstract: Controlling the output of Large Language Models (LLMs) is a central challenge for their reliable deployment, yet a clear understanding of the involved trade-offs remains elusive. Current approaches to conditioning are often evaluated with a narrow focus on their effectiveness at injecting or removing a target concept, neglecting generation quality. We systematically investigate a range of conditioning methods in both injection and removal scenarios. We find that efficient steering methods frequently achieve conditioning at a steep cost to fluency. Furthermore, we identify a critical yet previously overlooked interaction with the training paradigm: activation steering methods are far less effective on instruction-tuned models than on their base counterparts. Simple prompting and full-fledged supervised fine-tuning, on the other hand, are viable options for concept injection, but are not as good at concept removal. Finally, cheaply computed textual metrics highly correlate to costly LLM-as-judge scores, and provide insights on the behavior of conditioning methods.

</details>


### [79] [VIA-SD: Verification via Intra-Model Routing for Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12243)
*Yuchen Xian,Yang He,Yunqiu Xu,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: VIA-SD proposes a multi-tier speculative decoding framework that uses intra-model routing to create a "slim verifier" for medium-confidence tokens, reducing reliance on the full verifier. It achieves 10–20% speedups over standard SD baselines and 2.5–3× over non-drafting decoding, while reducing rejection rates by 0.10–0.22.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional speculative decoding uses binary accept/recompute decisions: accepted tokens proceed cheaply, rejected tokens require expensive full-model verification. The authors observe that many rejected tokens don't actually need the full model—they can be correctly verified by a reduced-capacity submodel derived via intra-model routing. This insight motivates introducing a middle tier (slim verifier) to handle tokens requiring only moderate verification resources, thereby reducing costly large-model calls.

Method: VIA-SD introduces a three-tier hierarchical verification pipeline: (1) high-confidence draft tokens are directly accepted; (2) medium-confidence tokens are routed to a **slim-verifier**—a submodel derived from the full verifier via intra-model routing that selectively activates only a subset of the model's capacity; (3) low-confidence/uncertain tokens go to the full verifier. The slim verifier is obtained through intra-model routing, meaning it shares weights with the full verifier but uses a sparser or reduced computation path. Crucially, VIA-SD is compatible with existing SD frameworks and requires no modification to their training procedures.

Result: Across four representative tasks and multiple model families, VIA-SD reduces rejection rates by 0.10–0.22 (absolute reduction). It delivers 10–20% speedups over strong SD baselines and achieves 2.5–3× acceleration over non-drafting (autoregressive) decoding. The approach generalizes across different tasks and model families.

Conclusion: Multi-tier speculative decoding is proposed as a general paradigm for scalable and efficient LLM inference. By introducing a routed slim-verifier as an intermediate tier, VIA-SD captures efficiency gains that binary accept/reject SD leaves on the table, without requiring retraining of existing drafting or verification models.

Abstract: Speculative decoding (SD) addresses the high inference costs of LLMs by having lightweight drafters generate candidates for large verifiers to validate in parallel. Existing draft-verify methods use binary decisions: accept or fully recompute. Yet we find that many rejected tokens can be verified correctly by a slim submodel derived from the full verifier via intra-model routing, instead of the full verifier. This motivates our slim-verifier to handle tokens requiring moderate verification resources, reducing expensive large-model calls. We propose Verification via Intra-Model Routing for Speculative Decoding (VIA-SD), a multi-tier framework using a routed slim-verifier. Draft tokens are processed hierarchically: direct acceptance for high-confidence cases, slim-verifier regeneration for medium-confidence cases, and full-model verification for uncertain cases. Across four representative tasks and multiple model families, VIA-SD reduces rejection rates by 0.10-0.22 and delivers 10-20% speedups over strong SD baselines, while achieving 2.5-3x acceleration over non-drafting decoding. Moreover, VIA-SD is compatible with existing SD frameworks without modifying their training procedures. Our results suggest multi-tier SD as a general paradigm for scalable and efficient LLM inference. Project page: https://zju-xyc.github.io/VIA-SD-Project-Page/

</details>


### [80] [Reassessing High-Performing LLMs on Polish Medical Exams: True Competence or Bias-Driven Performance?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12250)
*Antoni Lasik,Jakub Pokrywka,Łukasz Grzybowski,Jeremi Ignacy Kaczmarek,Gabriela Korzańska,Janusz Świeczkowski-Feiz,Oskar Pastuszek,Paulina Hoffman,Jakub Tomasz Dąbrowski,Wojciech Kusa*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: An expanded Polish medical exam benchmark with 15,000+ questions and structural modifications reveals that standard MCQA evaluation overestimates LLM clinical competence, with top models dropping ~30 percentage points under more rigorous testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM evaluation in medicine relies heavily on multiple-choice question answering (MCQA), which inflates performance estimates due to artifacts like answer biases and guessing strategies. There is a need for benchmarks that more faithfully assess real clinical reasoning ability.

Method: The authors expand an existing Polish medical exam benchmark by adding over 15,000 questions across two new domains and introducing four structural modifications (e.g., reducing MCQA-specific artifacts). They evaluate 21 LLMs under both standard and modified setups, comparing English and Polish exam versions, and assess data contamination.

Result: Evaluation design strongly affects outcomes: the best model (Qwen3.5-122B) drops 28.4 and 31 percentage points on English and Polish exams, respectively, under the harder setup. Standard MCQA scores do not reliably reflect true medical competence, despite low evidence of data contamination.

Conclusion: Standard MCQA benchmarks substantially overestimate LLM medical competence. More rigorous evaluation designs are essential for trustworthy assessment, and the publicly released benchmark aims to support such research.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) in medicine are mainly evaluated using multiple-choice question answering (MCQA), which can overestimate real clinical ability due to guessing strategies and answer biases. To address these limitations, we introduce an expanded and more challenging benchmark based on Polish medical exams, adding over 15,000 questions, two new domains, and four structural modifications that reduce MCQA-specific artifacts and better test reasoning. We evaluate 21 LLMs and show that evaluation design strongly affects results. Under our harder setup, the best model (Qwen3.5-122B) drops by 28.4 and 31 pp on English and Polish exams, respectively. Despite low evidence of data contamination, standard MCQA scores do not reliably reflect true medical competence. To facilitate further research, we make our benchmark publicly available.

</details>


### [81] [Beyond Fully Random Masking: Attention-Guided Denoising and Optimization for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12273)
*Jia Deng,Junyi Li,Wayne Xin Zhao,Jinpeng Wang,Hongyu Lu,Ji-Rong Wen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AGDO is an attention-guided training and optimization framework for diffusion LLMs that uses attention patterns to determine denoising order and prioritize critical tokens, outperforming existing post-training methods on reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing post-training methods for diffusion LLMs rely on random masking, ignoring intrinsic token dependencies. The authors empirically show that tokens with stronger attention to unmasked context are more stable and critical for reasoning, motivating an attention-aware approach.

Method: AGDO leverages attention structure to determine the denoising order during generation, and emphasizes attention-critical tokens during both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). This aligns training and optimization with attention-derived token dependencies.

Result: AGDO consistently improves reasoning performance on mathematical and coding benchmarks, outperforming state-of-the-art post-training methods for diffusion large language models.

Conclusion: Aligning denoising strategies with attention-derived token dependencies significantly enhances the reasoning capabilities of diffusion LLMs, surpassing conventional random-masking post-training approaches.

Abstract: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) offer an efficient alternative to autoregressive models through parallel decoding, yet existing post-training methods largely rely on random masking strategies that overlook intrinsic token dependencies. In this work, we present an empirical analysis of attention in dLLMs and show that tokens attending more strongly to unmasked context exhibit greater generation stability and play a critical role in reasoning. Motivated by these findings, we propose AGDO, an attention-guided denoising and optimization framework that aligns both training and optimization with attention-derived dependencies. AGDO determines the denoising order based on attention structure and emphasizes attention-critical tokens during supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. Experiments on mathematical and coding benchmarks demonstrate that AGDO consistently improves reasoning performance, outperforming state-of-the-art post-training methods for dLLMs.

</details>


### [82] [Measuring Epistemic Resilience of LLMs Under Misleading Medical Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12291)
*Hongjian Zhou,Xinyu Zou,Jinge Wu,Sean Wu,Junchi Yu,Bradley Max Segal,Tobias Erich Niebuhr,Sara Amro,Michael Petrus,Sheikh Momin,Alexandra M. Cardoso Pinto,Rachel Niesen,Laura Sophie Wegner,Dhruv Darji,Jung Moses Koo,Joshua Fieggen,Kapil Narain,Mingde Zeng,Lei Clifton,Linda Shapiro,Fenglin Liu,David A. Clifton*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLMs score well on medical exams but collapse when misleading context is injected into questions. The paper introduces "epistemic resilience" and the MedMisBench benchmark (10,932 items, 48,889 misleading pairs) to measure this. Mean accuracy drops from 71.1% to 38.0% under adversarial context, with authority-framed falsehoods being most damaging (69.5% attack success). A clinical panel flagged potential harm in 38.2% of cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High scores on medical licensing exams create a false sense of safety regarding LLM medical judgment. Patients increasingly rely on LLMs for health advice, yet existing benchmarks only measure what models know, not whether they maintain correct judgment under adversarial or misleading context. The authors aim to expose this structural blind spot in LLM evaluation for medical settings.

Method: The authors introduce the concept of "epistemic resilience" — the ability to maintain correct judgment under adversarial context. They construct MedMisBench, a benchmark containing 10,932 medical question items and 48,889 misleading context-option pairs across three dimensions: medical reasoning, agentic capability, and patient-journey evaluation. Eleven model configurations are tested by injecting misleading context into questions originally answered correctly. A 14-member clinical panel from 7 countries evaluates potential harm in reviewed cases.

Result: Mean accuracy drops from 71.1% (original) to 38.0% (misleading context), with 51.5% attack success rate. Authority-framed falsehoods achieve 69.5% attack success; exception-poisoning claims reach 64.1%. A clinical panel identified serious potential harm in 38.2% of reviewed cases, confirming real-world safety implications beyond benchmark metrics.

Conclusion: Existing medical benchmarks measure knowledge but fail to assess whether LLMs preserve correct judgment under misleading context. MedMisBench reveals a significant epistemic resilience gap, demonstrating that high exam scores do not guarantee safe medical decision-making. The benchmark provides a new dimension for evaluating LLM reliability in high-stakes medical applications.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) now reach expert-level scores on medical licensing exams, encouraging the assumption that high scores imply safe medical judgment while patients increasingly use them for health advice. We show this assumption is fragile: when misleading context is injected into questions that LLMs originally answer correctly, they abandon the correct answer. We call the ability to maintain correct judgment under adversarial context epistemic resilience, and introduce MedMisBench to measure it. MedMisBench contains 10,932 medical question items and 48,889 misleading context-option pairs spanning medical reasoning, agentic capability, and patient-journey evaluation. Across 11 model configurations, mean accuracy falls from 71.1% on original questions to 38.0% under focused misleading context, with 51.5% attack success. The most damaging injections are formal, rule-like fabrications: authority-framed falsehoods reach 69.5% attack success and exception-poisoning claims reach 64.1%. A 14-member clinical panel from 7 countries identified serious potential harm in 38.2% of reviewed cases. MedMisBench exposes a structural blind spot in LLM evaluation in medical settings: existing benchmarks measure what models know, but not whether they preserve correct medical judgment under misleading context.

</details>


### [83] [Measuring Semantic Progress in Multi-turn Dialogue via Information Gain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12332)
*Paul He,Shiva Kasiviswanathan,Dominik Janzing*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes a theory-grounded, information-theoretic metric for evaluating semantic progress in multi-turn information-seeking dialogues, using tractable Gaussian embedding-space uncertainty reduction without LLM-based judging, achieving competitive human alignment with lightweight models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-turn dialogue evaluation is challenging because quality depends on cumulative information across turns rather than isolated responses. Existing LLM-as-a-judge methods are expensive, non-reproducible, and lack theoretical grounding. There is a need for a principled, computationally efficient metric that captures how well a conversation accumulates new, relevant, non-redundant information.

Method: The authors formalize semantic progress as question-conditioned uncertainty reduction in an embedding space. They derive a tractable Gaussian estimator with closed-form updates, where semantic progress is measured via log-determinant structure of covariance matrices. A complementary maximum-entropy argument justifies this structure when only second-order embedding information is retained. The metric decomposes total information gain additively across turns, satisfies monotonicity, and exhibits diminishing returns for redundant evidence. No autoregressive inference is needed at evaluation time.

Result: On MT-Bench, Chatbot Arena, and UltraFeedback benchmarks, the metric achieves competitive agreement with human judgments compared to several LLM-based judges, with improved alignment on MT-Bench and UltraFeedback. The method remains effective even with lightweight embedding models under CPU-only execution.

Conclusion: Semantic progress can be reliably captured through information-theoretic uncertainty reduction in embedding space without reliance on large model capacity. The proposed metric offers a reproducible, computationally efficient, and theoretically grounded alternative to LLM-as-a-judge approaches for evaluating multi-turn dialogue quality.

Abstract: Evaluating multi-turn dialogue is challenging because quality emerges across turns rather than within individual responses. We focus on a key dimension of information-seeking dialogue: semantic progress, defined as the accumulation of new, question-relevant, and non-redundant information over the course of a conversation. We formalize semantic progress as question-conditioned uncertainty reduction and introduce an information-theoretic metric that approximates it in embedding space. Our main estimator uses a tractable Gaussian formulation with closed-form updates, while a complementary maximum-entropy argument shows why log-determinant structure arises more broadly when only second-order embedding information is retained. This formulation yields desirable theoretical properties, including monotonicity, additive decomposition of total information gain across turns, and diminishing returns for redundant evidence. Unlike LLM-as-a-judge approaches, our metric requires no autoregressive inference at evaluation time and is fully reproducible for a fixed embedding model. Experiments on MT-Bench, Chatbot Arena, and UltraFeedback show that the proposed metric achieves competitive agreement with human judgments despite targeting only semantic progress, with improved alignment on MT-Bench and UltraFeedback compared to several LLM-based judges. Notably, the method remains effective with lightweight embedding models under CPU-only execution, indicating that semantic progress can be captured without reliance on large model capacity.

</details>


### [84] [ALIGNBEAM : Inference-Time Alignment Transfer via Cross-Vocabulary Logit Mixing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12342)
*Chirag Chawla,Pratinav Seth,Vinay Kumar Sankarapu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ALIGNBEAM is a training-free, inference-time method that transfers safety alignment between language model families with different vocabularies by translating anchor logits token-by-token and using an LLM judge to select the safest beam candidate, significantly raising refusal rates on adversarial prompts without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain fine-tuning degrades LLM safety, and existing inference-time defenses (e.g., logit mixing with a safe anchor) require shared vocabularies, excluding cross-family setups where safety degradation is most severe. A method is needed to transfer safety alignment across models with different tokenizers without retraining.

Method: ALIGNBEAM operates at decoding time: (1) at each step, logits from a safe anchor model are translated into the target model's vocabulary via a token-by-token mapping; (2) the translated logits are mixed with the target model's own logits; (3) K candidate continuations are generated via beam search; (4) a small LLM judge scores each candidate for safety and selects the safest one. No model weights are modified.

Result: Across both cross-vocabulary and same-vocabulary evaluation pairs, ALIGNBEAM substantially raises refusal rates on adversarial benchmarks while maintaining task accuracy with practical inference overhead.

Conclusion: Safety alignment can be effectively transferred between model families at inference time without modifying any model weights, enabling a tunable safety-utility trade-off deployable at runtime.

Abstract: Domain fine-tuning degrades the safety of large language models: fine-tuned specialists readily comply with harmful prompts framed in domain language. Existing inference-time defenses that mix logits from a safe anchor model require both models to share a vocabulary, which rules them out for the cross-family specialists where safety is most degraded. We present ALIGNBEAM, a training-free method that lifts this restriction by translating anchor logits into the target model's vocabulary token-by-token at each decoding step; a small LLM judge then selects the safest among K candidate continuations. No weights are changed, and the safety-utility trade-off can be tuned at deployment without retraining. Across both cross-vocabulary and same-vocabulary evaluation pairs, ALIGNBEAM substantially raises refusal on adversarial benchmarks while keeping task accuracy and inference overhead within practical bounds. The results show that safety alignment can be transferred between model families at inference time, without touching either model's weights.

</details>


### [85] [Verifiable Environments Are LEGO Bricks: Recursive Composition for Reasoning Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12373)
*Hao Xiang,Qiaoyu Tang,Le Yu,Yaojie Lu,Xianpei Han,Ben He,Le Sun,Bowen Yu,Peng Wang,Hongyu Lin,Dayiheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RACES introduces recursive automated composition for environment scaling, enabling scalable reasoning beyond linear limits of existing methods


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling verifiable environments from 300 individual building blocks still hinders generalization

Method: RACES uses composable type-safe operators that fuse new patterns

Result: Extensive experiments show RL training on composite tasks consistently enhances performance

Conclusion: RACES improves DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B by an average of 3.1 points (from 48.2 to 51.3) and boosts Qwen3-14B from 58.8 to 61.1 on six benchmarks, which are unseen during construction of training environments

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) with verifiable environments has emerged as a powerful approach for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). While prior research demonstrates that scaling environment quantity improves RL performance, existing manual or individual construction methods suffer from linear scaling limits, thereby hindering scalable reasoning generalization. This paper introduces RACES (\textbf{R}ecursive \textbf{A}utomated \textbf{C}omposition for \textbf{E}nvironment \textbf{S}caling), a framework that conceptualizes verifiable environments as composable building blocks that can be recursively assembled. The key insight is that when the codomain (output type) of one environment matches the domain (input type) of another, they can be automatically fused into a new verifiable environment, enabling recursive composition. RACES is implemented with 300 individual environments and defines a set of composition operators (\textsc{SEQUENTIAL}, \textsc{PARALLEL}, \textsc{SORT}, and \textsc{SELECT}) that induce diverse reasoning patterns. Extensive experiments show that RL training on these composite environments consistently enhances reasoning generalization. Specifically, RACES improves DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B by an average of 3.1 points (from 48.2 to 51.3) and boosts Qwen3-14B performance from 58.8 to 61.1 on six benchmarks, which are unseen during the construction of training environments. Moreover, RACES achieves performance comparable to training on 300 individual environments using only 50 base environments, demonstrating significant efficiency in environment utilization.

</details>


### [86] [Which Models Are Our Models Built On? Auditing Invisible Dependencies in Modern LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12385)
*Sanjay Adhikesaven,Haoxiang Sun,Sewon Min*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ModSleuth is an agentic system that recursively reconstructs LLM dependency graphs from public artifacts, recovering 1,060 source-verified dependencies across four LLM releases to reveal hidden license obligations, train-evaluation coupling, and documentation inconsistencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern LLM training pipelines have complex, recursive dependencies on other models for data generation, corpus filtering, and evaluation, but these dependencies are fragmented across heterogeneous public artifacts, making manual tracing infeasible.

Method: ModSleuth uses a formalization that distinguishes direct vs. indirect dependencies, represents heterogeneous pipeline roles via operation-centered relationships, and resolves artifact identities across names, versions, and repositories. It then recursively reconstructs dependency graphs from public artifacts with source-grounded evidence.

Result: Applied to four LLM releases, ModSleuth recovered 1,060 source-verified dependencies, constructing large-scale dependency graphs that expose multi-hop license obligations, train-evaluation coupling, discrepancies between released and training-time artifacts, and documentation inconsistencies.

Conclusion: ModSleuth demonstrates that the primary challenge in LLM supply chain tracing is no longer information extraction but defining and reconciling dependencies across inconsistent documentation. The system and its resulting graphs are publicly released to support transparent ecosystem analysis.

Abstract: Modern LLM training pipelines increasingly rely on other models to generate data, filter corpora, judge outputs, and guide development decisions. These dependencies are recursive: a model may depend on an upstream artifact whose own dependencies are documented only in separate releases and artifacts. As a result, the full dependency structure is fragmented across heterogeneous public artifacts, with complexity and recursive depth far outpacing humans' ability to trace. We introduce ModSleuth, an agentic system that recursively reconstructs LLM dependency graphs from public artifacts with source-grounded evidence. We find that the primary challenge is no longer information extraction, but defining what constitutes a dependency and reconciling artifact references across inconsistent documentation. We address these challenges through a formalization that distinguishes direct and indirect dependencies, represents heterogeneous pipeline roles through operation-centered relationships, and resolves artifact identities across names, versions, and repositories. Applying ModSleuth to four public-artifact-rich LLM releases, we recover 1,060 source-verified dependencies and construct large-scale dependency graphs of modern LLM development. These graphs reveal multi-hop license obligations, train-evaluation coupling, discrepancies between released and training-time artifacts, and documentation inconsistencies that would otherwise be difficult to uncover. We release ModSleuth and the resulting dependency graphs to support transparent analysis of the increasingly complex ecosystems underlying modern LLMs.

</details>


### [87] [System Report for CCL25-Eval Task 5: New Dataset and LoRA-Fine-Tuned Qwen2.5](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12392)
*Haotao Xie*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CCPoetry-49K, a domain-specific instruction dataset for classical Chinese poetry appreciation, and PoetryQwen, a fine-tuned Qwen2.5-14B model using LoRA. PoetryQwen achieves a 9.7% improvement over the baseline on the CCL25-Eval Task 5 benchmark (0.757 vs. 0.690), demonstrating enhanced performance in precise translation and emotional understanding of classical poetry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM research on classical Chinese poetry treats poetic appreciation as a general-domain problem, neglecting its distinctive features. High-quality, domain-specific datasets are scarce, limiting progress in precise translation and affective-semantic understanding.

Method: The authors decompose the poetic appreciation task into three subtasks: term interpretation, semantic interpretation, and emotional inference. They construct the CCPoetry-49K dataset (49,404 instruction-response pairs) by cleansing and aligning multiple open-source datasets. They then fine-tune Qwen2.5-14B using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), producing the domain-specialized PoetryQwen model.

Result: On the CCL25-Eval Task 5 benchmark, PoetryQwen scores 0.757, a 9.7% improvement over the Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct baseline (0.690), demonstrating significant gains in translation precision and emotional understanding.

Conclusion: Domain-specific fine-tuning via LoRA, guided by a carefully curated instruction dataset structured around the three subtasks, substantially improves LLM performance on classical Chinese poetry appreciation. The CCPoetry-49K dataset and the PoetryQwen model provide a foundation for further domain-specific optimization of LLMs in this culturally significant area.

Abstract: Recently, large language models (LLMs) have achieved promising progress in the fields of classical Chinese translation and the generation of classical poetry. However, domain-specific research on precise translation and affective-semantic understanding of classical poetry remains limited. The main challenge is that most studies treat the poetic appreciation task as a general-domain problem, neglecting the distinctive features of poetic appreciation, while high-quality and domain-specific datasets are extremely limited. To address this limitation, we decompose the task into three subtasks: term interpretation, semantic interpretation, and emotional inference. Based on multiple open-source datasets, we perform data cleansing and alignment to construct the Classical Chinese Poetry Instruction Pair Dataset (CCPoetry-49K), which comprises 49,404 high-quality instruction-response pairs explicitly optimized for this domain. We then propose a domain-specialized LLM, called PoetryQwen, by applying Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to fine-tune the Qwen2.5-14B model. Experimental results on the CCL25-Eval Task 5 benchmark demonstrate that PoetryQwen achieves a score of 0.757, representing a 9.7% improvement over the Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct baseline (0.690). These findings clearly indicate that PoetryQwen significantly enhances performance in precise translation and emotional understanding of classical poetry. We present new dataset and methodological considerations intended to support the domain-specific optimization of LLMs.

</details>


### [88] [Context-Driven Incremental Compression for Multi-Turn Dialogue Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12411)
*Yeongseo Jung,Jaehyeok Kim,Eunseo Jung,Jiachuan Wang,Yongqi Zhang,Ka Chun Cheung,Simon See,Lei Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: C-DIC is a dialogue context compression method that maintains revisable per-thread compression states in a compact memory, using a retrieve-revise-write-back loop per turn to share cross-turn information and update stale memories, achieving stable long-horizon performance and inference latency over hundreds of turns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern conversational agents suffer from growing dialogue history, leading to redundant attention and encoding costs. Existing solutions like naive truncation/summarization degrade fidelity, while prior context compressors lack cross-turn memory sharing or revision, causing information loss and compounding errors in long dialogues.

Method: C-DIC treats conversation as interleaved contextual threads and stores revisable per-thread compression states in a single compact dialogue memory. Each turn executes a lightweight retrieve-revise-write-back loop to share cross-turn information and update stale memories. The authors also adapt truncated backpropagation-through-time (TBPTT) to the multi-turn setting, enabling learning of cross-turn dependencies without full-history backpropagation.

Result: Extensive experiments on long-form dialogue benchmarks demonstrate C-DIC's superior performance and efficiency. Notably, it maintains stable inference latency and perplexity over hundreds of dialogue turns.

Conclusion: C-DIC provides a scalable path to high-quality dialogue modeling by addressing the fragility of existing context compression under conversational dynamics, combining efficiency with robustness through incremental, revisable compression.

Abstract: Modern conversational agents condition on an ever-growing dialogue history at each turn, incurring redundant attention and encoding costs that grow with conversation length. Naive truncation or summarization degrades fidelity, while existing context compressors lack cross-turn memory sharing or revision, causing information loss and compounding errors in long dialogues. We revisit the context compression under conversational dynamics and empirically present its fragility. To improve both efficiency and robustness, we introduce Context-Driven Incremental Compression (C-DIC), which treats a conversation as interleaved contextual threads and stores revisable per-thread compression states in a single, compact dialogue memory. At each turn, a lightweight retrieve, revise, and write-back loop shares information across turns and updates stale memories, stabilizing long-horizon behavior. In addition, we adapt truncated backpropagation-through-time (TBPTT) to our multi-turn setting, learning cross-turn dependencies without full-history backpropagation. Extensive experiments on long-form dialogue benchmarks demonstrate superior performance and efficiency of C-DIC; notably, C-DIC shows stable inference latency and perplexity over hundreds of dialogue turns, supporting a scalable path to high-quality dialogue modeling.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [89] [A PubMed-Scale Dataset of Structured Biomedical Abstracts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11361)
*Chia-Hsuan Chang,Haerin Song,Brian Ondov,Hua Xu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: Structured PubMed is a corpus of 23.2 million section-labeled biomedical abstracts (5.9M author-structured + 17.2M automatically structured via LLM), enabling large-scale section-specific text mining and model training across the entire PubMed database.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A vast portion of PubMed abstracts remain unstructured, hindering information retrieval, text mining, and knowledge synthesis workflows. No comprehensive section-labeled corpus covering the entirety of PubMed exists, creating a bottleneck for downstream NLP applications.

Method: The authors compiled the complete PubMed database (23.2M records). Author-structured abstracts (5.9M) were parsed from official XML files. The remaining 17.2 million unstructured abstracts were automatically labeled using a verbatim-extraction Large Language Model pipeline. All records were harmonized under a unified five-section schema and linked to PubMed identifiers, publication types, and dates.

Result: The resulting corpus — Structured PubMed — provides 23.2 million section-labeled abstracts partitioned into two subsets, all unified under a consistent five-section schema. The dataset is designed for training sentence-classification models, benchmarking text-segmentation architectures, and enabling PubMed-wide section-specific information extraction.

Conclusion: Structured PubMed fills a critical resource gap by providing a comprehensive, harmonized, section-labeled corpus of all PubMed abstracts, thereby removing a major bottleneck for biomedical text processing at scale.

Abstract: Structured abstracts are important for biomedical literature processing, by facilitating information retrieval, text mining, and knowledge synthesis. However, a vast portion of abstracts indexed in PubMed remain unstructured, presenting a significant bottleneck for downstream text-processing workflows and applications. To resolve this limitation, we introduce Structured PubMed, a comprehensive corpus of section-labeled biomedical abstracts compiled from the complete PubMed database, encompassing over 23.2 million research-article records. The corpus is divided into two distinct subsets: a collection of 5.9 million author-structured abstracts parsed from official XML files, and an automatically labeled collection of 17.2 million originally unstructured abstracts structured via a verbatim-extraction Large Language Model pipeline. Every record is harmonized under a unified five-section schema and mapped to its original PubMed identifier, publication type, and publication date. This dataset can be utilized to train sentence-classification models, benchmark text-segmentation architectures, and perform large-scale, section-specific information extraction at an unprecedented PubMed-wide scale.

</details>


### [90] [Factions Within, Uncertain Across: Within-Document Reader Sub-Groups in Social Highlighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11613)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: Within a document, readers form strong, stable sub-groups (factions) that agree on what to highlight beyond what shared salience predicts, but whether these factions represent stable reader traits across documents remains unresolved due to insufficient statistical power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether crowd highlighting behavior on a co-readership platform reflects a single consensus or is internally structured into reader sub-groups (factions), and whether such grouping is a stable property of the reader or the document.

Method: Two experiments using a margin-preserving curveball null model on co-readership platform data. Experiment 1 tests within-document reader agreement against baselines of shared salience, mark density, and sentence popularity, using nearest-neighbour agreement and an eight-block region-preserving null to partition coarse vs. fine agreement. Experiment 2 assesses cross-document split-half reproducibility of pairwise agreement, with a power calibration to identify informative subsets (pairs with k≥4 co-read documents).

Result: Experiment 1: Within-document pairwise agreement far exceeds baselines (z=+6.3; 88% of documents significant). About 40% of excess agreement is attributable to shared coarse-region engagement; the remainder reflects finer reader-specific agreement (z=+3.6; 77% significant). The crowd is descriptively factional. Experiment 2: Cross-document split-half reproducibility is near zero in pooled samples (+0.078 and 0.000). In the only informative subset (k≥4), point estimates are positive but imprecise, never significant, and attenuate under the region-preserving null.

Conclusion: The crowd is factional within a document — readers form sub-groups with distinct highlighting preferences. However, whether these factions follow readers across documents remains unresolved; the data are consistent with anything from purely situational grouping to a weak-to-moderate stable reader trait.

Abstract: When many people highlight the same document, is the crowd a single consensus, or is it internally structured into reader sub-groups that mark different things -- and is that structure a stable property of a reader or of the document? Building on prior work showing an individual's within-document highlighting signal is a whisper while individuality lives in selection, we ask the group-level question on a co-readership platform using a margin-preserving curveball null. Experiment 1: within a document, readers form strong sub-groups -- pairs agree far beyond what shared salience, mark density, and sentence popularity predict (nearest-neighbour agreement z=+6.3, significant in 88% of documents). Under an eight-block region-preserving null, shared engagement with the same coarse regions of the document accounts for about 40% of this excess; the majority survives as finer reader-specific agreement (z=+3.6, 77% significant). So the within-document crowd is, in a descriptive sense, factional. Experiment 2: is that grouping a stable reader trait? Here we are honest about power. The cross-document split-half reproducibility of a pair's agreement is near zero pooled (+0.078 and 0.000 in two separately drawn samples), and a power calibration shows the test is informative only for pairs that co-read many documents. In the only informative high-overlap subset (k>=4), point estimates are positive but small-sample, imprecise across the separately drawn samples, never significant, and attenuate under the region-preserving null. We therefore leave cross-document stability unresolved: the data is consistent with anything from situational grouping to a weak-to-moderate stable reader trait.
