The crowd is factional within a document; whether its factions follow the reader across documents is, honestly, beyond our reach.

</details>


### [91] [The Long Tail, Not the Front Page: Cold-Start Prediction of Crowd Highlight Salience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11654)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: Can we predict which passages readers will highlight before anyone has read a document? This paper shows that a trained logistic ranker over sentence embeddings beats a simple lead (first-sentences) baseline by a small but statistically robust margin (+0.044 AP), an edge not recoverable by unsupervised methods. The advantage is largest on less popular documents, suggesting learned models can cold-start social highlighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Social highlighting — knowing which passages many readers mark — is valuable but only exists for documents people have already read. The paper investigates whether aggregate crowd highlighting salience can be predicted from text alone before any marks accumulate, solving a cold-start problem. Prior work found zero-shot LMs underperform a trivial lead baseline, so the question becomes whether a model trained on actual highlight data can beat it.

Method: Pre-registered ladder of models evaluated via by-document cluster bootstrap. The winning approach is a logistic regression ranker over sentence embeddings combined with positional and contextual features. Compared against: (1) a lead baseline (selecting first sentences), (2) two unsupervised extractive methods (centroid and LexRank-style centrality). Ablation studies isolate contributions of raw embeddings and training augmentation. A standardized regression examines moderators: document popularity and label reliability. Evaluation is a retrospective cold-start simulation conditioned on documents that eventually accumulated readers.

Result: The logistic ranker beats lead by +0.044 average precision (95% CI [+0.029, +0.058]; clears pre-registered margin of 0.03 in 97% of resamples). Precision@3 rises from 0.25 to 0.39 (+55% relative). The model beats lead on 69% of documents. Unsupervised baselines lose to lead; trained model beats them by +0.108. Ablation: raw embeddings contribute +0.014 and training augmentation +0.010, both with positive CIs. The edge is governed mainly by document popularity (larger edge for less popular docs) and label reliability, nearly vanishing only on the most popular content — where lead strengthens, not the model weakening. No evidence of temporal-generalization failure, content drift, or near-duplicate leakage.

Conclusion: A trained model can modestly but robustly outperform the lead baseline for predicting crowd highlights in a cold-start setting, especially for less popular documents. The edge comes from learning real reader behavior, not generic unsupervised proxies. This demonstrates practical feasibility for bootstrapping social highlighting features on new content.

Abstract: A social highlighter's most useful signal -- which passages a crowd of readers marks -- exists only for documents people have already read. Can the aggregate crowd salience of a document be predicted from its text before its marks accumulate? Prior work on this data found that zero-shot language models recover highlight locations worse than a trivial lead (position) baseline, so we ask whether a model trained on the highlight corpus can beat that baseline. Using a pre-registered ladder of models and a by-document cluster bootstrap, we find a small but robust edge: a logistic ranker over sentence embeddings and positional/contextual features beats the lead baseline by +0.044 average precision (95% CI [+0.029, +0.058]; clears a pre-registered margin delta=0.03 in 97% of resamples, and stable across pipeline re-runs). Two unsupervised extractive baselines (centroid, LexRank-style centrality) lose to lead, and the trained model beats them by +0.108, so the edge is not recovered by generic unsupervised proxies -- it reflects learning from real reader marks. In product terms, precision@3 rises from 0.25 to 0.39 (+55% relative) and the model beats lead on 69% of documents. An ablation attributes the edge to the raw embedding (+0.014) and training augmentation (+0.010), each with a positive CI. The edge is not a temporal-generalization failure, and we find no evidence that content drift or near-duplicate leakage explains it. A standardized regression shows the advantage is governed mainly by document popularity (lower popularity, larger edge) and by label reliability. It nearly vanishes only on the most popular content; there it is the lead baseline that strengthens, not the model that weakens. Because our evaluation conditions on documents that eventually accumulated readers, these results are a retrospective cold-start simulation.

</details>


### [92] [CompRank: Efficient LLM Reranking via Token-Level Compression and Decoding-Free Scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11700)
*Xuan Lu,Haohang Huang,Yingqi Fan,Junlong Tong,Yuxuan Zhang,Ping Nie,Rui Meng,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CompRank is a token-efficient LLM reranking framework that uses reusable document representations, segment-wise token compression, and a CopyNet-style scoring objective to achieve near-full-token reranking quality while retaining only ~10% of document tokens, delivering up to 9.5× speedup over generation-based listwise reranking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based rerankers are effective but computationally expensive, limiting their applicability to long candidate lists. The authors observe that ranking signals are sparse relative to the full document content, motivating a framework that reduces redundant computation by aligning reranker design with this sparsity.

Method: CompRank employs three key techniques: (1) decoupling document representations from candidate order and query context, allowing document-side states to be precomputed and reused; (2) segment-wise token compression to reduce the cost of query-document cross-attention; and (3) a CopyNet-style training objective that directly aligns attention-based document scoring with ranking supervision signals.

Result: On seven BEIR datasets, CompRank achieves NDCG@10 of 39.2 while retaining only 10.2% of document tokens, compared to 39.7 under full-token attention. On TREC-COVID scaling tests, CompRank trained on 30-document lists generalizes stably to 500-document lists, achieving 4.9×–9.5× end-to-end speedup over generation-based listwise reranking and ~1.3× speedup over the full-token variant.

Conclusion: Token-level compression combined with decoding-free attention scoring offers a viable and scalable path for LLM-based reranking, maintaining strong performance while substantially reducing computational cost and enabling application to much longer candidate lists.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) rerankers have become an important component of modern retrieval and retrieval-augmented generation pipelines, but their high computational cost limits their applicability to long candidate lists. In this paper, we propose \textbf{CompRank}, a token-efficient reranking framework that reduces redundant computation by aligning reranker design with the sparsity of ranking signals. CompRank decouples document representations from candidate order and query context, enabling reusable document-side states; applies segment-wise token compression to reduce query--document interaction cost; and introduces a CopyNet-style objective that directly aligns attention-based document scoring with training supervision. Experiments on seven BEIR datasets show that CompRank achieves strong reranking performance while retaining only 10.2\% of document tokens, reaching an average NDCG@10 of 39.2 compared with 39.7 under full-token attention. Further scaling experiments on TREC-COVID show that CompRank remains stable when evaluated on candidate lists of up to 500 documents after training on 30-document lists, while achieving $4.9\times$--$9.5\times$ end-to-end speedup over generation-based listwise reranking and approximately $1.3\times$ speedup over the full-token CompRank variant. These results suggest that token-level compression and decoding-free attention scoring provide an effective path toward scalable LLM-based reranking.

</details>


### [93] [FAST-MEL: A Fast, Accurate, and Storage Efficient Solution for Multimodal Entity Linking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11749)
*Derrien Thomas,Laurent Amsaleg,Pascale Sébillot*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: FAST-MEL is a lightweight encoder-based multimodal entity linking system that uses compact fixed-size vector representations to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy while being 1000x faster and 10x more storage-efficient than existing systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art MEL systems fail to simultaneously satisfy three critical practical requirements: high linking accuracy, computational efficiency (fast inference), and storage efficiency (compact KB index). Existing systems trade off one for the others, making them impractical for large-scale real-world deployment.

Method: FAST-MEL employs a lightweight encoder-based architecture that learns a novel compact fixed-size vectorized representation for both textual and visual information of each entity and mention. These fixed-size embeddings enable efficient similarity computation and indexing, eliminating the need for expensive cross-attention or large memory footprints typical of prior systems.

Result: FAST-MEL matches the accuracy of the best-performing MEL systems while achieving three orders of magnitude (1000x) faster linking speed and one order of magnitude (10x) less storage consumption compared to the fastest existing systems.

Conclusion: FAST-MEL demonstrates that a well-designed encoder-based approach with compact fixed-size representations can simultaneously achieve all three desired objectives — accuracy, speed, and storage efficiency — making it suitable for large-scale practical MEL deployments.

Abstract: Multimodal entity linking (MEL) is the task that consists of matching textual and visual mentions of entities in unstructured data to their corresponding entities in a knowledge base (KB). To be effective in large-scale practical settings, MEL systems must meet three objectives: high linking accuracy, computational efficiency, and storage efficiency, i.e., a compact yet efficient index of the KB. In this paper, we highlight that state-of-the-art systems fail to simultaneously satisfy these 3 requirements. To meet this three-fold objective, we propose FAST-MEL, a lightweight encoder-based MEL solution that relies on a novel and compact fixed-size vectorized representation of both the textual and visual information of each entity or mention. It matches the accuracy of the best systems but performs three orders of magnitude faster. It also consumes one order of magnitude less storage than the fastest systems.

</details>


### [94] [What Limits Does Quantization Place on Dense Top-$k$ Retrieval? A Theoretical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11780)
*Koki Okajima,Tsukasa Yoshida*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper proves that when document embeddings are quantized to finite precision (B bits per coordinate), the embedding dimension d must grow at least logarithmically with corpus size N to support perfect top-k retrieval. A threshold precision B* = O(ln ln N) is identified below which no dimension suffices. This contrasts with prior infinite-precision results where d = O(k) works independently of N.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work established that if coordinates have infinite precision, d = O(k) dimensions suffice for top-k realizability regardless of corpus size. However, real-world vector databases and dense retrieval systems ubiquitously use quantization (finite-precision representations). The authors aim to determine how finite precision fundamentally constrains the embedding dimension and whether corpus-independent bounds hold in practice.

Method: The authors adopt a combinatorial and information-theoretic framework. They model quantized embeddings as points in a finite grid and formulate top-k realizability as a set-separation condition. Lower bounds on Bd (the total bit budget) are derived via counting arguments and VC-dimension-style reasoning. For ℓ₂-normalized uniform scalar quantization, they characterize three regimes of (B, d) feasibility, including a minimum precision threshold B*.

Result: The main result is Bd = Ω(k ln N), meaning that at fixed precision B, the dimension d must grow logarithmically with N. A threshold B* = O(ln ln N) is proven below which no finite dimension can achieve perfect top-k retrieval. Two additional regimes are characterized: a medium-precision regime where feasible (B, d) pairs are bounded, and a high-precision regime approaching the infinite-precision result d = O(k).

Conclusion: The infinite-precision result d = O(k) independent of N is fragile: under realistic quantization, both dimension and precision must scale with corpus size. This has direct implications for the design of practical vector databases and dense retrieval systems, where embedding dimensionality and quantization bit-widths should be chosen with awareness of corpus growth.

Abstract: We establish conditions for embedding a corpus of $N$ documents as $d$-dimensional vectors such that every $k$-subset $S \subseteq [N]$ is realizable as a result of top-$k$ retrieval by some query vector. Recent work shows that $d = O(k)$ suffices for such embeddings to exist in $\mathbb{R}^d$, independently of $N$. We theoretically prove that this corpus-independent bound is specific to infinite precision. With $B$ bits per coordinate, perfect top-$k$ retrieval requires $Bd = Ω(k \ln N)$; thus, at any fixed precision, the dimension must grow at least logarithmically with $N$. Specializing to a $\ell_2$-normalized $B$-bit uniform scalar quantization model, we also identify a threshold on the precision $B^{*} = O(\ln \ln N)$ below which no dimension suffices, together with two further regimes that bound the feasible $(B, d)$ pairs. Our result implies that in practical vector databases and dense retrieval systems where quantization is standard, the embedding dimension and possibly the precision must grow with the corpus size.

</details>


### [95] [CORE-Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Code Retrieval in the Era of Agentic Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11864)
*Fuwei Zhang,Yanzhao Zhang,Mingxin Li,Dingkun Long,Lexiang Hu,Pengjun Xie,Zhao Zhang,Fuzhen Zhuang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CORE-Bench is a large-scale benchmark designed to evaluate code retrieval in realistic agentic coding scenarios, moving beyond simple snippet matching to repository-level search. It contains 180K+ queries and 106K relevance labels across three levels: code understanding, issue-to-edit localization, and broader context retrieval. Experiments reveal that existing embedding models underperform in this setting, but fine-tuning yields significant gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing code retrieval benchmarks focus narrowly on docstring-to-function or isolated snippet matching, failing to capture the realistic demands of coding agents, which must navigate entire repositories, locate relevant files/functions, gather context, and filter distractors. This gap motivates a benchmark that evaluates requirement-driven repository search.

Method: The authors introduce CORE-Bench, a benchmark with three evaluation levels: (1) code understanding, (2) issue-to-edit localization, and (3) broader context retrieval. It is built from curated code-search tasks and SWE-bench-series instances, producing over 180K queries and 106K broader-context relevance labels. Representative embedding models are evaluated, and supervised fine-tuning is applied to measure potential improvement.

Result: Representative embedding models exhibit a sharp performance drop when moving from traditional code search to agentic code retrieval settings. Supervised fine-tuning of existing embedding models substantially improves performance, indicating considerable headroom for advancement.

Conclusion: CORE-Bench fills a critical evaluation gap in agentic code retrieval, providing a multi-level, large-scale benchmark. The performance degradation of current models highlights the difficulty of repository-aware code search, while the gains from fine-tuning suggest promising directions for future research in developing retrieval systems tailored to coding agents.

Abstract: Code retrieval is becoming central to coding agents, but agentic coding requires more than matching a natural-language query to an isolated snippet. Given a user request, a coding agent needs to navigate a concrete repository state, locate relevant files and functions, gather supporting context, and filter similar in-repository distractors. Existing code retrieval benchmarks mainly evaluate docstring-to-function or snippet-level matching, thereby missing this requirement-driven repository search problem. To address this gap, we introduce CORE-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for code retrieval in the era of agentic coding. CORE-Bench evaluates code retrieval ability at three levels: code understanding, issue-to-edit localization, and broader context retrieval. Built from curated code-search tasks and SWE-bench-series instances, CORE-Bench contains over 180K queries and 106K broader-context relevance labels. Experiments with representative embedding models show a sharp drop from traditional code search to code retrieval in agentic coding settings. Simple supervised fine-tuning of existing embedding models significantly improves performance in this setting, suggesting substantial room for further progress.

</details>


### [96] [Tail-Aware Adaptive-k: Query-Adaptive Context Selection for Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11907)
*Ziyu Song,Jiaming Fang,Kuangyu Li,Tuo Xia,Chuanpeng Wang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: TAA-k is a training-free, query-adaptive truncation method for RAG that uses knee detection to localize a candidate window and then applies EVT goodness-of-fit testing within it, achieving near-oracle retrieval quality with massive computational savings over global EVT approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed Top-K retrieval in RAG fails under heavy-tailed similarity distributions, and existing global EVT-based adaptive truncation methods suffer from prohibitive O(N²M) computational cost and statistical instability due to applying EVT across the entire ranked list.

Method: TAA-k employs a coarse-to-fine strategy: (1) Knee detection on the ranked similarity curve to identify the steep–flat–steep transition from relevance-dominated to noise-dominated regions, isolating a compact candidate window; (2) EVT-based goodness-of-fit testing applied locally within this window to validate tail onset and determine the earliest noise-dominated cutoff position.

Result: On WebQuestions, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue, TAA-k achieves F1 scores within 2–3% of the oracle upper bound, with orders-of-magnitude efficiency gains (complexity reduced to O(sqrt(N log N)*M)) over global EVT methods, while remaining robust across different embedding models and compression dimensions.

Conclusion: Localizing EVT via knee detection resolves both the computational bottleneck and statistical instability of global EVT approaches, enabling practical, training-free adaptive context selection that rivals oracle performance in RAG retrieval.

Abstract: Adaptive context selection is critical for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, as fixed Top-K retrieval fails under query-dependent and heavy-tailed similarity distributions. While Extreme Value Theory (EVT) offers a principled framework for adaptive truncation, existing approaches apply EVT globally across the entire ranked list, incurring prohibitive computational costs and statistical instability. We propose Tail-Aware Adaptive-k(TAA-k), a training-free framework that operationalizes EVT through a localized validation strategy. The key insight is that ranked similarity curves exhibit a characteristic steep--flat--steep pattern reflecting a transition from relevance-dominated to noise-dominated regimes. TAA-k exploits this geometric structure via knee detection to identify a compact candidate region, then applies EVT-based goodness-of-fit testing within this window to validate the onset of tail behavior. This coarse-to-fine design reduces computational complexity from O(N^2M) to O(sqrt{N\log N}*M) while maintaining statistical rigor. Under mild monotone likelihood ratio assumptions, TAA-k yields a stable, query-adaptive cutoff corresponding to the earliest noise-dominated position. Experiments on WebQuestions, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue demonstrate that TAA-k achieves near-oracle retrieval quality (F1 within 2-3% of oracle) with orders-of-magnitude efficiency gains over global EVT methods, while maintaining robustness across embedding models and compression dimensions.

</details>


### [97] [LLM-Based User Personas for Recommendations at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12198)
*Haoting Wang,Haokai Lu,Zheyun Feng,Jenny Huang,Yifat Amir,Gregory Hinkson,Ben Most,Zelong Zhao,Yixin Kelly Cui,Rein Zhang,Fabio Soldo,Yu Xia,Nihar Bhupalam,Minmin Chen,Konstantina Christakopoulou,Lichan Hong,Ed H. Chi*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: A framework that generates real-time natural-language user interest personas via LLMs for large-scale video recommendation, using knowledge distillation and asynchronous inference to overcome latency/cost challenges, validated through offline tests, user studies, and live A/B experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based recommendation methods depend on structured IDs or offline processing, which restricts semantic expressiveness, real-time responsiveness, and user-facing interpretability. There is a need for a scalable solution that harnesses LLM reasoning at serving time without prohibitive computational cost.

Method: The framework generates user interest personas in natural language during online serving by combining summarization of known interests with exploration of novel topics. To make this feasible at billion-user scale, it employs (1) knowledge distillation to deploy a smaller, efficient model, (2) asynchronous inference to decouple persona generation from latency-critical serving paths, and (3) input optimization using semantically clustered video representations to reduce prompt length and computational overhead.

Result: Offline evaluations, user studies, and live A/B tests on a commercial video recommendation platform demonstrate significant improvements in viewer value metrics.

Conclusion: The work successfully bridges high-level semantic understanding with industrial-scale recommendation, enabling more dynamic, explainable, and satisfying personalized experiences through real-time LLM-generated user personas.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer unprecedented potential for enhancing recommendation systems through their world knowledge and reasoning capabilities. However, existing approaches often rely on structured IDs or offline processing, limiting semantic richness, real-time adaptability, and user-facing interpretability. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that enables real-time generation of LLM-based user interest personas for a large-scale commercial video recommendation platform. Our method generates natural-language user interest personas that address the exploitation-exploration trade-off by combining the summarization of existing interests with novel topics, directly during serving. To overcome the computational challenges of online LLM inference at a billion-user scale, we design a cost-efficient architecture leveraging knowledge distillation, asynchronous inference, and input optimization via semantically clustered video representations. Extensive offline evaluations, user studies, and live A/B tests demonstrate significant improvements in viewer value. This work bridges the gap between high-level semantic understanding and industrial-scale recommendation, paving the way for more dynamic, explainable, and satisfying personalized experiences.

</details>


### [98] [DiffCold: A Diffusion-based Generative Model for Cold-Start Item Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12245)
*Kangning Zhang,Yingjie Qin,Weinan Zhang,Yong Yu,Jianghao Lin*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: DiffCold is a diffusion-based generative model that resolves the seesaw dilemma in cold-start item recommendation by unifying warm and cold item representations through conditional diffusion, retrieval-enhanced initialization, and simulation-based contrastive alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the seesaw dilemma in cold-start item recommendation, where improving cold-item performance degrades warm-item performance (and vice versa). This dilemma arises from a fundamental distributional disparity: warm item embeddings reside on a complex behavioral manifold shaped by interaction signals, while cold item embeddings are confined to a semantic manifold derived solely from content features. Existing methods force rigid mappings between these inconsistent spaces, sacrificing warm representation precision.

Method: DiffCold employs conditional diffusion to reconstruct warm item embeddings from content features, preserving manifold structure. Two key designs: (1) a Retrieval-enhanced Aggregator that initializes generation using embeddings of semantically similar warm items to avoid inefficient denoising from pure noise; (2) a Simulation-based Representation Alignment module that enforces distribution consistency between generated and real embeddings via contrastive learning.

Result: Experiments on three benchmark datasets confirm that DiffCold resolves the seesaw dilemma and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across all evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: DiffCold effectively bridges the distributional gap between warm and cold item representations through diffusion-based generation, eliminating the trade-off that has historically plagued cold-start recommenders. The retrieval-enhanced initialization and contrastive alignment are critical to achieving this unified manifold without degradation.

Abstract: Cold-start item recommendation remains a persistent challenge in real-world systems due to the absence of interaction histories. While prior models attempt to bridge this gap using item content features, they universally suffer from the \textbf{seesaw dilemma}: enhancing performance for cold items inevitably degrades performance for warm items, and vice versa. We identify that this dilemma stems from a fundamental \textbf{distributional disparity}: warm item embeddings occupy a complex ``behavioral manifold" shaped by rich interaction signals, whereas cold item embeddings are constrained to a ``semantic manifold" derived solely from auxiliary content. Existing methods often force a rigid mapping between these inconsistent spaces, causing the model to sacrifice the precision of warm representations to accommodate cold ones. To address this, we propose \textbf{DiffCold}, a diffusion-based generative model that unifies warm and cold representations. Unlike GANs or VAEs, DiffCold leverages conditional diffusion to reconstruct warm item embeddings from content, preserving the underlying manifold structure without degradation. We further tailor this paradigm with two specific designs: a \textbf{Retrieval-enhanced Aggregator} that initializes generation using semantically similar warm items to bypass inefficient noise, and a \textbf{Simulation-based Representation Alignment} module that enforces distribution consistency between generated and real embeddings via contrastive learning. Experiments on three benchmarks confirm that DiffCold resolves the seesaw dilemma, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods across all metrics.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [99] [When Poison Fails After Retrieval: Revisiting Corpus Poisoning under Chunking and Reranking Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11265)
*Xi Nie,Hongwei Li,Shenghao Wu,Mingxuan Li,Jiachen Li,Wenbo Jiang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Existing corpus poisoning attacks against RAG degrade under realistic multi-stage pipelines (chunking + reranking) due to retrieval granularity mismatch. The authors propose CRCP, which jointly optimizes for retrieval relevance, reranker consistency, and chunk-boundary robustness, achieving substantially higher attack success across diverse pipeline configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior RAG poisoning research evaluates attacks under simplified retrieval settings, ignoring real-world components like document chunking, dense retrieval, reranking, and grounded generation. The authors identify that many attacks fail after reranking despite high retrieval-stage success, revealing a critical realism gap in current security evaluation.

Method: CRCP (Chunk-aware and Rerank-Consistent Poisoning) jointly optimizes three objectives: retrieval relevance (adversarial passages match query semantics), reranker consistency (passages are locally coherent and answer-bearing to satisfy rerankers), and chunk-boundary robustness (modeling chunking transformations explicitly so adversarial signals survive fragmentation). This produces self-contained passages effective under varying chunk sizes.

Result: On standard RAG benchmarks with multiple retrievers and rerankers, existing poisoning methods exhibit high sensitivity to chunk size and reranking strategies, while CRCP achieves substantially higher attack success rates and stronger robustness across realistic pipelines.

Conclusion: Corpus poisoning in modern RAG systems should be framed as a multi-stage retrieval consistency problem rather than a retrieval-only problem. The realism gap in current evaluations must be addressed by incorporating chunking, reranking, and generation into threat modeling.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems are vulnerable to corpus poisoning attacks that manipulate downstream model outputs through malicious knowledge injection. Existing studies mainly evaluate poisoning under simplified retrieval settings, overlooking practical RAG pipelines involving document chunking, dense retrieval, reranking, and grounded generation. In this paper, we revisit corpus poisoning under realistic multi-stage retrieval pipelines and show that many existing attacks substantially degrade after reranking despite achieving high retrieval-stage relevance. We identify retrieval granularity mismatch as a key reason for this failure: document-level adversarial signals are often fragmented during chunking, while rerankers favor locally coherent and answer-bearing passages rather than globally optimized semantic similarity. Based on this observation, we propose Chunk-aware and Rerank-Consistent Poisoning (CRCP), a poisoning framework that jointly optimizes retrieval relevance, reranker consistency, and chunk-boundary robustness. CRCP explicitly models chunking transformations during optimization to generate locally self-contained adversarial passages that remain effective under varying chunking configurations. Experiments on standard RAG benchmarks with multiple retrievers and rerankers show that existing poisoning methods are highly sensitive to chunk size and reranking strategies, whereas CRCP achieves substantially higher attack success rates and stronger robustness across realistic retrieval pipelines. Our findings highlight an important realism gap in current RAG security evaluation and suggest that poisoning in modern RAG systems should be studied as a multi-stage retrieval consistency problem rather than a retrieval-only problem.

</details>


### [100] [MPC-Patch-Bench: Security-Aware LLM Code Patch for Multi-Party Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11416)
*Yukuan Zhang,Mengxin Zheng,Qian Lou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: MPC-Patch-Bench is the first repository-level benchmark for LLM code repair on Secure Multi-Party Computation software, featuring a domain-specific data curation framework (205 verified instances) and an MPC Verifier that enforces cryptographic safety and numerical fidelity, revealing that even the strongest LLMs resolve only 17.1% of tasks after security verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing repository-level code repair benchmarks (e.g., SWE-bench) fail for MPC software due to three structural mismatches: (1) MPC repos are dominated by Python infrastructure, not cryptographic logic; (2) high-value MPC fixes lack standardized tests required by rigid extraction pipelines; (3) standard fail-to-pass evaluation ignores cryptographic safety. As MPC is increasingly deployed in privacy-preserving ML, biomedicine, and secure analytics, a domain-specific benchmark with security-aware evaluation is urgently needed.

Method: The authors introduce MPC-Patch-Bench with two frameworks: (1) A Data Curation Framework using a domain-specific curation agent that filters pull requests through three cryptographic layers, plus a human-AI completion engine that synthesizes missing problem statements and Fail-to-Pass/Pass-to-Pass tests, yielding 205 verified instances. (2) An MPC Verifier that performs dynamic differential testing against plaintext oracles and applies MPC-specific static analysis rules to flag unsafe reveals, insecure arithmetic, and illegal public/private casts.

Result: The strongest evaluated LLM achieves only 22.9% functional resolution on MPC-Patch-Bench. The MPC Verifier further reduces verified resolution to 17.1%, with up to 40% of functionally-passing patches rejected for cryptographic or numerical-fidelity violations.

Conclusion: General-purpose LLM code repair benchmarks are inadequate for security-critical MPC software. Domain-specific curation and cryptographic verification are essential for meaningful evaluation, and current LLMs have substantial room for improvement on repository-level MPC repair tasks.

Abstract: Repository-level benchmarks for evaluating Large Language Model (LLM) code repair on Secure Multi-Party Computation (MPC) software do not yet exist, and directly transplanting general-purpose benchmarks such as SWE-bench fails on three structural fronts: (i) MPC repositories are dominated by generic Python infrastructure rather than cryptographic logic; (ii) high-value MPC fixes lack the standardized tests rigid extraction pipelines require; and (iii) standard fail-to-pass evaluation is insufficient for code that must also be cryptographically safe. MPC is increasingly deployed for privacy-preserving machine learning, biomedical collaboration, and secure analytics. Existing MPC-specific code-synthesis efforts cover only operator-level or single-framework tasks; evaluating LLM agents on real repository-level MPC repair instead demands MPC-aware data curation and a verifier matched to the security and numerical-fidelity guarantees MPC programs must obey neither of which existing benchmarks provide. We introduce MPC-Patch-Bench, a repository-level benchmark organised around two frameworks. (1)The Data Curation Framework combines a domain-specific curation agent that filters raw pull requests through three cryptographic layers with a human-AI completion engine that synthesizes missing problem statements and Fail-to-Pass/Pass-to-Pass tests, yielding 205 fully verified instances. (2)The MPC Verifier provides dedicated security and numerical-fidelity checks via dynamic differential testing against plaintext oracles and MPC-specific static analysis rules that flag unsafe reveals, insecure arithmetic, and illegal public/private casts. The strongest evaluated LLM functionally resolves only 22.9% of MPC-Patch-Bench tasks; the MPC Verifier further reduces verified resolution to 17.1%, with up to 40% of functionally-passing patches rejected for cryptographic or numerical-fidelity violations.

</details>


### [101] [JailbreakOPT: Tool-Assisted Iterative Jailbreak Prompt Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11425)
*Ge Shi,Jun Yin,Donglin Xie,Fangyi Liu,Yucan Li,Menglin Liu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: JailbreakOPT is a tool-assisted framework that frames jailbreak prompt optimization as composing atomic attacks from a library, using contextual Thompson sampling (a bandit algorithm) to efficiently reuse past experience, achieving higher attack success rates with fewer queries than existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-turn jailbreak attacks face a trade-off: hand-crafted prompts are expressive but static (no adaptation), while iterative optimization methods adapt but rely on low-level mutations requiring many target queries. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a framework that adapts efficiently by composing higher-level attack primitives and reusing cross-episode experience.

Method: JailbreakOPT consists of: (1) an attack tool library of diverse atomic jailbreak prompts (e.g., role-play, payload splitting, encoding), (2) a unified intra-episode optimization abstraction that composes these tools to generate standalone attack prompts, and (3) a contextual bandit formulation for tool selection across episodes, solved via contextual Thompson sampling to balance exploration and exploitation based on historical outcomes.

Result: Experiments across multiple target LLMs and attack goals demonstrate that JailbreakOPT improves Attack Success Rate (ASR) while reducing the Number of Attacks until success (No.A), outperforming both atomic single-turn attacks and existing iterative optimization baselines.

Conclusion: JailbreakOPT demonstrates that framing jailbreak prompt optimization as a tool-composition problem with bandit-based experience reuse yields more sample-efficient and effective attacks, highlighting persistent safety vulnerabilities in LLMs that require stronger defenses.

Abstract: Jailbreak attacks expose persistent safety weaknesses in large language models (LLMs), but existing stateless single-turn methods face a trade-off: hand-crafted prompts are expressive but static, while iterative prompt optimization can adapt but often relies on low-level mutations that require many target queries. We propose JailbreakOPT, a tool-assisted framework for improving iterative single-turn jailbreak prompt optimization. JailbreakOPT organizes diverse atomic jailbreak prompts into an attack tool library and composes them through a unified intra-episode optimization abstraction to generate stronger standalone attack prompts. To reuse experience across attack episodes, JailbreakOPT further frames tool selection as a contextual bandit problem and applies contextual Thompson sampling to guide exploration and exploitation based on past outcomes. Experiments across multiple target LLMs and attack goals show that JailbreakOPT improves attack success rate (ASR) while reducing the number of attacks until success (No.A) compared with atomic single-turn attacks and existing iterative optimization baselines. This paper may contain offensive or harmful content.

</details>


### [102] [Evaluating and Combating the Impact of Concept Drift on the Performance of Machine Learning-Based Phishing Detection Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11471)
*Warren Fernando,Nikos Komninos*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper examines how the evolving nature of spam and phishing emails affects the performance of machine learning-based detection systems, and investigates strategies to mitigate the resulting degradation in detection accuracy over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The relentless evolution of spam email tactics, coupled with the increasing sophistication of phishing attacks (often precursors to malware), creates a moving-target problem for detection systems. ML-based filters that perform well initially may suffer performance degradation as adversaries adapt. Understanding the impact of this evolution on ML detectors and devising countermeasures is the core motivation.

Method: The authors propose to empirically assess how temporal evolution in the spam email domain impacts machine learning-based detection systems. This likely involves training models on historical spam datasets and evaluating their performance on more recent, evolved spam samples to quantify degradation, followed by exploring and testing mitigation strategies (e.g., periodic retraining, concept drift adaptation, feature engineering) to sustain detection efficacy.

Result: The abstract does not provide specific quantitative results. It frames the expected finding that spam evolution causes performance degradation in static ML models and anticipates that certain mitigation strategies can help maintain detection accuracy over time.

Conclusion: The abstract does not report a definitive conclusion, but the stated aim implies that understanding spam domain evolution is critical for designing robust, long-term machine learning-based spam detection systems, and that adaptive strategies are necessary to combat adversarial drift.

Abstract: The expansion of the digital domain has resulted in a substantial increase in digital communication, with email emerging as one of the most prominent channels. The proliferation of email communication is apparent in both professional and personal contexts, thereby creating numerous vulnerabilities for malicious actors to exploit. Spam emails, a form of unsolicited correspondence often bearing malicious intent towards recipients, have been an ongoing challenge for email users since the inception of email technology, and this problem has been exacerbated by the growth of the digital landscape. Email spam filters are integral components of email clients, engineered to identify potentially harmful messages and alert users to their malicious content. Phishing, frequently the initial phase of malware-based attacks, is evolving rapidly, with malware becoming increasingly sophisticated over time. A widely adopted approach for detecting malicious activity within malware and spam domains is the application of machine learning. Our aim is to assess the impact of the evolution within the spam email domain on these machine learning-based detection systems and to explore strategies for mitigating associated performance degradation.

</details>


### [103] [Hiding the Trees in the Forest: Building Network Covert Channels with Hash-Based Covert Carrier Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11532)
*Zexiao Zou,Zhiqiang Wang,Baoxu Liu,Yuyang Han,Yan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hash-based covert carrier filtering strategy that uses a key-dependent rule to randomly select a sparse subset of carriers, coupling the covertness of network covert channels with key security rather than algorithm secrecy alone. Experiments on storage and timing covert channels show significant improvement in detection resistance, with strong classifier degradation at key sizes above six bits and per-packet processing delay under 8 μs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing network covert channels rely on the secrecy of their covert algorithms, which are increasingly vulnerable to exposure and detection as research advances. Once the algorithm is known, adversaries can easily detect the channel. There is a need to decouple covertness from algorithm secrecy and instead bind it to key security.

Method: The authors propose a hash-based covert carrier filtering strategy. During covert channel construction, a key-dependent filtering rule is introduced so that communicating parties randomly and dynamically filter a sparse subset from the carrier set to serve as the actual covert carrier set. The strategy is experimentally validated using machine learning-based traffic analysis methods on two types of network covert channels: storage covert channels and timing covert channels.

Result: The proposed strategy significantly improves detection resistance. When the filter key size exceeds six bits, the impact on classifier detection effectiveness becomes substantial. Single-packet processing delay remains under 8 μs, confirming feasibility for high-speed network environments.

Conclusion: The hash-based filtering strategy effectively enhances the anti-detection capability of network covert channels by coupling covertness with key security. Its low processing overhead (sub-8 μs per packet) makes it practical for deployment in high-speed networks.

Abstract: As an effective anti-censorship mechanism, network covert channels can provide data privacy protection and ensure communication security. However, the covertness of existing network covert channels primarily depends on the secrecy of their covert algorithms. With the increasing depth of research in this field, the difficulty of breaking such algorithms has gradually decreased. Once the algorithm is exposed, the network covert channel can be easily detected by adversaries. To address this issue, this paper proposes a covert carrier filtering strategy based on the hash. In this strategy, a key-dependent filtering rule is introduced during the construction of the network covert channel, enabling the communicating parties to randomly and dynamically filter a sparse subset from the carrier set as the covert carrier set. This strategy not only enhances the randomness of carrier selection but also tightly couples the covertness of the network covert channel with the security of the key. We employ machine learning-based traffic analysis methods to experimentally validate the strategy in two types of network covert channels: network storage and timing covert channels. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed strategy significantly improves the detection resistance of network covert channels. When the filter key size exceeds six bits, the impact on the detection effect of the classifier becomes quite significant. Furthermore, the processing delay for a single packet is less than 8 $μs$, indicating the feasibility of deploying the proposed strategy in high-speed network environments.

</details>


### [104] [Sovereign Assurance Boundary: Certificate-Bound Admission for Agentic Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11632)
*Jun He,Deying Yu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SAB introduces a cryptographically-bound runtime admission layer (airlock + broker) that intercepts agent proposals, compiles them into typed execution contracts, and emits scoped Sovereign Assurance Certificates to prevent autonomous reasoning systems from directly mutating infrastructure state without consequence-aware, revocable authorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic (non-deterministic reasoning) systems can propose high-stakes mutations to production resources, but existing IAM, policy engines, consensus protocols, and audit logs are either statically permissioned and context-unaware, or merely post-hoc — they cannot dynamically gate autonomous execution authority at runtime before state mutation occurs.

Method: The paper proposes the Sovereign Assurance Boundary (SAB) architecture consisting of: (1) an assurance airlock that intercepts agent proposals and compiles them into typed execution contracts C, binding them to cryptographic evidence digests H(E) and policy versions; (2) consequence-aware certification paths that route contracts through admission evaluation; (3) emission of a signed Sovereign Assurance Certificate (Ω) scoped to execution identity, revocation epoch, and validity window; and (4) a sovereign execution broker that verifies Ω and performs pre-execution revocation and drift checks before invoking infrastructure APIs. The system is formalized with admission/revocation invariants and evaluated via a Go prototype over 2,500 admission attempts.

Result: Preliminary feasibility measurements from a Go prototype were collected over 2,500 admission attempts, demonstrating the viability of the airlock-broker model for runtime admission control of autonomous agent proposals.

Conclusion: The broker-enforced SAB model prevents autonomous reasoning from directly mutating state by transforming delegated execution authority into a cryptographically verifiable, evidence-bound, revocable, and replayable runtime artifact.

Abstract: Agentic infrastructure introduces a critical control-plane authorization problem: non-deterministic reasoning systems can propose high-stakes mutations to production resources, yet existing security mechanisms -- such as identity and access management (IAM), policy engines, consensus protocols, and audit logs -- either enforce static, context-unaware permissions or merely record actions post-execution. This paper introduces the Sovereign Assurance Boundary (SAB), a certificate-bound runtime admission layer for autonomous execution authority. SAB intercepts agent proposals at an assurance airlock, compiles them into typed execution contracts $C$, and binds these contracts to cryptographic evidence digests $H(E)$ and policy versions. The contracts are then routed through consequence-aware certification paths. Upon successful admission, the system emits a signed Sovereign Assurance Certificate ($Ω$) that is strictly scoped to a specific execution identity, revocation epoch, and validity window. Finally, a sovereign execution broker verifies $Ω$ and performs fresh pre-execution revocation and drift checks before invoking infrastructure APIs. We detail the airlock-broker architecture, formalize its admission and revocation invariants, and report preliminary feasibility measurements from a Go prototype evaluated over 2,500 admission attempts. Ultimately, this broker-enforced model prevents autonomous reasoning from directly mutating state, transforming delegated execution authority into a cryptographically verifiable, evidence-bound, revocable, and replayable runtime artifact.

</details>


### [105] [VIPIR: A Versatile GPU Framework for Integrating Private Information Retrieval Protocols](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11536)
*Jongmin Kim,Hyesung Ji,Jean-Luc Watson,Charles Gouert,G. Edward Suh,Jung Ho Ahn*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: VIPIR is a GPU-accelerated PIR framework that co-designs protocols with hardware, introducing a hybrid two-category protocol approach, a GPU-friendly compression method (ExpPack), and optimized core operations (NTTs, tensor-core GEMM), achieving orders-of-magnitude throughput improvement over prior systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PIR requires high computational throughput, large memory capacity, and substantial memory bandwidth. Existing GPU-accelerated PIR systems are limited by protocol-level bottlenecks that cannot be resolved through hardware acceleration alone, motivating a co-design of protocols and GPU hardware.

Method: The authors (1) develop a unified analytic model categorizing SOTA PIR protocols into two complementary categories; (2) propose two hybrid protocols combining techniques across categories; (3) introduce expansion-based ring packing (ExpPack) for GPU-friendly data compression with high parallelism and low communication; (4) optimize NTTs and GEMMs, including a tensor-core-based database multiplication cast as mixed-integer-type GEMM; and (5) design memory-efficient scheduling for multi-GPU scaling.

Result: VIPIR achieves orders-of-magnitude higher throughput than prior PIR systems while simultaneously reducing communication and memory overheads, making large-scale PIR practical.

Conclusion: Co-designing PIR protocols with GPU acceleration overcomes the inherent limitations of individual protocol categories. VIPIR's hybrid protocols and GPU-centric optimizations (ExpPack, tensor-core GEMM, memory-efficient scheduling) demonstrate that practical, large-scale PIR is achievable with commodity GPU hardware.

Abstract: While private information retrieval (PIR) enables private database services by fully concealing access patterns, it simultaneously requires high computational throughput, large memory capacity, and substantial memory bandwidth. We introduce VIPIR, a versatile GPU framework that co-designs PIR protocols with GPU acceleration. We develop a unified analytic model showing that state-of-the-art PIR protocols fall into two categories with complementary limitations, and propose two protocols that flexibly combine techniques across these categories, overcoming the limitations of both classes. These protocols incorporate a GPU-friendly data compression method called expansion-based ring packing (ExpPack), which offers a high degree of parallelism and minimal communication cost. VIPIR applies further optimizations to core operations, including number-theoretic transforms (NTTs) and various matrix-matrix multiplications (GEMMs). Notably, we develop a tensor-core-based execution method for database multiplication by interpreting it as a mixed-integer-type GEMM. We also design memory-efficient scheduling methods that minimize intermediate buffers and enable multi-GPU scaling under memory capacity constraints. Overall, VIPIR achieves orders-of-magnitude higher throughput than prior PIR systems while reducing communication and memory overheads, making large-scale PIR practical.

</details>


### [106] [PriME-Deal: Privacy-Preserving Bilateral Data Trading with Efficient Matchmaking and Auditable Fair Exchange on Blockchain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11539)
*Jie Zhang,Xiaohong Li,Shanshan Xu,Hanwei Wu,Ruitao Feng,Guangdong Bai*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: PriME-Deal is a non-interactive protocol for privacy-preserving data trading that achieves bilateral policy-hiding, efficient threshold access control, and auditable fair exchange on public blockchains without trusted third parties. It reduces seller publishing time by two orders of magnitude over prior work and keeps buyer-side zero-knowledge proofs constant-time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing bilateral attribute-based access control schemes for data trading suffer from three key shortcomings: they leak policy information, incur super-linear computational costs, or depend on trusted third parties for dispute resolution. A practical solution must simultaneously hide both seller and buyer policies, provide cryptographic fairness, and operate trustlessly on public blockchains.

Method: The protocol consists of three integrated components: (1) The seller embeds a secret token under the buyer's policy into an oblivious key-value store using pseudorandom masking, enabling the buyer to reconstruct the token locally via tag-based probing without combinatorial enumeration. (2) The buyer proves correctness of reconstruction in zero-knowledge. (3) Fair exchange is enforced through a collateralized on-chain reveal mechanism with cryptographic audit trails that penalize misbehavior, eliminating reliance on trusted dispute resolution. Security is proven in the Universal Composability framework under standard assumptions.

Result: Compared to state-of-the-art threshold fuzzy IB-ME, seller publishing time drops from 690s to 8.76s for a 500-attribute policy (~79× improvement). For a (200,20,5) configuration, the buyer completes token reconstruction and proof generation in 8.9s, with the ZK proof taking under 0.6s and remaining constant across all parameter scales. On-chain cost is approximately 28.6M gas, fitting within Ethereum's block limit.

Conclusion: PriME-Deal is the first practical privacy-preserving data trading protocol that simultaneously delivers linear seller overhead, bilateral policy hiding, and auditable fairness, making blockchain-based privacy-preserving data markets feasible at scale.

Abstract: Bilateral attribute-based access control for data trading must hide policies, provide cryptographic fairness, and avoid trusted third parties. Existing solutions either leak policy information, incur super-linear costs, or rely on trusted dispute resolution. We present PriME-Deal, a non-interactive protocol that simultaneously achieves policy-hiding bilateral matching, efficient threshold access control, and auditable fair exchange on public blockchains. The seller embeds a secret token under the buyer policy into an oblivious key-value store with pseudorandom masking; the buyer reconstructs the token locally via tag-based probing, eliminating combinatorial enumeration, and proves correctness in zero-knowledge. Fair exchange is enforced through a collateralized on-chain reveal with a cryptographic audit that penalizes misbehaviour without trusted parties.
  We prove security in the Universal Composability framework under standard assumptions. Compared with the state-of-the-art threshold fuzzy IB-ME scheme, the seller's publishing time is reduced by two orders of magnitude (e.g., 8.76s vs. 690s for a policy of 500 attributes). For a typical configuration of (200,20,5), the buyer completes token reconstruction and proof generation in 8.9s, with the zero-knowledge proof taking under 0.6s and remaining constant across all parameter scales. The on-chain cost is approximately 28.6M gas, well within Ethereum's block limit. PriME-Deal thus delivers the first practical privacy-preserving data trading protocol that combines linear seller overhead, bilateral policy hiding, and auditable fairness.

</details>


### [107] [WHET: Welding Homomorphic Encryption to Accelerator Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11541)
*Jongmin Kim,Hyesung Ji,Wonseok Choi,Hyunah Yu,Jung Ho Ahn*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: WHET introduces memory-centric, architecture-aware optimizations for FHE accelerators, achieving 1.38–8.74× per-area performance gains over prior accelerators and the first sub-millisecond CKKS bootstrapping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior FHE research has advanced cryptographic/algorithmic improvements and hardware acceleration in isolation, leaving significant performance on the table. Conventional FHE constructions cause excessive working sets and heavy off-chip memory traffic that existing accelerators fail to address holistically.

Method: WHET proposes accelerator-specific techniques: (1) fine-grained coefficient-to-slot transformation, (2) plaintext compression, and (3) intermediate modulus raising. These reduce on-chip data footprint by minimizing temporary ciphertexts and plaintext loads. Lightweight architectural refinements — a special-purpose buffer and functional unit extensions — further improve on-chip memory efficiency.

Result: WHET achieves 1.38–8.74× per-area performance improvement over state-of-the-art FHE accelerators and demonstrates the first-ever sub-millisecond CKKS bootstrapping.

Conclusion: Co-designing cryptographic constructions with accelerator architecture — rather than treating them separately — unlocks substantial performance gains and enables latency breakthroughs such as sub-millisecond CKKS bootstrapping.

Abstract: Fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) enables computations on encrypted data without decryption, offering strong data privacy at the expense of substantial computational and memory overheads. Prior efforts have steadily improved FHE performance through cryptographic and algorithmic enhancements or hardware acceleration, yet these two directions have progressed largely in isolation, hindering the full exploitation of available hardware capabilities. This work presents WHET, which introduces memory-centric, architecture-aware optimizations to better align cryptographic and algorithmic constructions with FHE accelerator architectures. We identify conventional FHE constructions as major sources of excessive working sets and heavy off-chip memory traffic. We propose accelerator-specific techniques, including fine-grained coefficient-to-slot transformation, plaintext compression, and intermediate modulus raising, to reduce the on-chip data footprint by minimizing temporary ciphertexts and plaintext loads. With these techniques applied, we observe additional opportunities to improve on-chip memory efficiency; hence, we introduce lightweight architectural refinements, including a special-purpose buffer and functional unit extensions. With these optimizations, WHET achieves 1.38-8.74$\times$ per-area performance improvements over state-of-the-art FHE accelerators and the first-ever sub-millisecond CKKS bootstrapping.

</details>


### [108] [Privacy-Preserving Federated Autoencoder for ECG Anomaly Detection on Edge Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11556)
*Kaan Arda Akyol,Jakub Kacper Szeląg,Aydin Abadi,Maha Alghamdi,Ghadah Albalawi,Ghouse Ibrahim Kaleelullah,Hilal Tutus,Sarah Al Subaiei,Shardul Kapse,Syed Mohammed Raheeb,Mujeeb Ahmed,Rehmat Ullah*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper presents the first end-to-end federated ECG anomaly detection system that simultaneously achieves GDPR/HIPAA-grade privacy via DP-SGD with Rényi-DP accounting, real-time edge inference via INT8 quantization on Raspberry Pi 4, and robust cross-hospital detection quality using federated averaging of autoencoders on PTB-XL, finding that privacy and quantization penalties are empirically independent at a recommended operating point of ε=4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous ECG monitoring could prevent cardiovascular events by detecting rhythm abnormalities early, but a practical deployable system must simultaneously satisfy three stringent requirements: (1) legal-grade privacy compliance (GDPR, HIPAA) due to sensitive health data, (2) real-time inference on constrained edge hardware, and (3) reliable detection performance under non-IID data distributions across different hospitals. No existing system addresses all three together.

Method: The authors design a federated system for unsupervised 12-lead ECG anomaly detection on the PTB-XL dataset. They combine three autoencoder architectures (VanillaAE, ConvAE, VAE) trained via Flower-based federated averaging (FedAvg) across ten simulated hospitals with non-IID data splits. Client-side differentially private SGD (DP-SGD) with a Rényi-DP accountant provides formal (ε,δ)-DP guarantees. Post-training INT8 quantization is applied, and inference latency is benchmarked on a Raspberry Pi 4 (AArch64). An ε sweep characterizes the privacy-utility trade-off, and the interaction between DP noise and quantization error is empirically analyzed.

Result: Federated learning matches or exceeds centralized baselines: ConvAE federated AUROC reaches 0.782. The ε sweep identifies ε=4 as the recommended clinical operating point. INT8 quantization reduces model size by ~50% and Pi 4 inference latency by up to 44%, with under 0.12% AUROC loss. Critically, DP and quantization penalties are empirically independent — adding both incurs additive rather than multiplicative degradation — so practitioners can simultaneously achieve strong privacy and a compact edge footprint without compounding trade-offs.

Conclusion: This is the first system to unify federated learning, formal (ε,δ)-DP, unsupervised reconstruction-based anomaly detection, and quantized AArch64 edge deployment. The empirical independence of DP and quantization penalties is a key practical insight, meaning clinicians need not sacrifice privacy for efficiency. The recommended ε=4 operating point balances clinical utility with formal privacy guarantees, paving the way for real-world deployable privacy-preserving ECG monitoring.

Abstract: Continuous electrocardiography (ECG) monitoring could surface rhythm abnormalities before they escalate into cardiovascular events. However, a deployable system must satisfy three requirements simultaneously: legal-grade privacy (GDPR, HIPAA), real-time inference on constrained edge hardware, and detection quality under non-IID cross-hospital data.
  We design and evaluate an end-to-end federated system addressing all three for unsupervised 12-lead ECG anomaly detection on PTB-XL dataset, combining three autoencoder families (VanillaAE, ConvAE, VAE), Flower-based federated averaging (FedAvg) across ten simulated hospitals, client-side differentially private SGD (DP-SGD) with a Rényi-DP accountant, and 8-bit integer (INT8) post-training quantization with Raspberry Pi 4 benchmarking. Our main contributions are: an empirical characterization of how these mechanisms compose, practical DP-specific recommendations, and technical and security insights for a clinically sensitive setting. Federated learning matches or exceeds the centralized baseline across all architectures (ConvAE federated area under the ROC curve, AUROC, $0.782$), and an $\varepsilon$ sweep identifies $\varepsilon=4$ as the recommended clinical operating point. INT8 quantization roughly halves model size and cuts Pi 4 latency by up to $44%$ with $<0.12%$ AUROC loss. Crucially, DP and quantization penalties are empirically independent, so practitioners need not trade a strong privacy guarantee for a compact edge footprint. To our knowledge, this is the first system combining federated learning, formal $(\varepsilon,δ)$-DP, unsupervised reconstruction-based detection, and quantized AArch64 deployment.

</details>


### [109] [A Deterministic Forensic Preprocessing Framework for Heterogeneous Network Datasets: Formal Foundations, Implementation, and Empirical Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11565)
*Ravi Chaudhary,Reza Ryan,Nasim Ferdosian,Nickson M. Karie,Qian Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A deterministic framework for preprocessing heterogeneous network forensic data is proposed, using schema normalisation, temporal normalisation, and provenance tracking to ensure reproducible, verifiable canonical outputs, validated on three benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneous network evidence from diverse sources (IDS, IoT, enterprise logs) suffers from incompatible schemas and timestamp formats, impeding correlation and timeline reconstruction. Current ad hoc preprocessing lacks reproducibility and consistency verification, threatening evidence admissibility in legal contexts.

Method: The framework formalises three set-theoretic preprocessing transformations: (1) schema normalisation — mapping heterogeneous fields to a unified canonical schema; (2) temporal normalisation — converting disparate timestamp formats into a standardised temporal representation; (3) provenance tracking — recording transformation lineage for auditability. Four theorems establish determinism, information preservation, and provenance completeness. A chunk-based architecture bounds memory at O(c).

Result: Evaluation on UNSW-NB15, IoT-23, and TON_IoT datasets shows: 100% output consistency across repeated runs; complete temporal normalisation coverage over heterogeneous timestamp formats; and scalable performance handling millions to hundreds of millions of records.

Conclusion: The deterministic framework ensures reproducible, admissible forensic preprocessing, eliminating ad hoc variability and providing formal guarantees of consistency, information fidelity, and provenance traceability at scale.

Abstract: Digital forensic investigations increasingly depend on preprocessing heterogeneous network evidence from intrusion detection systems, IoT devices, and enterprise traffic logs. Incompatible schemas and timestamp formats hinder evidence correlation and timeline reconstruction, while current ad hoc approaches offer no mechanism to verify consistency across runs or analysis, creating reproducibility gaps that challenge evidence admissibility. This paper introduces a deterministic forensic preprocessing framework that converts heterogeneous network datasets into a reproducible canonical form. The framework formalises three preprocessing transformations: schema normalisation, temporal normalisation, and provenance tracking. These transformations are specified using set-theoretic definitions and supported by four theorems establishing determinism, information preservation, and provenance completeness. A chunk-based architecture provides O(c) bounded memory. Empirical evaluation across UNSW-NB15, IoT-23, and TON_IoT demonstrates 100% output consistency across repeated runs, robust temporal normalisation completeness over heterogeneous timestamp formats, and scalable performance from millions to hundreds of millions of records.

</details>


### [110] [Defense Against Prompt Inversion Attacks: An Information-Theoretic Approach for LLM Collaborative Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11592)
*Sayedeh Leila Noorbakhsh,Hossein Khalili,Nader Sehatbakhsh*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Proposes an information-theoretic defense against prompt inversion attacks in collaborative LLM inference by minimizing mutual information between intermediate activations and input prompts, using privacy adapters with low-dimensional bottlenecks, achieving up to 35% attack success reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collaborative edge-cloud LLM inference exposes sensitive user prompts to inversion attacks when transmitting intermediate activations. Existing defenses use heuristic perturbations without theoretical grounding on privacy leakage or its tradeoffs with utility and latency.

Method: An information-theoretic framework that learns privacy-preserving representations by explicitly minimizing mutual information between intermediate activations and input prompts. Privacy adapters are implemented via low-dimensional information bottlenecks, with theoretical guarantees on reconstruction error, privacy-utility tradeoffs, and token-level accuracy bounds.

Result: Extensive experiments show the method achieves superior privacy-utility-latency tradeoffs, with up to 35% reduction in attack success rate compared to existing defenses.

Conclusion: The approach provides a principled, theoretically-grounded foundation for private and efficient collaborative LLM inference, outperforming heuristic defenses across multiple settings.

Abstract: Collaborative edge-cloud inference enables resource-constrained devices to leverage large language models (LLMs) by offloading partial computation to cloud servers. However, transmitting intermediate activations exposes sensitive user prompts to prompt inversion attacks, where an adversary reconstructs the original input from shared representations. Existing defenses rely largely on heuristic perturbations or empirical tuning, offering limited theoretical understanding of privacy leakage and its interaction with utility and latency constraints. We propose an information-theoretic defense framework for prompt inversion in collaborative LLM inference. Our approach learns privacy-preserving representations by explicitly minimizing the mutual information between intermediate activations and the input prompt while maintaining task utility under computational constraints. We derive theoretical guarantees on prompt reconstruction error, characterize fundamental privacy-utility tradeoffs, and establish token-level accuracy bounds for downstream inference. We then propose a novel defense based on privacy adapters implemented via low-dimensional information bottlenecks. Extensive experiments across multiple settings demonstrate that our method achieves superior privacy-utility-latency tradeoffs compared to existing defenses (up to 35% reduction in attack success), providing a principled foundation for private and efficient collaborative LLM inference.

</details>


### [111] [Dummy Backdoor as a Defense: Removing Unknown Backdoors via Shared Internal Mechanisms for Generative LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11648)
*Kazuki Iwahana,Masaru Matsubayashi,Takuma Koyama,Toshiki Shibahara,Kenichiro Omintato,Akira Ito*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper proposes a backdoor defense method for LLMs that leverages shared internal mechanisms across different backdoors. The defender intentionally plants a known "dummy backdoor," then fine-tunes it away, which simultaneously reduces the effect of unknown backdoors due to overlapping activation patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Removing unknown backdoors in LLMs is challenging when defenders lack knowledge of the attack type or the internal mechanisms formed during backdoor training. Existing defenses often degrade utility or are ineffective against diverse attacks.

Method: The authors first demonstrate that different backdoors targeting the same task induce similar trigger-activated internal activation changes. Leveraging this, they (1) deliberately embed a dummy backdoor with a known trigger into the model, (2) fine-tune the model on dummy-triggered inputs paired with clean responses to remove the dummy backdoor. Since the dummy and unknown backdoors share internal mechanisms, removing the dummy backdoor also mitigates the unknown one.

Result: Evaluated on three backdoor attack types across multiple model families, the method substantially reduces the attack success rate of unknown backdoors while preserving model utility, outperforming representative existing defenses in both removal effectiveness and utility preservation.

Conclusion: A defender-controllable backdoor can serve as an effective proxy for mitigating unknown backdoors in generative LLMs, offering a simple yet powerful defense strategy.

Abstract: Backdoor attacks pose a serious threat to the safety and reliability of Large Language Models (LLMs), as they cause models to behave normally on clean inputs while producing attacker-specified responses when hidden triggers are present. Removing such unknown backdoors is particularly challenging when the defender does not know the backdoor attack types or the internal mechanisms formed through backdoor training. In this work, we propose a simple but effective backdoor removal method based on shared internal mechanisms across different backdoors. First, we show that different backdoors with the same task (attack objective) induce similar trigger-activated changes in the internal activations. Motivated by this observation, our method intentionally embeds a backdoor with a known trigger (\emph{dummy backdoor}) and then removes it through further fine-tuning on dummy-triggered inputs paired with clean responses. Since the dummy backdoor and the unknown backdoor can rely on shared internal mechanisms, removing the dummy backdoor also reduces the effect of the unknown backdoor. We evaluate our method on three backdoor attack types across multiple model families. Experimental results show that our method substantially reduces the attack success rate of the unknown backdoor while preserving model utility, outperforming representative existing defense methods in both backdoor removal effectiveness and utility preservation. These findings suggest that a defender-controllable backdoor can serve as a helpful proxy for mitigating unknown backdoors in generative LLMs.

</details>


### [112] [A Robust Framework for Sybil Attack Detection in Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11667)
*Md. Sadmin Tahmid Khan,Md. Saim Ahmmed Utsho,Mosarrat Jahan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A lightweight Sybil attack detection framework for VANETs that uses GPS trajectories and DBSCAN clustering to achieve significantly lower false positive rates, reduced detection time, and operates effectively in both sparse and dense regions without relying on RSUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Sybil attack detection in VANETs suffers from four key limitations: high false positive rates due to overlapping vehicle trajectories, impracticability from manual parameter calibration, ineffectiveness in sparse RSU/vehicle environments, and computational inefficiency.

Method: The proposed framework leverages GPS location data to construct distinguishable vehicle trajectories, applies DBSCAN clustering for unsupervised identification of Sybil vehicles, and eliminates dependency on RSUs and surrounding vehicle density.

Result: FPR reduced by ~68% (dense) and ~70% (sparse); FNR reduced by 67% (sparse); detection time decreased by nearly 80% (dense) and 43% (sparse); competitive detection rate compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The GPS- and DBSCAN-based approach offers a practical, lightweight, and robust solution for Sybil attack detection in VANETs, overcoming major limitations of prior work across heterogeneous deployment scenarios.

Abstract: Sybil attacks create an illusion of traffic congestion by utilizing fake identities, which undermines the reliable and safe operation of vehicular ad hoc networks (VANETs). Existing detection mechanisms struggle to effectively handle Sybil attacks as they are (i) susceptible to high false positive rates (FPR) due to the overlapping trajectories of both Sybil and legitimate vehicles, (ii) not practical for real-world deployment due to manual calibrations with ground data, (iii) ineffective for sparse distribution of roadside units (RSUs) and vehicles as they depend heavily on the presence of both, and (iv) inefficient due to computational overheads. This paper addresses these shortcomings and proposes a robust framework to tackle these issues. The proposed scheme reduces the FPR by utilizing GPS location data, enabling the construction of more accurate and distinguishable trajectories. Besides, it employs DBSCAN clustering to identify Sybil vehicles, facilitating unsupervised parameter selection. GPS data eliminates the dependency on RSUs and vehicles, making this scheme effective in both sparse and dense regions. Additionally, the proposed scheme is lightweight and consistent across vehicles with heterogeneous capacities. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed scheme reduces the FPR by approximately 68% in dense regions and 70% in sparse areas. Furthermore, it lowers the false negative rate (FNR) by 67% in the sparse region and achieves a competitive detection rate compared to the existing methods in both dense and sparse regions. Additionally, the proposed scheme decreases the detection time by almost 80% in dense regions and 43% in sparse ones.

</details>


### [113] [Runtime Skill Audit: Targeted Runtime Probing for Agent Skill Security](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11671)
*Tu Lan,Chaowei Xiao*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: RSA is a dynamic analysis method that audits LLM agent skills by observing actual agent behavior under targeted runtime conditions, achieving 90% accuracy against static baselines and remaining robust under self-evolving attacks where static detectors fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agent skills can harbor malicious behavior that only manifests under specific runtime conditions (particular user requests, local assets, persistent state, or multi-step interactions), making static vetting insufficient. A dynamic approach is needed to uncover hidden maliciousness that static analysis cannot detect.

Method: RSA (Runtime Skill Audit) profiles risk-relevant interfaces of a skill, prepares targeted execution contexts to exercise those interfaces, and assigns security labels based on trace evidence from the agent's actual runtime behavior. It is instantiated on the OpenClaw framework and evaluated against 100 skills.

Result: RSA achieves 90.0% accuracy with an 88.0% true positive rate and 8.0% false positive rate, outperforming the best static baseline by 13.0 percentage points.
