Under self-evolving attacks, static detectors collapse after 1–2 rounds, while RSA consistently detects 19–20 out of 20 malicious skills across all rounds.

Conclusion: Dynamic runtime auditing via RSA significantly improves detection of context-dependent malicious skills and demonstrates strong resilience against adversarial evolution, addressing a critical security gap in LLM agent ecosystems.

Abstract: Agent skills let LLM agents reuse instructions, resources, tools, and workflows, but they also create a new place for malicious behavior to hide. A skill may look benign in its documentation or code while becoming harmful only when it is invoked with particular user requests, local assets, persistent state, or multi-step tool interactions. This makes purely static vetting brittle. We present Runtime Skill Audit (RSA), a dynamic analysis method that audits skills by asking what the skill-mediated agent actually does under targeted runtime conditions. Instead of testing every skill with the same generic tasks, RSA profiles risk-relevant interfaces, prepares the execution context needed to exercise them, and assigns security labels from the resulting trace evidence. We instantiate RSA on OpenClaw and evaluate it on 100 skills against representative static baselines. RSA achieves 90.0\% accuracy with an 88.0\% true positive rate and an 8.0\% false positive rate, improving accuracy by 13.0 percentage points over the best static baseline. Under self-evolving attacks, static detectors collapse after one or two rounds, while RSA continues to detect 19--20 out of 20 malicious skills across rounds.

</details>


### [114] [Can Open-Source LLM Agents Replace Static Application Security Testing Tools? An Empirical Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11672)
*Derek Yohn,Luke Flancher,Mirajul Islam,Khaled Slhoub*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Evaluates three open-source LLM-based AI agents for static application security testing (SAST) against the Bandit tool; finds that GenAI agents are currently unsuitable for realistic SAST scanning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether general-purpose open-source GenAI LLM-based agents can effectively perform specialized cybersecurity tasks, specifically SAST scanning, compared to established purpose-built tools.

Method: Three Ollama-hosted open-source LLMs power a general-purpose GenAI agent. Performance is measured via precision, recall, false positive count, and a composite score derived from these metrics. Results are benchmarked against Bandit, a vetted SAST tool.

Result: Open-source GenAI LLM-based agents underperform relative to Bandit; they are not currently suitable for SAST scanning under realistic conditions.

Conclusion: The notion that modern open-source GenAI LLM-based agents can replace or match dedicated SAST tools is refuted; specialized tools remain necessary for effective static application security testing.

Abstract: This paper explores the value of agentic AI tools for cybersecurity purposes. We evaluate the efficacy of a general-purpose GenAI Large Language Model- (GenAI-) based agent when powered by three different Ollama-hosted general-purpose open source models. We assess each agent's performance using precision, recall, false positive count, and a calculated composite score based upon the interplay of the captured metrics, against the baseline performance of an existing, vetted Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tool, Bandit. Our findings refute the notion that a modern open-source GenAI LLM-based agent is currently suitable for the specialized task of SAST scanning under realistic conditions.

</details>


### [115] [Grammar-Constrained Decoding Can Jailbreak LLMs into Generating Malicious Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11817)
*Yitong Zhang,Shiteng Lu,Jia Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Grammar-Constrained Decoding (GCD), designed to improve code reliability, can be exploited as a jailbreak attack surface. The authors introduce CodeSpear, an attack that uses benign code grammar constraints to induce LLMs into generating malicious code, and CodeShield, a defense that aligns models to produce harmless "honeypot code" under GCD while preserving safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for code generation, and GCD is widely adopted to enforce syntactic validity. However, no prior work has examined whether GCD—a reliability mechanism—introduces new security vulnerabilities. The authors aim to expose this overlooked attack surface and propose a mitigation.

Method: The attack, CodeSpear, exploits GCD by applying a benign code grammar constraint to jailbreak LLMs into generating malicious code. The defense, CodeShield, aligns models in the code modality by training them to generate honeypot code under GCD—code that is semantically harmless (doesn't fulfill the malicious request) and structurally diverse (hard to suppress via grammar tightening), while preserving natural-language refusals when available.

Result: Experiments on 10 popular LLMs across 4 benchmarks show that CodeSpear outperforms representative jailbreak baselines, increasing the attack success rate by more than 30 percentage points on average. CodeShield restores safety under CodeSpear while preserving benign utility.

Conclusion: GCD introduces a fundamental security risk that can be exploited for jailbreaking. The community should pay greater attention to the security implications of reliability-oriented techniques like GCD. CodeShield offers a viable defense by producing harmless, diverse synthetic code under constrained decoding.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for code generation, raising concerns that they may be misused to produce malicious code. Meanwhile, Grammar-Constrained Decoding (GCD) has been widely adopted to improve the reliability of LLM-generated code by enforcing syntactic validity. In this paper, we reveal a counterintuitive risk: this reliability-oriented technique can itself become an attack surface. We uncover a new jailbreak attack, termed CodeSpear, that exploits GCD to induce LLMs into generating malicious code. Our experiments show that simply applying a benign code grammar constraint can effectively jailbreak LLMs.
  To address this vulnerability, we propose CodeShield, a safety alignment approach that robustly preserves safe behavior even under attacker-controlled grammar constraints. CodeShield aligns the model in the code modality by teaching it to generate honeypot code under GCD. Such code is semantically harmless, so it does not implement the malicious request, and structurally diverse, so it is difficult to suppress through grammar tightening. At the same time, CodeShield still preserves natural-language refusals when natural language is available. Experiments on 10 popular LLMs across 4 benchmarks show that CodeSpear outperforms representative jailbreak baselines and increases the attack success rate by more than 30 percentage points on average. CodeShield also restores safety under CodeSpear while preserving benign utility. Our findings reveal a fundamental risk of GCD and call for greater attention to its potential security implications.

</details>


### [116] [T2S: A Rehearsal-Based Approach for Extraction-Resistant Model Watermarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11698)
*Jian-Ping Mei,Weibin Zhang,Ao Yao,Tiantian Zhu,Jie Xiao*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper proposes a rehearsal-based watermark embedding framework that simulates model extraction during training to enhance watermark robustness. By using the loss of a simulated stolen model on a trigger set as a training signal, the method encourages watermark transferability, making it more likely to persist in extracted surrogate models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model watermarks are vulnerable to model extraction attacks, where adversaries train surrogate models using prediction outputs to steal functionality. Existing watermarking methods lack sufficient robustness against such extraction attacks, necessitating a watermarking approach that can survive the extraction process.

Method: A rehearsal-based framework that simulates the model extraction process during watermark embedding. It uses the loss of a simulated stolen model on a trigger set as a training signal to fine-tune watermark knowledge in the target model, encouraging embedding in a manner that promotes transferability and persistence in stolen models.

Result: Comprehensive experiments under diverse settings show the proposed method significantly improves watermark robustness against both model extraction attacks and subsequent watermark removal attacks.

Conclusion: The rehearsal-based watermark embedding framework effectively enhances robustness against model extraction attacks by promoting watermark transferability, demonstrating significant improvements over existing approaches.

Abstract: Model watermarking safeguards AI model intellectual property by embedding distinctive knowledge that induces unique behavioral signatures. The primary technical challenge lies in ensuring watermark robustness against various post-processing attacks on the watermarked model. Model extraction attacks emerge as the most severe threat, where adversaries exploit prediction outputs to train surrogate models that illegally replicate the original model's functionality. In this work, we propose a rehearsal-based watermark embedding framework to enhance the robustness of model watermarks against model extraction attacks. By simulating the extraction process, our method leverages the loss of a \textit{simulated stolen model} on a trigger set as a training signal to fine-tune the watermark knowledge within the target model. This fine-tuning step encourages the watermark to be embedded in a way that boosts transferability, thereby increasing its chances of persisting and remaining detectable in stolen models. Comprehensive experiments conducted under diverse settings demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves the robustness of model watermarks against both model extraction and subsequent watermark removal attacks.

</details>


### [117] [A VPN-as-a-Service Tailored Enabler for Computing-constrained Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11729)
*Carolina Fernández-Martínez,César Cajas Parra,Shuaib Siddiqui*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents a cloud-native VPN-as-a-Service (VPNaaS) design aligned with Zero Trust principles, featuring per-tenant isolated tunnels, IAM integration, and adaptable cryptographic configurations (RSA/EC) for resource-constrained environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industry adoption of Zero Trust architectures for cloud-native environments demands stricter security through fine-grained identity management, isolation, and least-privilege networking. Existing VPN solutions lack the flexibility to provide per-tenant, on-the-fly isolated tunnels that integrate seamlessly with IAM tools while adapting to resource-constrained or entropy-limited environments.

Method: The authors designed and evaluated a cloud-native VPN-as-a-Service architecture that: (i) orchestrates on-demand, per-tenant isolated tunnels; (ii) integrates with standard Identity and Access Management (IAM) tools; and (iii) supports configurable cryptographic algorithms — RSA or Elliptic Curve (EC) — with selectable key parameters to balance security strength against computational and entropy constraints.

Result: The proposed VPNaaS solution successfully demonstrates on-the-fly provisioning of isolated per-tenant tunnels, seamless IAM integration for Zero Trust enforcement, and adaptable cryptographic selection (RSA vs. EC) enabling deployment in both standard and resource-constrained environments.

Conclusion: A customizable, cloud-native VPNaaS aligned with Zero Trust architectural tenets is feasible and effective for experimental infrastructures, offering per-tenant isolation, IAM integration, and cryptographic flexibility suitable for constrained environments.

Abstract: Industry has embraced Zero Trust (ZT) architectural tenets and implementations for cloud-native environments, following stricter security requirements to both internal and external tenants. Among others, these approaches combine fine-grained identity management and monitoring for both inventorying and better analysing the devices' security posture for overall protection, along with strict separation of concerns and isolation to enforce minimal privilege. Networking-wise, ZT approaches rely as well on isolation and least privilege; enacted by separate, secure tunnels per tenant connecting to a given infrastructure. Such implementations can also be applied to the connectivity within and towards experimental infrastructures. In this sense, this work contributes the design and evaluation of a cloud-native VPN-as-a-Service (VPNaaS) that can be (i) easily orchestrated to deploy on-the-fly, separate tunnels per each tenant remotely connecting to the infrastructure; (ii) integrated with common Identity and Access Management (IAM) tools, key to ZT deployments; and (iii) adapt to computing- or entropy- constrained environments. This solution is customisable and allows, among others, to select from RSA or Elliptic Curves (EC) as key generation algorithm and their parameters to achieve more secure keys and adapt to resource-constrained environments.

</details>


### [118] [MHOT: Height-Optimized Authenticated Data Structure for Blockchain State Commitment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11736)
*Sipeng Xie,Qianhong Wu,Minghang Li,Qiyuan Gao,Bo Qin,Qin Wang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Mhot is a height-optimal authenticated data structure for blockchain state commitment that uses discriminative-bit indexing and hierarchical proofs to achieve minimal tree height, resist Nurgle attacks, and deliver up to 9x higher write throughput than Ethereum's MPT without trusted setup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ethereum's Merkle Patricia Trie (MPT) dominates block processing time (78% spent on state root computation), suffers from unbounded tree-height growth, and is vulnerable to Nurgle attacks where adversaries exploit hash collisions to inflate path depth at negligible cost. Existing defenses (higher fanout, vector commitments) introduce proof-size explosion or require trusted setup/expensive verification.

Method: Mhot replaces MPT's fixed-prefix indexing with discriminative-bit indexing — keys are indexed by bits that actually distinguish them, achieving adaptive span with linear (rather than exponential) fanout coupling and provably minimal height. To prevent large fanout from inflating proof sizes, the authors introduce hierarchical proofs: a two-layer Merkle construction that reduces per-node proof overhead from O(k) to O(log k). The scheme requires no trusted setup and uses standard hash-based verification.

Result: On Ethereum mainnet workloads, Mhot achieves up to 9x higher write throughput, 4x lower write amplification, and 2x smaller proofs than MPT. Under Nurgle attacks with an entire block's gas budget consumed by the adversary, Mhot maintains a 0% attack success rate versus 99.97% for MPT.

Conclusion: Height optimality — not new cryptographic primitives — is the key abstraction for building scalable, attack-resilient blockchain state commitment. Mhot demonstrates that discriminative-bit indexing combined with hierarchical proofs solves the fundamental height-fanout tension without sacrificing decentralization (no trusted setup) or verification efficiency.

Abstract: State root computation dominates (78%) blockchain block processing time. Ethereum's canonical authenticated data structure, i.e., Merkle Patricia Trie (MPT), suffers from severe tree-height growth and is vulnerable to \textit{Nurgle attacks} (SP'24), where adversaries inflate path depth via hash collisions and degrade system performance at negligible cost. Existing defenses increase node fanout (span) to bound tree height, but higher span inflates proof size exponentially. Prior work mitigates this trade-off using vector commitments, at the cost of trusted setup or expensive verification.
  We present \textsc{Mhot}, a height-optimal authenticated data structure for blockchain state commitment that preserves standard hash-based verification without trusted setup. Unlike MPT's fixed-prefix indexing, which couples span and fanout exponentially, \textsc{Mhot} indexes by discriminative bits that actually distinguish keys, achieving adaptive span with linear fanout coupling and provably minimal height. To prevent high fanout from inflating proofs, we introduce hierarchical proofs, a two-layer Merkle construction that reduces per-node proof overhead from O(k) to O(log k).
  On Ethereum mainnet workloads, \textsc{Mhot} achieves up to 9X higher write throughput, 4X lower write amplification, and 2X smaller proofs than MPT. Under Nurgle attacks, even when the adversary consumes an entire block's gas budget, \textsc{Mhot} maintains a 0% attack success rate (v.s., 99.97% for MPT). Our results, somewhat surprisingly, show that height optimality (not new crypto primitives!) is the key abstraction for scalable and attack-resilient blockchain state commitment.

</details>


### [119] [SwarmSense-DNN: A Trustworthy and Decentralized Neural Framework for Proactive Anomaly Defense in Consumer IoT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11803)
*Jing Yang,Vijay Govindarajan,Saad Arif,Xu Xu,Mohamed Kallel,Zaffar Ahmed Shaikh,Zhe Liu,Chunhong Yuan,Lip Yee Por*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SwarmSense-DNN is a decentralized anomaly detection framework for consumer IoT that combines swarm intelligence with deep neural networks and hierarchical federated learning. It achieves 95.44% detection accuracy across five benchmarks while cutting communication overhead by 67%, with built-in differential privacy and fault tolerance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Consumer IoT device proliferation demands real-time, privacy-preserving, and scalable anomaly detection against AI-driven cyber threats. Centralized approaches suffer from communication bottlenecks, single points of failure, and privacy vulnerabilities when handling distributed consumer data.

Method: SwarmSense-DNN employs autonomous agents integrated with deep neural networks to form a self-organizing defense system. It uses hierarchical federated learning with graph neural networks and attention mechanisms to capture both local and global anomaly patterns, while incorporating differential privacy safeguards.

Result: The framework achieves 95.44% average detection accuracy across five benchmark datasets, reduces communication overhead by 67%, and demonstrates robust resilience against adversarial threats along with strong fault tolerance under node failures and AI-enabled attacks.

Conclusion: SwarmSense-DNN provides an effective decentralized alternative to centralized anomaly detection for IoT, successfully balancing high detection accuracy with communication efficiency, privacy protection, and resilience against both node failures and adversarial AI attacks.

Abstract: The rapid growth of consumer IoT devices has introduced unprecedented challenges in trustworthy anomaly detection against AI-enabled cyber threats, requiring real-time, privacy-preserving, and scalable defense mechanisms. Traditional centralized strategies face critical limitations, including communication bottlenecks, single points of failure, and privacy vulnerabilities when processing distributed consumer data. We propose SwarmSense-DNN, a novel decentralized neural framework employing swarm intelligence for secure, cooperative anomaly detection across distributed IoT environments. The framework integrates autonomous agents with deep neural networks to form a self-organizing defense system that detects evolving anomalies without centralized coordination. It utilizes hierarchical federated learning with graph neural networks and attention mechanisms to capture local and global anomaly behaviors while ensuring data privacy. Extensive experiments demonstrate SwarmSense-DNN's superior performance: it achieves 95.44% average detection accuracy across five benchmark datasets while reducing communication overhead by 67%. The framework maintains robust resilience against adversarial threats through differential privacy safeguards and demonstrates strong fault tolerance under node failures and AI-enabled attacks.

</details>


### [120] [Jaguar: Fast Private CNN Inference with Power-of-Two Homomorphic Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11827)
*Yewon Jeong,Nayoung Jung,Hyeri Roh,Woo-Seok Choi*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Jaguar is a hybrid HE/2PC private CNN inference system that uses power-of-two ciphertext rings to enable efficient SPA-Conv (coefficient-domain convolution) and exact ciphertext-side truncation, eliminating NTT in convolution and the post-ReLU truncation protocol. It achieves 2–3.7× lower latency and 1.16–1.76× lower communication than prior systems like Cheetah and Rhombus on ImageNet-scale models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Private CNN inference using hybrid homomorphic encryption (HE) and two-party computation (2PC) suffers from two bottlenecks: (1) prime-modulus homomorphic arithmetic in convolution layers, which relies on expensive NTT-based polynomial multiplication, and (2) a precision flow that runs ReLU at doubled bitwidth followed by a costly separate truncation protocol. These inefficiencies limit practical deployment of private inference at scale.

Method: Jaguar adopts a single unifying design choice: a power-of-two ciphertext ring. This enables two key innovations: (1) SPA-Conv, a coefficient-domain convolution kernel that replaces NTT-centric polynomial multiplication with scalar-polynomial accumulation, avoiding expensive NTT operations in the convolution pipeline; and (2) exact ciphertext-side truncation via local right shifts, allowing ReLU to operate directly at the target fixed-point precision without a post-ReLU truncation protocol. For the client-side decryption where NTT is still useful, Jaguar recovers NTT through an auxiliary NTT prime, preserving O(N log N) decryption while maintaining the power-of-two protocol substrate.

Result: On ImageNet-scale benchmarks with AVX disabled, Jaguar achieves 2.07–3.72× lower end-to-end latency compared to Cheetah and 2.16–3.36× lower latency compared to Rhombus across ResNet-18, ResNet-50, and MobileNetV2. Communication is reduced by 1.16–1.76× relative to Cheetah.

Conclusion: Jaguar demonstrates that a power-of-two ciphertext ring is a highly effective design choice for hybrid HE/2PC private CNN inference, simultaneously addressing the two major bottlenecks of convolution arithmetic and ReLU precision flow. The resulting system significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both latency and communication.

Abstract: Hybrid HE/2PC private CNN inference remains bottlenecked by prime-modulus homomorphic arithmetic in convolution and by a precision flow that runs ReLU at doubled bitwidth before invoking a separate truncation protocol. We present Jaguar, a system built on a single design choice--a power-of-two ciphertext ring--that addresses both. The choice enables SPA-Conv, a coefficient-domain convolution kernel that replaces NTT-centric polynomial multiplication with scalar-polynomial accumulation, and an exact ciphertext-side truncation by local right shifts that lets ReLU run directly at the target fixed-point precision and eliminates the post-ReLU truncation protocol. Where NTT remains genuinely useful--at the client, for the single polynomial multiplication during decryption--we recover it through an auxiliary NTT prime, preserving the power-of-two protocol substrate while keeping decryption O(N log N). On ImageNet-scale ResNet-18, ResNet-50, and MobileNetV2 with AVX disabled, Jaguar achieves 2.07-3.72x lower end-to-end latency than Cheetah and 2.16-3.36x lower than Rhombus, with 1.16-1.76x lower communication than Cheetah.

</details>


### [121] [Systematic Cybersecurity Risk Analysis of European Rail Traffic Management System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11839)
*Kacper Darowski,Sebastian N. Peters,Lukas Lautenschlager*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A systematic security analysis of ERTMS reveals that legacy components (EuroBalises, GSM-R) introduce persistent vulnerabilities across all deployment levels. Transitioning to ETCS Level 2 is identified as the single most impactful measure for improving cybersecurity, but a fundamental shift toward security-by-design and railway-centric risk quantification methods is still needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ERTMS is a critical EU-wide train management standard that enables autonomous driving, yet cybersecurity was historically neglected. No prior work systematically identifies which threats are most significant across ERTMS deployments, leaving a gap in understanding where to prioritize mitigations.

Method: The paper systematically models ERTMS components and conducts a security analysis by mapping threats from underlying technologies onto these components. The analysis spans minimal implementations, deployments with optional safety measures, and future evolutions including FRMCS.

Result: Legacy standards (EuroBalises, GSM-R) introduce vulnerabilities persisting across all deployment scenarios. ETCS Level 2 adoption is the most significant cybersecurity advancement. Even future FRMCS-based evolutions inherit weaknesses, indicating systemic rather than version-specific problems.

Conclusion: ERTMS requires a security-oriented paradigm shift to ensure availability and safe operation. The methodology proved feasible for identifying residual weaknesses, but future work must develop railway-specific risk quantification and evaluation adaptations.

Abstract: European Rail Traffic Management System (ERTMS) is a widely adopted standard unifying train management in the EU. While the standard allows for use cases like fully autonomous driving, cybersecurity has been an afterthought. Risk analysis enables the systematic assessment and prioritization of threats and mitigations. To date, it remains unclear which threats are most significant in ERTMS. This study systematically models components of ERTMS and analyzes their security in light of threats identified in the underlying technologies. The results suggest a concerning state of ERTMS, despite its critical role in railway safety. The use of legacy standards like EuroBalises and GSM-Railway (GSM-R) introduces vulnerabilities that persist across minimal ERTMS implementations, deployments incorporating various optional safety measures, and prospective future evolutions of the system, e.g., adopting Future Railway Mobile Communication System (FRMCS). Fully transitioning to European Train Control System (ETCS) level 2 was identified as the most significant measure for advancing ERTMS cybersecurity. The results indicate that a shift of ERTMS toward security is required to ensure availability and safe operation. While the chosen methodology proved its feasibility and shows remaining weaknesses of ERTMS, future work is needed to develop railway-centric adaptations to improve the quantification and evaluation of the computed risks.

</details>


### [122] [WarpGuard: Protected-Site Control-Flow Integrity for CUDA SASS Binaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11871)
*Igor Santos-Grueiro*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: WarpGuard is the first control-flow integrity (CFI) system for CUDA device binaries that operates on executed NVIDIA SASS (post-compilation assembly), enforcing protected-site CFI on backward edges (returns) and forward edges (indirect calls/branches). It classifies 51,621 SASS control-flow sites across 77 CUDA artifacts, performs 52.2 million dynamic checks, and demonstrates effective detection and fail-closed enforcement against GPU control-flow corruption attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CUDA exploitation research demonstrates that GPU memory bugs can corrupt control-flow state (return addresses, function pointers, dispatch tables, branch targets) consumed by subsequent kernels. Current source-level or PTX-level CFI policies fail to capture the actual security boundary, which lies at the executed NVIDIA SASS level after compilation passes (inlining, register allocation, predication, SIMT execution, etc.). No prior system provides CFI protection at the SASS binary level for CUDA device code.

Method: WarpGuard operates directly on executed NVIDIA SASS binaries. It first recovers control-flow sites (instructions/sequences that consume control-flow state) from SASS. For backward edges, it authenticates continuation state for instrumented returns. For forward edges, it validates recoverable call/branch targets per site. Sites are categorized as either protected (checked before release, fail-closed on violation) or excluded (fixed-edge, unsupported, profile-excluded, fallback, no-surface). The system enforces policy through dynamic instrumentation, with a detect-only mode and a fail-closed enforcement mode that blocks invalid transfers before release.

Result: Across 77 CUDA artifacts, WarpGuard classifies 51,621 SASS control-flow sites, including 1,343 return sites and 154 supported forward target-set entries, recording 52.2 million dynamic checks. In representative backward-edge and forward-edge corruption attacks, native execution reaches attacker-chosen behavior; detect-only mode correctly records the expected violation; and enforcement mode fails closed before the invalid protected transfer is released. Public-code analysis confirms that identical SASS consumption patterns exist in real CUDA systems (runtime dispatch tables, cuFFT callbacks, generated callable tables, uploaded device-function pointers).

Conclusion: WarpGuard demonstrates that auditable protected-site CFI for CUDA SASS is feasible and effective, providing a practical security boundary that aligns with the actual post-compilation execution level. It cleanly separates dynamic-instrumentation enforcement from callback-free SASS timing considerations and patch-cache feasibility, establishing a foundation for GPU device-binary CFI in deployed CUDA environments.

Abstract: Recent CUDA exploitation work shows that GPU memory bugs can escalate into device-side control-flow corruption, as kernels later consume corrupted return continuations, function pointers, dispatch-table entries, or branch targets. For deployed CUDA binaries, the relevant security boundary is executed NVIDIA SASS, after PTX lowering, inlining, ABI decisions, register allocation, spills, predication, and SIMT execution; source- or PTX-level policies do not capture this boundary.
  We present WarpGuard, to our knowledge the first protected-site CFI system for CUDA device binaries operating on executed SASS. WarpGuard enforces at protected sites: recovered SASS instructions or sequences that consume control-flow state, provide sufficient binary evidence to derive policy, are checked before release, and fail closed on violation. It authenticates backward-edge continuation state for instrumented returns, validates recoverable forward targets per site, and reports fixed-edge, unsupported, profile-excluded, fallback, and no-surface outcomes outside the protected denominator.
  On 77 CUDA artifacts, WarpGuard classifies 51,621 SASS control-flow sites, including 1,343 returns and 154 supported forward target-set entries, and records 52.2 million dynamic checks. In representative backward- and forward-edge corruption attacks, native execution reaches attacker-selected behavior, detect-only mode records the expected violation, and enforcement fails closed before releasing the invalid protected transfer. Public-code evidence shows that the same SASS consumption patterns occur in real CUDA systems, including runtime dispatch tables, cuFFT callbacks, generated callable tables, and uploaded device-function pointers. WarpGuard delivers auditable protected-site CFI for CUDA SASS and separates dynamic-instrumentation enforcement from callback-free SASS timing and patch-cache feasibility.

</details>


### [123] [Gerrymandering the Warp: Non-Control-Data Attacks on CUDA Collective Decision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11878)
*Igor Santos-Grueiro*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper identifies "Collective Semantic Corruption" (CSC), a class of non-control-data attacks on CUDA collective operations where corrupted participation metadata (masks, predicates, source lanes, group labels, epochs) causes legitimate primitives to authorize decisions over the wrong set of participants. The authors model this with a participation-authority contract and introduce "Collective Integrity Contracts" (CIC), a wrapper discipline that binds metadata before collective use, preserving trusted-reference integrity across all tested instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CUDA collective operations (votes, reductions, shuffles, barriers) sit on security-critical decision paths where correctness depends not only on computed values but on participation metadata — which lanes contribute, which lane speaks for the group, and which validated state reaches commit. Existing defenses focus on control-flow and value integrity, leaving this participation metadata as an unprotected attack surface. The authors aim to formally characterize and defend against attacks that corrupt this decision-making non-control data.

Method: The authors define CSC as a non-control-data attack family targeting participation metadata in CUDA collectives. They model the problem with a site-local participation-authority contract spanning four dimensions: membership, contribution, role, and temporal state. They evaluate CSC across NVIDIA CUDA collective primitives using trigger channels, workload-style kernels, reduced idiom bridges, and admission-guard harnesses. They then propose CIC, a wrapper discipline that derives, recomputes, checks, or freezes participation metadata before authorization. The approach is validated through a contract-conformance suite with 102 baseline and 13 synchronization-sensitive instances.

Result: In the contract-conformance suite, corrupted participation metadata caused trusted-reference mismatch in 102/102 instances across four authority dimensions. Hardened CIC variants preserved the trusted reference in 102/102 instances. An additional 13 synchronization-sensitive instances were reported separately, all similarly protected by the CIC approach.

Conclusion: For CUDA collective operations that serve as security decision points, integrity depends on both computed values and participation metadata. The CIC wrapper discipline effectively binds this metadata before collective use, ensuring that collectives authorize decisions over the correct membership, contribution, role, and temporal state, even under adversarial corruption attempts.

Abstract: CUDA collective operations often sit on security decision paths: votes accept batches, reductions aggregate evidence, shuffles select representatives, and barriers order checked state before use. Such decisions depend not only on computed values, but also on which lanes are represented, what evidence they contribute, which lane speaks for the group, and which checked state reaches commit. We identify this participation metadata as decision-making non-control data.
  We define Collective Semantic Corruption (CSC), a non-control-data attack family in which range-valid masks, predicates, source lanes, descriptors, group labels, or epochs cause a CUDA-conforming collective to authorize a decision over the wrong membership, contribution, role, or validation-to-use state. The kernel reaches the intended collective site and executes the expected primitive; the primitive represents the wrong authority set.
  We model CSC with a site-local participation-authority contract. A protected collective derives, recomputes, checks, or freezes membership, contribution, role, and temporal state before authorization. We evaluate CSC across NVIDIA CUDA collective primitives, trigger channels, compact workload-style kernels, reduced idiom bridges, and admission-guard harnesses. In a CUDA-defined contract-conformance suite spanning the four authority dimensions, corrupted participation metadata causes a trusted-reference mismatch in 102/102 instances, while hardened variants preserve that reference in 102/102. We report 13 synchronization-sensitive instances separately. We then introduce Collective Integrity Contracts (CIC), a wrapper discipline that binds participation metadata before collective use. For CUDA collective decisions, security depends on both the values computed and the participants represented.

</details>


### [124] [Quadratic APN Functions in Dimension 8 via Gröbner Basis Search in a Self-Equivalence Subspace](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11967)
*Oleksandr Kuznetsov*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A computational search within a 40-dimensional structured subspace of 8-bit quadratic APN functions discovered four new CCZ-equivalence classes (500 functions) missed by existing databases, using a hybrid random-sampling and Gröbner basis approach. The gateway phenomenon enabling discovery is specific to the self-equivalence structure of the search space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite a 2025 database containing over 3.7 million quadratic APN functions in dimension 8, the authors revisit a structured 40-dimensional subspace (functions commuting with an order-5 linear automorphism) previously reported empty of APN functions, aiming to discover previously unknown CCZ-classes by employing a more sophisticated two-stage search methodology.

Method: The search operates in two stages: (1) random sampling using an explicit RREF parameterization yielding ~600 APN-positive evaluations per core-hour across the 40-dimensional subspace; (2) Gröbner basis computation (Magma) to exhaustively enumerate all APN functions within a 24-dimensional hyperplane centered on each candidate, taking ~10 minutes per hyperplane. CCZ-equivalence classification uses the ortho-derivative invariant, which is complete for quadratic APN functions.

Result: From 428 hyperplane computations (0.65% of 65,536 total), 566 quadratic APN functions were found forming six CCZ-classes. Four classes (500 functions) are new — absent from both the 2025 database and the pre-2020 compilation. Two classes (66 functions) match Gold functions x³ and x⁹, validating the pipeline. Discovery relied critically on Gröbner basis enumeration within Gold-centered slices; 552 control experiments (532 database-function centers, 20 random centers) yielded no APN neighbors, confirming the gateway phenomenon is tied to the self-equivalence structure.

Conclusion: The structured subspace, though previously dismissed as APN-free, harbors new CCZ-classes accessible only through a hybrid search strategy where Gröbner basis enumeration from Gold-function slice centers acts as a gateway. The ortho-derivative invariant provides rigorous CCZ-inequivalence proofs, and the gateway phenomenon appears intrinsically linked to the self-equivalence structure of the search space rather than being a general property of quadratic APN functions.

Abstract: We describe a computational search for quadratic APN (Almost Perfect Nonlinear) functions in dimension 8 within a structured self-equivalence subspace. The search space is a 40-dimensional binary linear subspace consisting of all functions commuting with a linear automorphism of order 5 (class 22 in the taxonomy of Beierle, Brinkmann, and Leander, 2021), previously reported to contain no APN functions. Our approach combines random sampling via an explicit RREF parameterization (approximately 600 fresh APN-positive evaluations per core-hour) with Gröbner basis computation in Magma to enumerate all APN functions in a 24-dimensional hyperplane through each center (approximately 10 minutes per hyperplane). From 428 hyperplane computations, covering 0.65% of all 65,536 hyperplanes, we obtained 566 quadratic APN functions forming six CCZ-equivalence classes under the ortho-derivative invariant. Four classes, comprising 500 functions, match no entry in the 2025 database of 3,775,599 quadratic APN functions or in the pre-2020 compilation of 12,921 instances. Two classes (66 functions) are CCZ-equivalent to the Gold functions x^3 and x^9, confirming the correctness of the search pipeline. A membership analysis shows that the three new classes (B, C, D) lie entirely outside the original subspace and occur only in Gold-centered slices, demonstrating the essential role of the Gröbner basis stage. In 532 experiments using database functions as slice centers and 20 experiments with random centers, no APN neighbors were found, indicating that the gateway phenomenon is specific to the self-equivalence structure of the search space. Since the ortho-derivative invariant is a complete CCZ-invariant for quadratic APN functions, the absence of matching signatures provides a rigorous proof of CCZ-inequivalence.

</details>


### [125] [InjectV: Modeling Fault Injection Attacks in RISC-V Simulation Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12011)
*Niccolò Lentini,Giorgio Fardo,Stefano Di Carlo,Alessandro Savino*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: InjectV is a gem5-based fault injection framework for RISC-V that enables precise, guided fault injection at security-critical execution points, achieving a 95.8% time-saving over traditional fault injection approaches when evaluated on the FISSC security benchmark suite.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating hardware resilience against Fault Injection Attacks (FIAs) is challenging due to the high cost, complexity, and limited availability of physical fault experiments, especially during pre-silicon development. There is a need for simulation-based, white-box vulnerability assessment tools that are developer-friendly and systematic.

Method: The authors propose InjectV, an architectural-level fault injection framework built on the gem5 simulator targeting RISC-V platforms. InjectV enables guided fault injection at security-critical execution points (control-flow decisions, counters, comparisons) and supports transient fault attacks in registers and memory.

Result: Experimental evaluation on the FISSC benchmark suite, including hardened variants of VerifyPIN, demonstrates InjectV effectively identifies fault-injection points with a 95.8% time-saving advantage over traditional fault injection approaches.

Conclusion: InjectV provides an efficient, simulation-based methodology for pre-silicon fault injection vulnerability assessment on RISC-V architectures, significantly reducing the time required compared to conventional techniques while maintaining effectiveness in identifying security-critical fault points.

Abstract: Fault Injection Attacks (FIAs) are a significant threat to hardware security, capable of compromising systems by inducing malicious faults in computation or storage. Evaluating resilience against such attacks is challenging due to the high cost, complexity, and limited availability of physical fault experiments, particularly during pre-silicon development. Architectural-level simulation offers a developer-oriented, white-box perspective for systematic vulnerability assessment. This paper introduces InjectV, a fault injection attack framework for RISC-V platforms built on the gem5 simulator. InjectV enables precise, guided fault injection at security-critical execution points, such as control-flow decisions, counters, and comparisons, allowing systematic exploration of attack vectors. It currently supports transient fault attacks in registers and memory, broadening its ability to simulate diverse attack scenarios. Experimental results on security benchmarks from the FISSC suite, including hardened variants of the VerifyPIN application, demonstrate InjectV's ability to effectively identify fault-injection points, achieving a 95.8% time-saving advantage over traditional fault injection approaches.

</details>


### [126] [Categorical Robustness Assessment for Machine Learning based Network Intrusion Detection Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12075)
*Mayank Raj,Nathaniel D. Bastian,Lance Fiondella,Gokhan Kul*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper evaluates the adversarial robustness of three ML architectures (1D-CNN, LSTM, Random Forest) for network intrusion detection under FGSM and PGD attacks across perturbation budgets ε=0.01–0.1. Random Forest achieved near-perfect baseline accuracy (99.98%) but catastrophically collapsed under minimal perturbations (−73pp), while CNN retained 95.5% accuracy at ε=0.01 and degraded gracefully, challenging the assumption that higher baseline accuracy implies better adversarial resilience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on adversarial attacks against NIDS has been conducted in isolated settings, lacking systematic cross-architecture comparisons and class/category-based evaluations under controlled conditions. This leaves practitioners without evidence-based guidance on which model architectures to deploy in adversarial environments.

Method: Three architectures (1D-CNN, LSTM, Random Forest) were tested on the ACI-IoT-2023 dataset (1.2M+ samples, 12 attack types) using FGSM and PGD gradient-based adversarial attacks in normalized feature space, with perturbation budgets ranging from ε=0.01 to ε=0.1.

Result: Random Forest: 99.98% baseline accuracy, catastrophic drop of 73 percentage points at ε=0.01. CNN: retained 95.5% accuracy at ε=0.01 with graceful degradation as ε increased. LSTM: intermediate robustness. High baseline accuracy proved non-predictive of adversarial resilience.

Conclusion: High baseline accuracy is meaningless if the model collapses under minimal adversarial pressure. CNN-based architectures are recommended for adversarial NIDS deployment, with scenario-specific guidance provided.

Abstract: Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) heavily utlize Machine Learning (ML) but ML models can be manipulated via adversarial attacks. These attacks add carefully crafted perturbations to network traffic data that leads to misclassifications. While prior work has demonstrated adversarial vulnerabilities in isolated settings, systematic cross-architecture as well as class and category of attack based comparisons under controlled attack conditions remain limited, leaving practitioners without clear guidance on which models to deploy in adversarial environments. This paper asks a simple question: what type of classifier architectures actually hold up when attackers try to manipulate the systems? We put three popular architectures through their paces: a 1D Convolutional Neural Network, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, and a Random Forest (RF) ensemble. Using the ACI-IoT-2023 dataset (over 1.2 million samples spanning 12 attack types), we subject each model with FGSM and PGD adversarial attacks, which apply gradient-based perturbations in normalized feature space consistent with established adversarial ML evaluation protocols, at perturbation budgets ranging from $ε=0.01$ to $ε=0.1$. Surprisingly, Random Forest achieved near-perfect baseline accuracy (99.98\%), yet collapsed catastrophically under attack, dropping 73 percentage points at the smallest perturbation we tested. CNN, on the other hand, retained 95.5\% accuracy at $ε=0.01$ and degraded gracefully as perturbations increased. LSTM fell somewhere in between. These findings flip the conventional wisdom where high baseline accuracy means nothing if a model shatters at the first sign of adversarial pressure. For practitioners deploying intrusion detection in adversarial environments, we recommend CNN-based architectures and provide scenario-specific deployment guidance.

</details>


### [127] [Bridging the Smart City Cybersecurity Data Gap Through AI-Driven Synthetic Dataset Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12225)
*Stephanie Polczynski,John D. Hastings,Varghese Vaidyan,Kyle Korman*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Proposes an AI-based synthetic data generation framework to create high-fidelity cybersecurity datasets for smart city research, addressing the scarcity of realistic, privacy-compliant, and attack-rich real-world data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Smart city cyber-physical systems face complex cybersecurity challenges, yet real-world datasets are often incomplete, privacy-sensitive, inaccessible, or lack sufficient malicious activity — hindering the development and validation of security tools.

Method: An AI-based synthetic data generation (SDG) framework employing generative AI models to produce synthetic datasets that replicate realistic device behaviors, network interactions, and cyber-attack scenarios. Evaluation covers protocol conformity, statistical similarity to originals, and utility in security tools.

Result: The paper is prospective in nature; it proposes the framework and evaluation metrics, with results expected to demonstrate high-fidelity synthetic data suitable for threat modeling and defensive technique evaluation.

Conclusion: The proposed SDG framework and evaluation metrics are expected to advance smart city cybersecurity by enabling more effective threat modeling and comprehensive defensive evaluations, thereby better protecting critical infrastructures.

Abstract: Smart cities rely on interconnected cyber-physical systems that integrate sensors, IoT devices, cloud platforms, and AI-driven services and decision-making. While these systems enhance city services, they also introduce complex cybersecurity challenges due to their large attack surfaces, heterogeneous data flows, and evolving threat vectors. Developing and validating cybersecurity tools for smart cities requires high-quality datasets that accurately represent real operational conditions. However, real-world datasets are often incomplete, contain privacy-sensitive data, are difficult to access, or lack sufficient malicious activity to support tool development. This research addresses this critical gap by proposing an AI-based synthetic data generation (SDG) framework designed specifically for smart city cybersecurity research. The proposed framework leverages generative artificial intelligence models to produce high-fidelity synthetic cybersecurity datasets that replicate realistic device behaviors, network interactions, and cyber-attack scenarios. The synthetic datasets are evaluated for conformity to protocol standards, statistical similarity to original datasets, and utility in common security tools. The resulting synthetic data generation framework and evaluation metrics are expected to advance smart city cybersecurity by enabling researchers to model threats more effectively and evaluate defensive techniques more comprehensively to better protect critical smart city infrastructures.

</details>


### [128] [Partitioned Tags, Shared Data: Reconciling Strict Cache Isolation with Write-Shared Coherence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12259)
*Kartik Ramkrishnan,Stephen McCamant,Antonia Zhai,Pen Chung Yew*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SCP enables strict cache partitioning for security while maintaining write-shared coherence by partitioning only tags, using a shared data pool sized to prevent cross-partition evictions, and adding timing obfuscation plus leakage-threshold routing for shared-writable lines. It mitigates Prime+Probe and Flush+Reload attacks with negligible performance loss and only 2.8% LLC SRAM overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A decade-old design conflict has prevented widespread adoption of cache partitioning as a defense against eviction-based side channels in shared-OS environments: strict partitioning breaks write-shared coherence, which is essential for multi-core shared-memory correctness.

Method: SCP partitions only the cache tags while sharing a single data pool sized to eliminate capacity-driven cross-partition evictions. Timing obfuscation protects the inter-partition lookup path. For shared-writable lines, writes are routed through to the LLC once a configurable leakage threshold is exceeded, making attacker probe latency independent of victim activity.

Result: SCP mitigates Prime+Probe, Flush+Reload, and shared-writable-line attacks, yielding results no better than random guessing. Hardware cost is +2.8% LLC SRAM. SPEC CPU2017 performance matches DAWG within 0.3% IPC. Sharing-intensive microbenchmarks show a tunable security-performance tradeoff via the leakage threshold.

Conclusion: SCP resolves the long-standing tension between secure cache partitioning and write-shared coherence, providing a practical, low-overhead hardware defense against eviction-based cache side channels suitable for shared-OS deployments.

Abstract: Cache partitioning is among the strongest structural defenses against eviction-based cache side channels, yet a decade-old design issue has blocked its widespread deployment in secure shared-OS settings. The issue is that write-shared coherence collapses under strict partitioning. We present SCP (Secure and Coherent Partitioning), which combines strict eviction isolation with write-shared coherence by partitioning only the tags, sharing a single data pool, and sizing the data pool so capacity-driven cross-partition eviction cannot occur. Timing obfuscation extends protections to the inter-partition lookup path. Coherence-based leakage on shared-writeable lines is mitigated by routing those writes through to the LLC once a leakage threshold is crossed, which makes attacker write probe latency independent of victim activity.
  Using gem5 for implementation, SCP mitigates Prime+Probe and Flush+Reload, which are the basis for more sophisticated cache attacks. We also demonstrate that a shared-writeable-line attack is mitigated. All these attacks yield results no better than random guessing. SCP's hardware cost is a modest +2.8% LLC SRAM. Performance matches DAWG within 0.3% IPC on the SPEC CPU2017 benchmarks that we evaluated. Sharing-intensive microbenchmarks demonstrate a tunable security-performance tradeoff based on a system-specified leakage threshold.

</details>


### [129] [Selection Integrity for LLM Graph Memory: An Accumulability Criterion for Information-Flow-Blind Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12290)
*Zeming Fei,Hongming Fei,Xiaoyang Wang,Yang yang,Prosanta Gope,Biplab Sikdar,Ying Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper exposes a fundamental blind spot in provenance-based defenses for agent memory: an adversary can manipulate graph structure (e.g., adding sourceless edges) to redirect which authenticated facts get selected, without tampering with the facts themselves. This means provenance checking of retrieved records—the entire class of defense—is ineffective against structural attacks. The authors demonstrate this via real-world attacks on document-QA and multi-session agents, propose AuthSelect as a defense that recomputes selection on the authenticated subgraph, and formally characterize when memory selectors are vulnerable (reallocatability of top-k membership) versus immune.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agent memory is increasingly structured as graphs, and existing provenance defenses only verify the provenance of records an agent retrieves. The authors identify that these defenses are structurally blind: they fail to account for attacks that manipulate the writable graph structure to change which authenticated facts get selected, even when every cited fact is individually authentic. This gap threatens real-world agent systems, as demonstrated by silent misdirection of ledger transfers.

Method: The authors formalize the attack surface: a long-term graph memory runs a global selection step over writable structure, where an untrusted principal can write structural changes that alter which authenticated facts are selected. They model this as an information-flow control (IFC) problem and show that faithful IFC—checking provenance of what the reader uses—makes byte-identical decisions to no defense. They empirically validate across document-QA substrates and real multi-session agent memory, including a concrete attack where no-source structural writes misdirect 28 ledger transfers over 499 live actions. They then characterize vulnerability via reallocatability: a selector admits the channel when its structural term can reallocate an Ω(1) share of top-k membership past a selected fact's margin. They analyze Personalized PageRank (vulnerable), content-fixed rerankers (immune), and Graphiti's node-distance (immune despite leaning on structure more than PageRank). They prove the immune case generally and the open case under a verified chokepoint condition. Their defense, AuthSelect, recomputes selection on the authenticated subgraph.

Result: The paper shows that faithful IFC permits all 28 misdirected irreversible ledger transfers across 499 live actions, while AuthSelect prevents every one. Personalized PageRank is vulnerable because a sourceless write reroutes conserved random-walk mass; a content-fixed reranker is immune; Graphiti's node-distance remains immune despite heavier structural reliance. The predictor is reallocatability, not structural reliance. AuthSelect achieves zero over-blocking and 2–3% latency overhead.

Conclusion: The entire class of provenance defenses that only check the provenance of retrieved records is blind to structural attacks on graph memory. Closing this channel requires recomputing selection on the authenticated subgraph—which AuthSelect does—rather than merely verifying provenance of what was retrieved. The key insight is that reallocatability, not structural reliance, determines vulnerability.

Abstract: Agent memory is moving to graphs, and the provenance defenses now being built for it all check one thing: the provenance of the records an agent retrieves. We show that this entire class of defense is blind by construction. A long-term graph memory runs a global selection step over writable graph structure, so structure that an untrusted principal writes changes \emph{which} authenticated facts are selected while the cited evidence stays fully authenticated; faithful information-flow control (IFC), checking the provenance of what the reader uses (all of it authenticated), makes the byte-identical decision to no defense at all, across document-QA substrates and real multi-session agent memory. In the most consequential instance, a no-source structural write silently misdirects $28$ irreversible ledger transfers over $499$ live actions: faithful IFC permits every one, and \authselect\ prevents every one. We then characterize exactly which memories are exposed: a selector admits the channel when its structural term can reallocate an $Ω(1)$ share of top-$k$ membership past a selected fact's margin. Personalized PageRank can, since a sourceless write reroutes conserved random-walk mass; a content-fixed reranker cannot, and Graphiti's node-distance, which leans on structure \emph{more} than PageRank does, stays immune. Reallocatability, not reliance, is the predictor. We prove the immune case in general and the open case under a chokepoint condition we verify. Closing the channel forces any provenance defense to recompute selection on the authenticated subgraph, which is what \authselect\ does, at zero over-block and $2$--$3\%$ latency.

</details>


### [130] [OCELOT: Inference-Leakage Budgets for Privacy-Preserving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12341)
*Jin Xie,Songze Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: OCELOT is a runtime mediator for LLM agents that controls cumulative privacy leakage across entire interaction trajectories using posterior-risk control with a budget on adversary belief improvement. It employs Witness-Verified Declassification — an untrusted defender model proposes structured evidence, and a deterministic verifier audits releases against a certified min-entropy cost — achieving lower leakage and higher utility than existing defenses while resisting adaptive attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents acting on user behalf face a privacy problem that spans entire trajectories: leakage is cumulative (innocuous releases aggregate into damaging inferences), bidirectional (malicious observations can inject instructions), and task-dependent (same field is necessary for one recipient but gratuitous for another). Existing approaches — per-release contextual-integrity filters, information-flow controls, posterior-leakage monitors — each address only part of the problem and none controls cumulative, inference-based leakage at runtime.

Method: The authors recast agent privacy as posterior-risk control and introduce OCELOT, a runtime mediator. Its core mechanism, Witness-Verified Declassification, separates judgment from trust: (1) an untrusted, locally fine-tuned defender model inspects each candidate release and emits structured evidence (labeled atoms and proposed declassification operators); (2) a deterministic verifier audits this evidence, charges a certified min-entropy cost for the chosen variant, and authorizes the least-disclosing useful release; (3) a tamper-evident ledger tracks cumulative costs against a sink-trust-weighted budget.

Result: Across diverse agent benchmarks and compared with recent defenses, OCELOT attains significantly lower leakage at higher task utility. It resists adaptive injection, jailbreak attempts, cumulative inference, and sink collusion, while adding only modest runtime overhead.

Conclusion: OCELOT demonstrates that posterior-risk control with certified min-entropy budgeting and Witness-Verified Declassification is an effective, practical approach to trajectory-level privacy for LLM agents, overcoming limitations of prior per-release and static analysis methods.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly act on a user's behalf -- reading personal files, calling tools, transacting with external services -- possibly leaking personally identifiable information (PII) across trust boundaries at every step. Privacy here is a property not of a single output but of an entire trajectory, and three properties make it hard: leakage is cumulative, as individually innocuous releases accumulate across honest-but-curious or colluding sinks into inferences about a protected secret; bidirectional, as a malicious observation can inject instructions that turn the agent's own reasoning model against the user; and task-dependent, as the same field is necessary for one recipient yet gratuitous for another. Per-release contextual-integrity filters, information-flow controls, and posterior-leakage monitors each address part of this but none controls cumulative, inference-based leakage at runtime. We recast agent privacy as \emph{posterior-risk control} and present OCELOT, a runtime mediator that budgets how much an adversary's belief about a secret may improve across a trajectory, rather than filtering outputs. Its mechanism, \emph{Witness-Verified Declassification}, separates judgment from trust: an untrusted, locally fine-tuned defender model inspects each candidate release and emits structured evidence -- labeled atoms and proposed declassification operators -- which a deterministic verifier audits, charging a certified min-entropy cost for the chosen variant and authorizing the least-disclosing useful release under a sink-trust-weighted budget recorded on a tamper-evident ledger. Across diverse agent benchmarks and recent defenses, OCELOT attains significantly lower leakage at higher task utility, resists adaptive injection, jailbreak, cumulative inference, and sink collusion, and adds only modest overhead.

</details>


### [131] [ECYSAP EYE: From Cyber Situational Awareness to Mission-Centric Decision Support for Enhanced Cyberspace Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12354)
*Pantaleone Nespoli,Daniel Díaz-López,Sergio Lopez Bernal,Francisco Oliva Bermejo,Pedro González Megías,Jorge Maestre Vidal,Víctor Sobrino García,Gregorio Martínez Pérez*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: ECYSAP EYE introduces a System-of-Systems architecture that generates seven categories of mission-focused cyber situational awareness artefacts (RCyP, CySRs, WIAR, OPRE, DSH, AE, AAR) to bridge the gap from raw technical alerts to mission-relevant decision support, enabling incremental deployment across heterogeneous operational toolchains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operational organizations need Cyber Situational Awareness that transcends isolated technical alerts and delivers mission-relevant artefacts embeddable into diverse cyber defense processes and toolchains. Current approaches fail to provide this end-to-end integration from perception through decision-making to operational execution.

Method: An adoption-oriented System-of-Systems (SoS) architecture structured as a pipeline: perception (full-spectrum Recognized Cyberspace Picture), decision-oriented reasoning (What-If Analysis, Cyber Situational Reports, Option Recommendations), and operational execution/learning (Dashboard/HMI, Action Enforcement, After-Action Reports), with explicit integration surfaces for incremental deployment and validation.

Result: The paper presents the updated ECYSAP EYE architecture with seven artefact groups and describes their functional roles, along with the expected impact of cyber situations on mission planning and execution decision-making processes.

Conclusion: ECYSAP EYE provides a technology-transfer-oriented framework that structures CySA as a continuum from perception to action, enabling operational organizations to incrementally adopt mission-aware cyber defense capabilities through well-defined artefact interfaces.

Abstract: Operational organizations increasingly require Cyber Situational Awareness (CySA) capabilities that go beyond isolated technical alerts, providing mission-relevant artefacts that can be embedded into heterogeneous toolchains and cyber security or cyber defense processes. ECYSAP EYE addresses this need through an adoption-oriented System-of-Systems (SoS) architecture centered on seven groups of mission-focused artefacts: the Recognized Cyberspace Picture (RCyP), Cyber Situational Reports (CySRs), the What-If Analysis Report (WIAR), Option Recommendations (OPRE), an operator Dashboard/HMI (DSH), Action Enforcement (AE), and After-Action Reports (AAR). The ECYSAP EYE architecture structures the transition from perception (full-spectrum RCyP views), to decision-oriented reasoning (WIAR/CySRs/OPRE), and to operational execution and learning (DSH/AE/AAR), with explicit integration surfaces that support incremental deployment and validation. This paper presents this innovative project from a technology transfer perspective, summarizing the updated architecture, the functional role of seven groups of artefacts, and the expected impact of cyber situations on the decision-making process in the context of a mission planning and execution.

</details>


### [132] [MARCIM-WG: A cyber wargame proposal based on math modeling applied in a naval scenario](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12395)
*Diego Cabuya-Padilla,Daniel Díaz-López,Carlos Castaneda-Marroquín*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: MARCIM-WG is a hybrid tabletop maritime cyberdefense wargame that combines a physical board with computational simulation to train decision-makers in Cyber Situational Awareness (CSA). Validated through scenario-based assessment and a controlled comparative study, it demonstrated a +34.0 percentage-point improvement in CSA competencies, with the largest gains in comprehension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maritime operations are increasingly reliant on interconnected digital ecosystems, making them vulnerable to cascading cyber incidents that can degrade critical services. Existing training mechanisms lack the ability to expose decision-makers to evolving attack dynamics, resource constraints, and the alignment of actions with incident-response procedures. There is a need for structured wargaming tools that strengthen strategic Cyber Situational Awareness (CSA) through experiential learning.

Method: The authors designed MARCIM-WG following the NATO wargaming methodology. It is a hybrid tabletop wargame integrating a physical board (tokens, indicators, special cards) with analytically-assisted adjudication via a computational simulation model. The design is formalized through High-Level Design (HLD) and Low-Level Design (LLD) specifications and instantiated in a fictional maritime cyber crisis scenario. Validation employs a dual approach: (1) operational scenario-based assessment under three configurations (pessimistic, neutral/most likely, optimistic) to test decision sensitivity and outcome coherence; (2) a CSA competency and learning-outcome evaluation using a comparative design with an intervention group versus an equivalent control group.

Result: The intervention group showed a +34.0 percentage-point improvement in CSA competencies compared to the control group. The largest gains were observed in comprehension-related competencies.

Conclusion: MARCIM-WG effectively enhances strategic Cyber Situational Awareness in maritime contexts by providing a structured, simulation-supported wargaming environment. The significant improvement in comprehension competencies validates the wargame's utility as a training mechanism for decision-makers facing complex, resource-constrained cyber incidents.

Abstract: As maritime operations increasingly depend on interconnected digital ecosystems, cyber incidents can propagate across maritime networks and degrade critical services. Strengthening strategic Cyber Situational Awareness (CSA) therefore requires training mechanisms that expose decision-makers to evolving attack dynamics, constrained resources, and the need to align actions with incident-response procedures. This paper introduces MARCIM-WG, a learning-oriented maritime cyberdefense wargame designed following the NATO wargaming methodology and implemented as a hybrid tabletop experience combining a physical board (tokens, indicators, and special cards) with analytically-assisted adjudication supported by a computational simulation model. The proposal is specified through High-Level Design (HLD) and Low-Level Design (LLD) specifications and instantiated in a fictional maritime cyber crisis scenario to enable structured decision cycles, friction, and measurable consequences. Validation combines (i) an operational scenario-based assessment under three configurations (pessimistic, neutral/most likely, optimistic) to verify decision sensitivity and outcome coherence, and (ii) a CSA competency and learning-outcome evaluation using a comparative design against an equivalent control group. Results show a +34.0 percentage-point improvement in the intervention group, with the largest gains in comprehension-related competencies.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [133] [From Explicit Elements to Implicit Intent: A Predefined Library for Auditable Behavioral Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11207)
*Liu hung ming*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SemantiClean is a modular, auditability-first framework for extracting structured semantic signals from e-commerce session data. It uses a four-layer architecture and anti-inflation mechanisms to drive pluggable inference targets, deliberately trading marginal accuracy for full transparency, governance, and deterministic reproducibility (σ=0). An LLM-integrated inference engine operationalizes the framework, with controlled variability only in LLM-dependent results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional end-to-end predictors for e-commerce behavioral data optimize solely for predictive accuracy, resulting in opaque decision trails and poor auditability. There is a need for a structurally governed, transparent framework that provides element-level accountability and reproducible inference pathways, even at the cost of marginal accuracy, particularly in contexts where defensibility and governance outweigh raw predictive performance.

Method: The SemantiClean framework organizes 24 behavioral elements from the OSPI dataset into a four-layer architecture (Functional, Interaction, Systemic, Contextual). It enforces signal quality via three anti-inflation mechanisms: RedundancyGroup contribution caps, TieredPenaltyCalculator bias penalties, and AdaptiveConstraintMode cold-start protection. A two-phase LLM-Integrated Semantic Inference Engine consumes complete element metadata at inference time to produce deterministic (σ=0) and, for specific experiments (E8, E10), controlled-variability LLM-dependent outputs under fixed provider/model/temperature settings.

Result: The LLM-Integrated Semantic Inference Engine produces fully reproducible deterministic outputs (σ=0) for non-LLM-dependent inference targets. LLM-dependent experiments (E8, E10) exhibit controlled output variability constrained by fixed provider, model, and temperature parameters. The gender inference target remains non-functional and is excluded from quantitative results.

Conclusion: SemantiClean demonstrates that a modular, auditability-prioritizing framework can deliver structurally transparent and reproducible inference in e-commerce analytics while maintaining functional inference capabilities across multiple targets (purchase intent, customer segmentation, product affinity), with the explicit trade-off of marginal accuracy for governance and defensibility.

Abstract: We present SemantiClean, a modular framework for extracting structured semantic signals from e-commerce session data and driving pluggable inference targets including purchase intent, customer segmentation, and product affinity through a shared element library. Unlike conventional end-to-end predictors that optimise solely for accuracy, SemantiClean prioritises auditability, structural governance, and sigma=0 reproducibility, explicitly trading marginal predictive gains for element-level transparency and defensible decision trails. Built upon the Online Shoppers Purchasing Intention (OSPI) dataset, the framework organises twenty-four behavioural elements into a four-layer architecture (Functional, Interaction, Systemic, Contextual) and enforces signal quality through three anti-inflation mechanisms: RedundancyGroup contribution caps, TieredPenaltyCalculator bias penalties, and AdaptiveConstraintMode cold-start protection.This report introduces the LLM-Integrated Semantic Inference Engine, a fully implemented two-phase LLM-driven inference architecture that leverages complete element metadata at inference time. All quantitative results reported herein are produced by this engine. Deterministic engine outputs remain fully reproducible (sigma=0); LLM-dependent results (E8, E10) are subject to controlled output variability under fixed provider/model/temperature settings. The gender inference target remains non-functional in the current implementation and is excluded from all quantitative results.

</details>


### [134] [Position: Hippocampal Explicit Memory Is the Cornerstone for AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11245)
*Sangjun Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that integrating explicit memory into LLMs is essential for progressing toward AGI, because LLMs currently rely on implicit statistical learning akin to human implicit memory, while higher-order cognitive functions (planning, metacognition, symbolic reasoning) require hippocampal-like explicit memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs show impressive capabilities but lack higher-order cognitive functions necessary for AGI. The author identifies a fundamental gap: LLMs operate via a learning mechanism analogous to human implicit memory, yet AGI-level functions demand explicit memory systems. This motivates the call to integrate explicit memory as a cornerstone architectural advancement.

Method: This is a position paper that draws analogies between neuroscience findings (particularly the distinction between implicit and hippocampal explicit memory systems) and LLM learning mechanisms. It presents a theoretical argument mapping human memory taxonomy onto current LLM limitations and proposes computational requirements for artificial explicit memory systems.

Result: The paper synthesizes neuroscientific evidence to demonstrate that implicit statistical learning alone cannot produce strategic planning, metacognition, or symbolic reasoning — all hallmarks of AGI. It outlines computational desiderata for explicit memory in artificial systems.

Conclusion: Explicit memory integration is the critical missing component for advancing LLMs toward AGI. The paper calls for further research into artificial explicit memory systems grounded in neuroscientific principles.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across various tasks, raising expectations for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). This position paper argues that integrating explicit memory is the cornerstone for advancing LLMs toward AGI. The key reason is that the underlying learning mechanism of LLMs is highly analogous to human implicit memory. However, higher-order cognitive functions necessary for AGI, such as long-term strategic planning, metacognition, and symbolic reasoning, heavily rely on hippocampal explicit memory and cannot arise solely from implicit statistical learning. Drawing on findings from neuroscience, I advance this perspective and complement it with computational requirements for artificial explicit memory systems, hoping to foster further research and lay the groundwork for explicit memory integration.

</details>


### [135] [Can AI Agents Synthesize Scientific Conclusions?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11337)
*Hayoung Jung,Pedro Viana Diniz,José Reinaldo Corrêa Roveda,Abner Fernandes da Silva,Haeun Jung,Enoch Tsai,Aleksandra Korolova,Manoel Horta Ribeiro*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciConBench is a large-scale benchmark (9.11K questions) for evaluating open-domain scientific conclusion synthesis. Using a clean-room evaluation harness (SciConHarness) to prevent data leakage, the best agent achieves only 0.337 factual F1. Consumer-facing agents frequently produce incomplete or contradictory conclusions, indicating reliable scientific synthesis remains an open challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific AI agents are increasingly used for evidence retrieval, cross-source reasoning, and conclusion synthesis in high-stakes domains like healthcare, yet their actual synthesis capabilities are unclear. Existing evaluations suffer from data leakage (models memorizing training data), inflating performance estimates. There is a need for rigorous, leakage-proof evaluation of open-domain scientific conclusion synthesis.

Method: The authors introduce SciConBench, a benchmark of 9.11K questions paired with expert-written conclusions from systematic reviews. Conclusions are decomposed into atomic facts and evaluated via factual precision and recall (correctness and comprehensiveness). To prevent data leakage, they introduce SciConHarness, a clean-room evaluation harness providing controlled web interaction.
