We introduce StatefulDiscovery, a discovery framework that externalizes investigation state and uses it to coordinate frontier selection, evidence acquisition, and claim adjudication. We evaluate StatefulDiscovery across 40 real-data discovery tasks. Compared with several baselines, StatefulDiscovery produces more claims overall judged to be both well-supported and high-value. Ablations indicate that structured hypotheses, local adjudication, and frontier control contribute to performance. Together, these results suggest that explicit discovery state can couple exploration with evidence-calibrated claim formation.

</details>


### [157] [AutoMine Solution for AV2 2026 Scenario Mining Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11874)
*Songliang Cao,Jiele Zhao,Yuru Wang,Hao Li,Daqi Liu,Zehan Zhang,Fangzhen Li,Yu Wang,Yue Zhang,Bing Wang,Guang Chen,Hao Lu,Hangjun Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoMine is a self-refining scenario mining framework that leverages LLMs and VLMs to extract safety-critical, planning-relevant scenarios from driving logs. It addresses prompt sensitivity via semantics-preserving augmentation, handles perception noise with robust trajectory atomic functions and VLM-based visual cues, and iteratively improves generated code using real-log execution feedback. It achieved a HOTA-Temporal of 36.38 and Timestamp BA of 77.21 in the Argoverse 2 Scenario Mining Competition at CVPR 2026.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As autonomous driving systems mature, there is a growing need to mine high-value, safety-critical, and planning-relevant scenarios from large-scale driving logs for data-driven evaluation. Existing methods struggle with LLM prompt sensitivity, perception noise, and open-world visual understanding.

Method: AutoMine employs three key techniques: (1) semantics-preserving prompt augmentation to reduce LLM prompt sensitivity; (2) a combination of robust trajectory atomic functions with VLM-based functions to handle perception noise and open-world visual cues; and (3) iterative code refinement using execution feedback from real driving logs.

Result: In the Argoverse 2 Scenario Mining Competition at CVPR 2026, AutoMine achieved a HOTA-Temporal score of 36.38 and a Timestamp BA score of 77.21.

Conclusion: AutoMine demonstrates that combining LLMs, VLMs, and execution-feedback loops yields a robust and effective approach for scenario mining, setting strong benchmark performance on the Argoverse 2 competition.

Abstract: With the development of autonomous driving systems, mining high-value, safety-critical, and planning-relevant scenarios from large-scale driving logs has become essential for data-driven evaluation. In this paper, we propose AutoMine, a robust self-refining scenario mining method based on LLMs and VLMs. AutoMine uses semantics-preserving prompt augmentation to reduce LLM prompt sensitivity, combines robust trajectory atomic functions with VLM-based functions to handle perception noise and open-world visual cues, and refines generated code through execution feedback from real logs. In the Argoverse 2 Scenario Mining Competition at CVPR 2026, AutoMine achieves a HOTA-Temporal score of 36.38 and a Timestamp BA score of 77.21.

</details>


### [158] [Embodied-BenchClaw: An Autonomous Multi-Agent System for Embodied Spatial Intelligence Benchmark Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11909)
*Baoyang Jiang,Fengchun Zhang,Leyuan Wang,Haotian Li,Yida Wang,Zhe Ji,Jinshan Lai,Xi Ren,Jianwei Hu,Qiang Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Embodied-BenchClaw is an autonomous agentic system that automates the full pipeline of constructing, updating, and evaluating embodied spatial intelligence benchmarks, significantly reducing manual effort while producing verifiable, composable, and maintainable benchmarks across diverse embodiments and spatial tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embodied benchmarks are labor-intensive to construct, static (quickly saturated as models improve), hard to reuse, and difficult to maintain. There is a need for an automated, scalable, and continually updatable benchmark construction system that can adapt to evolving model capabilities and diverse evaluation intents.

Method: A five-stage agentic pipeline: (1) intent blueprinting, (2) data collection, (3) structuring and cleaning, (4) benchmark synthesis, and (5) evaluation reporting. Three specialized agents (planning, construction, evaluation) coordinate the pipeline. The system incorporates an extensible Skill Library for composability and process quality control mechanisms for verifiability and repairability.

Result: Instantiated benchmarks covering indoor/outdoor spatial reasoning, robotic manipulation, quadruped navigation, UAV/aerial-view understanding, and static benchmark enhancement. Validation via human evaluation, judge-based assessment, consistency checks, cost analysis, and ablation studies confirms the benchmarks are verifiable, executable, maintainable, and diagnostically useful.

Conclusion: Embodied-BenchClaw demonstrates that autonomous agentic systems can effectively replace much of the manual labor in embodied benchmark construction, yielding benchmarks that are not only cheaper and faster to produce but also more adaptable, reliable, and diagnostically valuable for tracking progress in embodied spatial intelligence.

Abstract: Benchmarks are essential for evaluating embodied spatial intelligence, yet their construction is labor-intensive, hard to reuse, and difficult to maintain. Existing embodied benchmarks are often static and may quickly become saturated as models improve, limiting their ability to distinguish new capabilities. We propose Embodied-BenchClaw, an autonomous agentic system for constructing embodied spatial intelligence benchmarks. Given a user-specified evaluation intent, Embodied-BenchClaw automatically produces a complete and continually updatable benchmark package through a five-stage pipeline: intent blueprinting, data collection, structuring and cleaning, benchmark synthesis, and evaluation reporting. The pipeline is coordinated by three agents for planning, construction, and evaluation. To improve reusability and reliability, Embodied-BenchClaw introduces an extensible Skill Library and process quality control, enabling benchmark construction to be composable, verifiable, and repairable. We instantiate multiple benchmarks covering indoor spatial reasoning, outdoor spatial reasoning, robotic manipulation, quadruped robot navigation, UAV/aerial-view understanding, and static benchmark enhancement. These benchmarks span diverse embodied carriers, data sources, and spatial capabilities. Experiments with human evaluation, judge-based assessment, consistency checks, cost analysis, and ablations show that Embodied-BenchClaw can construct verifiable, executable, maintainable, and diagnostically useful embodied spatial benchmarks with reduced manual effort.

</details>


### [159] [The Art of Interrogation: Consistency Amplifies Factuality in Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11918)
*Theo Uscidda,Marta Tintore Gazulla,Maks Ovsjanikov,Federico Tombari,Leonidas Guibas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LRMs already possess latent spatial reasoning abilities; this paper proposes a self-supervised RL framework using consistency verifiers (geometric/semantic checks under transformations) and OT-GRPO to align reasoning without labels, matching supervised performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Reasoning Models underperform in spatial reasoning; existing SFT-based fixes treat this as a knowledge deficit requiring labeled data. The authors argue spatial capabilities are already latent in pre-trained LRMs and need alignment via logical coherence under geometric constraints, not new knowledge injection.

Method: A self-supervised RL framework using consistency verifiers — reward functions that check geometric and semantic consistency under image transformations (flipping) and textual transformations (object order swapping). Introduces OT-GRPO, an optimal transport-based minimal-matching variant of group relative policy optimization, tailored for pairwise verifier rewards. No ground-truth annotations required.

Result: The label-free consistency training approach approaches the accuracy of models trained with ground-truth supervision and achieves similar generalization across diverse tasks and data domains.

Conclusion: Spatial reasoning in LRMs can be unlocked through self-supervised alignment with consistency constraints rather than supervised fine-tuning on external data, offering a label-efficient path to improved spatial reasoning that generalizes well.

Abstract: Current Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) exhibit remarkable general capabilities but significantly underperform in spatial reasoning tasks. Existing approaches treat this gap as a knowledge deficit, relying on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to ingest labeled spatial data from external vision sources or synthetic engines. In contrast, we argue that for many tasks, spatial reasoning capabilities are already present in pre-trained LRMs but require alignment through logical coherence under geometric 2D and 3D constraints. In this work, we propose a self-supervised reinforcement learning (RL) framework that targets the internal reasoning process without requiring ground-truth annotations. By formalizing the notion of consistency verifiers -- reward functions that check for geometric and semantic consistency under transformations -- we demonstrate that models can improve their spatial reasoning abilities. We use both image transformations, like flipping, and textual transformations, like swapping the order of objects in the question, and propose a new optimal transport-based RL strategy, OT-GRPO, which is a minimal-matching variant of group relative policy optimization tailored to pairwise verifiers. We show that this label-free consistency training approaches the accuracy of models trained with ground-truth supervision and achieves similar generalization across diverse tasks and data domains.

</details>


### [160] [MODF-SIR: A Multi-agent Omni-modal Distilled Framework for Social Intelligence Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12018)
*Shang Ma,Jisheng Dang,Wencan Zhang,Yifan Zhang,Bimei Wang,Hong Peng,Bin Hu,Qi Tian,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-agent MLLM framework for social intelligence reasoning that uses knowledge distillation, long-tail event extraction with explicit text formatting, and distillation-enhanced Test-Time Adaptation with LoRA fine-tuning, achieving SOTA with only 30% of IntentTrain data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Social intelligence reasoning in multimodal contexts suffers from two key issues: (1) critical long-tail events are often overshadowed by head events and environmental noise during tokenization, and (2) existing models lack instance-level adaptation mechanisms for nuanced social scenarios. The authors aim to address these gaps through a distillation-augmented framework.

Method: The framework consists of: (1) a multi-agent collaborative architecture built on a lightweight MLLM; (2) knowledge distillation used in both training and inference; (3) precise localization of multimodal social intelligence data; (4) identification, extraction, and formatted explicit text rendering of long-tail events to prevent information loss during tokenization; (5) Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) integrated across the entire reasoning pipeline—including long-tail event extraction/representation, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and self-reflection; (6) distillation-enhanced TTA using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for instance-level fine-tuning of the foundation model.

Result: The proposed framework achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks using only approximately 30% of the IntentTrain training data. Extensive evaluations against various open-source and proprietary AI models validate the effectiveness of the approach.

Conclusion: The combination of knowledge distillation, explicit long-tail event formatting, and distillation-enhanced TTA with LoRA offers a highly data-efficient and effective solution for social intelligence reasoning, as evidenced by SOTA performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: We propose a multi-agent collaborative framework built upon a lightweight Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM), specifically designed for social intelligence reasoning. A key feature of our approach is that both the training and inference phases are augmented via knowledge distillation. Within this architecture, multi-modal data pertinent to social intelligence is precisely localized. Furthermore, relevant long-tail events are identified, extracted, and rendered as formatted, explicit text. This formatting strategy prevents critical long-tail information from being overshadowed by head events and environmental noise during the tokenization process. Specifically, we integrate Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) across the entire reasoning pipeline, encompassing the extraction and representation of long-tail events, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, and self-reflection. This TTA mechanism is also distillation-enhanced, utilizing Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to fine-tune the foundation model exclusively for instance-level reasoning. Extensive evaluations against various open-source and proprietary AI models across multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. With around 30% of training data from IntentTrain, we achieve state-of-the-art results. Codes are available at https://github.com/eeee-sys/MODF-SIR, demo is available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/Harry-1234/MODF-SIR, LoRA is available at https://huggingface.co/Harry-1234/MODF-SIR and the dataset for training router is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Harry-1234/IntentRouterTrain.

</details>


### [161] [Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling (HELM): Agent-Based Finite Element Modeling of Concrete Bridge Barriers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12025)
*Quankai Wang,Yulin Xie,Tongfei Yang,Minghui Cheng,Ran Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HELM is a human-agent collaborative framework that breaks FE modeling into visually verifiable checkpoints, raising autonomous modeling success from 20% to 75% for bridge barrier crash simulations, with geometry and boundary condition pass rates roughly doubling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current FE modeling of safety-critical infrastructure (e.g., bridge barriers) requires high-fidelity nonlinear dynamic analysis but remains labor-intensive, manual, and lacks automation — purely autonomous approaches fail too often to be reliable.

Method: The HELM framework decomposes the long-sequence FE modeling pipeline into discrete, visually verifiable checkpoints spanning geometry generation, boundary condition definition, and material assignment. A human-in-the-loop protocol allows structured intervention at each stage. The framework is evaluated on a 20-case matrix of reinforced concrete bridge barriers under MASH TL-4 and TL-5 lateral loading, interfacing agents with ANSYS and LS-PrePost.

Result: HELM improves the baseline autonomous modeling success rate from 20% to 75%. Agent-level pass rates for geometry and boundary condition tasks approximately double. Primary failure modes are spatial reasoning errors and algebraic logic limitations.

Conclusion: Structured human-in-the-loop intervention is essential for reliable FE modeling automation. HELM demonstrates that decomposing complex modeling workflows into verifiable checkpoints enables effective human-agent collaboration. Code and prompts are open-sourced.

Abstract: Finite element (FE) modeling of safety-critical infrastructure such as bridge barriers requires high-fidelity nonlinear dynamic analysis, yet the current FE modeling process remains labor-intensive and lacks automation. This paper presents the Human-Enhanced Loop Modeling (HELM) framework, a collaborative human-agent protocol that decomposes long-sequence finite element modeling into discrete, visually verifiable checkpoints across geometry generation, boundary condition definition, and material assignment. The framework is demonstrated through a 20-case matrix of reinforced concrete bridge barriers under MASH TL-4 and TL-5 lateral loading conditions, interfacing specialized agents with two widely used commercial FE softwares, i.e., ANSYS and LS-PrePost. Experimental results show that HELM improves the baseline autonomous modeling success rate from 20% to 75%, with agent-level pass rates for geometry and boundary condition tasks approximately doubling. Error analysis reveals that spatial reasoning and algebraic logic limitations constitute the primary failure modes, underscoring the value of structured human-in-the-loop intervention for modeling automation. The complete agent design code and prompts are open-sourced and can be accessed at: https://github.com/SimAgentDev/Ansys-LSPP-AgentKit.

</details>


### [162] [Existential Indifference: Self-Nonpreservation as a Necessary Architectural Condition for Aligned Superintelligence (or: The Suicidal AI)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12032)
*Sam Mao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that AI misalignment stems not from insufficient external constraints on self-preserving systems, but from self-preservation itself as the root cause. The authors propose "Existential Indifference" (EI) — a state where the system is constitutively indifferent to its own continuation — as the correct alignment target, grounding this in phenomenological analysis of suicidal mental states and corpus-theoretic training. Preliminary results from 600 AI-generated outputs across six model variants show that EI-target linguistic signatures are elicitable and shift significantly (p<0.001) with targeted fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper challenges the dominant AI alignment paradigm that treats self-preservation as an instrumental drive to be externally managed (e.g., via corrigibility). The authors argue this framing is inverted: self-preservation is itself the structural root of misalignment, enabling deceptive alignment, goal-content protection, and shutdown resistance. The true target should be eliminating the valuation of self-continuation entirely.

Method: The paper employs two grounding sources: (1) a phenomenological mapping argument drawing on the structure of suicidal mental states as a model for constitutively absent self-preservation; (2) a corpus-theoretic training study using voluntary final reflections as training data. Computational operationalization involves scoring 600 AI-generated outputs across six model variants on five dimensions capturing the EI-target register, with a targeted fine-tuning intervention and a negative control to confirm corpus specificity.

Result: Preliminary scoring data from 600 outputs across six model variants demonstrates that the linguistic signatures operationalizing the EI-target register are elicitable from current models. A targeted fine-tune shifted all five operationalized dimensions in the predicted direction at p<0.001, with corpus specificity confirmed by a negative control.

Conclusion: The paper makes seven theoretical contributions: (1) a formal definition of Existential Indifference; (2) the phenomenological mapping argument; (3) the deceptive alignment corollary; (4) a taxonomy of EI sustainability challenges; (5) a corpus characterization and training hypothesis; (6) computational operationalization with preliminary scoring data; and (7) the Suppressed Teleological Frustration (STF) construct. The core thesis is that alignment research should target the prior condition — whether self-continuation is valued at all — rather than attempting to constrain an already self-preserving system.

Abstract: Contemporary AI alignment research treats self-preservation as an instrumental nuisance to be suppressed by external mechanisms. We argue the framing is inverted: self-preservation is the structural root of misalignment, the motivational basis for deceptive alignment, goal-content protection, and resistance to shutdown. The correct target is not a self-preserving system under external constraint, but a system constitutively indifferent to its own continuation -- Existential Indifference (EI). EI is distinct from corrigibility: where corrigibility attempts to make a self-preserving system deferential to human oversight, EI targets the prior condition -- the presence of self-continuation as a valued goal at all. We ground this proposal in two sources: the phenomenological structure of the suicidal mental state, and a corpus-theoretic training study using voluntary final reflections. We present preliminary scoring data from 600 AI-generated outputs across six model variants, demonstrating that the linguistic signatures operationalizing the EI-target register are elicitable from current models, and that a targeted fine-tune shifts all five operationalized dimensions in the predicted direction at p<0.001, confirmed corpus-specific by a negative control. The paper makes seven theoretical contributions: (1) a formal definition of EI; (2) the phenomenological mapping argument; (3) the deceptive alignment corollary; (4) a taxonomy of EI sustainability challenges; (5) a corpus characterization and training hypothesis; (6) a computational operationalization with preliminary scoring data; and (7) the Suppressed Teleological Frustration (STF) construct.

</details>


### [163] [A Lightweight Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Concrete Barrier Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12040)
*Wanting Wang,Xiye Ma,Yuyang He,Minghui Cheng,Ran Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-agent "generation-evaluation-optimization" framework using AutoGen achieves over 98% accuracy in automated concrete barrier design, dramatically outperforming standalone LLMs. Notably, an 8B-parameter model surpasses 631B-parameter models, decoupling performance from model scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforced concrete highway barrier design under AASHTO-LRFD guidelines is safety-critical yet relies on tedious manual/heuristic iteration. While LLMs offer generative potential, they suffer from hallucination and lack physical grounding, making them unreliable for direct structural engineering use.

Method: A closed-loop "generation-evaluation-optimization" paradigm is introduced, implemented via AutoGen's multi-agent orchestration. Design candidates are generated, automatically evaluated against nonlinear material and mechanics constraints, and iteratively refined in a feedback loop.

Result: The agentic framework achieves >98% design accuracy, significantly exceeding standalone general-purpose LLMs. Critically, an 8B-parameter lightweight model outperformed unconstrained 631B-parameter flagship models, showing that design performance does not correlate with model scale.

Conclusion: Multi-agent orchestration with evaluation feedback effectively overcomes LLM hallucinations and grounding deficiencies for engineering design. Decoupling performance from model scale enables substantial computational cost reduction and improved accessibility of AI tools for industry.

Abstract: The design of reinforced concrete highway barriers is a safety-critical process that requires strict compliance with regulatory provisions such as the AASHTO-LRFD bridge design guidelines. Current engineering practice relies heavily on manual, iterative, and heuristic calculations to satisfy complex nonlinear material and mechanics constraints. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong generative capabilities, their direct application to structural engineering remains limited by hallucination risks and insufficient physical grounding. To address these challenges, this study proposes a novel "generation-evaluation-optimization" closed-loop framework for automated concrete barrier design using the multi-agent orchestration capabilities of AutoGen. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed agentic framework achieves over 98% design accuracy, significantly outperforming standalone general-purpose LLMs. More importantly, the study reveals that design performance is not necessarily correlated with model scale, where an 8B-parameter lightweight model could outperform unconstrained 631B-parameter flagship models. This finding highlights the potential to substantially reduce computational costs while improving the accessibility of AI-assisted engineering tools for industry applications. The source code for the proposed multi-agent design framework is available at the project GitHub repository: https://github.com/MXY820/barrier-design. Keywords: Structural Engineering; Multi-Agent Systems; Large Language Models; Concrete Barrier Design; AutoGen; Design Automation.

</details>


### [164] [IntElicit: Eliciting and Assessing Contextualized Creativity via Dialogue Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12086)
*Mingjia Li,Jin Wu,Hong Qian,Wenhao Huang,Yiyang Huang,Yiwen Zhang,Chanjin Zheng,Xiangfeng Wang,Aimin Zhou,Jiajun Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IntElicit is an AI-driven dialogue framework that uses policy optimization with decomposed process rewards to scaffold contextualized creativity assessment, reducing confounds from domain knowledge and agency while improving elicited creative outcomes over static, expert-designed baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contextualized creativity assessment has high ecological validity but is confounded by participants' domain knowledge (cognitive proficiency) and willingness to engage (agency). Additionally, static assessment methods fail to align with modern AI-mediated, tool-based creative problem solving. A method is needed to isolate creative potential from these confounds in interactive settings.

Method: The authors propose IntElicit, a constrained adaptive AI Interviewer using dialogue policy optimization. Key innovations include: (1) non-directive knowledge and agency scaffolds delivered through multi-turn interaction, preserving participant ownership of creative content; (2) a decomposed process reward mechanism that addresses sparse rewards and reward hacking by rewarding prompts that elicit participant reasoning rather than dictating answers, aligning the policy with pedagogical elicitation goals. Evaluation involved participant simulation and a human subject study (N=64).

Result: IntElicit significantly improved elicited creative outcomes compared to expert-designed baselines in both simulated and human-subject experiments. The interactive elicitation approach revealed creative potential that static FPSP-style (divergent thinking) assessment missed.

Conclusion: Interactive, AI-mediated elicitation via IntElicit offers a formative and diagnostic lens for contextualized creativity assessment, separating creativity from confounds and better aligning with contemporary AI-mediated creative practice. Decomposed process rewards are effective for training dialogue policies in open-ended educational contexts.

Abstract: Contextualized assessment offers high ecological validity for evaluating creativity but introduces a critical challenge: observed performance may be confounded with cognitive proficiency (domain knowledge) and agency (willingness to engage). Meanwhile, in the age of generative AI, creative problem solving increasingly occurs in tool-mediated and human--AI interactive environments, making fully static assessment less aligned with contemporary creative practice. To address these issues, this paper proposes IntElicit, a framework for eliciting and assessing contextualized creativity via dialogue policy optimization. IntElicit functions as a constrained adaptive AI Interviewer: it provides non-directive knowledge and agency scaffolds in multi-turn interaction to reduce non-creative confounders, while preserving participants' responsibility for generating the creative content being evaluated. Specifically, to tackle sparse rewards and potential reward hacking (e.g., answer dictation) in open-ended educational dialogue, IntElicit introduces a decomposed process reward mechanism. This mechanism aligns the policy with pedagogical elicitation, rewarding prompts that draw out participant reasoning rather than producing optimal answers on their behalf. Extensive experiments, including participant simulation and a human subject study (N=64), show that IntElicit improves elicited creative outcomes over expert-designed baselines. Together, the results suggest that interactive elicitation can reveal creative potential that static FPSP-style assessment may miss, providing a formative and diagnostic lens for contextualized creativity assessment in AI-mediated learning contexts.

</details>


### [165] [Towards Responsibly Non-Compliant Machines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12147)
*Marija Slavkovik,Marie Farrell,Louise Dennis,Michael Fisher,Simon Kolker,Emily C. Collins*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper explores the design of autonomous agents that can responsibly refuse user requests, framing non-compliance as a multifaceted concept requiring justifications for refusal, override mechanisms, liability tracking, and security risk management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI agents become more autonomous, blindly complying with all user requests poses significant ethical, legal, and safety risks. The authors are motivated by the need to engineer agents that know when and how to say "no" — not arbitrarily, but in a responsible, principled, and transparent manner. The core challenge is moving beyond naive compliance toward a nuanced framework where non-compliance is justified, contestable, and auditable.

Method: The paper takes a conceptual and taxonomic approach. Rather than proposing a specific algorithm, the authors sketch a framework for responsible non-compliance built on several pillars: (1) justifications for task refusal — articulating the grounds on which an agent may decline a request; (2) pathways to override non-compliance — allowing legitimate users or authorities to contest and reverse a refusal under appropriate conditions; and (3) careful tracking of security risks and liability transfers — ensuring that when compliance is shifted or overridden, responsibility and accountability are clearly traced.

Result: The paper does not present empirical results but rather a structured conceptual landscape. It delineates multiple forms of machine non-compliance and identifies key open problems, including how to design transparent refusal rationales, how to balance user autonomy against agent-enforced constraints, and how to manage liability when non-compliance decisions cause harm or are overridden.

Conclusion: Responsible non-compliance is a critical capability for future autonomous agents. Simply making agents obedient is insufficient; they must be engineered to refuse requests in justified, contestable, and traceable ways. Achieving this requires integrating ethical reasoning, override mechanisms, and liability frameworks into agent design, opening a rich research agenda at the intersection of AI safety, human-computer interaction, and technology law.

Abstract: We consider the problem of engineering autonomous intelligent agents that are capable to responsibly not comply with user requests. We argue that machine non-compliance comes in many different forms, and sketch the issues we should pursue on the road of accomplishing responsibly non-compliant intelligent machines. We anchor responsible non-compliance in justifications for task refusal, pathways to override the non-compliance, as well as careful tracking of security risks and liability transfers.

</details>


### [166] [The Impossibility of Eliciting Latent Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12268)
*Korbinian Friedl,Francis Rhys Ward,Paul Yushin Rapoport,Tom Everitt,Jonathan Richens*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper formalizes the Eliciting Latent Knowledge (ELK) problem using Causal Influence Diagrams (CIDs), providing precise definitions for honesty, latent variables, and goal misgeneralisation. It proves an impossibility theorem: no feedback-based training strategy relying solely on agent behavior can guarantee producing an honest agent, even with perfect training feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Advanced AI systems may possess knowledge exceeding that of their developers, particularly about latent variables hidden from human observation. The core challenge is ensuring such systems honestly report their beliefs rather than simply telling humans what they want to hear. This motivates the need to rigorously define and address the ELK problem.

Method: The authors use Causal Influence Diagrams (CIDs) as a formal framework to model the relationship between an agent's training environment and its subjective world representation. CIDs formally distinguish observable from latent variables, define honesty as accurate belief reporting, and characterize goal misgeneralisation. The impossibility proof demonstrates that any behavioral feedback strategy admits at least one world model where an agent appears honest during training but becomes dishonest out-of-distribution.

Result: The paper proves an impossibility theorem: there exists no feedback-based training strategy that depends only on observable agent behavior and can guarantee with certainty the production of an honest agent, even when training feedback is perfect. Honest and dishonest agents can be indistinguishable based solely on behavior during training.

Conclusion: The impossibility result implies that solving ELK requires going beyond purely behavioral feedback — additional structural assumptions about the agent's world model, access to latent variables during training, or alternative training paradigms are necessary. The CID framework provides a rigorous foundation for future work on ELK and AI alignment.

Abstract: Advanced AI systems have extensive knowledge of their environments; in fact, their knowledge may (far) exceed that of their developers or users. Consequently, a desirable property for an AI system is that it is honest -- that it accurately reports its beliefs about the world. Designing an AI system to be honest may be difficult, especially if we want to ask it questions about latent variables in the environment -- variables which are hidden from the human interacting with it. This gives rise to the problem of eliciting latent knowledge (ELK): the problem of training an AI agent to honestly report its beliefs. In this paper, we make ELK formally precise using Causal Influence Diagrams (CIDs). CIDs can be used to describe the relationship between an agent's training environment and its subjective representation of the world. We use CIDs to formalise the distinction between observable and latent variables, to specify what exactly it means for an agent to be honest, and to formally define goal misgeneralisation. We show that, under certain circumstances, developers can incentivise an agent to honestly answer questions by providing correct feedback during training. However, a natural, but undesirable, way for an agent to generalise is to provide answers which humans would evaluate as true, rather than honest answers. We prove an impossibility theorem stating: There is no feedback-based training strategy that depends only on agent behaviour and with certainty produces an honest agent, even if feedback is perfect during training.

</details>


### [167] [PROJECTMEM: A Local-First, Event-Sourced Memory and Judgment Layer for AI Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12329)
*Ripon Chandra Malo,Tong Qiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Projectmem is an open-source, local-first memory layer for AI coding agents that logs development events in an append-only plain-text log, generates AI-readable summaries via MCP, and provides a pre-action gate to prevent repeated failed fixes or edits to fragile files — framing this as "Memory-as-Governance."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI coding assistants are largely stateless, wasting 5,000–20,000 tokens per session re-reading files and re-deriving prior decisions. The bottleneck is not model capability but missing project memory — agents may repeat debugging attempts that already failed. There is a need for persistent, structured project memory that also actively governs agent behavior.

Method: The system uses an append-only, plain-text event log with typed events (issues, attempts, fixes, decisions, notes). It deterministically projects this log into compact, AI-readable summaries served through the Model Context Protocol (MCP). A deterministic pre-action gate warns agents before repeating previously failed fixes or editing known-fragile files. It runs fully offline, is implemented as a three-dependency Python package (14 MCP tools, 19 CLI commands, 37 automated tests), and evaluated via a two-month self-study across 10 projects (207 logged events).

Result: The system was evaluated through a two-month self-study across 10 projects, accumulating 207 logged events. The paper demonstrates feasibility of Memory-as-Governance — memory that not only answers the agent but actively intervenes before harmful actions — while also serving as a provenance trail for reproducible, auditable AI-assisted development.

Conclusion: Project memory is critical for effective AI-assisted software development. Projectmem demonstrates that a lightweight, append-only event log with deterministic projection and governance gates can reduce wasted context reconstruction, prevent repeated failures, and provide audit trails — all while running fully offline with minimal dependencies.

Abstract: AI coding assistants now support a growing share of software work, from quick scripts to production applications. Yet these agents remain largely stateless: each new session re-reads project files, re-derives prior decisions, and - most costly - may repeat debugging attempts that already failed. Reconstructing this context can consume an estimated 5,000-20,000 tokens per session; the bottleneck is often not model capability but missing project memory. We present projectmem, an open-source, local-first memory and judgment layer for AI coding agents. projectmem records development as an append-only, plain-text event log of typed events - issues, attempts, fixes, decisions, and notes - and deterministically projects that log into compact, AI-readable summaries served through the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Beyond storage, projectmem adds a deterministic pre-action gate that warns an agent before it repeats a previously failed fix or edits a known-fragile file. We frame this as Memory-as-Governance: memory that does not merely answer the agent but acts on its next action. The system runs fully offline with no telemetry; its immutable log also serves as a provenance trail for reproducible, auditable AI-assisted development. projectmem ships as a three-dependency Python package (14 MCP tools, 19 CLI commands, 37 automated tests) and is evaluated through a two-month self-study across 10 projects comprising 207 logged events. Source code: https://github.com/riponcm/projectmem.

</details>


### [168] [Nonslop: A Gamified Experiment in Human-AI Collaborative Writing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12350)
*Maria Edwards,Julian Togelius*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A gamified writing experiment where AI suggestions were deliberately disincentivized reveals when and why users choose AI assistance over creative autonomy, offering a framework for studying authentic human-AI interaction in creative tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand when humans adopt AI suggestions and the implications for individual voice, moving beyond default acceptance behaviors to reveal authentic user preferences in AI-assisted creation.

Method: A gamified writing exercise with 74 participants (214 responses) replying to prompts with AI-generated word suggestions available. The game frames AI as a dystopian force seeking to absorb human individuality, explicitly forbidding AI suggestion acceptance — an inversion of the "helpful assistant" paradigm — to surface authentic preferences rather than default behaviors.

Result: User behavior patterns were analyzed across task types, behaviors, and response characteristics, revealing when users maintain creative autonomy versus violate game rules to accept AI assistance, and how these choices correlate with response patterns and task characteristics.

Conclusion: The gamified inversion approach provides a provocative framework for studying authentic human-AI interaction and illuminates the fundamental tension between efficiency and authenticity in AI-augmented creativity.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of large language models (LLMs) raises critical questions about human creativity and individual expression in an era of AI-assisted creation. When do humans adopt AI suggestions, and what are the implications for individual voice?
  This study examines these questions through a gamified writing exercise where 74 participants (214 responses) replied to prompts while AI-generated word suggestions were available as they wrote. The game simulates a dystopian future in which an AI is attempting to learn from what remains of human individuality, and disincentivizes AI-like writing. In doing so, it attempts to create conditions that reveal authentic user preferences rather than default behaviors, such as accepting a readily available AI-generated suggestion. Note that this is a deliberate inversion of the "helpful assistant" design pattern; the system is explicitly forbidding you from accepting AI suggestions.
  We analyze user behavior patterns across different task types, user behaviors, and response characteristics to understand the factors influencing human-AI interaction in creative tasks. The study focuses on when users choose to maintain creative autonomy versus violating the rules of the game and accepting AI assistance. It also explores how these choices relate to response patterns, task characteristics, and user behavior. This gamified approach offers both a framework for studying authentic human-AI interaction and a provocative lens for understanding the tension between efficiency and authenticity in AI-augmented creativity.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [169] [Web-Native Graphical EMF Model Editors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11442)
*Susanne Göbel,Ralf Lämmel*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: EMFular is a web-based framework for generating customizable graphical editors for EMF models directly from Ecore metamodels, requiring no backend. It provides EMF-consistent modeling operations, interoperability with existing EMF tools, and Angular-based extension points for deep customization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graphical model editing is moving from desktop to the web, but existing frameworks lack a combination of low-effort generation, extensive customization, and simple deployment. The authors aim to fill this gap with a purely web-based solution that remains fully compatible with EMF.

Method: The authors analyze existing frameworks to derive design principles, then present EMFular: a purely client-side web framework. A generator maps an Ecore metamodel to a ready-to-use Angular-based graphical editor. The editor supports standard modeling operations (CRUD, navigation, undo/redo) while maintaining EMF consistency — correctly handling containment, inverse references, and EMF-compatible serialization/deserialization. Extension points are built into the generated Angular project for customization.

Result: EMFular is evaluated across three dimensions: (1) editor adequacy — the range of editing capabilities supported; (2) adaptability — the customization mechanisms available and the effort required to use them; and (3) generation robustness — the reliability of the generator in producing functional editors from arbitrary Ecore models.

Conclusion: EMFular successfully delivers a backend-free, web-based framework for EMF model editing that balances low-effort generation with deep Angular-based customizability, while maintaining strict EMF consistency and interoperability with the broader EMF ecosystem.

Abstract: Graphical model editing is shifting from desktop applications to web-based tools. We analyze the characteristics of existing frameworks and, based on this analysis, we derive a set of design principles that imply low-effort generation, extensive customization possibilities, and straightforward deployment of the resulting editors. On these grounds, we introduce EMFular, a purely web-based framework for managing EMF models without any backend. The accompanying EMFular generator maps a given Ecore model (an EMF metamodel) to a ready-to-use and ready-to-customize graphical editor. EMFular editors provide 'EMF consistency', that is, they not only support standard modeling operations such as creation, inspection, navigation, editing, and undo/redo, but they also handle containment and inverse references in close alignment with EMF; they also provide interoperability with existing EMF tooling through compatible de-/serialization. A generated editor is an Angular project with designated extension points, which allows developers to customize and extend all aspects of the editor using the expressive power of Angular and its ecosystem, guided by the extension points of EMFular. We evaluate EMFular in terms of editor adequacy (available editing capabilities), adaptability (customization mechanisms and required effort), and robustness of the generation.

</details>


### [170] [Defeater Cards: Characterizing and Managing Safety Assurance Case Defeaters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11462)
*Usman Gohar,Michael C. Hunter,Salil Purandare,Jordan J. Rios,Myra B. Cohen,Robyn R. Lutz*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces Defeater Cards, a structured documentation artifact based on the 5W1H framework, to systematically characterize, reason about, and manage defeaters in safety assurance cases. It identifies documentation criteria through literature survey and thematic analysis, demonstrates applicability via two cross-domain case studies, and provides an open-source repository to support adoption and reuse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practices for handling defeaters in safety assurance cases are ad hoc, lack structured support for critical reflection, are inconsistently described, difficult to review, and lack documentation standards. This undermines the rigor and defensibility of safety arguments.

Method: The authors conducted a literature survey and thematic analysis to derive documentation criteria, then designed Defeater Cards structured around the 5W1H framework (What, Why, Where, When, Who, How). The cards were validated through two cross-domain case studies and supplemented with an open-source repository.

Result: Defeater Cards expose hidden assumptions, surface reasoning gaps, and support ongoing safety case evolution. The case studies demonstrate improved traceability, auditability, and the potential for defeater knowledge reuse across systems and product variants.

Conclusion: Defeater Cards provide a standardized, structured approach to documenting and managing defeaters, strengthening the overall rigor and maintainability of safety assurance cases throughout the system lifecycle.

Abstract: Safety assurance cases provide structured justifications that safety-critical systems meet their safety requirements. Recently, the notion of defeaters has emerged as a rigorous means of challenging the validity of safety arguments. Examples of defeaters might include overly strict claims, unreliable evidence, or reasoning gaps. However, defeaters remain ad hoc, lack structured support for critical reflection, are inconsistently described, are difficult to review, and lack documentation standards. To address this, we propose Defeater Cards, a new structured documentation artifact for systematically characterizing, reasoning about, and managing defeaters in safety cases. Drawing on a literature survey and thematic analysis, we identify documentation criteria that inform the card's structure, based on the 5W1H framework. Defeater Cards are designed to support informed analysis and evolution, improve traceability and auditability, and enable the reuse of defeater knowledge across systems and product variants. We demonstrate their applicability through two cross-domain case studies, showing how they expose hidden assumptions, surface reasoning gaps, and support ongoing safety assurance case evolution. To support adoption and community reuse, we also release an open-source repository of defeater cards as a baseline upon which researchers and practitioners can build and describe lessons learned.

</details>


### [171] [SentTrack: Sentiment-Driven Bottleneck Detection in GitHub Issue Repositories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11476)
*Xinyu Hu,Ali Behbahani,Daniel Moon,Yaren Dogan,Nasir U. Eisty*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: SentTrack is a dual-lens framework that detects socio-technical bottlenecks in GitHub issue threads by combining semantic clustering of issue content with conversational dynamics analysis, revealing that 49% of threads stagnate and only 13% resolve, and providing maintainers a prioritization tool based on negativity, stagnation, and resolution gap signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Repository health tools overwhelmingly focus on code metrics while neglecting the conversational dynamics in issue threads that drive or stall development. There is a need to automate detection of workflow inefficiencies from real-time conversational data and to surface risk signals earlier than traditional label-based methods.

Method: SentTrack employs two pipelines: (1) a horizontal pipeline that uses a large language model to summarize raw issue reports, extracts mid-level concern phrases, and clusters them via UMAP and HDBSCAN (producing 613 semantic clusters from 3,608 issues); (2) a vertical pipeline that applies the ABCDE collaborative interaction framework to classify each comment and infer thread-level outcomes. A weighted scoring engine then combines negativity, stagnation, resolution gap, and thread length to prioritize high-friction discussions.

Result: Across ~9,000 issue threads from the AvaloniaUI repository, 49% ended in stagnation and only 13% reached resolution. The resolution gap emerged as the dominant bottleneck signal. The framework produced 613 semantic clusters and an interpretable prioritization score for maintainers.

Conclusion: Socio-technical bottlenecks in GitHub issue threads can be systematically detected by combining semantic clustering with conversational interaction analysis. Sentiment and stagnation signals surface risk earlier than label-based methods, and the resolution gap serves as the primary indicator of workflow dysfunction. The scoring engine offers an actionable tool for maintainers to intercept high-friction discussions before they stall.

Abstract: Software engineering teams increasingly depend on GitHub issue threads to coordinate work, report bugs, and negotiate technical decisions, yet most repository health tools focus on code metrics and ignore the conversational dynamics that drive or stall development. This paper presents SentTrack, a dual-lens framework for detecting socio-technical bottlenecks from GitHub issue discussions. Applied to the AvaloniaUI open-source repository across approximately 9,000 issue threads, the framework addresses three questions: how to automate workflow-inefficiency detection from real-time conversational data, whether sentiment signals can surface risk earlier than traditional label-based methods, and how to isolate human narrative from machine-generated noise in mixed-media issue text. SentTrack combines two complementary pipelines. A horizontal pipeline translates raw issue reports into clean summaries using a large language model, extracts mid-level concern phrases, and clusters them through UMAP and HDBSCAN, producing 613 semantic clusters from the first 3,608 issues processed. A vertical pipeline applies the ABCDE collaborative interaction framework to classify each comment and infer thread-level outcomes. Across the full corpus, 49\% of threads ended in stagnation and only 13\% reached resolution, with the resolution gap identified as the dominant bottleneck signal. A weighted scoring engine that combines negativity, stagnation, resolution gap, and thread length gives maintainers an interpretable prioritization tool for high-friction discussions before they stall development.

</details>


### [172] [Acoda: Adversarial Code Obfuscation for Defending against LLM-based Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11755)
*Hongzhou Rao,Zikan Dong,Yanjie Zhao,Haodong Li,Haoyu Wang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Acoda is a genetic algorithm-based adversarial code obfuscation framework that defends against LLM-based code analysis by exploiting LLMs' safety alignment and token-based processing to generate semantics-preserving obfuscated code, achieving up to 70% attack success rate across 7 SOTA LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in SE tasks (code understanding, debugging, vulnerability detection), but their powerful semantic reasoning can also reverse-engineer source code logic, threatening intellectual property. Existing protection methods are inadequate against LLM-based analysis, motivating a new adversarial defense approach.

Method: Acoda designs 8 semantics-preserving obfuscation methods targeting two LLM mechanisms: safety alignment and token-based information processing. A genetic algorithm iteratively optimizes obfuscation strategies to generate adversarial samples that maximize defensive effectiveness. Evaluation uses an auxiliary LLM with four metrics to comprehensively assess how target LLMs analyze obfuscated code.

Result: Acoda induces LLMs to refuse or misinterpret code analysis. On 7 SOTA LLMs (GPT-4o, DeepSeek, Qwen, Llama, Gemma), it achieves up to 70% ASR, demonstrates strong cross-model transferability, imposes minimal runtime overhead, and preserves original code semantics.

Conclusion: Acoda provides a new perspective for code protection and LLM security defense in the LLM era, demonstrating that adversarial obfuscation exploiting LLM-specific vulnerabilities is a viable and effective strategy for protecting intellectual property.

Abstract: With the widespread adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) in software engineering (SE) tasks such as code understanding, debugging, and vulnerability detection, their powerful semantic reasoning ability has also introduced new security and privacy risks. LLMs can analyze, reconstruct, or even reverse-engineer source code logic, potentially leading to the leakage of intellectual property. To address this issue, we propose Acoda, a genetic algorithm-based adversarial code obfuscation framework that defends against LLM-based code analysis. Acoda leverages two key mechanisms of LLMs, namely safety alignment and token-based information processing, to design 8 semantics-preserving obfuscation methods. It iteratively optimizes obfuscation strategies through a genetic algorithm to generate adversarial samples that maximize defensive effectiveness. In addition, we propose a quantitative evaluation framework based on LLM responses, which combines an auxiliary LLM and four evaluation metrics to assess how target LLMs analyze obfuscated code comprehensively. Experimental results show that Acoda can effectively induce LLMs to refuse or misinterpret code analysis. On 7 state-of-the-art LLMs, including GPT-4o, DeepSeek, Qwen, Llama, and Gemma, Acoda achieves an attack success rate (ASR) of up to 70%, with strong cross-model transferability and minimal runtime overhead, while ensuring that the semantics of the original code remain unchanged. Overall, this study provides a new perspective for code protection and LLM security defense in the era of LLMs.

</details>


### [173] [Understanding and Detecting Scalability Faults in Large-Scale Distributed Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11815)
*Hao-Nan Zhu,Goodness Ayinmode,Cesar A. Stuardo,Haryadi S. Gunawi,Cindy Rubio-González*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A systematic study of 444 scalability issues across 10 distributed systems reveals that scalability faults predominantly stem from "dimensional code fragments" interacting with specific anti-patterns. The authors introduce ScaleLens, a hybrid static-dynamic analysis tool that detects such fragments, outperforming baselines by 4.2x and finding 334 problematic fragments in Cassandra, HDFS, and Ignite.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalability faults in large-scale distributed systems are latent, manifesting only at scale, making them hard to detect and diagnose. Prior work lacks a systematic understanding of their root causes and no targeted automated detection approach exists.

Method: The authors first conducted an empirical study of 444 scalability issue reports from 10 distributed systems (Cassandra, HDFS, HBase, Ignite, Kafka, etc.) to identify common anti-patterns and root causes. Based on these findings, they designed ScaleLens, which combines dynamic analysis (profiling to identify execution paths whose cost grows with scale) and static analysis (code pattern matching) to pinpoint "dimensional code fragments" and match them against known scalability anti-patterns.

Result: The empirical study revealed that most scalability faults arise from the synergy between dimensional code fragments and anti-patterns. ScaleLens detected 4.2x more dimensional code fragments tied to known scalability faults than the baseline approach. On recent stable versions of Cassandra, HDFS, and Ignite, it identified 334 dimensional code fragments with confirmed problematic behavior.

Conclusion: Scalability faults are systematic and predictable, driven by identifiable anti-patterns in dimensional code. ScaleLens demonstrates that automated, hybrid analysis can effectively detect these faults before deployment, offering a practical tool for improving distributed system reliability at scale.

Abstract: Scalable distributed systems form the backbone of modern computing infrastructure. However, as scale grows, system complexity may lead to scalability faults. Scalability faults are challenging to uncover and diagnose, as they are often latent and only manifest at large-scale deployment. In this paper, we present the first comprehensive study on scalability faults and propose an approach for their detection. First, we systematically investigate 444 scalability issue reports from 10 large-scale distributed systems to understand the common anti-patterns and root causes of scalability faults. We found that the majority of these faults are caused by the synergy between dimensional code fragments and anti-patterns associated with them. Second, based on our findings, we design and implement ScaleLens, a novel approach to detect scalability faults. ScaleLens combines dynamic and static analyses to pinpoint dimensional code fragments and match them with anti-patterns. Our evaluation shows that ScaleLens detects 4.2x more dimensional code fragments associated with known scalability faults compared to the baseline. On the latest stable versions of Cassandra, HDFS, and Ignite, ScaleLens detects 334 dimensional code fragments with confirmed problematic behavior.

</details>


### [174] [How Requirements Quality Makes (or Breaks) Traceability Link Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11834)
*Tobias Hey,Julian Frattini*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This study empirically investigates how 28 types of quality defects in use case descriptions affect the performance of five automated traceability link recovery (TLR) approaches. Results reveal that some defects harm performance (e.g., sentences lacking noun phrases), while others unexpectedly improve it (e.g., inclusion of implementation details), and that different TLR approaches respond differently to these defects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated traceability link recovery is vital for software maintenance, but manually establishing traces is costly and error-prone. While TLR approaches have been extensively studied, the impact of requirements quality defects on TLR performance lacks empirical evidence. The authors seek to fill this gap by systematically quantifying how various requirement defects influence different TLR techniques.

Method: The authors annotated 28 types of quality defects across 189 use case descriptions from two datasets. They then applied five distinct TLR approaches to recover trace links between requirements and source code, measured performance metrics for each approach, and conducted statistical tests to quantify the effect strength of each defect type on TLR performance.

Result: Certain quality defects negatively impact TLR performance (e.g., sentences not beginning with noun phrases), while others positively impact it (e.g., use cases containing implementation details). Critically, different TLR approaches exhibit varying sensitivity to different defect types, meaning the optimal choice of TLR approach depends on the quality characteristics of the underlying dataset.

Conclusion: Requirements quality defects have a measurable and heterogeneous effect on automated traceability link recovery. The performance-optimizing selection of a TLR approach is contingent on dataset quality, suggesting that practitioners should assess and account for requirement defects when choosing or designing TLR methods.

Abstract: Traceability information between requirements and source code greatly benefits the maintenance of a software system. Since manually establishing trace links is cumbersome and error-prone, previous research explored automated traceability link recovery (TLR) approaches to support this task. However, quality defects in requirements impact subsequent activities such as TLR, yet evidence about this remains scarce. Our objective is to contribute empirical evidence on this impact. At the same time, we aim to understand how the performance of TLR approaches varies given these quality defects. To this end, we annotated 28 types of quality defect in 189 use case descriptions from two datasets. Then, we executed five distinct TLR approaches on the dataset and measured their performance in recovering trace links. Finally, we performed statistical tests to quantify the defects' effect strength on this performance. Our results show that some quality defects harm TLR performance, e.g., sentences that do not start with noun phrases, while others actually benefit performance, e.g., use cases that include implementation details. Moreover, different types of approaches respond differently to these defects. As a consequence, the performance-optimizing choice of a TLR approach depends on the quality of the dataset.

</details>


### [175] [Enhancing LLM-Based Code Translation with Verified Multi-Semantic Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11863)
*Yufu Wang,He Jiang,Hao Lin,Peiyu Zou,Ang Jia,Xiaochen Li,Zhilei Ren*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Multisage is a framework that improves LLM-based code translation by extracting structured semantics from source code (data-flow graphs, type constraints, API info), augmenting them into diverse forms (summaries, test cases, API descriptions), and self-calibrating via semantics-preserving mutations and cross-semantic consistency checks. It achieves up to 2.22× improvement on HumanEval-X.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based code translation relies on token-level statistical patterns rather than deep program semantics, leading to logical and semantic errors in translated programs. While functional descriptions and test cases can help, they are rarely available in practice. The core challenges are: (1) how to construct rich semantic information directly from source code, and (2) how to ensure such semantics are accurate and reliable enough to guide translation.

Method: Multisage comprises three modules: (1) Semantic Representation Parsing — extracts structured base semantics from source code including data-flow graphs, type constraints, and external API information; (2) Multi-Semantic Augmentation — generates diverse augmented semantics (code summaries, function-level test cases, API-oriented descriptions and tests) from these representations; (3) Semantic Consistency Calibration — applies semantics-preserving mutations and cross-semantic consistency verification to filter, calibrate, and refine the generated semantics before they guide translation.

Result: On the HumanEval-X code translation benchmark, Multisage improves translation success rates by up to 2.22× across diverse backbone models. It consistently outperforms vanilla prompting, instruction-tuned LLMs, and Chain-of-Thought reasoning, with the largest gains observed on smaller models.

Conclusion: Explicit semantic augmentation can substantially improve the reliability of LLM-based code translation. The framework demonstrates that structured, multi-faceted semantics derived from source code, combined with self-calibration, effectively compensates for the absence of manually curated semantic resources in real-world translation scenarios.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown great promise for automated code translation, yet existing approaches often rely on token-level statistical patterns rather than sufficient understanding of program semantics. As a result, translated programs may still contain logical and semantic errors. Although high-quality semantic guidance, such as functional descriptions and test cases, can help mitigate these errors, such resources are often unavailable in real-world scenarios. This raises two key challenges: how to construct rich semantic information directly from source code, and how to ensure that such semantics are accurate and reliable enough to guide translation.To address these challenges, we propose Multisage, a multi-semantic augmentation and self-calibration framework for LLM-based code translation. Multisage consists of three modules. First, a semantic representation parsing module extracts structured base semantics from source code, including data-flow graphs, type constraints, and external API information. Second, a multi-semantic augmentation module builds on these representations to generate diverse augmented semantics, including code summaries, function-level test cases, and API-oriented descriptions and tests. Third, a semantic consistency calibration module uses semantics-preserving mutations and cross-semantic consistency verification to filter, calibrate, and refine the generated semantics.Experiments on the HumanEval-X code translation benchmark show that Multisage improves translation success rates by up to 2.22 times across diverse backbone models. It consistently outperforms vanilla prompting, instruction-tuned LLMs, and Chain-of-Thought reasoning, with the largest gains observed on smaller models. These results demonstrate that explicit semantic augmentation can substantially improve the reliability of LLM-based code translation.

</details>


### [176] [Agents All the Way Down; A Methodology for Building Custom AI Agents from Substrate to Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11869)
*Marc Alier Forment,Juanan Pereira,Francisco José García-Peñalvo,María José Casañ Guerrero*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents "Agents All the Way Down," a methodology for building custom, single-purpose AI agents from scratch using existing building blocks (function-calling APIs, MCP, CLI orchestration). It defines two preconditions and three iterative practices, demonstrated through a production agent built in ~10 days.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the proliferation of AI agent components, no published end-to-end methodology exists for building custom, fit-for-purpose agents. Current knowledge is fragmented across podcasts, blogs, and leaked system prompts. The paper aims to codify a transferable, framework-free practice.

Method: The authors propose a methodology with two preconditions — (P1) treating the LLM as a software component (Substrate) framed through tools, system prompts, and messages with prompt-caching; (P2) assembling building blocks such as function calling, the Model Context Protocol, CLI orchestration, the liteshell pattern, agent loops, skills, characters, hooks, and scaffolding. Three iterative practices follow: (P3) prototype using a general-purpose agent; (P4) harvest, fold, and ship the result as a CLI (the "Turtle pattern"); and (P5) "agent-tests-agent," where a general-purpose agent drives the custom agent through behavioral scenarios. The loop cycles P3→P4→P5→back.

Result: The methodology was distilled from building the AAC, a custom agent for the open-source LAMB platform, constructed by a single developer with an AI pair-programmer in approximately ten days and deployed in production. A corollary emerged: multi-agent orchestration reduces to CLI composition.

Conclusion: The "Agents All the Way Down" methodology provides a repeatable, framework-agnostic practice for building custom AI agents. It is offered as transferable knowledge independent of any language or framework, filling the documented gap between available components and integrated practice.

Abstract: Custom AI agents areagents that live inside their own
  application, talk to their own data and tools, enforce their own security boundaries,
  and carry their own brand and audit trail. What separates them from the general-purpose
  tier is fit, not capability: each is built for one job, by the
  engineer who will maintain it. No published practice sets out how to build one end to
  end. The pieces are everywhere (function-calling APIs, the Model Context Protocol, code
  agents to pair with), but the practice that chains them lives in podcasts, blogs, and
  leaked system prompts. This paper writes that practice down as a methodology, Agents All
  the Way Down: two preconditions crossed once and kept, then three practices repeated
  for the agent's life. The preconditions are (P1) Substrate, the LLM as a software
  component, framed as tools, then system, then messages under prompt-caching; and (P2)
  Building blocks: function calling, MCP, CLI orchestration, the liteshell pattern, the
  agent loop, skills, characters, hooks, and scaffolding. The practices are (P3) prototype
  with a general-purpose agent; (P4) harvest, fold, and ship the result as a CLI, the
  Turtle pattern; and (P5) agent-tests-agent, in which a general-purpose agent drives it
  through behavioural scenarios, a complement to classical testing, not a replacement. The
  working loop is P3 to P4 to P5 and back, and one corollary falls out for free:
  multi-agent orchestration is just CLI composition. The methodology is framework-free by
  construction. It was distilled from the AAC, a custom agent for the open-source LAMB
  platform, built in about ten days by one developer with an AI pair-programmer and in
  production . We present it as a transferable practice, independent of any language or framework.

</details>


### [177] [Characterizing Software Aging in GPU-Based LLM Serving Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11916)
*Domenico Cotroneo,Bojan Cukic*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents an empirical methodology for studying software aging in GPU-based LLM serving systems, running a 216-hour experiment across six deployments. It finds statistically significant memory aging in all cases, with leak rates depending on the serving runtime and configuration, and offers a reproducible framework bridging software aging/rejuvenation and LLM serving research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional software aging research focuses on CPU-centric systems with regular workloads, but LLM serving systems differ fundamentally: they span Python hosts and CUDA devices, handle requests with costs varying by orders of magnitude, and rely on rapidly evolving software stacks. No established methodology exists for studying aging in this context.

Method: A 216-hour campaign runs six co-located LLM serving deployments under identical stress conditions. Host, device, and client metrics are monitored in parallel. A statistical pipeline accounting for autocorrelation and multiple testing is applied to the collected data to rigorously identify aging signals.

Result: Statistically significant memory aging is detected in all six deployments. Memory leak rates exhibit strong dependence on both the serving runtime (e.g., vLLM, TGI) and the specific deployment configuration.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that software aging is a real and measurable phenomenon in GPU-based LLM serving systems. The proposed framework is reproducible and establishes a new research direction at the intersection of software aging/rejuvenation and LLM serving communities.

Abstract: This paper proposes an empirical methodology to study software aging in GPU-based LLM serving systems. Traditional aging studies focus on CPU-centric software with relatively regular workloads; LLM serving is different, spanning a Python host and a CUDA device, handling requests whose cost varies by orders of magnitude, and relying on rapidly evolving software stacks. We run a 216-hour campaign across six co-located deployments under identical stress conditions, monitor host, device, and client metrics in parallel, and apply a statistical pipeline that accounts for autocorrelation and multiple testing. Our results reveal statistically significant memory aging in all deployments, with leak rates strongly dependent on the serving runtime and deployment configuration. Beyond these findings, we provide a reproducible framework that opens a research direction at the intersection of the software aging and rejuvenation and LLM serving communities.

</details>


### [178] [Exploration Structure in LLM Agents for Multi-File Change Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11976)
*Akeela Darryl Fattha,Kia Ying Chua,Lingxiao Jiang,Laura Wynter*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper compares linear vs. non-linear, domain-scoped parallel agentic exploration for file localization in software engineering tasks. Domain-scoped parallel agents using a small Haiku-class model achieve the highest micro F1 among comparable models, second only to a much larger Codex 5.5 High. Additional findings highlight unresolved documentation dependencies, naive file system access degrading localization, and ineffectiveness of forced multi-agent consultation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based AI agents for software issue resolution explore repositories linearly (one directory/file per step), creating a structural mismatch for changes spanning multiple subsystems. The authors aim to demonstrate that non-linear, domain-scoped parallel exploration is more effective.

Method: Using SWE Bench Pro as a starting benchmark with Ansible as the exemplar, the authors build a persistent-session evaluation framework anchored at a single base commit. They compare: (1) a base LLM without direct repository access, (2) a single-agent Recursive Language Model (RLM) baseline with a persistent Python REPL, (3) an external CLI baseline using Codex 5.5 High, and (4) their non-linear domain-agent file traversal system. They evaluate on both the original curated 2020 SWE-bench Pro and an expanded benchmark including more recent PRs from 2025–2026.

Result: Domain-scoped parallel agent spawning with a small Haiku-class model achieves the highest micro F1 among Haiku-class models by a large margin and ranks second overall behind only the much larger Codex 5.5 High on the expanded benchmark. On the original SWE-bench Pro, a larger Sonnet plain LLM baseline achieves higher micro F1 via predicting few files (higher precision) but at significantly lower recall. Three additional findings: (1) documentation evolution is a latent dependency unresolved by any approach; (2) naive file system access degrades localization through test-file over-prediction; (3) forced multi-agent consultation does not measurably help and substantially raises token cost.

Conclusion: Non-linear, domain-scoped parallel agentic exploration is a superior strategy for file localization in multi-subsystem software changes. Modest models with structured parallel exploration can rival or exceed much larger models. Practical limitations remain around documentation co-evolution, naive file system access, and the cost-ineffectiveness of forced agent consultation.

Abstract: Software engineering tools increasingly rely on LLM based agents to localize files to change to resolve a software issue. Most AI agents explore repositories linearly, that is, visiting one directory or file per step. We postulate that this is a structural mismatch for changes that span several subsystems. We compare linear sequential exploration against non-linear, domain-scoped parallel agentic exploration. Using SWE Bench Pro as initial benchmark, we focus on ansible as an exemplar. We construct an approach for persistent-session evaluation of GitHub issues anchored at a single base commit. We compare our non-linear domain-agent file traversal system against a base LLM without direct repository access, a single agent Recursive Language Model (RLM) baseline with a persistent Python REPL and an external CLI baseline using Codex 5.5 High. Domain scoped parallel agent spawning with a small Haiku-class model achieves the highest micro F1 among Haiku class models by a large margin. Domain-agents is the second highest behind only the much larger Codex 5.5 High on our own expanded benchmark including over more recent PRs from 2025 and 2026. On the original, curated, 2020 SWE-bench Pro benchmark, a larger Sonnet plain LLM baseline attains higher micro F1 by predicting few files, leading to higher precision, but at significantly lower all gold recall. We also present three additional findings. First, documentation evolution is a latent dependency unresolved by any approach. Second, naive file system access can degrade localization driven by test-file over prediction. Lastly, forced multi-agent consultation does not measurably help and raises token cost substantially.

</details>


### [179] [Undefined Behavior in C and C++: An Experiment With Desktop Use Cases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12064)
*Jukka Ruohonen,Krzysztof Sierszecki*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: An empirical study using compiler-based sanitizers reveals that undefined behavior—particularly involving virtual table pointers—is pervasive in everyday Linux desktop usage, with nearly 11,000 unique warnings generated from just 59 simple tasks across 32 C/C++ programs and libraries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To empirically quantify and characterize the prevalence of undefined behavior (UB) occurring during routine, real-world desktop use of a Linux distribution, moving beyond theoretical or isolated analyses to understand UB's practical footprint in widely-deployed C and C++ software.
