Method: Instrumented a compiler with an undefined behavior sanitizer and conducted 59 typical desktop tasks on a Linux distribution. Collected and categorized unique UB warnings—by program/library, by UB type, by stack trace length—to profile where and how UB manifests in actual use.

Result: Nearly 11,000 unique UB warnings were triggered by 32 distinct programs/libraries. The Mesa graphics library dominated, with over 500 warnings merely from logging into GNOME. The vast majority of warnings concerned virtual table pointer issues, and stack traces associated with UB were generally lengthy.

Conclusion: Undefined behavior is not a rare edge case but is common and concentrated in specific components (graphics stack, particularly Mesa). The predominance of vtable-related UB and long stack traces suggests UB often originates deep in complex, heavily-used system libraries, contributing empirical grounding to discussions of C/C++ language safety.

Abstract: Undefined behavior is idiomatic to C and C++ programming; such behavior is a use of an erroneous program construct for which the languages impose no requirements, such as integer overflows. The paper presents an empirical experiment seeking to probe the extent of undefined behavior executing underneath typical desktop use of a Linux distribution. The analysis is based on an undefined behavior sanitizer implemented in a compiler. According to the results, undefined behavior is common. By completing 59 simple experimental tasks, nearly 11 thousand unique undefined behavior warnings were generated by 32 unique programs and libraries written in C or C++. Of these warnings, most were associated with the Mesa graphics library and generated by interacting with graphical user interfaces. Merely logging into the GNOME desktop environment generated over 500 unique warnings. Of all warnings, the clear majority was about virtual table pointers. The associated stack traces were also lengthy in general. With these and other results, the paper contributes to the empirical literature on C and C++.

</details>


### [180] [Mind your key: An Empirical Study of LLM API Credential Leakage in iOS Apps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12212)
*Pinran Gao,Lingxiang Wang,Ying Zhang,Fan Yang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: First empirical study of LLM API key leakage in iOS apps, using a custom dynamic analysis framework (LLMKeyLens) on 444 apps. Found 282 apps (63.5%) leaking exploitable LLM credentials, with three leakage patterns identified. Only 28% were remediated after three months of responsible disclosure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are rapidly integrated into mobile apps, creating a new attack surface: leaked API credentials that enable unauthorized (and costly) access to LLM inference services. Prior research only studied Android credential leakage; no systematic study exists for iOS, leaving a significant empirical gap given iOS's distinct security model and app ecosystem.

Method: The authors built a dataset of 444 iOS apps (filtered from 1,092 candidates) and developed LLMKeyLens, a dynamic analysis framework that operates without source code or binary decryption. It works via: (1) traffic interception to capture network communications, (2) provider-specific key extraction using pattern matching against known LLM provider API key formats, and (3) active validity confirmation by testing extracted credentials against actual provider endpoints.

Result: 282 of 444 apps (63.5%) exposed exploitable LLM API keys across ≥10 providers. Three leakage patterns emerged: JWT-based token leakage (48%), unauthenticated backend proxy access (33%), and plaintext API key transmission (19%). After responsible disclosure and a 3-month waiting period, only 28% of the 282 vulnerable apps had been remediated; 72% remained exploitable, with root causes traced to unauthenticated backends and broken JWT implementations.

Conclusion: LLM API key leakage is both prevalent and persistent in the iOS ecosystem, revealing a systemic gap between developer practices and secure integration principles. The authors argue that addressing this requires not just developer education but also explicit security guidance from LLM providers and platform-level enforcement mechanisms.

Abstract: The rapid integration of large language models (LLMs) into mobile applications has introduced a new class of credential security risk: leaked credentials that grant unauthorized access to LLM inference services, causing financial damage to developers. Prior work on credential leakage has focused primarily on Android apps; to date, no empirical study has systematically investigated LLM API key leakage in iOS applications.
  We present the first in-depth empirical study of API key leakage in LLM-integrated apps. We construct a high-quality dataset of 444 iOS applications, filtered from 1092 candidates through a standardized process, and develop LLMKeyLens, a dynamic analysis framework that detects LLM API key leakage via traffic interception, provider-specific key extraction, and active validity confirmation, requiring neither source code access nor binary decryption. Our analysis reveals that 282 applications expose exploitable LLM API credentials in network traffic, spanning at least ten providers. We identify three leakage patterns: JWT-based token leakage (48%), unauthenticated backend proxy access (33%), and plaintext API key transmission (19%). To assess remediation, we re-analyzed the same 282 vulnerable applications three months after responsible disclosure; only 28% had remediated the reported vulnerability, while 72% remained exploitable, with persistent issues stemming from unauthenticated backends and broken JWT implementations.
  Our findings show that LLM API key leakage is both prevalent and persistent in the iOS ecosystem, exposing a systemic gap between developer practice and secure integration principles, and suggest that secure LLM integration requires not only developer awareness but also explicit security guidance from providers and platform-level enforcement.

</details>


### [181] [Rule Taxonomy and Evolution in AI IDEs: A Mining and Survey Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12231)
*Guangzong Cai,Ruiyin Li,Peng Liang,Zengyang Li,Mojtaba Shahin*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: An empirical study of AI IDE "Rules" — persistent context injections for LLMs — based on mining 7,310 rules from 83 open-source projects and surveying 99 practitioners. The authors establish a taxonomy (5 primary, 25 secondary categories), reveal a mismatch between practitioner priorities (architectural constraints) and actual rule content (workflow/formatting), analyze 1,540 evolution events showing rules are frequently updated primarily via context expansion, and demonstrate that rule updates improve artifact compliance by ~23%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-powered IDEs have introduced "Rules" as a mechanism for developers to persistently inject project-specific constraints into LLM context, yet the taxonomy, evolution patterns, and practical impact of these rules remain unexplored. There is no systematic understanding of what types of rules exist, how they evolve, or whether they actually improve AI-generated code quality.

Method: A mixed-methods empirical study: (1) mining 83 open-source repositories to extract 7,310 rules and constructing a taxonomy (5 primary, 25 secondary categories); (2) surveying 99 practitioners on rule priorities and modification practices; (3) analyzing 1,540 rule evolution events from repository histories; and (4) conducting an artifact compliance assessment on 160 evolution events to measure whether rule updates improve adherence.

Result: The taxonomy covers 5 primary and 25 secondary categories. A key contrast emerged: practitioners rate architectural constraints highly, but repositories are dominated by workflow and code formatting rules. Rule evolution is frequent and driven by context expansion (29.17%) and enrichment (26.59%), whereas developers self-report modifying rules primarily to correct AI errors (77.78%), typically adding negative constraints rather than editing existing ones. Rule updates improve artifact compliance by 22.99% on average (from 49.14% to 72.13%).

Conclusion: AI IDE rules are a dynamic and impactful artifact class. The mismatch between developer intent and actual rule content, combined with the demonstrated compliance benefits of rule updates, suggests that tool builders should design automated conflict-detection and context-management mechanisms, while developers can optimize prompting strategies based on empirical insights from the taxonomy and evolution patterns.

Abstract: The adoption of AI-powered Integrated Development Environments (AI IDEs) has introduced "Rules" as a novel software artifact, allowing developers to persistently inject project-specific constraints and architectural guidelines into the context of Large Language Models (LLMs). Despite their role in aligning AI behavior with developer intent, the taxonomy, evolution, and practical impact of these rules remain largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we conducted a mixed-methods empirical study on AI IDE rules. By mining 83 open-source projects and extracting 7,310 rules, we established a comprehensive taxonomy comprising 5 primary and 25 secondary categories. We then triangulated these artifacts with survey responses from 99 practitioners. Our analysis identified a contrast between developer priorities and actual configurations: while practitioners rate architectural constraints as highly important, rule files in repositories primarily consist of low-level workflow and code formatting constraints. Furthermore, our analysis of 1,540 rule evolution events revealed that rules are updated frequently. Repository data further indicate that rule evolution is primarily driven by constructive context expansions (29.17%) and enrichments (26.59%). In contrast, surveyed developers reported modifying rules primarily to correct AI errors (77.78%), typically by adding new negative constraints rather than editing existing ones. Finally, an artifact compliance assessment of 160 rule evolution events revealed that updating rules significantly improves the adherence of software artifacts, with the average artifact compliance rate increasing by 22.99% (from 49.14% to 72.13%) following an update. Our study provides empirical insights that can help developers optimize prompting strategies and guide tool builders in designing automated conflict-detection and context-management mechanisms for AI IDEs.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [182] [Multi-agent rendezvous in fluid flows via reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11274)
*Bocheng Li,Jingran Qiu,Lihao Zhao*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: MARL-trained agents learn physics-informed rendezvous strategies in vortical flows that break symmetry to avoid trapping in separate vortices, significantly outperforming naive approaches. The learned strategy transfers across flow conditions and swarm sizes, and theoretical analysis confirms that fluid deformation (measured by finite-time Lyapunov exponents) impedes rendezvous.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rendezvous is critical for multi-agent systems, but achieving coordination at an unspecified location in fluid environments is challenging. It is unclear how agents can exploit underlying flow kinematics to facilitate convergence, motivating the need for strategies that leverage agent-fluid interactions.

Method: The authors employ multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to develop rendezvous strategies in vortical flows. They compare MARL strategies against a naive baseline (agents navigating directly toward counterparts), test transferability across vortex intensities, scales, and swarm sizes, extract a heuristic strategy, and perform theoretical analysis using finite-time Lyapunov exponents (FTLEs) to characterize fluid deformation effects.

Result: MARL strategies significantly improve rendezvous rates over the naive strategy. The learned policy breaks state-action map symmetry, preventing agents from becoming trapped in separate vortices. MARL strategies transfer well across varying flow conditions and swarm sizes. The extracted heuristic also outperforms the naive strategy. FTLE analysis shows large values identify regions where fluid deformation separates adjacent agents, indicating targets should be planned in weak-deformation regions.

Conclusion: Agent-fluid interactions play a crucial role in multi-agent tasks. MARL effectively discovers swarm intelligence in complex flow environments, yielding strategies that exploit flow kinematics rather than fight them. Planning targets in low-deformation (low-FTLE) regions is key to successful rendezvous in fluid settings.

Abstract: Rendezvous is a critical task for multi-agent systems, requiring agents to coordinate to meet at an unspecified location. However, achieving this in fluid environments presents a challenge, as it remains unclear how agents can exploit underlying fluid kinematics to facilitate convergence. In this study, we adopt a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) approach to develop physics-informed rendezvous strategies in vortical flows. Compared to a naive strategy, where agents navigate toward their counterparts, MARL strategies significantly improve the rendezvous rate. MARL strategies also show transferability across varying vortex intensities, vortex scales, and swarm sizes. By breaking the symmetry of the state-action map, MARL strategy leverages a non-intuitive mechanism that prevents agents from becoming trapped in separate vortices, thereby enhancing rendezvous success. Additionally, a heuristic strategy is extracted from the learned strategy and also outperforms the naive strategy. Furthermore, a theoretical analysis demonstrates that fluid deformation impedes the rendezvous process. Large finite-time Lyapunov exponents identify where fluid effects separate adjacent agents, suggesting that targets should be planned in weak-deformation regions. Our findings reveal the important role that agent-fluid interactions play in multi-agent tasks and highlight the MARL capability to explore swarm intelligence in complex flow environments.

</details>


### [183] [Phi-Actor-Critic: Steering General-Sum Games to Pareto-Efficient Correlated Equilibria](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11284)
*Wongyu Lee,Francesco Lelli,Omran Ayoub,Massimo Tornatore*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Φ-Actor-Critic (Φ-AC) is a deep MARL framework that uses swap regret minimization and a centralized attention critic to steer learning toward high-welfare correlated equilibria, with a Lagrangian mechanism for equilibrium selection. It outperforms standard methods on matrix games, MPE, and Melting Pot environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In general-sum multi-agent settings, standard MARL methods either converge to socially inefficient Nash equilibria (policy gradients) or are limited by monotonicity constraints (value decomposition). The core challenge is selecting socially desirable equilibria among many suboptimal ones.

Method: Φ-AC employs: (1) swap regret minimization to guide convergence toward correlated equilibria rather than arbitrary Nash equilibria; (2) a centralized attention critic that computes vector-valued counterfactual regrets in a single forward pass, eliminating costly per-action simulations; and (3) a Lagrangian-based equilibrium selection mechanism that maximizes social welfare subject to regret-based stability constraints.

Result: Φ-AC demonstrates efficient and stable coordination across matrix games, Multi-Agent Particle Environments (MPE), and the Melting Pot Harvest scenario, achieving high collective return and competitive fairness compared to standard MARL baselines.

Conclusion: By integrating swap regret minimization with deep MARL, Φ-AC successfully addresses the equilibrium selection problem in general-sum games, enabling agents to learn socially efficient and stable coordination strategies without sacrificing fairness or convergence guarantees.

Abstract: Real-world multi-agent systems, from traffic coordination to resource allocation, are often modeled as general-sum games where individual incentives conflict with collective welfare. In these settings, the central challenge is not merely finding an equilibrium, but selecting socially desirable outcomes among many suboptimal Nash equilibria. Standard deep multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods struggle with this problem, as value-decomposition approaches are constrained by monotonicity assumptions and policy-gradient methods often converge to stable but socially inefficient equilibria. To address this limitation, we propose $Φ$-Actor-Critic ($Φ$-AC), a framework that leverages swap regret minimization to steer learning toward high-welfare correlated equilibria (CE). To make counterfactual regret estimation tractable in deep MARL, $Φ$-AC employs a centralized attention critic that predicts vector-valued regrets in a single forward pass, avoiding computationally expensive counterfactual simulations. We further introduce a Lagrangian-based equilibrium selection mechanism that optimizes social welfare while enforcing stability through regret constraints. Experiments on matrix games, Multi-Agent Particle Environments (MPE), and the Melting Pot Harvest scenario demonstrate that $Φ$-AC learns efficient and stable coordination strategies across diverse mixed-motive settings while maintaining high collective return and competitive fairness.

</details>


### [184] [CCKS: Consensus-based Communication and Knowledge Sharing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12281)
*Jinyuan Zu,Xiaowei Lv,Yongcai Wang,Deying Li,Yunjun Han,Wenping Chen,Fengyi Zhang,Naiqi Wu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: CCKS proposes a consensus-based knowledge sharing framework for DTDE-MARL that uses contrastive learning to build consensus models from local observations, enabling agents to smartly filter teacher advice and improve cooperation efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current action-advising methods in DTDE-MARL blindly follow teacher guidance without evaluating teacher-student compatibility, leading to excessive advising, suboptimal stability, and degraded performance.

Method: CCKS uses contrastive learning to construct consensus models from local observations during training. During action selection, agents score and choose actions based on both consensus-derived constraints and shared knowledge. The framework is plug-and-play compatible with existing DTDE algorithms.

Result: Experiments on Google Research Football and StarCraft II Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) show CCKS significantly improves cooperation efficiency, learning speed, and overall performance compared to DTDE baselines.

Conclusion: CCKS effectively balances exploration with learning from experienced teachers via consensus-based constraints, demonstrating that selective, compatibility-aware knowledge sharing outperforms indiscriminate action advising in cooperative MARL.

Abstract: In Decentralized Training and Decentralized Execution (DTDE) for cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL), action-advising-based knowledge sharing promotes interpretable and scalable cooperation among agents. However, current action advising approaches often adhere too much to the teacher's guidance without evaluating teacher-student compatibility, which causes excessive advising, suboptimal stability, and degraded performance. To overcome these challenges, this paper presents a Consensus-based Communication and Knowledge Sharing (CCKS) framework, which allows agents to adopt recommendations based on consensus-derived constraints and to follow the teacher's instructions more smartly. This mechanism enables agents to balance exploration and learning from experienced teachers, improving overall performance. The key is the consensus model construction, for which we propose to employ contrastive learning to construct consensus models based on local observations in the agents' training phase. In action selection, agents score and choose actions based on consensus and shared knowledge. Designed as a plug-and-play solution, CCKS integrates seamlessly with existing DTDE algorithms. Experiments conducted in the Google Research Football environment and the complex StarCraft II Multi-Agent Challenge demonstrate that the integration with CCKS significantly improves cooperation efficiency, learning speed, and overall performance compared with current DTDE baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/yuanxpy/CCKS.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [185] [Towards a Joint Understanding of Remote Operation for Vehicles in Public Road Traffic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11336)
*Elisabeth Shi,Maria-Magdalena Wolf,Nina Theobald,Bettina Abendroth,Eugen Wige,Johannes Springer,Katharina Hottelart,Andreas Schrank,Thorben Brandt,Michael Oehl,Frank Diermeyer,Lena Plum*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified terminological framework for remote operation (teleoperation) of automated vehicles, tracing definitions back to differences in human and vehicle information processing. It aims to bridge interdisciplinary communication gaps among researchers, engineers, psychologists, legal experts, and regulators as driverless mobility services move toward real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current driving automation systems cannot handle all traffic situations, creating a need for remote operation to enable driverless mobility services without an in-vehicle fallback driver. However, the interdisciplinary discourse surrounding remote operation—spanning engineering, psychology, law, and informatics—suffers from inconsistent terminology and differing expectations across stakeholders. A common conceptual ground is urgently needed, especially as early legal frameworks for remote operation on public roads begin to emerge.

Method: The article conducts a conceptual analysis that traces terminology back to the fundamental differences in information processing on both the human side (remote operator) and the vehicle side (automation system). It maps the spectrum of remote operation methods—from remote assistance (e.g., providing information, releasing maneuvers) to remote driving (direct vehicle control from a distance)—onto this information-processing framework, thereby clarifying the distinct functional requirements of each form.

Result: The resulting framework explicitly specifies what is needed to engage a diverse audience of researchers and stakeholders. It integrates recently discussed forms of teleoperation into a coherent structure, enabling clearer differentiation between remote assistance and remote driving based on how information flows and decisions are allocated between human and machine.

Conclusion: A shared, information-processing-based terminology is essential for productive interdisciplinary collaboration on remote operation. The framework provides a foundation for aligning stakeholders—including service providers, operators, manufacturers, and regulators—as remote operation moves toward safe implementation in public road traffic.

Abstract: Sustained driving automation systems are envisioned to be used as the foundation for driverless mobility services. However, both researchers and practitioners acknowledge that current driving automation systems are not yet able to handle all traffic situations that a human driver can handle. To bridge this gap and enable mobility services without an in-vehicle human driver or fallback, remote operation (or teleoperation) is increasingly discussed. Recently, first legal actions have been taken to enable some forms of remote operation on public roads. Remote operation encompasses a broad spectrum of methods to support a driving automation system, ranging from remote assistance, which includes providing information or releasing a maneuver, to remote driving, which includes driving the vehicle from a remote location. As such, safe implementation of remote operation in public road traffic challenges the collaboration of multiple academic disciplines (e.g. engineering, psychology, informatics, law, etc.) and stakeholders (e.g. remote operation service providers, remote operators, vehicle manufacturers, regulatory authorities, etc.). At the same time, the interdisciplinary discourse is often challenging due to differing expectations and language. To build a common ground, this article traces terminology back to the original differences in information processing both on human and vehicle side. This framework aims to help further discourse by directly specifying what is needed to engage a diverse audience including researchers and stakeholders of different backgrounds and interests. Recently discussed forms of teleoperation are integrated into this framework.

</details>


### [186] [3-Key-Input: Exploring the Theoretical Minimum Keys for Text Entry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11642)
*Naoki Kimura*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper evaluates text entry with 2–5 physical keys augmented by language models (Trie, GPT-2, GPT-4o). It finds 3 keys with GPT-4o as a practical minimum (CER 9.46%, WER 12.20%), with diminishing returns beyond 3 keys. Letter-to-key mapping matters little, and technical text is significantly harder than business text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reducing physical keys on keyboards increases hardware design flexibility for constrained settings (assistive devices, mobile/wearable form factors). The paper asks: how few keys can we get away with when modern language models handle the disambiguation?

Method: Systematic evaluation on a 300-sentence English corpus (Business/Conversational/Technical, 100 each). Factors varied: number of keys (2–5), letter-to-key mappings (layout-based, frequency-based, intentionally worst-case), and decoders (Trie-only, GPT-2 beam search, GPT-4o selection). Key-stream entropy and error rates (CER/WER) are measured.

Result: 3 keys + GPT-4o: CER 9.46%, WER 12.20% (59% CER reduction vs. 2-key CER 23.3%). 5 keys improve to CER 5.4% but with diminishing marginal gains. Key-stream entropy at 3 keys: 1.54 bits/char. Mapping choice has minimal impact under standard designs (ΔCER < 0.5 pp); even worst-case mapping adds only +0.5 pp CER. Technical sentences show ~2× error rate of Business sentences.

Conclusion: Under a strong LM prior in an offline setting, 3 physical keys represent a practical minimum for usable general English text entry. Diminishing returns, mapping robustness, and domain sensitivity are key takeaways for keyboard design.

Abstract: How far can we reduce the number of physical keys if we endow an ambiguous keyboard with modern language models? Fewer keys increase hardware design freedom in constrained settings such as assistive devices and mobile form factors. This paper systematically evaluates text entry systems using 2-5 physical keys combined with language-model-based disambiguation. On a 300-sentence English corpus (100 sentences each for Business / Conversational / Technical), we compare key counts (2-5), letter-to-key mappings (layout-based / frequency-based / intentionally worst-case), and decoders (Trie-only, GPT-2 beam search, GPT-4o selection). We find that 3 keys + GPT-4o achieves character error rate (CER) 9.46% and word error rate (WER) 12.20%, reducing CER by 59% relative to 2 keys (CER 23.3%). At 3 keys, the key-stream entropy is 1.54 bits/char; while increasing to 5 keys improves accuracy (CER 5.4%), the marginal gains diminish. Mapping choice has a small impact under standard designs (ΔCER < 0.5 pp), and even an intentionally worst mapping degrades CER by only +0.5 pp, whereas Technical sentences yield roughly twice the error rate of Business. These results suggest that, in our evaluated offline setting under a strong LM prior, 3 keys are a practical minimum for general English.

</details>


### [187] [Learning by Chatting? Investigating the Impact of Generative AI on Information Seeking and Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11669)
*Shravika Mittal,Su Lin Blodgett,Q. Vera Liao*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A field experiment comparing ChatGPT vs. Google Search for informal learning found that ChatGPT users experienced diminished agency, greater meta-cognitive load, biased/distorted information access (favoring solutions over principles), reduced knowledge exploration, and worse learning outcomes—especially for higher-order critical learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GenAI tools are rapidly reshaping how people seek information, yet their implications for learning and knowledge acquisition remain poorly understood. The authors aim to empirically investigate whether and how offloading information seeking to GenAI (vs. traditional search) affects cognitive processes and learning outcomes.

Method: Between-subjects field experiment over 8 days: participants pursued informal learning using either ChatGPT or Google Search. Data were collected in-situ via a daily diary protocol capturing real-time information-seeking processes. Outcomes measured included perceived agency, meta-cognitive load, information access distortions (output biases and behavioral shifts), and learning outcomes (including higher-order critical learning).

Result: ChatGPT users offloaded information selection to AI, reducing their perceived agency and increasing meta-cognitive load from loss of control. Two distortion sources were identified: (1) ChatGPT outputs biased toward solution-oriented artifacts rather than principled knowledge; (2) the conversational paradigm reduced users' exploration of the broader knowledge space. Consequently, ChatGPT users demonstrated worse learning outcomes than Google users, with pronounced deficits in higher-order critical learning.

Conclusion: There exist inherent tensions between offloading information seeking to GenAI and achieving meaningful learning. The findings raise concerns about AI's potential risks to human cognition, particularly by diminishing user agency, distorting information access, and impairing deeper learning outcomes.

Abstract: Generative AI (GenAI) tools offer increasing opportunities for augmenting human cognitive tasks. Among these tasks, information seeking is being rapidly reshaped by GenAI tools, with potentially profound implications for learning and knowledge acquisition. To investigate these implications, we conducted a between-subjects field experiment in which participants pursued informal learning by seeking information through either ChatGPT or Google Search over a span of 8 days. Using a daily diary protocol, we gathered in-situ data on their information-seeking processes. Our findings show that participants in the ChatGPT group experienced diminished agency in their information-seeking processes, as they offloaded much of the information selection to AI, and consequently experienced greater meta-cognitive load arising from this reduced sense of control. We further highlight two sources of distortion in information access when using ChatGPT: biases in ChatGPT outputs, particularly towards providing solution-oriented artifacts over principled knowledge; and systematic shifts in users' information-seeking behaviors, whereby the conversational and socially-oriented interaction paradigm of current GenAI tools may inadvertently reduce exploration of the broader knowledge space. As a result, on average, participants in the ChatGPT group had worse learning outcomes than those using Google, especially for higher-order critical learning. Our work suggests inherent tensions between offloading information seeking to AI and meaningful learning, and provides broader implications for understanding AI's risks to human cognition.

</details>


### [188] [Understanding and Supporting Online Discussion with Opinionated Chatbots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11693)
*Tianqi Song,Chi-Lan Yang,Zihan Liu,Zhengtao Xu,Yibin Feng,Yi-Chieh Lee*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: An experiment (N=83) found that opinionated chatbots influence users' opinion shifts and subsequent communication styles: opposing chatbots increased openness to changing views, while reinforcing chatbots promoted agreeable communication. The study reveals a design trade-off between fostering cognitive flexibility and maintaining user trust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Opinionated chatbots are increasingly deployed on online platforms and may shape public discourse, yet the impact of interacting with them on subsequent online behavior remains unexplored.

Method: A controlled experiment with 83 participants exposed to three types of opinionated chatbots (opposing, reinforcing, balanced viewpoints) and measured effects on opinion shifts, communication styles, trust, and perceptions of both chatbots and human interlocutors in subsequent discussions.

Result: Opposing chatbots induced greater opinion shifts, suggesting increased openness to revising stances. Reinforcing chatbots led to more agreeable communication styles in later human interactions. Different chatbot types also produced varying levels of trust and divergent perceptions of both the chatbots and human conversation partners.

Conclusion: Opinionated chatbots present a design trade-off: they can promote cognitive flexibility in opinion change but risk undermining user trust and experience. Designers must balance fostering constructive discourse with maintaining positive user interactions.

Abstract: Opinionated chatbots are increasingly present on online platforms and have the potential to shape public discourse by influencing individuals' viewpoints before they engage in discussions. Despite their growing presence, the impact of interacting with opinionated chatbots on subsequent online interactions remains largely unexplored. This study investigated how exposure to different types of opinionated chatbots, specifically those expressing opposing, reinforcing, or balanced viewpoints, affected participants' subsequent online discussions. In a controlled experiment with 83 participants, we found that interacting with an opinionated chatbot that consistently opposed participants' arguments led to greater shifts in opinion, indicating enhanced openness to revising one's initial stance. Conversely, participants who interacted with a chatbot that consistently reinforced their views were more likely to adopt more agreeable communication styles in subsequent conversations with others. Furthermore, interactions with different types of opinionated chatbots resulted in varying levels of trust, as well as different perceptions of chatbots and human interlocutors. Our findings indicate that opinionated chatbots can influence both individuals' opinions on social topics and their communication behaviors in online environments. This presents a trade-off for future designers seeking to facilitate cognitive flexibility in changing opinions while maintaining positive user experiences and trust in the chatbots during public discourse. We discuss the implications for designing opinionated chatbots to promote more constructive and less polarized online

</details>


### [189] [Designing AI-Supported Focus Groups: A Role x Modality Playbook](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11835)
*Zhiqing Wang,Steven Dow*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper proposes a playbook for integrating generative AI into focus groups, organizing AI supports by role (tool, co-host, host) and modality (text, voice, embodied), while characterizing methodological trade-offs and identifying open research questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Focus groups are essential in design research for surfacing lived experiences and collective sensemaking, but they are resource-intensive and highly sensitive to facilitation. While generative AI offers capabilities like prompting, turn regulation, thematic mapping, and real-time summarization for live conversations, UXR teams lack a systematic understanding of how these capabilities translate into focus group methodology and what risks they introduce.

Method: The authors synthesize prior work on AI-supported live conversation and translate these capabilities into a focus-group-specific playbook. The playbook is organized along two dimensions: AI role (tool — assisting the moderator; co-host — sharing facilitation duties; host — autonomously facilitating) and modality (text, voice, embodied interaction). They characterize interactional trade-offs associated with each configuration.

Result: The paper delivers a structured playbook mapping AI supports onto focus group facilitation, highlighting interactional trade-offs such as shifts in participation balance, topic control, and psychological safety. It also identifies open research questions regarding how to evaluate AI-supported focus groups as methodological configurations.

Conclusion: Integrating generative AI into focus groups holds promise for reducing resource burdens and enhancing facilitation, but careful methodological scrutiny is required. The proposed playbook serves as a roadmap for UXR practitioners and researchers to explore AI-supported focus groups while remaining attentive to the nuanced interactional dynamics that define quality in qualitative research.

Abstract: Collecting participants' lived experiences is central to design research. Focus groups are uniquely valuable because participants not only share individual accounts but also respond to one another, surfacing comparison, disagreement, and collective sensemaking. However, focus groups are resource-intensive and highly sensitive to facilitation: moderators must probe for specificity, balance participation, manage topic flow, and sustain psychological safety, and subtle facilitation choices can shape what becomes salient. Recent HCI work and commercial meeting tools show that generative AI can scaffold live conversation through prompting, turn regulation, thematic mapping, and real-time summarization. Yet UXR teams lack a clear map of what these capabilities mean in focus groups and what methodological risks they introduce. We synthesize AI supports for live conversation and translate them into a focus-group-specific playbook organized by AI role (tool, co-host, host) and modality (text, voice, embodied).We synthesize prior work on AI-supported live conversation and propose a focus-group-specific playbook of AI supports organized by role (tool, co-host, host) and modality (text, voice, embodied). We characterize interactional trade-offs and identify open questions for evaluating AI-supported focus groups as methodological configurations.

</details>


### [190] [PAPEL: A Collaborative System for Parental Guidance during Preschool Play-Based English Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11896)
*Xutong Wang,Yu Mei,Qinwei Li,Muyu Liu,Xiwen Yao,Chang Liu,Zhoutong Ye,Jie Cai,Chun Yu,Yuanchun Shi*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: PAPEL is a parent-AI collaborative system designed to help parents integrate English-as-a-Foreign-Language (EFL) learning into play-based interactions with preschoolers. Through formative studies, four challenges were identified (content selection, language expression, balancing instruction and play, problem solving), and PAPEL addresses them with four modules. A within-subjects study (N=16 dyads) showed PAPEL led to more integrated parent utterances and conversational turns compared to a chatbot baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parents struggle to convert open-ended play into effective EFL learning at home despite recognizing its potential. There is a gap between the opportunity play presents and parents' ability to exploit it pedagogically, motivating exploration of AI-based support.

Method: Formative studies (interviews + Wizard-of-Oz) identified four challenges. PAPEL was built as a parent-AI collaborative system with four modules: content generation, language adaptation, balance assessment, and extended response — all grounded in the ongoing play scene. Evaluated via a counterbalanced within-subjects study with 16 parent-child dyads comparing PAPEL against a lightweight chatbot baseline.

Result: PAPEL significantly outperformed the chatbot baseline on two key metrics: more integrated parent utterances (blending playful and instructional content) and more parent-child conversational turns.

Conclusion: AI can effectively support parents in transforming everyday play into meaningful EFL learning by providing scene-grounded, module-based scaffolding that enhances both the quality and quantity of parent-child linguistic interaction.

Abstract: Play-based parent-child interaction offers preschoolers rich opportunities for everyday foreign language learning, yet many parents struggle to turn open-ended play into effective English-as-a-Foreign-Language (EFL) learning experiences at home. To explore how AI might support this process, we conducted formative studies through interviews and a Wizard-of-Oz study. We identified four key challenges: content selection, language expression, balancing instruction and play, and problem solving. To address these challenges, we present PAPEL, a parent-AI collaborative system that grounds suggestions in the ongoing play scene and organizes support into four core modules: content generation, language adaptation, balance assessment, and extended response. In a counterbalanced within-subjects study with 16 parent-child dyads, PAPEL was associated with more integrated parent utterances that combined playful and instructional content, as well as more parent-child conversational turns, than the lightweight chatbot baseline used in our study.

</details>


### [191] [Frozen Multimodal Embeddings for Personality and Cognitive Ability Assessment in Asynchronous Video Interviews](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11930)
*Kuo-En Hung,Hung-Yue Suen,Shih-Ching Yeh,Hsiang-Wen Wang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Frozen multimodal encoders (CLIP, Whisper, RoBERTa, E5, DeBERTaV3) with low-capacity downstream models achieve strong personality trait prediction (19.1% MSE reduction over baseline) but reveal dataset shortcuts undermining cognitive ability inference from asynchronous video interviews.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting psychological traits from asynchronous video interviews (AVIs) is challenging due to limited labeled datasets and high-dimensional multimodal signals. The work targets the ACM Multimedia AVI Challenge 2026, aiming to improve personality (HEXACO) and cognitive ability prediction while addressing small-sample representation learning constraints.

Method: Frozen pretrained multimodal encoders are used without fine-tuning: CLIP for visual, Whisper for acoustic and transcripts, and RoBERTa/E5/DeBERTaV3 for text. For Track 1 (personality), a trait-specific regression with late fusion is employed. For Track 2 (cognitive ability), a compact subject-attribute baseline and multimodal ensemble are tested. Ablations compare global models, per-trait models, and per-trait late fusion.

Result: Track 1: trait-specific late fusion achieves 0.2696 avg validation MSE (baseline: 0.3334), a 19.1% relative reduction. Ablation shows progressive gains: global model (0.3189) → per-trait (0.2871) → per-trait late fusion (0.2696). Track 2: subject-attribute baseline reaches 0.5781 accuracy, multimodal ensemble 0.5313, both above baseline (0.4062), but results suggest subject-attribute shortcuts rather than genuine cognitive inference.

Conclusion: AVI-based psychological assessment benefits from trait-specific multimodal modeling. However, cognitive ability prediction is vulnerable to dataset shortcuts (e.g., subject-attribute leakage), requiring careful experimental controls to ensure valid inference from AVI content.

Abstract: Predicting psychological traits from asynchronous video interviews (AVIs) is a challenging multimodal learning problem because labeled datasets are limited while each response contains high-dimensional visual, acoustic, and verbal signals. This paper presents our solution for the ACM Multimedia AVI Challenge 2026, which evaluates two tasks: Track~1 predicts self-reported HEXACO personality traits from personality-related interview responses, and Track~2 classifies cognitive ability levels from structured AVI responses. We treat the problem as a small-sample representation learning task. Instead of fine-tuning large pretrained models, we use frozen multimodal encoders, including CLIP for visual features, Whisper for acoustic features and transcripts, and RoBERTa, E5, and DeBERTaV3 for textual representations, followed by low-capacity downstream models. For Track~1, our trait-specific regression and late-fusion system achieves an average validation MSE of 0.2696, improving over the official baseline of 0.3334. Ablation results show a three-step improvement from a global model (0.3189), to per-trait modeling (0.2871), to per-trait late fusion (0.2696), corresponding to a 19.1\% relative MSE reduction over the official baseline. For Track~2, a compact subject-attribute baseline reaches 0.5781 accuracy, while our multimodal ensemble reaches 0.5313, both above the official baseline of 0.4062. We interpret this result as evidence of possible subject-attribute shortcuts in the validation split rather than robust cognitive inference from AVI content. Overall, our findings suggest that AVI-based psychological assessment benefits from trait-specific multimodal modeling, but cognitive ability prediction requires careful control of dataset shortcuts.

</details>


### [192] [Somewhere Over the Desktop: A Research Agenda for Ubiquitous Analytics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11980)
*Niklas Elmqvist,Panagiotis D. Ritsos,Peter W. S. Butcher*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The convergence of spatial computing, generative AI, and open web standards — embodied in three major spatial OS platforms and evolving wearable displays — creates a pivotal moment for ubiquitous analytics (UA). This paper traces UA's intellectual genealogy across seven clusters (cognition, context, interaction, platforms, visualization, collaboration, evaluation), then systematically cross-pollinates them to identify 42 open research challenges, arguing that evidence-based design alternatives are urgently needed before proprietary conventions become entrenched.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Proprietary spatial computing platforms (Android XR, Meta Horizon OS, visionOS) are rapidly settling design conventions for ubiquitous analytics without evidence-based alternatives. Without structured scholarly intervention, these conventions risk calcification. The authors argue UA has now matured sufficiently that its intellectual history warrants systematic mapping to guide future research.

Method: The authors construct a structured genealogy of UA's intellectual foundations, contributions, and lineages. They organize this genealogy into seven thematic clusters (cognition, context, interaction, platforms, visualization, collaboration, and evaluation) and then perform a cross-cluster analysis, systematically intersecting each cluster with every other to surface underexplored research spaces, yielding 42 distinct future research challenges.

Result: The genealogy reveals UA as a coherent field with traceable lineages across its seven constituent clusters. The cross-cluster analysis produces a matrix of 42 concrete research challenges at the intersections — for instance, how spatial cognition models should inform cross-device interaction design, or how collaborative sensemaking is affected by platform-level scene understanding capabilities.

Conclusion: The convergence of spatial OS platforms, generative AI, and wearable displays marks an inflection point for UA. By systematically mapping the field's genealogy and generating a structured agenda of 42 challenges, the paper provides a roadmap for evidence-based, open research that can resist premature calcification of proprietary design conventions.

Abstract: Spatial computing, generative AI, and open web standards are converging. Three spatial operating systems -- Android XR, Meta Horizon OS, and Apple visionOS -- now ship with platform-level scene understanding. Wearable displays span the range from full headsets to slim smartglasses. Agentic AI operates on the same spatial substrates as the human user. This convergence enables new opportunities for \textit{ubiquitous analytics} (UA): the use of many, physically distributed, networked devices to support data sensemaking anytime and anywhere. But proprietary platforms are settling design conventions that will calcify without evidence-based alternatives. UA has now matured to the point where its intellectual history can be read as a structured genealogy of foundations, contributions, and lineages. We trace this genealogy and organize it into clusters spanning cognition, context, interaction, platforms, visualization, collaboration, and evaluation. Finally, we cross these clusters with each other, yielding a total of 42 future research challenges.

</details>


### [193] [Channels and Substrates: Distributed Cognition as an Interaction Model for Ubiquitous Analytics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11986)
*Niklas Elmqvist,Panagiotis D. Ritsos,Peter W. S. Butcher*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Traditional HCI models fail for cross-device and ubiquitous analytics. The authors propose modeling interaction via distributed cognition, framing it as propagation of representational state across substrates (minds, bodies, devices, artifacts). They introduce input/output channels as generalizations of visualization channels, enabling context-dependent analysis of ubiquitous analytics systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HCI interaction models assume a single monolithic interface and a stable sensorimotor loop, which is inadequate for cross-device (XVA) and ubiquitous analytics (UA) scenarios where sensemaking spans multiple devices, artifacts, people, and contexts (office to factory floor). A new theoretical framework is needed to capture the distributed, multi-surface nature of these interactions.

Method: The authors apply distributed cognition theory to reframe interaction in ubiquitous analytics. Rather than modeling interaction as traffic through a single interface, they conceptualize it as the propagation of representational state across heterogeneous substrates — minds, speech, bodies, artifacts, and devices. They then introduce the construct of input and output channels, generalizing the concept of visual channels from data visualization. These channels carry representational state through substrates whose availability, suitability, and preferability are context-dependent. The framework is demonstrated through reanalysis of several existing ubiquitous, immersive, and situated analytics systems.

Result: The paper presents a novel theoretical framework — the channels and substrates framework — and demonstrates its descriptive and analytical utility by reanalyzing multiple existing ubiquitous, immersive, and situated analytics systems, showing how interaction can be understood as propagation of representational state rather than single-interface traffic.

Conclusion: The distributed cognition lens and the channels-and-substrates framework provide a more faithful and generalizable model for understanding and designing interaction in ubiquitous analytics, overcoming the limitations of traditional monolithic HCI interaction models.

Abstract: Traditional HCI interaction models assume a single monolithic interface and a stable sensorimotor loop. These models fit poorly with cross-device (XVA) and ubiquitous analytics (UA), where interactive data sensemaking unfolds across multiple devices, artifacts, and people in disparate settings from the office to the factory floor. In this paper, we show how interaction in ubiquitous analytics can be modeled using distributed cognition as propagation of representational state across substrates -- minds, speech, bodies, artifacts, and devices -- rather than as traffic through a single interface. On this basis we introduce input and output channels as generalizations of the visual channels from data visualization: just as visual channels carry data through properties of the visual substrate, input and output channels carry representational state through substrates whose availability, suitability, and preferability depend on context. We demonstrate the channels and substrates framework by reanalyzing several ubiquitous, immersive, and situated analytics systems.

</details>


### [194] [Identifying cybersickness causes in virtual reality games using symbolic machine learning algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12214)
*Thiago Porcino,Erick Oliveira Rodrigues,Flavia Bernardini,Daniela Trevisan,Esteban Clua*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This study uses symbolic machine learning to identify and rank causes of cybersickness in VR games, comparing a flight game and a race game across 37 participants. Rotation and acceleration were dominant triggers in the flight game, while prior VR experience significantly modulated susceptibility in the race game. Distinct causal factors emerged for short-term vs. long-term VR exposure, leading to proposed mitigation strategies for each scenario.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite growing VR adoption across education, military, entertainment, and health domains, cybersickness remains a significant barrier to user comfort and prolonged engagement. The authors aim to move beyond descriptive accounts toward a systematic, data-driven ranking of cybersickness causes, enabling targeted mitigation strategies tailored to specific game genres and exposure durations.

Method: A total of 88 volunteers were recruited, yielding 37 valid samples after screening. Participants experienced two distinct VR games (flight and race) under 6 experimental protocols. Symbolic machine learning was employed for causal analysis, while classical machine learning techniques were used to estimate and rank the impact of each identified cybersickness cause.

Result: Rotation and acceleration were the most potent triggers in the flight game compared to the race game. VR-naïve subjects exhibited greater susceptibility overall. Prior experience was a stronger protective factor in the race game, attributed to its greater user-controlled acceleration, multiple displacement options, and controller freedom. Distinct causal profiles emerged for short-term versus long-term VR exposure, with different dominant triggers in each temporal window.

Conclusion: Cybersickness causation is context-dependent, varying by game genre (flight vs. race) and exposure duration (short-term vs. long-term). Mitigation strategies should be tailored accordingly: addressing rotation/acceleration in flight experiences and leveraging user control and habituation in more open-ended games like racing. The symbolic ML approach proves viable for disentangling and ranking complex cybersickness triggers.

Abstract: Virtual reality (VR) and head-mounted displays are constantly gaining popularity in various fields such as education, military, entertainment, and health. Although such technologies provide a high sense of immersion, they can also trigger symptoms of discomfort. This condition is called cybersickness (CS) and is quite popular in recent virtual reality publications. This work proposes a novel experimental analysis using symbolic machine learning to rank potential causes of CS in VR games. We estimate CS causes and rank them according to their impact using classical machine learning. Experiments are performed using two virtual reality games and 6 experimental protocols along with 37 valid samples from a total of 88 volunteers. Our results show that rotation and acceleration triggered cybersickness more frequently in a flight game in contrast to a race game. We could also observe that subjects that are less experienced with VR are more prone to feel discomfort. Former experience plays a more important role on the race game, as this game provides more liberty to the user in terms of controllers, more displacement alternatives and a more user-controlled acceleration. Furthermore, different causes that trigger discomfort arise based on short or long term VR exposures. We suggest strategies for mitigating CS for these two scenarios: short and long term exposure experiences and compare the two highlighted scenarios (race and flight).

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [195] [Restless bandits with imperfect binary feedback: PCL-indexability analysis and computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11192)
*José Niño-Mora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a Partial Conservation Laws (PCL) framework to analyze restless bandits with binary latent states and imperfect binary feedback, motivated by opportunistic spectrum access. It establishes indexability and evaluates the Whittle index analytically in several threshold regimes, and provides efficient numerical schemes for the remaining regime, with experiments showing the resulting Marginal Productivity (MP) index policy outperforms benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work is motivated by opportunistic spectrum access (OSA) in cognitive radio networks, where a secondary user must decide whether to sense and access a channel whose occupancy state (binary) is latent and observed only through imperfect (error-prone) binary feedback. This maps to a restless bandit problem with partially observable states, where establishing indexability and computing the Whittle index is challenging due to the belief-state dynamics.

Method: The authors employ a PCL-based framework built on a verification theorem for real-state discounted restless bandits. They analyze the stochastic belief-state dynamics through an associated deterministic skeleton, renewal decompositions, and combinatorics on words to derive tractable expressions for discounted reward and resource metrics in several threshold regimes. For regimes where full analytic verification is not achieved, they develop efficient numerical schemes for computing marginal metrics and the MP index.

Result: Tractable closed-form expressions for discounted reward and resource metrics are obtained in multiple threshold regimes, enabling full PCL-indexability verification there. For the remaining regime, extensive computational experiments provide strong evidence that indexability holds across broad parameter ranges without stringent restrictions required by prior work. The MP index policy consistently outperforms standard benchmark policies, often by substantial margins.

Conclusion: The PCL framework provides a robust methodology for establishing indexability and computing the Whittle index in restless bandits with imperfect observations. The MP index policy is both computationally viable and empirically near-optimal, offering a practical solution for opportunistic spectrum access and related partially observable restless bandit problems.

Abstract: We study restless bandits with binary latent states and imperfect binary feedback, motivated by opportunistic spectrum access with sensing errors. For the associated belief-state model, we develop a partial conservation laws (PCL)-based analytical and computational framework for establishing indexability and evaluating the Whittle index, building on a verification theorem for real-state discounted restless bandits. The framework analyzes the stochastic dynamics via an associated deterministic skeleton, renewal decompositions, and combinatorics on words. It yields tractable expressions for discounted reward and resource metrics in several threshold regimes, enabling full verification of the PCL-indexability conditions there. For the remaining regime, where a complete analytic verification is not achieved in this paper, we derive efficient numerical schemes for computing the relevant marginal metrics and the marginal productivity (MP) index, which equals the Whittle index when those conditions hold. Extensive computational experiments provide strong evidence that these conditions also hold in that regime across broad parameter ranges and without the stringent parameter restrictions imposed in prior work. The experiments further show that theMP index policy typically outperforms standard benchmark policies, often by a substantial margin.

</details>


### [196] [To Intervene or Not: Guiding Inference-time Alignment with Probabilistic Model Blending](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11201)
*Jin Gan,Xin Li,Jun Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BlendIn is an inference-time alignment framework that replaces binary model selection with a hybrid distribution blending approach, weighting each model's contribution by its reliability. It improves performance by up to 50% on challenging model pairs by preserving beneficial guidance while downweighting unreliable suggestions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference-time alignment methods apply guidances extracted from aligned models without assessing their reliability. Systematic evaluation shows guidance effectiveness varies drastically across models, and ineffective guidances cause further confusion and excessive interventions, leading to poor performance. There is a need for more effective and efficient intervention mechanisms.

Method: BlendIn shifts from binary intervention decisions to creating hybrid probability distributions that integrate knowledge from both the aligned and unaligned models. It employs quality-aware alignment with proportional weighting of each model's contribution based on assessed reliability, preserving beneficial guidance while downweighting unreliable suggestions. The framework provides both diagnostic signals and mitigation strategies for misaligned guidance.

Result: BlendIn achieves consistent performance improvements and up to 50% improvement on challenging model pairs compared to existing inference-time alignment methods.

Conclusion: BlendIn demonstrates that quality-aware, proportional blending of model distributions at inference time is a more effective and efficient alignment strategy than binary intervention approaches, with significant performance gains and built-in diagnostic capabilities.

Abstract: The wide deployment of LLMs has made model alignment necessary to make newly trained models safely and effectively respond to user instructions. Among different methods, inference-time alignment is often cheaper as it intervenes (i.e., offers guidances) only during output generation. Existing proposals apply guidances extracted from certain aligned models without properly assessing their reliability. Nonetheless, our systematic evaluation reveals that guidance effectiveness varies drastically across models; since ineffective guidances lead to further confusion and thus further interventions, the resulting excessive interventions typically indicate poor performance. To make interventions more effective and thus more efficient, we introduce BlendIn, an inference-time alignment framework that shifts from binary decisions to creating hybrid distributions integrating both models' knowledge. BlendIn stabilizes inference-time alignment by performing quality-aware alignment and proportionally weighting each model's contribution based on reliability. Compared with existing works, it preserves beneficial guidance while downweighting unreliable suggestions. BlendIn provides both diagnostic signals and mitigation strategies for misaligned guidance, achieving consistent and up to 50% performance improvement on challenging model pairs. Our code is available at: https://github.com/DecayingSeart/BlendIn.

</details>


### [197] [Dual-Stance Evaluation of Sycophancy: The Structure of Agreement and the Limits of Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11205)
*Matthew James Buchan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Activation steering for sycophancy reduction also suppresses agreement with factual statements because steering directions project equally onto both geometrically distinct but inseparable subspaces, revealing a read-write gap in activation representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sycophancy-reduction evaluations do not test whether steering also suppresses factual agreement. The authors aim to determine whether a model's representations of sycophantic versus factual agreement can be differentially targeted by activation steering.

Method: The authors introduce dual-stance evaluation (testing both stances per topic) and apply centroid-difference steering to Llama-3-8B-Instruct, comparing the geometric properties of activation subspaces for sycophantic and factual agreement.

Result: Sycophantic and factual agreement occupy geometrically distinct subspaces, but the steering direction projects equally onto both and cannot differentially target either. Consequently, steering reduces agreement with both sycophantic and factually correct statements. All other static properties of the two activation groups are matched.

Conclusion: The dissociation between static representational readability and dynamic writability illustrates that representations decodable from activations may not be steerable through them, likely due to generation dynamics or sub-residual-stream structure.

Abstract: Activation steering can shift LLM behaviour, but standard evaluations do not typically test whether a sycophancy-reduction direction also suppresses agreement with factually correct statements. We introduce dual-stance evaluation, which tests both stances of each topic, and apply it to centroid-difference steering on Llama-3-8B-Instruct. We find a dissociation: the model represents sycophantic and factual agreement in geometrically distinct subspaces, yet the steering direction projects equally onto both and cannot differentially target either. The direction accordingly reduces agreement with factually correct statements (e.g. that the Earth is round) as well as sycophantic ones. All other static properties of the two activation groups are matched, suggesting the behavioural dissociation arises from generation dynamics or from finer-grained structure that residual-stream analysis cannot resolve. The pattern illustrates a general gap: representations that are readable from activations may not be writable through them.

</details>


### [198] [Few-Shot Resampling for Scalable Statistically-Sound Data Mining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11235)
*Leonardo Pellegrina,Fabio Vandin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FewRS is a novel resampling-based method for assessing statistical significance of data mining results. It dramatically reduces the number of resampled datasets needed — by up to two orders of magnitude — by leveraging a derived bound on the supremum deviation of test statistics, while maintaining rigorous false discovery guarantees and high statistical power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current resampling-based approaches for evaluating statistical significance of data mining results (e.g., in pattern mining, graph analysis) require generating and analyzing thousands of resampled datasets, making them impractical for large-scale data or computationally intensive analyses. No general, scalable solution with rigorous guarantees existed.

Method: The authors derive a novel bound on the supremum deviation of test statistics that represent the quality of data mining results. This bound enables FewRS to require only an extremely small number of resampled datasets, rather than thousands. The approach is general-purpose and applies to any context where resampling-based significance testing is used.

Result: FewRS achieves up to two orders of magnitude reduction in running time compared to state-of-the-art methods on pattern mining and network analysis tasks, while preserving high statistical power. It enables statistical validation on large-scale real-world datasets that were previously impractical to analyze.

Conclusion: FewRS provides a highly scalable, theoretically grounded, and widely applicable solution for statistical significance evaluation in data mining, overcoming the computational bottleneck of traditional resampling methods without sacrificing statistical rigor.

Abstract: A key step in knowledge discovery is the evaluation of data mining results. In several applications, including pattern mining, graph analysis, and others, this step includes the evaluation of the statistical significance of the results, to avoid spurious discoveries due only to noise or random fluctuations in the data. While specialized procedures have been developed for some specific applications, resampling-based approaches are widely used, in particular for complex analyses where analytical results cannot be derived. However, current resampling-based approaches require the generation and analysis of thousands of resampled datasets, and are therefore impractical for large datasets or computationally intensive analyses.
  In this paper, we introduce FewRS, a simple and effective resampling-based approach to assess the statistical significance of data mining results with rigorous guarantees on the probability of false discoveries. Our approach can be used in every situation where resampling-based approaches are applied. FewRS builds on our derivation of a novel bound to the supremum deviation of test statistics representing the quality of data mining results. We prove that FewRS needs to generate and analyze an extremely small number of resampled datasets, leading to a highly scalable approach with wide applicability. We test our approach on common tasks such as pattern mining and network analysis. In all cases, our approach results in a reduction of up to two orders of magnitude in running time compared to the state of the art, while preserving high statistical power, enabling the statistical validation of data mining results on large-scale real-world datasets.

</details>


### [199] [ProHiFlo: Hierarchical Flow Matching with Functional Guidance for De Novo Protein Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11243)
*Chuanzhen Wang,Meade Cleti,Pete Jano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProHiFlo is a hierarchical flow matching framework for de novo protein generation that operates coarse-to-fine (backbone → all-atom), incorporates functional guidance via pretrained predictors without retraining, and uses an adaptive SE(3)-equivariant architecture, achieving state-of-the-art performance with 4× fewer sampling steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion and flow matching methods for protein generation operate at a single resolution and lack mechanisms for incorporating functional constraints, limiting their efficiency and applicability in goal-directed design tasks such as therapeutic and enzyme engineering.

Method: ProHiFlo introduces three key innovations: (1) a coarse-to-fine hierarchical flow matching pipeline that first models backbone geometry then refines to all-atom coordinates; (2) functional guidance that leverages pretrained property predictors to steer generation toward desired functions without requiring retraining; and (3) an adaptive SE(3)-equivariant architecture optimized for efficient multi-scale processing.

Result: ProHiFlo achieves state-of-the-art performance on unconditional generation, motif scaffolding, and functional design tasks while requiring 4× fewer sampling steps than comparable methods. Notably, on enzyme active site scaffolding, it attains a 58.9% success rate compared to RFDiffusion's 41.2%.

Conclusion: Hierarchical flow matching with functional guidance offers a more efficient and functionally aware paradigm for protein generation, significantly outperforming prior methods in both speed and success rate on biologically relevant design tasks.

Abstract: De novo protein generation has transformative potential in therapeutic design, enzyme engineering, and synthetic biology. While diffusion-based and flow matching approaches have achieved progress, they typically operate at single resolution and lack mechanisms for incorporating functional constraints. We introduce ProHiFlo, a hierarchical flow matching framework with three innovations: (1) coarse-to-fine generation that models backbone geometry before refining to all-atom coordinates, reducing computational cost while maintaining accuracy; (2) functional guidance leveraging pretrained predictors to steer generation toward desired properties without retraining; (3) adaptive SE(3)-equivariant architecture for efficient multi-scale processing. Experiments on unconditional generation, motif scaffolding, and functional design demonstrate state-ofthe-art performance while requiring 4 fewer sampling steps. On enzyme active site scaffolding, ProHiFlo achieves 58.9% success rate compared to 41.2% for RFDiffusion.

</details>


### [200] [Physics-informed generative AI for semiconductor manufacturing: Enforcing hard physical constraints in generative models by construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11247)
*Yaser Mike Banad,Sarah Sharif*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Semiconductor manufacturing demands generative models that enforce physical constraints by construction rather than post-hoc filtering. This Perspective surveys physics-informed generative architectures (diffusion, PDE-constrained, neural-operator, conservation-law-respecting) and proposes a research agenda for physics-fidelity benchmarks, differentiable simulators, and multimodal foundation models, arguing that the fab is the ultimate testbed where physical validity is the binding success criterion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI is increasingly applied to physical systems (e.g., semiconductor manufacturing for masks, layouts, defect data, process recipes), but these systems are governed by hard physical constraints — lithography, transport, reaction, and device physics. Physically invalid outputs are not merely implausible but entirely unusable. Current approaches that rely on post-hoc filtering are insufficient; the paper argues for physics-informed-by-construction architectures as a fundamental computational-science challenge.

Method: This is a Perspective/position paper that: (1) surveys the emerging architectural toolkit for physics-informed generative models, including physics-informed diffusion models, PDE-constrained variational models, neural-operator priors, and conservation-law-respecting generative networks; (2) connects these to established tools like differentiable lithography, TCAD, process simulation, and autonomous experimentation; (3) identifies four integration patterns between generative models and physics-based simulators; and (4) proposes a research agenda with three pillars: physics-fidelity benchmarks, differentiable simulator infrastructure, and multimodal foundation models for physical design and manufacturing.

Result: The paper does not present experimental results but offers an analytical argument: architectures that enforce physical constraints by construction should systematically outperform those that filter for validity after the fact. Semiconductor manufacturing is identified as the domain where this distinction is sharpest, because physical validity is the binding criterion of success rather than a soft preference.

Conclusion: Generative AI for constrained physical domains must embed physics directly into model architecture and training, not treat it as a post-hoc filter. The semiconductor fab serves as an ideal, high-stakes testbed. The proposed roadmap — physics-fidelity benchmarks, differentiable simulator infrastructure, and multimodal foundation models — aims to catalyze a shift toward physics-informed-by-construction generative systems with broader implications for computational science and engineering.

Abstract: Generative models are increasingly used to propose designs, data, and control actions for physical systems, yet many such systems are governed by hard physical constraints rather than by perceptual plausibility. Semiconductor manufacturing provides a demanding test case: generated masks, layouts, synthetic defect data, and process recipes must obey lithography, transport, reaction, and device-physics constraints, because physically invalid samples are not merely low quality but unusable. This Perspective argues that semiconductor manufacturing exposes a broader computational-science challenge, namely that generative AI for constrained physical domains must be physics-informed by construction, not corrected only through post-hoc filtering. We survey the emerging architectural toolkit, including physics-informed diffusion, PDE-constrained variational models, neural-operator priors, and conservation-law-respecting generative networks, and show how it connects to differentiable lithography, TCAD, process simulation, and autonomous experimentation. We identify four integration patterns between generative models and physics-based simulators, and we propose a research agenda centered on physics-fidelity benchmarks, differentiable simulator infrastructure, and multimodal foundation models for physical design and manufacturing. The central claim is analytical rather than rhetorical: where physical validity is the binding criterion of success, architectures that enforce it by construction should be expected to outperform those that filter for it after the fact, and the fab is the setting where this distinction is sharpest.

</details>


### [201] [Mechanical Field Networks: Structured Neural Dynamics for Multivariate Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11251)
*Xingji Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MF-Net is a recurrent dynamical model that jointly learns state transitions and interpretable interaction structures by representing variables in a shared field state evolved through a learned mechanical transition law. It achieves competitive forecasting performance while recovering ground-truth coupling structures across physical, chaotic, neural, and ecological systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamical models either impose predefined interpretable dynamics or learn flexible but opaque state transitions. The interaction structure—the coupling graph among variables—is either specified a priori or remains implicit in learned weights, preventing structural interpretability. There is a need for models that simultaneously learn to forecast and recover interpretable interaction relations from data.

Method: MF-Net introduces a field-state representation where each variable carries a field component. These field components evolve jointly through a learned "mechanical" transition—a relation-to-motion framework where: (1) learned pairwise relations shape state-dependent flows; (2) field responses capture how variables react to the shared field; and (3) motion tendencies produce the forward update. The same internal quantities (the learned relation matrix) support both trajectory rollout and structural readout, making the interaction graph inspectable without post-hoc analysis.

Result: On the 40-dimensional Lorenz-96 benchmark, MF-Net achieves an eight-step R² of 0.798±0.018.
