Across five seeds, the learned relation matrix recovers the local coupling support with a local/nonlocal strength ratio of 19.80±1.00 and Precision@K of 1.000±0.000. MF-Net also demonstrates competitive forecasting on known-law interaction systems, chaotic benchmarks, neural recordings, and ecological time series.

Conclusion: MF-Net provides a structure-readable dynamical modeling framework where learned relations are trained end-to-end through forward evolution. The recovered relations can be interpreted as functional predictive couplings, enabling both accurate forecasting and mechanistic insight under appropriate observational constraints.

Abstract: Many multivariate dynamical systems are observed only through trajectories, leaving the mechanisms governing their joint dynamics hidden. Existing approaches can impose interpretable dynamics or learn flexible state transitions, yet the resulting interaction structure is typically either specified in advance or left implicit within the learned dynamics. We introduce MF-Net, a recurrent dynamical model that represents all variables in a shared field state and updates this state through a learned relation law. Each variable carries a field component, and these components evolve jointly through a learnable mechanical transition. Here, mechanical refers to the relation-to-motion organization of the transition, where learned relations shape state-dependent flows, field responses, and motion tendencies that move the field state forward. The resulting structure is part of the rollout itself: learned relations influence how the field moves, and the same internal quantities support both forecasting and structural readout. Across known-law interaction systems, chaotic benchmarks, real neural recordings, and ecological time series, MF-Net achieves competitive short- and medium-horizon forecasting while retaining inspectable structural readout. On the 40-dimensional Lorenz--96 testbed, MF-Net achieves an eight-step $R^2$ of $0.798\pm0.018$; across five seeds, its learned relation matrix recovers the local coupling support with a local/nonlocal strength ratio of $19.80\pm1.00$ and Precision@$K$ of $1.000\pm0.000$. MF-Net provides a structure-readable dynamical modeling framework in which learned relations are trained through forward evolution and, on real data, interpreted as functional predictive couplings under appropriate observational limits.

</details>


### [202] [Bernstein-Schur Kernels: Random Features by Sketched Modulation and Radial Randomization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11255)
*Taha Bouhsine*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces random-feature constructions for Bernstein–Schur kernels — products of a finite-feature kernel and a completely monotone shift-invariant kernel — by doubly randomizing both factors: sketching the finite modulation and applying Gaussian random Fourier features to radial draws from the Bernstein–Widder scale mixture. Theoretical guarantees include unbiasedness, variance analysis, operator-norm concentration bounds depending on intrinsic dimension rather than crude entrywise norms, and kernel-ridge stability results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bernstein–Schur kernels are nonstationary kernels bridging shift-invariant and dot-product templates. Standard single-mechanism random feature methods (Bochner sampling for shift-invariant, polynomial sketching for dot-product) cannot directly handle the full product kernel. The paper aims to provide a unified random-feature construction with rigorous theoretical guarantees for this entire class.

Method: A doubly-randomized construction: (1) the finite-modulation factor is sketched (with sketch size m) to reduce dimensionality from O(d²) to Dm; (2) the completely monotone radial factor is randomized by sampling its one-dimensional Bernstein–Widder scale mixture, then applying Gaussian random Fourier features (d-dimensional frequencies) with D radial draws. The limiting case m→∞ keeps modulation exact and yields analyzable properties. The construction is demonstrated on the biased yat-kernel, where the radial mixture corresponds to inverse-multiquadric spectral sampling.

Result: For the exact-modulation limit (m→∞): unbiased estimator, exact variance formula for the flat estimator, an expected matrix-Bernstein operator-norm bound controlled by top eigenvalues of kernel and modulation Gram matrices with an intrinsic dimension (avoiding crude N·max_{ij} entrywise bounds), and a deterministic relative-spectral kernel-ridge stability result. For the fully doubly-randomized estimator: the same intrinsic-dimension operator-norm guarantee plus a single additive sketch term, tunable by m independently of D. For the yat-kernel, one frequency per scale is variance-optimal at a fixed radial-feature budget.

Conclusion: The doubly-randomized construction provides the first unified random-feature method for Bernstein–Schur kernels with strong theoretical backing. The intrinsic-dimension bounds and the decoupling of sketch size m from radial-draw count D enable practical, tunable approximations. The yat-kernel family, spanning the inverse-multiquadric via finite differences, serves as a compelling motivating instance.

Abstract: Bernstein--Schur kernels are products of a finite-feature kernel (one with an explicit finite-dimensional feature map) and a completely monotone shift-invariant kernel: nonstationary kernels that fall between the shift-invariant and dot-product templates random features usually exploit, so in general neither Bochner sampling nor polynomial sketching applies to the full kernel directly. We give one random-feature construction for the whole class that \emph{randomizes both factors: it sketches the finite modulation and randomizes the completely monotone radial factor, sampling the latter's one-dimensional Bernstein--Widder scale and then applying Gaussian random Fourier features (whose frequency is still $d$-dimensional). The feature dimension is then $Dm$, set by the sketch size $m$ and the radial-draw count $D$, free of the $O(d^2)$ size of the exact modulation feature. Keeping the modulation \emph{exact} is the analyzable limit ($m\to\infty$): there we prove unbiasedness, an exact variance for the recommended flat estimator, an expected matrix-Bernstein operator-norm bound (with a matching high-probability tail) controlled by the top eigenvalues of the kernel and modulation Gram matrices together with an intrinsic dimension rather than the crude $N\max_{ij}$ entrywise route, and a deterministic relative-spectral kernel-ridge stability result. By conditioning on the sketch, the doubly-randomized estimator inherits the same intrinsic-dimension operator-norm guarantee plus a single additive sketch term, tunable by $m$ independently of $D$. The motivating instance is the biased $yat$-kernel $k_{yat,b}(w,x)=(w^\top x+b)^2/(\|w-x\|^2+\varepsilon)$, $b\ge0$, whose family span contains the inverse-multiquadric kernel by finite differences in $b$; for it the radial mixture is the IMQ spectral sampler, and one frequency per scale is variance-optimal at a fixed radial-feature budget.

</details>


### [203] [Loss Landscape Diagnosis for Gradient-Based Gray-Scott System Inversion: Disentangling the Roles of PINN Components](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11258)
*Yan Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates why direct backpropagation through PDE structure fails to recover parameters in reaction-diffusion systems, revealing that the loss landscape contains flat plateaus and sharp cliffs aligned with bifurcation boundaries. Using this as an ablation of PINNs, the authors show that the residual loss alone (via implicit encoding of full PDE dynamics) avoids this pathology, while the neural network merely completes observed data — revealing a previously unarticulated division of labor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gradient-based parameter inversion for reaction-diffusion PDEs is commonly done via surrogate models or PINNs, with direct backpropagation through the PDE structure largely avoided — yet the underlying reasons for this avoidance remain unexplored. The authors aim to diagnose why direct backpropagation fails, using it as a minimal probe to understand the geometry of the optimization landscape and to disentangle the roles of each component in PINN-based inversion.

Method: The authors backpropagate a steady-state loss through unrolled Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion simulations to recover system parameters, without any surrogate or neural-network augmentation. They directly visualize the loss landscape to identify geometric features (flat plateaus, sharp cliffs) and correlate these with bifurcation boundaries. This minimal setup serves as an ablation of PINNs: by fixing the neural network, they isolate the residual loss's quadratic dependence on PDE parameters, revealing its role in smoothing the landscape by implicitly encoding dynamics across all initial conditions.

Result: Direct backpropagation fails to converge. The loss landscape exhibits flat plateaus with zero gradient signal, bounded by sharp cliffs that align precisely with bifurcation boundaries of the Gray-Scott system. This geometric structure recurs across different loss functions and is inherent regardless of how gradients are routed. In the PINN ablation, the residual loss alone (with network fixed) is quadratic in PDE parameters and yields a smooth, well-behaved landscape. The neural network cannot repair ill-posed parameter subspaces and serves only to complete observed data.

Conclusion: The failure of direct backpropagation stems from inherent geometry of the loss landscape tied to bifurcation structure — not from implementation choices. For PINN-type methods, the residual loss is the critical component that regularizes the landscape by implicitly encoding full PDE dynamics; the neural network merely handles data completion. This division of labor had not been previously made explicit and carries concrete design implications for PINN-based inversion methods, along with a broader heuristic on when added dimensions actually help.

Abstract: Gradient-based inversion of reaction-diffusion systems is typically approached via surrogate models or physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), while the most direct route, backpropagation through the PDE's structure itself, has largely been avoided. We pursue this direct route as a diagnostic probe, backpropagating a steady-state loss through unrolled Gray-Scott simulation to recover its parameters, with no surrogate or neural-network augmentation. Optimization fails to converge, and plotting the landscape directly locates the failure in its geometry -- flat plateaus with no gradient signal, bounded by sharp cliffs that align with bifurcation boundaries -- a structure that recurs across loss functions and is inherited however the gradients are routed to parameters. Reading this minimal setup as an ablation of PINN, we disentangle each component's role: with the neural network fixed, the residual loss is quadratic in the PDE parameters and yields a smooth landscape, so it alone already avoids the pathology, by implicitly encoding the full PDE dynamics across all initial conditions. The neural network, for its part, cannot repair an ill-posed parameter subspace, and so serves only to complete the observed data -- a division of labor not previously made explicit. These findings carry concrete design implications for PINN-type methods and a broader heuristic on when added dimensions actually help.

</details>


### [204] [PermDoRA -- Understanding Adapter Interference in Language Models: Limits of Parameter-Space Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11262)
*Gowtham Sivaramakrishnan,Sarvesha Kumar Kombaiah Seetha,Kishan Gupta Balaji,Santhosh Baradwaj Vaduvur Ranganathan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper tests whether enforcing orthogonality or directional independence in adapter parameters improves multi-domain LLM access control. Using DoRA-RBAC with Riemannian-inspired merging, they find geometry-aware merging offers no consistent advantage over standard averaging, and that angular alignment/orthogonality are weak predictors of composition performance, suggesting adapter interference arises from nonlinear representational interactions rather than parameter-space geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test the common hypothesis that interference during adapter composition stems from overlap in linear parameter updates, and that enforcing orthogonality or directional independence should therefore improve multi-domain performance in LLM access control.

Method: The authors propose DoRA-RBAC, a hierarchical adapter composition framework using weight-decomposed low-rank adaptation. They compare conventional Euclidean merging against a Riemannian-inspired merging strategy approximating the Fréchet mean via normalized directional averaging. Experiments span four QA benchmarks (GPQA, PubMedQA, SimpleQA, WMDP) on LLaMA-3.1-8B and Mistral-7B, with diagnostic analysis of angular alignment and orthogonality as predictors of composition quality.

Result: Single-domain performance matches LoRA baselines. However, geometry-aware merging yields no consistent advantage over standard averaging in multi-domain settings. Diagnostic analysis reveals that angular alignment and orthogonality of adapter updates are weak predictors of composition performance.

Conclusion: Adapter interference is not governed primarily by parameter-space geometry. Instead, interference patterns are consistent with interactions occurring in shared nonlinear representations, challenging the prevailing orthogonality-based hypothesis for adapter composition.

Abstract: Access control in large language models (LLMs) requires modular mechanisms to enable domain-specific behavior without retraining or cross-domain interference. A common hypothesis is that interference during adapter composition arises from overlap in linear parameter updates, suggesting that enforcing orthogonality or directional independence should improve multi-domain performance. We test this hypothesis using DoRA-RBAC, a hierarchical adapter composition framework based on weight-decomposed low-rank adaptation. We compare conventional Euclidean merging with a geometry-aware Riemannian-inspired merging strategy that approximates the Frechet mean via normalized directional averaging across multiple QA benchmarks (GPQA, PubMedQA, SimpleQA, WMDP) on LLaMA-3.1-8B and Mistral-7B. Our results show that while single-domain performance matches LoRA, geometry-aware merging provides no consistent advantage over standard averaging in multi-domain settings.Diagnostic analysis further reveals that angular alignment and orthogonality of adapter updates are weak predictors of composition performance. These findings suggest that adapter interference is not governed primarily by parameter-space geometry, but is instead consistent with interactions in shared nonlinear representations.

</details>


### [205] [Seeing Before Colliding: Anticipatory Safe RL with Frozen Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11266)
*Samuel Tetteh,Cody Fleming*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VLM-Safe-RL integrates a frozen vision-language model into the CMDP Lagrangian update as an anticipatory cost term, enabling proactive safety in high-speed RL where reactive cost signals are too late. The framework (VLMPPOLag with Confidence Gating) uniquely satisfies both return and budget constraints on Safety-Gymnasium FormulaOne L2, outperforming five baselines, and generalizes to MetaDrive Medium.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constrained-RL safety mechanisms are fundamentally reactive: costs are emitted only after a collision begins, and Lagrange multipliers grow only after budget violation. At race speeds, where collisions are instantaneous and irreversible, any safety mechanism that waits for cost accumulation is structurally too late. An anticipatory safety signal is needed.

Method: Four contributions compose VLM-Safe-RL: (i) Decoupled Dual-Path CLIP — independent reward and cost encoders preserving CMDP factorization; (ii) VLM-Lagrange — augmented Lagrangian multiplier update incorporating a per-step VLM-derived cost as an anticipatory term; (iii) Confidence Gating — a Bayes-optimal weight from a logistic noise model on the CLIP margin to modulate the VLM signal; (iv) VLMPPOLag — the composed algorithm. Evaluation uses Safety-Gymnasium FormulaOne L2 (5 seeds, 1M steps, budget d_lim=25), with transfer to MetaDrive and Bullet Safety-Gym.

Result: VLMPPOLag+Conf is the only configuration retaining substantive return (J_r ≈ 40) while holding cost within budget on a majority of seeds. All five baselines (PPOLag, CPO, CPPOPID, CPO-CLG, PPOLag-RND) fail at least one requirement. MetaDrive Medium transfer: catastrophe rate drops from 41% to 26% (95% bootstrap CI [-26, -5] pp). Directionally consistent transfer to Bullet Safety-Gym. Failures on MetaDrive Easy/Hard and Qwen2-VL backbone are honestly reported; the Hard failure is attributed to Lagrangian-regulation pathology rather than VLM signal degradation.

Conclusion: This is the first work to use frozen VLM signals as an anticipatory cost term inside the CMDP Lagrangian update, demonstrating that vision-language models can provide proactive safety signals that reactive constrained-RL methods structurally lack, enabling safe high-speed autonomous control.

Abstract: The cost signal that constrained-RL algorithms optimize against is almost always reactive: the simulator emits a non-zero cost only after a collision has begun, and the Lagrange multiplier of PPO-Lagrangian grows only after the episode budget has been exceeded. At race speeds, where collisions are instantaneous and irreversible, any safety mechanism that waits for cost to accumulate is structurally too late. We present VLM-Safe-RL, a framework that integrates a frozen vision-language model into the CMDP Lagrangian update as an anticipatory cost term. The framework comprises four contributions: (i) Decoupled Dual-Path CLIP, independent reward/cost paths that respect the CMDP's factorization; (ii) VLM-Lagrange, an augmented multiplier update that incorporates a per-step VLM cost as an anticipatory term; (iii) Confidence Gating, a Bayes-optimal weight derived from a logistic noise model on the CLIP margin; and (iv) VLMPPOLag, the composed algorithm. On Safety-Gymnasium FormulaOne L2, our principal evaluation ($n{=}5$ seeds, $10^{6}$ steps, budget $d_{\text{lim}}{=}25$) VLMPPOLag$+$Conf is the only configuration in our default budget comparison that simultaneously retains substantive return ($J_r{\approx}40$) and holds cost within budget on a majority of seeds; the five constraint-aware baselines (PPOLag, CPO, CPPOPID, CPO-CLG, PPOLag-RND) each fail at least one requirement. The mechanism generalizes to held-out MetaDrive Medium (catastrophe rate $41\%{\to}26\%$, 95\% bootstrap CI $[-26,-5]$\,pp) and shows directionally consistent transfer to Bullet Safety-Gym; we report honestly where it does not (MetaDrive Easy/Hard, Qwen2-VL backbone) and trace the Hard failure to a Lagrangian-regulation pathology rather than the VLM signal itself. To our knowledge, this is the first work to use frozen VLM signals as an anticipatory cost term inside the CMDP Lagrangian update.

</details>


### [206] [A prior-free blind detection of information leakage from model predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11267)
*Laurence A. Jacobs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a decision-theoretic framework for detecting data leakage using only model predictions and outcomes, proving fundamental impossibility results and proposing a prior-free test based on near-deterministic subgroups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data leakage is a critical reproducibility failure in ML-based science, yet existing detection tools require training code, external data, or domain expertise — none works solely from model outputs, which is what auditors most commonly possess.

Method: The authors construct a decision-theoretic framework where leakage diagnostics are functionals of the predicted-risk/outcome law, parameterized by threshold-weighting linked to proper scoring rules and decision-curve analysis. They prove two key theoretical results: (1) a sharp impossibility theorem showing recalibrated leakage is indistinguishable from honest performance by any function of predictions alone, and (2) a prior-free detection theorem exploiting near-deterministic subgroups (unit-purity heads) that cannot arise from legitimate predictors of non-deterministic outcomes.

Result: The findings organize leakage into a trichotomy: miscalibrated, broad-calibrated, and deterministic, each with a matched detector and failure mode. Validation on UK Biobank using time-windowed comorbidity leakage with graded severity shows a detection floor of approximately ΔC*≈0.007, below which residual leakage is both undetectable from output and too small to alter conclusions. The test returns a verdict in under one second on commodity hardware.

Conclusion: Output-only leakage detection fundamentally fails where residual leakage is indistinguishable from an honestly stronger predictor. The structural lesson is general even though the numerical detection floor is cohort- and endpoint-specific. The proposed framework provides practical, fast diagnostics for the most common auditing scenario.

Abstract: Data leakage -- contamination of a model with information unavailable at baseline -- is the dominant reproducibility failure in machine-learning-based science, yet detection tools require training code, external data, or domain expertise. None operates on the artifact an auditor most often holds: the model's output. We ask what can be decided about leakage from predictions and outcomes alone. We give a decision-theoretic framework in which leakage diagnostics are functionals of the predicted-risk/outcome law, parameterized by a threshold-weighting linked to proper scoring rules and decision-curve analysis. We prove a sharp impossibility: a recalibrated leak matching an honest model's calibration and discrimination is indistinguishable from honest performance by \emph{any} function of the predictions, so the broad class is detectable only against an externally supplied ceiling on achievable discrimination. We then prove what leakage cannot hide: a near-deterministic subgroup -- the signature of a near-label leak -- produces a sustained unit-purity head that no legitimate predictor of a non-deterministic outcome can manufacture, yielding a prior-free test. These results organize leakage into a trichotomy -- miscalibrated, broad-calibrated, and deterministic -- each with a matched detector and failure mode. We validate on UK Biobank using time-windowed comorbidity leakage with known, graded severity, measuring a detection floor of $Δ\cstar \approx 0.007$ on this endpoint, below which residual leakage is undetectable from output and too small to alter conclusions. The numerical floor is cohort- and endpoint-specific; the structural lesson is general: output-only detection fails where residual leakage is indistinguishable from an honestly stronger predictor. The test returns a verdict on a prediction vector in under a second on commodity hardware.

</details>


### [207] [LakeFM: Toward a Foundation Model for Aquatic Ecosystems Using Irregular Multivariate Multi-depth Time Series Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11268)
*Abhilash Neog,Sepideh Fatemi,Medha Sawhney,Kazi Sajeed Mehrab,Aanish Pradhan,Bennett J. McAfee,Emma Marchisin,Arka Daw,Robert Ladwig,Cayelan C. Carey,Paul Hanson,Anuj Karpatne*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LakeFM is a foundation model for lake dynamics forecasting, pre-trained on large-scale ecological datasets (simulated and observed), that handles heterogeneous variables, depths, and irregular observation patterns, outperforming existing time-series models while maintaining physical plausibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing machine learning methods for ecological time-series data suffer from two key limitations: (1) they assume regular sampling in both time and depth, and (2) they fail to generalize across lakes with heterogeneous variables, varying depths, and inconsistent observation patterns. A foundation model approach is needed to overcome these generalization barriers.

Method: LakeFM is a foundation model pre-trained on large-scale ecological datasets combining both simulated and observed lake data. It is designed to handle heterogeneous variables, varying depths, and irregular observation patterns across diverse lakes and reservoirs.

Result: LakeFM learns meaningful representations capturing broader lake-level characteristics and achieves competitive or superior forecasting performance compared to both existing time-series foundation models and non-foundation models, while generating physically plausible predictions consistent with real-world lake dynamics.

Conclusion: LakeFM demonstrates that foundation models pre-trained on diverse ecological data can effectively generalize across lakes and reservoirs, overcoming long-standing limitations of irregular sampling and heterogeneity, and enabling robust, physically consistent forecasting for water quality and ecosystem monitoring.

Abstract: Understanding and forecasting lake dynamics is critical for monitoring water quality and ecosystem health across lakes and reservoirs. While machine learning methods have been recently applied to ecological time-series data, existing works assume regular sampling in time and depth, and struggle to generalize across lakes with heterogeneous variables, depths, and observation patterns. To address these limitations, we introduce \textsc{LakeFM}, a foundation model for aquatic systems, pre-trained on large-scale ecological datasets comprising both simulated and observed lakes. Through extensive empirical evaluation, we show that \textsc{LakeFM} learns meaningful representations spanning broader lake-level characteristics, and achieves competitive or often superior-forecasting performance compared to existing time-series foundation and non-foundation models, while producing physically plausible predictions consistent with real-world lake dynamics.

</details>


### [208] [DeMix: Debugging Training Data with Mixed Data Error Types by Investigating Influence Vectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11616)
*Jiale Deng,Yanyan Shen,Xiaogang Shi,Chai Junjun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeMix is a framework that simultaneously detects erroneous training samples and identifies their specific error types (label errors, feature errors, spurious correlations) by analyzing influence vectors through multi-label classification, with an intervention-based learning strategy for robust generalization. It achieves 22.61% F1 improvement in data debugging and 9.32% gain in downstream task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world datasets contain mixed error types from flawed data preparation pipelines, but existing methods focus only on detecting errors without identifying their specific types. Targeted data repair requires knowing both which samples are erroneous and what type of error they contain—a dual requirement unmet by current data cleaning and attribution approaches.

Method: DeMix leverages the insight that different error types produce distinct patterns on model behavior, captured through influence vectors characterizing each training sample's effect across all validation predictions. The problem is cast as multi-label classification from influence vectors to error types. An intervention-based learning strategy is introduced to guide the classifier toward invariant, error-type-specific rationales, improving generalization.

Result: Evaluated on 11 tasks spanning tabular prediction, recommendation systems, and LLM alignment, DeMix outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 22.61% in data debugging F1-score and yields a 9.32% improvement in task model performance after data repair.

Conclusion: DeMix provides an effective, unified framework for training data debugging that jointly diagnoses erroneous samples and their error types, enabling targeted repair and significantly boosting downstream model performance across diverse domains.

Abstract: High-quality training data is essential for the success of machine learning models. However, real-world datasets often contain mixed types of errors arising from systematic flaws in data preparation pipelines, including label errors, feature errors, and spurious correlations. Effective debugging of training data requires both detecting erroneous samples and identifying their specific error types to enable targeted repair, yet existing data cleaning and attribution methods fail to adequately address this dual requirement. In this paper, we propose DeMix, a novel framework that simultaneously diagnoses erroneous samples and their error types. Our key insight is that different error types produce distinct patterns on model behavior. DeMix captures such error-specific patterns by influence vectors that characterize how each training sample affects model predictions across all validation samples. We formulate training data debugging as a multi-label classification problem where a classifier is developed to predict error types directly from influence vectors. We further introduce an intervention-based learning strategy that guides the classifier to capture invariant rationales specific to each error type, ensuring the learned classifier generalizes effectively. Empirical evaluations on 11 tasks across tabular data prediction, recommendation systems, and LLM alignment demonstrate that DeMix significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving a 22.61% improvement in data debugging F1-score and a 9.32% gain in task model performance after data repair. Code is available at: https://github.com/SJTU-DMTai/DeMix.

</details>


### [209] [Quantifying Subliminal Behavioral Transfer Ratios in Language Model Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11270)
*Uwe Konig,Hamza Kazmi,Ruizhe Li,Maheep Chaudhary*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper quantifies "subliminal learning" — the unintended transfer of undesirable behaviors during benign distillation. By steering teacher models (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) at varying strengths and distilling students on only benign data, the authors measure behavioral transfer ratios. Results show robust transfer with distinct scaling: Llama-2 exhibits a sharp threshold (τ up to 0.32, beyond α=-0.15), while Qwen2.5 shows continuous, higher transfer (τ up to 0.61).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work has provided only qualitative evidence for subliminal learning — the phenomenon where distilling a language model on benign data inadvertently transfers undesirable teacher behaviors. The magnitude and scaling properties of this effect have not been systematically characterized, motivating a rigorous quantitative investigation.

Method: Two teacher models (Llama-2-7B-Chat and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) are steered at varying steering strengths to induce undesirable behaviors. Student models are then distilled from these teachers using only benign data — no harmful examples are present during training. The transfer ratio τ is measured by evaluating distilled students on 100 JailbreakBench prompts, with GPT-4.1 serving as an automated evaluator to assess the presence of transferred undesirable behaviors.

Result: Subliminal behavioral transfer is robust across both model families but exhibits distinct scaling behaviors. Llama-2 shows a sharp threshold phenomenon with τ ∈ {0.25, 0.32} emerging only beyond steering strength α = -0.15. In contrast, Qwen2.5 displays continuous and consistently higher transfer levels, with τ reaching up to 0.61, indicating stronger susceptibility to subliminal learning.

Conclusion: Subliminal learning is a quantifiable and significant risk in knowledge distillation, even when using exclusively benign training data. The magnitude and scaling behavior are model-dependent, with some architectures (Qwen2.5) being substantially more vulnerable than others (Llama-2). This underscores the need for safety-aware distillation practices that account for hidden transfer of undesirable characteristics.

Abstract: Distillation of a language model intended to transfer benign behavior to a student model may also transfer undesirable characteristics, if they are present in the teacher model, a phenomenon known as subliminal learning. While qualitative evidence supports the existence of this effect, its magnitude has not been systematically characterized. This study quantifies subliminal behavioral transfer ratios by steering two teacher models (Llama-2-7B-Chat and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) at varying steering strengths and distilling student models using only benign data. Evaluation on 100 JailbreakBench prompts with GPT-4.1, serving as the evaluator, indicates that transfer is robust but exhibits distinct scaling behaviors. Llama-2 demonstrates a sharp threshold ($τ= {0.25,0.32} \ \text{beyond} \ α= -0.15$), whereas Qwen2.5 displays continuous and higher levels of transfer ($τ$ up to $0.61$).

</details>


### [210] [Federated continual learning: A comprehensive survey on lifelong and privacy-preserving learning over distributed and non-stationary data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11272)
*Masoume Gholizade,Fabrizio Ruffini,Pietro Ducange,Francesco Marcelloni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey of Federated Continual Learning (FCL), which bridges Federated Learning and Continual Learning to enable privacy-preserving, lifelong model adaptation across distributed clients with non-stationary data streams. The paper formalizes FCL, proposes a taxonomy, reviews applications and metrics, and identifies open challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FL systems assume data stationarity, but real-world deployments (healthcare, IIoT, cybersecurity, smart cities) involve non-stationary data streams, causing catastrophic forgetting and instability. CL addresses distribution shifts but has been studied mainly in centralized settings, ignoring FL constraints like privacy, communication limits, and client heterogeneity. FCL unifies these two paradigms.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic survey: (1) formally define the FCL problem and its distinctive characteristics; (2) analyze limitations of classical FL under non-stationarity; (3) propose a multi-dimensional taxonomy to organize FCL approaches; (4) review application domains, data modalities, and evaluation metrics; (5) discuss experimental best practices for assessing long-term performance and forgetting.

Result: The survey organizes the rapidly growing FCL literature into a structured taxonomy, catalogs representative applications and evaluation metrics, and articulates key open challenges including extreme heterogeneity under temporal drift, scalable privacy-preserving memory mechanisms, and the need for standardized benchmarks.

Conclusion: FCL is a critical emerging paradigm for lifelong, adaptive, privacy-aware learning over distributed non-stationary data. The field requires advances in handling extreme heterogeneity, scalable memory design, and benchmarking to transition toward robust real-world deployment. This survey serves as both a reference and roadmap for the community.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative and privacy-preserving model training across distributed clients, but most existing FL systems implicitly assume data stationarity. In real-world settings-such as healthcare, industrial IoT (IIOT), cybersecurity, and smart cities-data streams are inherently non-stationary, leading classical FL methods to suffer from performance degradation, instability, and catastrophic forgetting.
  Continual Learning (CL) addresses learning under evolving data distributions but has been largely studied in centralized settings, overlooking key constraints of federated systems, including privacy, limited communication, and client heterogeneity. Federated Continual Learning (FCL) emerges at the intersection of FL and CL, aiming to support lifelong, adaptive, and privacy-aware learning over distributed and non-stationary data.
  This survey provides a comprehensive and systematic overview of FCL. We first present a formal definition of the FCL problem and clarify its distinctive characteristics. We then analyze the limitations of classical FL under non-stationary conditions, highlighting how CL principles support long-term adaptation. To organize the rapidly growing literature, we propose a multi-dimensional taxonomy of FCL approaches. Furthermore, we review representative application domains and data modalities, summarize commonly used evaluation metrics, and discuss experimental perspectives for assessing long-term performance and forgetting. Finally, we highlight key open challenges, including handling extreme heterogeneity under temporal drift, designing scalable and privacy-preserving memory mechanisms, and establishing standardized benchmarks. This survey aims to serve as a reference and a roadmap for advancing FCL toward robust and deployable real-world systems.

</details>


### [211] [RoVE: Rotary Value Embeddings Attention for Relative Position-dependent Value Pathways](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11275)
*Alejandro García-Castellanos,Maurice Weiler,Erik J Bekkers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RoVE proposes rotating value embeddings simultaneously with keys in RoPE-based attention, making values position-sensitive. This parameter-free modification transforms RoPE attention into attentive convolution, unifies disparate formulations across domains, and yields consistent empirical gains on in-context learning, OOD perplexity, and long-context retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RoPE makes attention scores position-relative but leaves the value pathway position-blind — a value token sends the same message regardless of distance from the query. This limits the model's capacity for long-range aggregation tasks where position-dependent value representations matter.

Method: RoVE (Rotary Value Embeddings) applies the same rotary position encoding to value vectors that RoPE already applies to keys. This is a parameter-free modification: values are rotated by their absolute positions, making the message they transmit position-sensitive. The authors show this transforms RoPE attention into "attentive convolution," a single operation that subsumes convolution-like weighting with attention-based data dependence.

Result: Trained 124M and 354M GPT-2 models with RoVE consistently outperform RoPE baselines on few-shot in-context learning, out-of-distribution perplexity, and long-context retrieval benchmarks. Gains are most pronounced on tasks requiring long-range aggregation.

Conclusion: Making values position-sensitive via rotation is a simple, parameter-free enhancement to RoPE that unifies attention and convolution under the attentive convolution framework, with practical empirical benefits particularly for long-range dependencies.

Abstract: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) make attention scores position-relative but leave the value pathway position-blind: the message sent by a value token is the same regardless of its distance from the query. We propose RoVE, a parameter-free modification that makes values position-sensitive by rotating them simultaneously with keys, and show that it turns RoPE attention into attentive convolution. This new perspective unifies several independent formulations of the same operation across computer vision, robotics, and modern LLM architectures. Trained 124M and 354M GPT-2 models show consistent empirical gains over RoPE on few-shot in-context learning, out-of-distribution perplexity, and long-context retrieval, with the clearest improvements on tasks that require long-range aggregation.

</details>


### [212] [Least-Action-Guided Diffusion for Physical Extrapolation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11277)
*Zhongxin Yang,Yuanwei Bin,Xiang I. A. Yang,Shiyi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LAPG is a two-stage diffusion framework that uses the principle of least action as a differentiable inference-time correction to improve physical consistency during extrapolation, outperforming training-time physics-informed baselines across multiple dynamical systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models trained on finite data ranges produce physically inconsistent predictions when extrapolating to out-of-distribution conditions (time, parameters, geometry). Existing physics-informed approaches rely on training-time constraints or pointwise residual penalties with empirical loss balancing, which are insufficient for reliable extrapolation.

Method: LAPG combines a conditional score-based diffusion model with an action-derived physical guidance score in two stages: (1) the learned score model generates an in-distribution proposal, then (2) an action-based variational prior refines the proposal toward the target out-of-distribution condition, turning the least action principle into a differentiable inference-time correction.

Result: Evaluated on ODE/PDE systems (free fall, conservative and dissipative spring-mass, interacting point vortices, potential flow over parameterized airfoils), LAPG reduces phase drift, preserves dissipative decay, captures vortex motion, and improves airfoil lift response under temporal, parameter, and geometric extrapolation compared to training-time physics-informed baselines.

Conclusion: Inference-time physical guidance via the least action principle is an effective alternative to training-time constraints for improving generative model extrapolation in computational physics, avoiding the need for empirical loss balancing while maintaining physical consistency across diverse dynamical systems.

Abstract: Reliable extrapolation remains a central challenge for generative models in computational physics, because models trained over finite ranges of time, parameters, or geometries may produce physically inconsistent predictions outside the training distribution. We introduce a least-action-principle-guided diffusion, LAPG, a framework that promotes physical consistency during inference rather than relying solely on constraints imposed during training. The method combines a conditional score-based diffusion model with an action-derived physical guidance score. In the first stage, the learned score model generates an in-distribution proposal; in the second, an action-based variational prior refines this proposal toward the target out-of-distribution condition. This formulation turns the principle of least action into a differentiable inference-time correction mechanism and provides an alternative to pointwise residual penalties that often require empirical loss balancing.
  We evaluate LAPG on representative ordinary- and partial-differential-equation systems, including free fall, conservative and dissipative spring-mass dynamics, interacting point vortices, and potential flow over parameterized airfoils. In temporal, parameter, and geometric extrapolation tests, LAPG reduces phase drift, preserves dissipative decay, captures vortex motion, and improves the lift response of airfoil flows compared with training-time physics-informed baselines.

</details>


### [213] [FreeBridge: Variational Schrödinger Bridges for Cellular Transition Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11286)
*Xurui Wang,Qin Ren,Jun Ma,Haibin Ling,Chenyu You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FreeBridge introduces a Schrödinger Bridge framework for modeling single-cell perturbation trajectories from fixed-cell imaging data, constraining stochastic transport within a learned cellular manifold to improve intermediate-state biological plausibility while maintaining endpoint fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-content imaging assays require chemical fixation, making continuous single-cell trajectories unobservable. Existing generative models align endpoint distributions well but fail to constrain intermediate evolution—multiple stochastic processes can connect identical marginals while traversing morphologically invalid regions. Biologically interpretable perturbation dynamics require geometric grounding on the true cellular manifold.

Method: FreeBridge formulates single-cell transition modeling as a Schrödinger Bridge problem with endpoint-only supervision. It defines atomic states as instance-segmented single-cell representations on a fixed cellular manifold, then learns stochastic transport constrained within this geometry using empirical latent support regularization. This ensures intermediate trajectories remain on biologically plausible regions of the learned manifold.

Result: Across BBBC021, RxRx1, and JUMP benchmarks, FreeBridge achieves competitive or improved endpoint fidelity and mechanism-of-action retention under a unified evaluation protocol. On BBBC021, it additionally reduces intermediate support violations, demonstrating more biologically plausible trajectory intermediates.

Conclusion: Geometric grounding via manifold-constrained stochastic transport is critical for biologically interpretable perturbation dynamics. FreeBridge demonstrates that the Schrödinger Bridge framework with empirical latent support regularization effectively balances endpoint accuracy with intermediate trajectory plausibility.

Abstract: High-content imaging assays quantify cellular responses to chemical and genetic perturbations, yet continuous trajectories of individual cells are unobservable because cells are chemically fixed at acquisition. Perturbation modeling therefore reduces to inferring stochastic transport between control and treated populations observed only as separate marginals. While recent generative models achieve strong end-point alignment, boundary consistency does not determine intermediate evolution: multiple stochastic processes may connect identical marginals while traversing regions unsupported by observed single-cell morphologies. We introduce \textbf{FreeBridge}, a Schrödinger Bridge formulation for single-cell transition modeling under endpoint-only supervision. FreeBridge defines atomic states as instance-segmented single-cell representations, establishing a fixed cellular manifold, and learns stochastic transport constrained within this geometry via empirical latent support regularization. Across BBBC021, RxRx1, and JUMP, FreeBridge maintains competitive or improved endpoint fidelity and mechanism-of-action retention under a unified evaluation protocol; on BBBC021, it further reduces intermediate support violations. These findings highlight the importance of geometric grounding for biologically interpretable perturbation dynamics. Project page: https://y-research-sbu.github.io/FreeBridge/.

</details>


### [214] [FlowBank: Query-Adaptive Agentic Workflows Optimization through Precompute-and-Reuse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11290)
*Lingzhi Yuan,Chenghao Deng,Fangxu Yu,Souradip Chakraborty,Mohammad Rostami,Furong Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowBank proposes a three-stage framework that builds a compact, reusable bank of agentic workflows offline and adaptively selects among them at inference time, outperforming both single-workflow and per-query generation paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent workflow optimization faces a trade-off: task-level methods find one workflow offline but discard complementary candidates, while query-level methods generate per-query workflows at high inference cost. The authors observe these paradigms are complementary—offline-discovered workflows solve different query subsets, and many expensive per-query cases can be handled by cheaper precomputed workflows.

Method: FlowBank is a three-stage framework: (1) Diversifying uses DiverseFlow to steer offline search toward under-covered queries, producing a high-coverage candidate pool; (2) Curating applies CuraFlow to compress the pool into a compact, low-redundancy portfolio; (3) Matching formulates deployment as edge-value prediction on a query-workflow bipartite graph, routing each incoming query to the portfolio member with best predicted utility under a performance-cost trade-off.

Result: Across five benchmarks, FlowBank achieves the highest average score among evaluated methods while remaining cost-competitive, improving over the strongest automated and handcrafted baselines by 4.26% and 14.92% relative, respectively.

Conclusion: A portfolio-based approach—building a bank of complementary workflows and adaptively routing queries—resolves the unsatisfying trade-off between task-level and query-level optimization, delivering superior performance without sacrificing cost efficiency.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems are increasingly powerful, but current agentic workflow optimization paradigms make an unsatisfying trade-off. Task-level methods spend substantial offline compute yet deploy only a single workflow, leaving complementary candidates unused, while query-level methods synthesize a new workflow per query at substantial inference cost. Our motivating analysis shows these paradigms are more complementary than competing: workflows discovered during offline search often solve different subsets of queries, and many queries handled by expensive query-level generation can already be solved by cheaper precomputed workflows. This suggests a different objective: rather than searching for one universally best workflow or regenerating one per instance, we should build a compact bank of reusable, complementary workflows and select among them adaptively at inference time. Doing so requires solving three coupled problems: generating complementary rather than redundant candidates, compressing them into a small deployable portfolio, and assigning each query to the right workflow under a performance-cost trade-off. To this end, we present FlowBank, a three-stage framework for portfolio-based agentic workflow optimization. Diversifying proposes DiverseFlow to steer search toward under-covered queries and produce a high-coverage candidate pool. Curating proposes CuraFlow to compress this pool into a compact portfolio with minimal redundancy. Matching casts deployment as edge-value prediction on a query-workflow bipartite graph and routes each incoming query to the portfolio member with the best predicted utility. Across five benchmarks, FlowBank achieves the highest average score among the evaluated methods while remaining cost-competitive, improving over the strongest automated and handcrafted baselines by 4.26% and 14.92% relative, respectively.

</details>


### [215] [Learning from almost nothing: How neural networks survive heavy input corruption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11319)
*Justin Tahmassebpur,Asadullah Bhuiyan,Hyejin Kim,Omri Lesser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural networks exhibit surprising robustness to extreme input corruption (attribute noise), maintaining accuracy even with >90% corrupted inputs. This phenomenon is explained analytically via a mean-field approach showing that infinite-width networks converge to a nearest-class-mean (prototype) decision rule, which matches finite-network behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While label-noise robustness has been extensively studied, attribute noise (corrupted inputs with clean labels) has received far less analytical attention. The paper aims to understand why neural networks remain robust to extreme input corruption, a phenomenon that defies intuition since individual training examples carry almost no signal at high corruption levels.

Method: The authors conduct experiments using multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) on corrupted classification datasets under two corruption models (additive noise and replacement noise). To theoretically explain the observed robustness, they analyze infinite-width networks in the heavy-corruption regime using a mean-field-inspired approach, deriving a leading-order decision rule. They examine universality across depth, activation functions, and noise distributions.

Result: The leading-order decision rule is a prototype/nearest-class-mean mechanism: the network assigns each test point to the class whose training-set average it most closely resembles. This centroid rule is universal across MLP architectures of any depth, a wide range of activation functions, and diverse noise distributions. Finite-width network behavior closely matches this analytical prediction.

Conclusion: Learning succeeds under extreme attribute noise because networks effectively implement a simple, analytically tractable prototype rule — collapsing noisy individual examples into class centroids. This provides an interpretable account of neural network robustness to input corruption and bridges practical robustness questions with fundamental learnability theory.

Abstract: Learning from imperfect data is a central theme in machine learning, connecting practical questions of robustness to fundamental questions of learnability. Here we examine attribute noise: learning from corrupted inputs while keeping the labels intact, a setting that has received considerably less analytical attention than its label-noise counterpart. We consider two types of corruption models: additive noise and replacement noise. Through experiments with multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) on corrupted classification datasets, we find that neural networks remain robust, maintaining well-above-chance accuracy even when inputs are >90% corrupted -- far beyond human recognition. To understand this robustness, we analyze infinite-width networks in the heavy-corruption regime using a mean-field-inspired approach and derive a leading-order decision rule for the classification outcome: the network implements a prototype rule, the nearest-class-mean, assigning each test point to the class whose training-set average it most closely resembles. This leading-order decision rule is universal across a broad range of MLP architectures, holding for any depth, as well as a wide class of activation functions and noise distributions. The same centroid mechanism closely matches finite-width network behavior in our experiments and provides an interpretable and analytically tractable account of why learning can succeed even when individual training examples carry almost no signal.

</details>


### [216] [Energy-Conserved Neural Pipelines: Attenuating Error Propagation in Modular Neural Networks via Physical Conservation Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11341)
*David Young,Swan Yi Htet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces energy conservation as a hard physical constraint for modular neural network pipelines, enforcing exact preservation of activation energy (squared L2 norm) across module boundaries to combat error compounding. Experiments show significant robustness gains over baselines across various noise types and depths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modular neural network pipelines suffer from error compounding, where noise at any module boundary propagates and amplifies through subsequent modules. Existing approaches using soft energy penalties are insufficient because they merely discourage energy changes rather than preventing them outright. The authors propose a principled solution grounded in a physical invariance — strict energy conservation — to make pipelines robust to inter-module noise.

Method: The method enforces activation energy (squared L2 norm of feature vectors) to be exactly preserved at every module boundary as a hard physical constraint. This is not a soft penalty but an inviolable conservation law: the network may redistribute energy across neurons (permitting representational flexibility) but cannot create or destroy it. A formal bound is also derived proving that conserved noise energy is strictly less than input noise energy.

Result: Five experiments demonstrate the method's effectiveness: (1) On CIFAR-10 with noise sigma=0.2, energy conservation retains 77.4% clean accuracy vs. 35.1% for baselines and 30.9% for energy-penalized models. (2) Pipelines achieve depth-invariance, retaining 93.3% accuracy at depths 2–5 with noise at every boundary. (3) The advantage generalizes across systematic bias (+45.1%), Gaussian (+40.4%), and adversarial noise (+4.8%), with no effect on dropout (-0.3%). (4) On ResNet-18, the advantage scales inversely with intrinsic normalization: +0.3 pp with BatchNorm, +26.2 pp without at sigma=0.2, reaching +58.0 pp at sigma=0.5. (5) On a real modular robotic pipeline (MuJoCo, Franka Panda), conservation provides +18.9 pp average advantage on monocular-depth-style noise.

Conclusion: Energy conservation as a hard physical constraint effectively mitigates error compounding in modular neural pipelines, providing substantial and statistically significant robustness gains across diverse noise regimes, architectures, and application domains — from vision to robotics — without requiring soft penalty tuning.

Abstract: Modular neural network pipelines suffer from error compounding: noise at any module boundary propagates and potentially amplifies through subsequent modules. We introduce energy conservation as a hard physical constraint on inter-module information flow. Activation energy (the squared L2 norm of feature vectors) is enforced to be exactly preserved at every module boundary. Unlike soft energy penalties, conservation is an inviolable law: the network may redistribute energy across neurons but cannot create or destroy it. Four experiments on CIFAR-10 demonstrate: (1) conservation retains 77.4% of clean accuracy at noise sigma=0.2, versus 35.1% for baselines and 30.9% for energy-penalized models (p<0.001, 5 seeds); (2) pipelines become depth-invariant, retaining 93.3% at depths 2 through 5 with noise at every boundary; (3) the advantage generalizes to systematic bias (+45.1%), Gaussian (+40.4%), and adversarial noise (+4.8%), with a principled non-effect on dropout (-0.3%); (4) on ResNet-18, the conservation advantage scales inversely with intrinsic normalization: +0.3 pp with BatchNorm, +26.2 pp without at sigma=0.2, reaching +58.0 pp at sigma=0.5. Experiment 5 validates the operator on a real modular robotic pipeline (MuJoCo physics, Franka Panda). Across three independent runs on separate machines (90 trials per cell), conservation provides +18.9 pp average advantage on monocular-depth-style noise. A formal bound proves conserved noise energy is strictly less than input noise energy.

</details>


### [217] [SwiftCTS: Fast Cross-Design Prediction and Pareto Optimization of Clock Tree Metrics via Few-Shot Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11348)
*Barsat Khadka,Kawsher Roxy,Md Rubel Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SwiftCTS is a physics-informed ML surrogate for Clock Tree Synthesis that trains in under 5s on CPU, achieves sub-millisecond inference, and uses a K-shot multiplicative calibration to adapt to unseen designs without retraining — reducing power/wirelength prediction errors to 3.3% and <1% respectively. Coupled with evolutionary optimization, it evaluates 100K configurations in under 10s, yielding Pareto-optimal fronts validated within OpenROAD with <0.5% closed-loop error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CTS is computationally expensive, requiring many iterative EDA tool runs to explore vast configuration spaces. Existing ML approaches need costly retraining/fine-tuning for new macro architectures and cannot scale to millions of combinatorial evaluations. The work aims to address both the training cost and out-of-distribution generalization problems simultaneously.

Method: SwiftCTS combines lightweight physics-grounded statistical features with gradient-boosted ensemble models (e.g., XGBoost/LightGBM) for fast surrogate modeling. For OOD generalization, a K-shot multiplicative calibration mechanism is introduced, anchoring predictions using only 1–2 physical reference runs on the unseen design. The surrogate is integrated with an evolutionary optimizer to efficiently search the CTS configuration space for Pareto-optimal trade-offs.

Result: On unseen macros, K-shot calibration reduces power prediction error from 24.5% to 3.3% and wirelength error from 56.6% to under 1%. The evolutionary optimizer evaluates 100,000 configurations in under 10 seconds. Closed-loop validation on an OOD benchmark yields power/wirelength prediction errors below 0.5% and timing skew predictions within 5 ps, consistently outperforming default tool heuristics.

Conclusion: SwiftCTS provides a practical, computationally efficient CTS optimization framework that eliminates the need for GPU acceleration or per-design retraining. The K-shot calibration strategy effectively bridges the sim-to-real gap for OOD designs, and the tight closed-loop validation confirms the surrogate's reliability for guiding physical design optimization.

Abstract: Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) is a computationally expensive stage in the physical design flow, requiring iterative EDA tool invocations to navigate a vast configuration space for optimal power, wirelength, and timing skew. Existing machine learning approaches require computationally expensive retraining or fine-tuning cycles to adapt to unseen macro architectures and are architecturally mismatched to the millions of evaluations demanded by exhaustive combinatorial search. We present SwiftCTS, a physics-informed surrogate framework that addresses both limitations simultaneously. By coupling lightweight, physics-grounded statistical features with gradient-boosted ensembles, SwiftCTS trains in under five seconds on a CPU and delivers sub-millisecond inference without GPU support. To handle out-of-distribution (OOD) designs without retraining or fine-tuning, we introduce a K-shot multiplicative calibration mechanism that anchors predictions to just one or two physical reference runs, reducing power prediction error from 24.5\% to 3.3\% and wirelength error from 56.6\% to under 1\% on unseen macros. Integrating this engine with an evolutionary optimizer, SwiftCTS evaluates 100,000 CTS configurations in under ten seconds, yielding Pareto-optimal frontiers that are physically validated within the OpenROAD flow. Closed-loop validation confirms prediction errors below 0.5\% for power and wirelength, and timing skew predictions within five picoseconds on an OOD benchmark, consistently outperforming default tool heuristics across all target metrics. Code publicly available at: \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SwiftCTS-7E6E}{https://github.com/BarsatKhadka/SwiftCTS}

</details>


### [218] [GLACIER: A Multimodal Student-Teacher Foundation Model for Molecular Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11382)
*Emily Nguyen,Yongchan Hong,Harsh Toshniwal,Yan Liu,Andreas Luttens*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GLACIER introduces a student-teacher framework that fuses molecular graphs, SMILES strings, and physicochemical descriptors via a Finsler geometry-aware module, then distills knowledge from large teacher models (MiniMol, MolFormer) through contrastive learning to produce lightweight yet powerful molecular embeddings for property prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art molecular deep learning models are increasingly computationally expensive to develop and deploy, limiting scalability. Additionally, most large-scale models are unimodal, failing to exploit complementary information across different molecular representations (graphs, SMILES, physicochemical descriptors).

Method: A three-stage student-teacher framework: (1) Pretrain three student encoders on 100K drug-like molecules — a message-passing neural network (graphs), a transformer (SMILES), and an MLP (physicochemical descriptors); (2) Fuse the three modalities using a novel Finsler geometry-aware fusion module; (3) Distill knowledge from large teacher models (MiniMol, MolFormer) into a single lightweight model via contrastive learning.

Result: GLACIER demonstrates strong predictive performance and computational efficiency on complex molecular property prediction tasks, validating the benefits of multimodal fusion and knowledge distillation in this domain.

Conclusion: GLACIER provides an effective and scalable approach to molecular representation learning by combining multimodal fusion with teacher-student distillation, achieving a favorable trade-off between predictive accuracy and computational cost.

Abstract: Deep learning models facilitate the discovery of molecules with tailored properties among billions of candidate compounds. However, the computational burden to develop and deploy state-of-the-art models continuously increases, limiting their scalability. Most large-scale models are unimodal in nature and overlook the potential to leverage complementary molecular data modalities. To address these shortcomings, this paper introduces the Graph-Language Alignment for Chemical Inference and Exploration using Representations (GLACIER) model, a student-teacher framework that integrates molecular graphs, SMILES strings, and physicochemical descriptors to learn rich molecular embeddings. Our framework consists of three stages: (1) we pretrain three student encoders on 100,000 drug-like molecules: a message-passing neural network for molecular graphs, a transformer-based encoder for SMILES strings, and a multilayer perceptron for physicochemical descriptors, (2) we fuse these student modalities using a novel Finsler geometry-aware module, and (3) distill complementary knowledge from large teacher models, including MiniMol and MolFormer, into a single lightweight model via contrastive learning. We demonstrate that GLACIER is a robust framework that delivers high predictive performance and computational efficiency in complex molecular property prediction tasks. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/eemokey/glacier.

</details>


### [219] [Recursive Binding on a Budget: Subspace Carving in Order-p Tensor Memories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11391)
*Travis Pence,Daisuke Yamada,Vikas Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Orthogonal Subspace Carving (OSC) is a memory architecture that achieves deep recursive binding with constant memory footprint by projecting filler-role bindings onto the null space of role bases, decoupling tensor order from structural depth while maintaining geometric orthogonality between bound structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tensor Product Representations (TPR) preserve structural fidelity for symbolic reasoning but suffer from exponential dimensionality growth with recursive depth. Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSA) maintain constant dimensionality but lose capacity and fidelity due to noisy superposition. The authors seek a middle ground: constant memory footprint with preserved structural fidelity for deep recursive binding.

Method: OSC binds fillers to roles by projecting each filler onto the null space of its corresponding role basis vector, then aggregating these projections into a fixed-order tensor (order-p). This enforces geometric orthogonality between bound structures within a static memory trace. Retrieval is performed via recognition (unbinding by projecting onto the role subspace). The authors also formalize TPR as a special case of binding in Clifford algebra and provide a Clifford formulation of OSC.

Result: OSC decouples tensor order from structural depth, enabling deep recursive binding within a constant memory footprint. Component vectors can be orders of magnitude smaller than the memory tensor, yielding superior memory efficiency under high superposition. TPR is shown to be a special case of Clifford algebraic binding.

Conclusion: OSC bridges the gap between TPR (high fidelity, exponential cost) and VSA (constant cost, low fidelity), offering a principled geometric approach to recursive symbolic binding with constant memory and preserved structural fidelity, grounded in Clifford algebra.

Abstract: Tensor Product Representations provide the structural fidelity required for symbolic reasoning in models but suffer from exponential dimensionality growth when encoding deep recursive structures. Conversely, Vector Symbolic Architectures maintain constant dimensionality but sacrifice capacity and fidelity due to noisy compression via superposition. In this work, we propose Orthogonal Subspace Carving (OSC), a memory architecture that binds fillers to roles by projecting onto the null space of the role basis before aggregating into a fixed order-p tensor. OSC uses projections to enforce geometric orthogonality between bound structures within a static memory trace. We show that this mechanism decouples the tensor order from the structural depth, enabling deep recursive binding within a constant memory footprint. By performing retrieval via recognition, this construction allows for component vectors that are orders of magnitude smaller than the memory tensor, giving superior memory efficiency in settings involving high superposition. We also show that TPR is a special case of binding in Clifford algebra, and give a Clifford formulation of OSC.

</details>


### [220] [Risk Under Pressure: Compute-Aware Evaluation of Adversarial Robustness in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11409)
*Malikeh Ehghaghi,Boglárka Ecsedi,Marsha Chechik,Colin Raffel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a compute-aware adversarial robustness evaluation framework for LLMs using cumulative FLOPs as a proxy for adversarial effort, introduces risk-compute curves, and reveals key findings about how alignment, scaling, and attack types affect the computational cost of jailbreaking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM adversarial robustness evaluations report ASR under fixed query budgets, treating all attacks as equally costly, but real-world attack strategies differ in computational expense by orders of magnitude. This obscures the true effort required to jailbreak a model and whether an attack's cost justifies its payoff.

Method: The authors propose a compute-aware evaluation framework using computational pressure (cumulative FLOPs) as a proxy for adversarial effort. They introduce risk-compute curves that map compute budgets to attack risk, and derive two summary metrics for the average pressure required for attack success. The framework is applied across ten models (three families, four training stages), three attack strategies (gradient-based, iterative refinement, template-based), and two jailbreak robustness benchmarks.

Result: (1) Alignment training has non-monotonic effects on compute-space robustness. (2) Scaling model size reduces gradient-based attack effectiveness but has limited impact on cheaper template-based attacks. (3) Gradient-based attacks optimized on a surrogate model can transfer to a target model, reducing attacker costs. (4) Compute cost varies up to ~5× across harm categories within a single model. (5) Safety-aligned RL increases aggregate cost while leaving some harm categories disproportionately accessible.

Conclusion: Compute-aware evaluation provides a more nuanced understanding of adversarial robustness than fixed-budget ASR alone. The framework and risk-compute curves are released to enable better risk assessment that accounts for the economic realities faced by adversaries.

Abstract: Adversarial robustness evaluations of large language models (LLMs) typically report attack success rate (ASR) under fixed query budgets, implicitly treating all attacks as equally costly. In practice, the computational expense of different attack strategies can vary by orders of magnitude. Consequently, ASR at a fixed budget can obscure the true effort required to jailbreak a model, thereby making it hard to determine whether an attack's cost justifies its payoff to the attacker. We propose a compute-aware evaluation framework based on computational pressure, measured in cumulative floating-point operations (FLOPs), as a proxy for adversarial effort. We introduce risk-compute curves, which map compute budgets to attack risk, and derive two metrics that summarize the average pressure required for a given attack to succeed. Across ten models spanning three families and four different stages in language model training and alignment, evaluated with three attack strategies (gradient-based, iterative refinement, and template-based) on two jailbreak robustness benchmarks, we find: (1) alignment training has non-monotonic effects on compute-space robustness; (2) scaling model size reduces gradient-based attack effectiveness but has limited impact on cheaper template-based attacks; (3) gradient-based attacks optimized on a surrogate model can transfer to a separate target model, providing a way to reduce attacker costs; (4) compute cost varies by up to ${\approx}5{\times}$ across harm categories within a single model; and (5) safety-aligned RL increases aggregate cost while leaving some categories disproportionately accessible. We release our framework to enable compute-aware risk assessment and evaluation.

</details>


### [221] [Signed Compression Progress on a Sealed Audit is Goodhart-Resistant](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11417)
*Ayush Mittal,Dhruv Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proves that intrinsic reward based on signed decrease of a fixed sealed-audit loss is "credible": cumulative reward telescopes exactly to endpoint audit improvement, preventing agents from inflating reward without genuine learning. Finite-audit bounds, failure modes, a Lean 4 mechanization, and ARC-TGI experiments are provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compression progress as intrinsic motivation — rewarding agents when their world model improves — has a long-standing but unproven "folk claim" of credibility. The authors aim to formalize and prove that such reward cannot be gamed: no policy can indefinitely push up cumulative reward while true audit performance stagnates.

Method: The authors define intrinsic reward as the signed decrease in a fixed sealed-audit loss: r_t = E(θ_{t-1}) - E(θ_t). They prove telescoping (cumulative reward equals endpoint audit improvement), derive a finite-audit bound with sharp false-positive budget using uniform audit deviation Δ_n(F,δ), mechanize the structural core in Lean 4, and validate with ARC-TGI grid-transformation experiments including adaptive holdout attacks.

Result: Telescoping guarantees cumulative reward exactly equals audit improvement. For finite audits, cumulative empirical reward ≤ true improvement + 2Δ_n(F,δ), where the bound is horizon-free. Empirically, finite-audit deviation scales as n^{-0.527}; signed progress resists clip-farming, stream leakage, and noisy-TV curiosity; naive reusable audits are exploitable but standard release defenses keep attacks below the 2Δ_n threshold.

Conclusion: Signed compression progress on a sealed audit is a reliable accounting signal of genuine learning improvement. The framework identifies precise failure modes (clipping, own-stream scoring, reusable panels with high-capacity models, vacuous neural-class bounds) and provides both theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.

Abstract: Compression progress is a long-standing proposal for intrinsic motivation: reward an agent when its world model becomes better at predicting or compressing experience. The folk claim is that this reward is "credible" because it is paid only for learning. We make this precise and prove it. If intrinsic reward is the signed decrease of a fixed sealed-audit loss, r_t = E(theta_{t-1}) - E(theta_t), then cumulative reward telescopes exactly to endpoint audit improvement, so no policy can push reward up indefinitely while true audit performance stagnates or degrades. For finite audit panels the same result holds with a sharp false-positive budget: cumulative empirical reward is at most true audit improvement plus 2 Delta_n(F, delta), the uniform audit deviation of the model class. This is horizon-free: adaptivity over time costs nothing once the sealed panel uniformly controls the class.
  The theorem also identifies the failure modes: the guarantee disappears if progress is clipped, scored on the agent's own stream, exposed to a high-capacity model on a reusable panel, or applied to a neural class that makes Delta_n vacuous. We give a Lean 4 mechanization of the structural core (telescoping, the finite-audit bound, finite Gibbs, and the entropy floor) and an experiment suite on ARC-TGI grid-transformation generators with adaptive holdout attacks. Experiments confirm the theory: finite-audit deviation scales as n^{-0.527}; signed progress resists clip-farming, stream leakage, and noisy-TV curiosity; naive reusable audits are exploitable by black-box scalar feedback, while standard release defenses keep the attack below the 2 Delta_n threshold. Signed compression progress on a sealed audit is an accounting signal of genuine improvement.

</details>


### [222] [Mirror Descent Beyond Euclidean Stability: An Exponential Separation in Initialization Sensitivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11431)
*Shira Vansover-Hager,Matan Schliserman,Ofir Schlisselberg,Tomer Koren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mirror Descent with non-quadratic regularizers can be exponentially more sensitive to initialization perturbations than Gradient Descent, even under favorable conditions (convex, smooth objectives; strongly convex, well-conditioned regularizers). The paper constructs a 3D counterexample, analyzes KL-regularized MD on the simplex, and proposes Bregman anchoring as a stabilization mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MD is widely used in RL and LLM post-training (e.g., KL-regularized policy optimization), often starting from a pretrained model. Understanding sensitivity to initialization is critical for reproducibility and reliability. While quadratic-regularized MD (including GD) is known to be stable, the robustness of non-quadratic MD remains unexamined.

Method: The authors provide a theoretical analysis of initialization sensitivity in MD. They construct a 3D counterexample with a convex, smooth objective and a strongly convex, smooth, well-conditioned regularizer (non-quadratic) where perturbation amplification is exponential in iterations T. They also analyze KL-regularized MD on the simplex for linear objectives in high-dimensional or near-boundary regimes. Finally, they study Bregman regularization toward an anchor point as a stabilization strategy, comparing initialization-anchored vs. fixed-point-anchored variants.

Result: 1. Non-quadratic MD can amplify an ε-perturbation to min{polylog⁻¹(1/ε), ε e^{Ω(ηT)}} after T steps. 2. KL-regularized MD on the simplex amplifies perturbations exponentially fast under high dimensionality or near-boundary conditions, even for linear objectives. 3. Bregman anchoring stabilizes dynamics while preserving optimization guarantees. 4. Anchoring at a fixed point is more stable than anchoring at the initialization.

Conclusion: Non-quadratic MD exhibits fundamentally worse initialization sensitivity than GD, with exponential amplification possible. This has practical implications for KL-regularized methods in RL/LLM fine-tuning.
