Bregman anchoring toward a fixed point offers an effective remedy, preserving optimization performance while improving stability.

Abstract: Mirror Descent (MD) extends Gradient Descent (GD) beyond Euclidean geometry and has recently reappeared as a lens for KL-regularized policy optimization in reinforcement learning and LLM post-training. This raises a basic robustness question, crucial to reproducibility and reliability: how sensitive are MD dynamics to their inputs? We focus on initialization, often itself a pretrained or previously aligned model. Quadratic-regularized MD, including GD and Mahalanobis geometries, is well-known to be stable for convex smooth objectives. We show a sharp contrast: once the regularizer is non-quadratic, MD can be exponentially more sensitive to initialization than GD, even with a well-conditioned regularizer in Euclidean norm. We give a three-dimensional construction with a convex, smooth objective and a strongly convex, smooth, well-conditioned regularizer where an initial $\varepsilon$ perturbation is quickly amplified to $\min\{\text{polylog}^{-1}(1/\varepsilon), \varepsilon e^{Ω(ηT)}\}$ after $T$ iterations of MD with step size $η$. For canonical KL-regularized MD on the simplex, we show that even linear objectives can amplify an initial $\varepsilon$ perturbation exponentially fast in high-dimensional or near-boundary regimes. Finally, we show that adding a Bregman regularization term toward an anchor point can stabilize the dynamics while largely preserving the optimization guarantees, and that the choice of anchor is crucial: anchoring at the initialization only partially mitigates the instability, whereas anchoring at a fixed point yields a more stable mechanism.

</details>


### [223] [LSTM-Based Detection of Structural Breaks in Property Insurance Loss Reserving: A Climate-Informed Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11463)
*Thomas Mbrice,Shashwat Panigrahi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This white paper proposes a research program testing whether LSTM neural networks can improve loss reserving accuracy by 15–20% for catastrophe-exposed years compared to traditional actuarial methods (Chain Ladder, Bornhuetter-Ferguson, Cape Cod), using Florida and Louisiana regulatory triangle data enriched with NOAA climate variables, and provides formal probabilistic guarantees for structural break detection to compensate for limited catastrophe observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Climate-driven catastrophes systematically violate the stability assumptions underlying traditional actuarial loss reserving methods (Chain Ladder, Bornhuetter-Ferguson, Cape Cod), which assume stationary development patterns. This creates a pressing need for reserving techniques that can detect and adapt to structural breaks more rapidly and accurately, thereby safeguarding insurer solvency in an era of accelerating climate risk.

Method: The research employs LSTM neural networks trained on 15+ years of regulatory development triangle data from Florida and Louisiana, enriched with NOAA hurricane intensity indices and sea surface temperature data. The LSTM is benchmarked against three traditional methods (Chain Ladder, Bornhuetter-Ferguson, Cape Cod). A theoretical framework is developed that formalizes LSTM structural break detection in probabilistic terms, yielding formal performance guarantees designed to address the statistical challenge posed by the limited number of catastrophe events in the test period.

Result: The paper presents a research design and hypotheses rather than empirical results. The central hypothesis anticipates a 15–20% improvement in reserve accuracy for catastrophe-exposed years using the LSTM approach, a threshold informed by prior neural network reserving literature and the formal convergence results developed in the paper.

Conclusion: The paper concludes by documenting the expected contributions — improved reserving accuracy under climate-driven structural breaks with formal theoretical backing — alongside a candid assessment of limitations. As a white paper, it frames the research agenda rather than reporting completed empirical findings.

Abstract: Accurate loss reserving is foundational to insurer solvency, yet accelerating climate driven catastrophes systematically violate the stability assumptions on which traditional actuarial methods depend. This white paper presents a research program testing whether Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks can detect and adapt to these structural breaks faster and more accurately than Chain Ladder, Bornhuetter Ferguson, and Cape Cod methods. Using 15 plus years of regulatory development triangle data from Florida and Louisiana, enriched with NOAA hurricane intensity indices and sea surface temperatures, we hypothesize a targeted improvement of 15, 20% in reserve accuracy for catastrophe exposed years, a threshold grounded both in the prior neural network reserving literature and in the formal convergence results developed here. Beyond empirical validation, we develop a theoretical framework grounding LSTM structural break detection in probabilistic terms, providing formal performance guarantees that compensate for the limited number of catastrophe events in the test period. We document the research design, methodology, expected contributions, and a candid assessment of limitations.

</details>


### [224] [CRUMB: Efficient Prior Fitted Network Inference via Distributionally Matched Context Batching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11473)
*Jamie Heredge,Mattia J. Villani,Pranav Deshpande,Akshay Seshadri,Niraj Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRUMB is an architecture-agnostic inference wrapper for Prior-Fitted Networks that scales to large datasets by clustering test queries and selecting small, distributionally matched training subsets via greedy MMD minimization, outperforming existing context selection methods on TabArena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PFNs rely on self-attention over the entire training set, which scales quadratically, making inference computationally prohibitive for large datasets. Existing context selection strategies are suboptimal, and no retraining-free solution adequately addresses this scaling bottleneck while preserving predictive performance.

Method: CRUMB operates in three stages: (1) cluster test queries into groups, (2) for each cluster, greedily select a small training subset that minimizes the Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) between the subset and the test cluster distributions, ensuring distributional alignment, and (3) run standard PFN inference on each reduced context batch. No retraining or architecture modification is required.

Result: On the 51-dataset TabArena benchmark across TabPFNv2, TabICLv1, and TabICLv2, CRUMB outperforms comparable state-of-the-art context selection strategies. It also demonstrates resilience to covariate shift due to the MMD-driven distribution matching.

Conclusion: CRUMB effectively addresses the quadratic scaling limitation of PFNs, enabling inference on large tabular datasets without retraining, while maintaining or improving accuracy and robustness to distribution shift.

Abstract: Prior-fitted networks (PFNs) are a promising class of tabular foundation models that perform in-context learning, whereby the entire labelled training set is supplied as context, and predictions for test queries are produced in a single forward pass. However, the quadratically scaling self-attention mechanism in many PFN architectures makes inference prohibitive for very large training datasets. We propose CRUMB (Clustered Retrieval Using Minimised-MMD Batching), a three-stage inference wrapper that (i) clusters the test queries, (ii) selects a small, distributionally matched training subset for each cluster by greedily minimising the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD), and (iii) runs exact PFN inference on each reduced-context batch. CRUMB is architecture-agnostic and requires no retraining. On the 51-dataset TabArena benchmark, evaluated across three PFN architectures (TabPFNv2, TabICLv1, TabICLv2), we show that CRUMB outperforms similar state-of-the-art context selection strategies. We also show that CRUMB is resilient to covariate drift, as the MMD-minimisation step naturally helps align the training context distribution to match the current test batch distributions.

</details>


### [225] [Mahalanobis-Guided Latent OOD Detection for Hybrid ES-DRL Control in Time-Varying Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11474)
*Shaifalee Saxena,Alexander Scheinker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Mahalanobis distance-based OOD detector in a VAE latent space decides when to switch from a learned RL controller to a robust extremum seeking (ES) controller in nonlinear time-varying systems, validated on particle accelerator beam control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL controllers perform well within their training distribution but degrade when time-varying dynamics produce out-of-distribution observations. A combined RL–ES architecture requires a principled, interpretable mechanism to decide when to switch from RL to the model-independent ES controller for safety-critical applications.

Method: A variational autoencoder (VAE) is trained on in-distribution beam-profile observations. At test time, the Mahalanobis distance in the VAE latent space quantifies deviation from the training distribution, and this OOD score drives a binary switch selecting either the RL policy or the bounded ES controller.

Result: Visualization of the VAE latent space confirms that the Mahalanobis-based OOD detection successfully identifies unseen beam profiles caused by spatial magnet motion, providing an interpretable switching signal for the combined controller.

Conclusion: Mahalanobis-guided latent OOD detection offers an effective and interpretable criterion for test-time switching between RL and ES controllers in safety-critical, nonlinear time-varying systems such as particle accelerators.

Abstract: In this paper, we study Mahalanobis-guided latent out-of-distribution (OOD) detection for test-time RL controller switching in nonlinear time-varying systems. RL controllers can quickly control high-dimensional systems within the training distribution, but their performance can degrade when time-varying dynamics produce unseen observations. We consider a combined ES--DRL controller, where RL provides fast in-distribution actions and bounded extremum seeking (ES) provides robust model-independent control under OOD operation. The key challenge is deciding when to switch. We train a variational autoencoder (VAE) on in-distribution beam-profile observations and use Mahalanobis distance in the VAE latent space to detect OOD beam profiles at test time. This OOD decision sets a binary switch that selects either the RL controller or the ES controller. We evaluate the approach in safety-critical particle accelerator control. In this setting, spatial magnet motion creates OOD beam profiles that were not seen during RL training. Visualization of the VAE latent space shows that the proposed method identifies this OOD scenario and provides an interpretable signal for switching between RL and ES in the combined controller.

</details>


### [226] [Accurate and Resource-Efficient Federated Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11480)
*Jebacyril Arockiaraj,Dhruv Parikh,Jayashree Adivarahan,Rajgopal Kannan,Viktor Prasanna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedRAN is a resource-efficient federated continual learning framework that uses compact random feature statistics and truncated-SVD to reduce communication costs from quadratic to linear, while supporting label-scarce settings via pseudo-labeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing federated continual learning methods rely on iterative optimization, replay, and full supervision, which are resource-intensive. Analytic alternatives using random features reduce computation but incur quadratic communication cost in the Gram matrix. There is a need for a method that is simultaneously communication-efficient, computation-efficient, and robust to label scarcity.

Method: FedRAN replaces gradient-based updates with compact random feature statistics. Each client computes a truncated-SVD of its local Gram matrix, transmitting a rank-reduced summary that lowers upload cost from O(M²) to O(M) for fixed rank. The server performs a two-level QR-SVD subspace merge (spatially across clients and temporally across tasks) and solves a closed-form ridge classifier. For label-scarce settings, prototype-based pseudo-labeling is integrated.

Result: On CIFAR-100, ImageNet-R, and VTAB, FedRAN improves average accuracy by up to 4.8 pp over the strongest baseline, reduces per-client communication by 30.6–121.8× compared to optimization-based FCL, and is 190.3× faster on average than gradient-based methods. With only 20% labels, pseudo-labeling boosts accuracy by up to 6.61 pp.

Conclusion: FedRAN demonstrates that accurate, resource-efficient FCL is achievable under communication, computation, and label constraints by combining analytic learning with truncated-SVD compression and pseudo-labeling.

Abstract: Federated continual learning (FCL) must learn from distributed task streams under limited resources, such as communication, computation, memory, and label availability. Existing FCL methods often rely on repeated local optimization, replay, and full supervision. Analytic alternatives avoid iterative training and replay, but using high-dimensional random features to improve accuracy requires a second-order feature statistic, the Gram matrix, which has a quadratic communication cost in the random feature size $M$. We propose FedRAN, a resource-aware analytic FCL framework that replaces gradient-based updates with compact random feature statistics. Each client transmits a truncated-SVD summary of its Gram matrix, reducing the dominant second-order upload from quadratic to linear in $M$ for fixed rank. The server performs a two-level QR-SVD subspace merge, spatially across clients and temporally across tasks, and solves a ridge classifier in closed form. FedRAN further supports label scarcity through prototype-based pseudo-labeling. Across CIFAR-100, ImageNet-R, and VTAB datasets, FedRAN improves average accuracy by up to 4.8 percentage points over the strongest baseline, uses 30.6-121.8$\times$ less per-client communication than optimization-based FCL, and is 190.3$\times$ faster on average than gradient-based baselines; with only 20% labels, pseudo-labeling improves average accuracy by up to 6.61 points. These results show that FedRAN enables accurate and resource-efficient FCL under communication, computation, and label constraints. The source code is available at https://github.com/JebacyrilArockiaraj/Fed-RAN-SSL.

</details>


### [227] [OmniLoc: A Geometry-Aware Foundation Model for Anchor-Free UE Localization Across Diverse Indoor Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11490)
*Lei Chu,Yuning Zhang,Omer Gokalp Serbetci,Anushka Katiyar,Bassel Abou Ali Modad,Andreas F. Molisch*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OmniLoc is a foundation model for anchor-free indoor localization that handles heterogeneous wireless signals across diverse buildings. It uses a unified tokenization module, a geometry-aware Transformer for AP-aware feature extraction, and geometry-conditioned location regression. Evaluated on large-scale and public datasets, it outperforms existing methods and generalizes well across environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor localization from wireless measurements is highly challenging in large-scale deployments due to variations in building geometry, differing sets of detectable APs, and heterogeneous received signals. Existing learning-based methods perform well only in narrow settings and degrade under environmental shifts, making robust, anchor-free localization across diverse indoor environments an unsolved problem.

Method: OmniLoc introduces three core designs: (1) a unified input tokenization module that converts heterogeneous wireless measurements into a common representation; (2) a geometry-aware Transformer that performs AP-aware feature extraction by emphasizing dominant APs while aggregating complementary evidence from supporting APs; and (3) a geometry-aware location estimation module that conditions regression on geometric embeddings to ensure geometrically consistent predictions.

Result: Evaluated on a large-scale in-house dataset and a public benchmark, OmniLoc significantly outperforms existing methods. Its individual design components consistently improve existing backbones when integrated, and the model demonstrates strong generalization in cross-environment evaluations.

Conclusion: OmniLoc is the first foundation-model-based approach built directly on wireless measurements for anchor-free indoor localization. Its unified tokenization, geometry-aware Transformer, and geometry-conditioned regression collectively enable robust, generalizable localization across diverse indoor environments, substantially advancing the state of the art.

Abstract: Indoor localization from wireless measurements remains challenging in large-scale deployments due to substantial variation in building geometry, the set of detectable access points (APs), and the heterogeneity of received signals. Existing learning-based methods often perform well only in limited settings and degrade under environmental shifts, making robust anchor-free localization across diverse indoor environments notoriously difficult. In this paper, we present OmniLoc, an environment-interactive foundation model for anchor-free user equipment localization across diverse indoor environments. To the best of our knowledge, OmniLoc is the first foundation-model-based approach built directly on wireless measurements for this task. OmniLoc is built on three key designs. First, a unified input tokenization module converts heterogeneous wireless measurements into a common representation that is more amenable to learning. Second, a geometry-aware Transformer performs AP-aware feature extraction by emphasizing dominant APs while aggregating complementary evidence from supporting APs. Third, a geometry-aware location estimation module conditions regression on geometric embeddings to produce geometrically consistent location predictions. We evaluate OmniLoc on both a large-scale in-house dataset and a public benchmark dataset. Results show that OmniLoc significantly outperforms existing methods, consistently improves existing backbones when its design components are integrated, and demonstrates strong generalization in cross-environment evaluations.

</details>


### [228] [Probabilistic Contrastive Pretraining for Multi-task ADME Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11508)
*Yifan Xue,Srimukh Prasad Veccham,Saee Paliwal,Tyler Shimko,Micha Livne*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A graph-transformer pretraining framework (Contrastive KERMT) that unifies SMILES reconstruction, contrastive mutual information learning, and chemistry-specific self-supervision into a single probabilistic objective, achieving 7.6–9.9% improvement over KERMT on ADME prediction across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ADME prediction is critical for drug discovery but hindered by noisy, interdependent endpoints and limited data. Existing pretraining methods treat auxiliary chemistry tasks as separately tuned regularizers, failing to integrate them coherently. A unified probabilistic framework is needed to better leverage domain-specific self-supervision and contrastive learning for molecular representation.

Method: The authors propose a molecular graph-transformer that encodes molecular graphs into latent variables and reconstructs SMILES strings from latent codes. The key innovation is formulating reconstruction, contrastive discrimination (cMIM), and chemistry-specific supervision as unit-weighted log-probability factors in a single probabilistic latent-variable objective — eliminating separate loss-weight tuning. For fine-tuning, a multi-task GNN readout with task-specific MLP heads preserves shared representations while mitigating negative transfer across heterogeneous ADME endpoints.

Result: Contrastive KERMT outperforms the KERMT baseline by 7.6% (Biogen), 9.9% (ExpansionRX), and 9.5% (ChEMBL-MT), averaged over significantly-improved endpoints. Adding ADME-adjacent molecules to the pretraining corpus further boosts transfer performance, and the contrastive component yields sharper, chemically meaningful latent neighborhoods.

Conclusion: Unifying contrastive learning with chemistry-specific self-supervision in a single probabilistic objective yields substantial gains for data-limited ADME prediction. The multi-task readout design effectively addresses negative transfer, and contrastive pretraining produces better-structured chemical latent spaces.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) properties is critical to drug discovery, but remains challenging because ADME endpoints are noisy, interdependent, and often data-limited. We propose a molecular graph-transformer pretraining framework that combines chemistry-specific self-supervision with contrastive mutual information machine learning (cMIM). Our method encodes molecular graphs into latent variables, reconstructs SMILES strings from the graph-derived latent codes, and augments the contrastive objective with domain-specific self-supervised chemistry tasks. Rather than treating these tasks as auxiliary regularizers with separately tuned loss weights, we formulate reconstruction, contrastive discrimination, and chemistry-specific supervision as unit-weighted log-probability factors in a single probabilistic latent-variable objective. For fine-tuning, we propose a multi-task GNN readout architecture with task-specific multilayer perceptron heads, preserving shared representation learning while mitigating negative transfer and improving the modeling of heterogeneous, nonlinear task relationships. Across Biogen, ExpansionRX, and ChEMBL-MT, the resulting Contrastive KERMT pretraining improves over the KERMT baseline by 7.6%, 9.9%, and 9.5% respectively (averaged over significantly-improved endpoints). Adding ADME-adjacent molecules to the pretraining corpus further improves transfer, and the contrastive component sharpens chemically meaningful latent neighborhoods.

</details>


### [229] [SirenFNO: Efficient and Full Frequency Learning of Fourier Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11518)
*Pengqing Shi,Jie Yin,Stephen Tierney,Junbin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SirenFNO replaces FNO's frequency truncation with sinusoidal representation networks (SIRENs) for full-grid spectral learning, and further applies functional tensor decomposition, achieving 4–73× parameter reduction while improving PDE solution accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fourier neural operators (FNOs) rely on frequency truncation (keeping only low-frequency modes) for efficiency, which introduces a spectral bias that impairs learning of PDEs with high-frequency oscillatory behavior. The authors aim to eliminate this truncation while maintaining parameter efficiency and discretization invariance.

Method: The authors propose SirenFNO, which uses SIRENs (implicit neural representations with periodic activations) to parameterize kernel weights mode-wise across the full frequency grid, achieving constant parameter count independent of discretization. They further introduce functional tensor decomposition variants (CP, Tucker, tensor-train) applied to the SIREN-parameterized weight tensor to drastically compress the model while preserving expressivity.

Result: SirenFNO consistently outperforms standard FNO across multiple PDE benchmarks with 4–15× fewer parameters. The tensor-decomposed variants achieve up to 73× parameter reduction while further improving performance. Discretization invariance is preserved throughout.

Conclusion: SIREN-based full-spectrum kernel parameterization effectively overcomes the spectral bias limitation of truncated FNOs. Combined with functional tensor decomposition, SirenFNO offers a highly parameter-efficient and accurate operator learning framework suitable for PDEs with high-frequency content.

Abstract: Fourier neural operators (FNOs) are effective and efficient surrogates for approximating solutions of PDEs and generalize across discretizations. However, owing to the reliance on frequency truncation to maintain learning efficiency of FNOs, empirical studies suggest that FNOs exhibit spectral bias toward low-frequency information, which may hinder the learning capability especially for certain PDEs with strong high-frequency oscillations. To address this limitation, we propose SirenFNO, a novel framework that leverages sinusoidal representation networks (SIRENs) to learn implicit neural representations and performs mode-wise kernel parameterization. Our SIREN parameterization learns a full-grid spectrum with a constant and discretization-independent parameter count, thereby eliminating the need for frequency truncation. We further extend SirenFNO with functional tensor decompositions to enhance parameter and learning efficiency. Empirical results show that our SirenFNO consistently outperforms FNO with approximately $4$ to $15$ times parameter reductions with preserved discretization invariance, and our functional decomposition variants obtain performance improvements with a maximum of $73$ times fewer parameters across multiple PDE benchmarks.

</details>


### [230] [Counterexample Guided Learning in the Large using Reasoning Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11521)
*Hongyi Liu,Frederic Sala,Thomas Reps,Adithya Murali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs struggle to effectively use heterogeneous feedback. This paper uses regex induction as a controlled testbed where counterexample-guided feedback is precise. Novel refinement strategies (regularization, symbolic counterexample clusters) and agentic loops substantially boost LLM regex learning, improving success rates dramatically (e.g., 3.2%→38.1%) where standard prompting fails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs and LLM agents should improve from feedback, but determining when and how they can effectively use feedback is difficult because real-world feedback is heterogeneous, domain-specific, and hard to control. A controlled setting with precise feedback mechanisms is needed to systematically study this capability.

Method: The authors frame the problem as regex induction with counterexample-guided learning: an LLM proposes candidate regular expressions from labeled strings, and a verifier returns counterexamples (strings where the candidate and target regex disagree). They introduce novel refinement strategies including regularization techniques and symbolic counterexample clusters, plus agentic strategies like reflection and repair loops. The verifier provides exact, structured feedback rather than treating counterexamples as mere additional data.

Result: Verifier feedback substantially improves sample efficiency on challenging regex tasks, reducing labeled examples needed. On the hardest task groups, success rates improve from 3.2% to 38.1% and from 38.9% to 74.1% across two regex domains. Standard prompting fails on complex targets, while the counterexample-guided framework succeeds.

Conclusion: LLMs can benefit significantly from rich, structured feedback beyond treating it as extra training data. Counterexample-guided refinement is a promising direction for LLM-based program synthesis and formal reasoning, suggesting robust verifier-guided methods are a viable path forward.

Abstract: LLMs and LLM agents should improve when given feedback, but identifying when they are able to do so is difficult: feedback is heterogeneous, domain-specific, and difficult to control. We approach this challenge by asking LLMs to perform regular-expression induction, a classical symbolic learning problem where precise mechanisms for feedback exist in the form of counterexamples. In counterexample-guided learning, a learner (LLM) proposes candidate regular expressions from positive/negative-labeled strings, and the teacher (verifier) returns counterexamples showcasing the difference between the candidate and target languages. We identify novel counterexample-guided refinement strategies that enable effective regex learning, such as regularization and symbolic counterexample clusters. We also explore agentic strategies such as reflection and repair loops. Empirically, we find that verifier feedback substantially improves sample efficiency on challenging regex-induction tasks, reducing the number of labeled examples required and enabling learning of complex target expressions where standard prompting fails. For example, on the hardest task groups, our counterexample-guided framework improves success from 3.2% to 38.1% and from 38.9% to 74.1% on two different regex domains. These results suggest that LLMs can benefit from rich feedback beyond treating it as additional data, opening the door for robust verifier-guided methods for LLM-based program synthesis and formal reasoning.

</details>


### [231] [APEX: A Network-Native Time-Series Foundation Model for Forecasting and Anomaly Detection for Wireless Edge Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11553)
*Swadhin Pradhan,Niloo Bahadori,Peiman Amini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: APEX is a decoder-only transformer pre-trained on large-scale enterprise Wi-Fi telemetry, achieving 18–38% lower MAE than foundation models and SARIMA on DHCP degradation forecasting, with an edge variant enabling privacy-preserving, sub-second inference on AP hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generic time-series foundation models fail on wireless network telemetry due to its distinct characteristics: bursty, zero-inflated signals with cross-layer coupling. There is a need for network-native pre-training tailored to these domain-specific properties to enable proactive wireless operations.

Method: APEX is a decoder-only transformer pre-trained on 10-channel multivariate telemetry from ~4,500 production enterprise wireless networks (~100K AP time series, 34 metrics per AP). It comes in two variants: APEX-Large (269M parameters, cloud) and APEX-Edge (10.5M parameters, edge). Evaluation is conducted on a 192-step (4-day) DHCP degradation forecasting benchmark, also assessing anomaly detection performance.

Result: APEX-Large reduces MAE by 18% over the strongest foundation-model baseline (Toto) and 38% over SARIMA on DHCP degradation forecasting, with anomaly-detection F1 = 0.93. APEX-Edge achieves sub-second, privacy-preserving inference on AP-class edge hardware.

Conclusion: Network-native pre-training is a practical and effective foundation for proactive wireless network operations, outperforming generic time-series models while being deployable at both cloud and edge scales.

Abstract: Generic time-series foundation models transfer poorly to wireless network telemetry whose signals are bursty, zero-inflated, and coupled across protocol layers. We present APEX, a network-native, decoder-only transformer for forecasting enterprise AP telemetry, and evaluate it on DHCP degradation as a representative network task. APEX is pre-trained on 10-channel multivariate telemetry from ~4,500 production wireless networks (~100K AP time series, 34 metrics per AP), and is available as APEX-Large (269M, cloud) and APEX-Edge (10.5M, edge). On a 192-step (4-day) DHCP degradation benchmark, APEX-Large reduces MAE by 18% over the strongest foundation-model baseline (Toto) and 38% over SARIMA, with anomaly-detection F1 = 0.93, while APEX-Edge enables sub-second, privacy-preserving inference on AP-class edge hardware. These results suggest network-native pre-training is a practical foundation for proactive wireless operations.

</details>


### [232] [GraphInfer-Bench: Benchmarking LLM's Inference Capability on Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11562)
*Zhuoyi Peng,Jingzhou Jiang,Hanlin Gu,Lixin Fan,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphInfer-Bench is a new benchmark for evaluating whether LLMs can perform genuine graph inference — producing answers that cannot be retrieved from a single node or along a path. Across 42,000 samples on six real-world graphs spanning five tasks (Description and Comparison), no LLM-based method matches plain GNNs, revealing graph inference as an open capability gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-QA benchmarks (algorithm simulation, node classification, single-node description, KG-QA, GraphRAG) all permit answers retrievable from a single node or path. They fail to test genuine graph inference — synthesizing open-ended answers from a node and its neighborhood that no individual node or retrievable path supports. Real-world applications like laundering ring detection, drug repurposing, and scientific theme inference require this capability.

Method: The authors construct GraphInfer-Bench with five tasks organized into two families: Description (what a region is — relational description, thematic summary, masked-node prediction) and Comparison (how regions differ — community partition, outlier detection). Ground-truth answers are designed so no single node contains them. The benchmark contains 42,000 samples across six real-world graphs, generated automatically and filtered through a four-layer quality-control protocol. Four method families are evaluated: graph-token alignment models, zero-shot frontier closed-source LLMs, Graph2Text supervised fine-tuning, and plain GNNs as a structural baseline.

Result: No method family closes the gap to GNNs. Graph-token alignment models handle some description tasks (relational, theme) but collapse on comparison. Frontier LLMs lead among LLM-based methods on outlier detection and community partition, but lag on masked-node prediction. Graph2Text SFT is strongest on description yet falls behind frontier LLMs on comparison. Plain GNNs match or beat all LLM-based methods across every task, with the largest margin on community detection.

Conclusion: Graph inference — synthesizing answers from neighborhood context that no single node contains — remains an open capability gap rather than a property solvable by any one architecture. GraphInfer-Bench provides a rigorous platform for measuring progress on this frontier.

Abstract: Graph analysis underlies many applications whose answers cannot be looked up in a single record or retrieved along a path: laundering rings, drug repurposing, user preference, and scientific theme are all inferred from a node together with its neighbourhood. We introduce GraphInfer-Bench, a benchmark for whether LLMs can perform this graph inference: producing an open-ended answer that no single node supports and no path retrieves. Existing graph-QA protocols cannot test this capability: algorithm simulation, node classification, single-node description, KG-QA, and GraphRAG all admit answers retrievable from one node or along a path. GraphInfer-Bench defines five tasks along Description (what a region is) and Comparison (how regions differ), each constructed so the ground truth lives in no single node. The release contains 42,000 samples across six real-world graphs, produced automatically and screened by a four-layer quality-control protocol. We evaluate four method families against the same tasks: graph-token alignment models, zero-shot frontier closed-source LLMs, Graph2Text supervised fine-tuning, and plain GNNs as a structural reference. No method family closes the gap. Graph-token alignment partially handles description tasks (relational, theme) but collapses on comparison tasks. Frontier LLMs lead on outlier detection and community partition among LLM-based methods but lag on masked-node prediction. Graph2Text SFT is the strongest LLM-based method on the description side yet falls behind frontier LLMs on comparison. Across every task, plain GNNs match or beat the strongest LLM-based row, with the largest margin on community detection. GraphInfer-Bench surfaces graph inference as an open capability gap rather than a property of any one architecture.

</details>


### [233] [Online Shift Detection and Conformal Adaptation for Deployed Safety Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11949)
*Jun Wen Leong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An online monitoring system uses sequential analysis to detect distributional shift in safety classifiers and then applies conformal abstention to maintain target error rates. Evaluated across 800 experimental conditions, detection is 86.6% valid. Weighted conformal prediction corrects coverage for DeBERTa but collapses for other classifiers due to extreme importance weights; PCA dimensionality reduction rescues performance. Variance decomposition shows classifier, shift type, and their interaction all significantly affect detection latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deployed safety classifiers experience distributional shift (e.g., jailbreaks, adversarial attacks) that degrades their reliability. Static thresholds become miscalibrated under shift, necessitating an automated system that can both detect when a classifier goes out-of-distribution and adapt decision thresholds to maintain a target error rate without manual retraining.

Method: The system has two stages: (1) Calibrated sequential statistics monitor for distributional shift online. (2) Upon detection, a conformal abstention layer using weighted conformal prediction adapts decision thresholds to recover a target error rate (epsilon=0.1). The factorial experiment crosses 4 classifiers, 5 shift conditions, 20 seeds, and 2 window sizes (800 cells). Three shift regimes are tested: synthetic onset, real temporal jailbreaks, and GCG adversarial attacks. PCA to 32 dimensions is explored as a remedy when importance weight collapse occurs. Variance decomposition (eta-squared) quantifies contributions of classifier, shift type, and their interaction to detection latency.

Result: Detection is valid in 86.6% of cells (693/800, 95% CI [84.1%, 88.8%]) with mean latency of 39.5 steps. By regime: synthetic onset 86.6%, real temporal jailbreaks 85% (17/20), GCG attacks also detected. Weighted conformal prediction recovers up to 39 percentage points of lost coverage for DeBERTa (ESS=46/300) but collapses for all other classifiers (ESS≈300) due to logistic density ratio estimation producing extreme importance weights. PCA to 32 dimensions breaks the collapse, recovering 33 pp for Llama Guard and 21 pp for ShieldGemma. Variance decomposition: classifier (η²=0.243), shift type (η²=0.237), and interaction (η²=0.185) all significantly contribute to latency variance (p<0.001).

Conclusion: Online distributional shift monitoring with conformal abstention is viable for maintaining safety classifier reliability, but its effectiveness is highly contingent on classifier architecture and shift type. The collapse of importance weighting in high-dimensional embedding spaces is a critical failure mode, mitigable via PCA. Per-classifier monitoring profiles are necessary, as classifier identity and classifier×shift interactions explain substantial detection latency variance.

Abstract: We present an online monitoring system for distributional shift in deployed safety classifiers, using calibrated sequential statistics to detect when a classifier has moved out of distribution. Upon detection, a conformal abstention layer adapts decision thresholds to recover a target error rate epsilon=0.1. In a pre-registered factorial evaluation (4 classifiers x 5 shift conditions x 20 seeds x 2 window sizes, 800 cells), the system achieves 86.6% valid detection (693/800, 95% CI [84.1%, 88.8%]) with mean latency of 39.5 steps. Detection holds across three ground-truth regimes: synthetic onset (86.6%), real temporal jailbreaks (85%, 17/20), and GCG adversarial attacks. Weighted conformal prediction recovers up to 39 pp of lost coverage for DeBERTa (ESS=46/300) but collapses for all other classifiers (ESS~300): logistic density ratio estimation achieves perfect source/target separability in high-dimensional embedding spaces, clipping all importance weights to the floor. DeBERTa shows a gradient from effective correction (paraphrase, ESS=46) to near-total collapse (adversarial suffix, ESS=206). PCA to 32 dimensions breaks the collapse, recovering 33 pp for Llama Guard and 21 pp for ShieldGemma. Variance decomposition reveals classifier (eta^2=0.243), shift type (eta^2=0.237), and their interaction (eta^2=0.185) all contribute substantially to detection latency variance (all p<0.001), indicating per-classifier monitoring profiles are necessary.

</details>


### [234] [Range-Aware Bayesian Optimization for Discovering Diverse Designs within Target Property Windows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11574)
*Shengli Jiang,Jason Wu,Charles M. Schroeder,Michael A. Webb*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A range-aware Bayesian optimization framework is proposed where the acquisition function scores the posterior probability of satisfying a target range, enabling recovery of multiple diverse valid designs. It outperforms standard BO and goal-seeking methods in benchmarks and two case studies (polymer synthesis and oligomer discovery).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many materials/product design problems require candidates meeting an acceptable range (not a single optimum), and recovering multiple distinct valid solutions is practically valuable for flexibility in cost, processability, or robustness. Existing BO methods are poorly suited for specification-driven design with range constraints and solution diversity.

Method: A range-aware Bayesian optimization framework where the acquisition function directly computes the posterior probability that a candidate satisfies a target specification range, naturally supporting parallel pursuit of multiple distinct specifications over a shared candidate space.

Result: The range-aware acquisition consistently recovers larger and more diverse sets of valid designs than standard BO baselines and recent goal-seeking methods across benchmarks. Demonstrated utility in two case studies: optimizing reaction conditions for polymer synthesis and discovering sequence-defined oligomers with prescribed optical absorption bands (supported by quantum chemical calculations).

Conclusion: Range-aware BO provides a practical, sample-efficient foundation for specification-driven design, especially when design flexibility and solution diversity are important.

Abstract: In many materials and product design problems, desirable candidates exhibit properties that fall within an acceptable range rather than achieve a single optimum. Recovering multiple, distinct solutions that satisfy such specifications is also practically valuable, as some candidates may be preferred for reasons of cost, processability, or robustness that are difficult to encode directly in an objective function. Here, we develop a range-aware Bayesian optimization (BO) framework in which the acquisition function directly scores the posterior probability that a candidate satisfies a target range. The framework naturally extends to parallel pursuit of multiple distinct specifications over a shared candidate space. Across benchmark tasks, range-aware acquisition consistently recovers larger and more diverse sets of valid designs than standard BO baselines and recent goal-seeking methods. Its utility is further demonstrated in two practically motivated design case studies involving optimizing reaction conditions for polymer synthesis and sequence-defined oligomer discovery for prescribed optical absorption bands, supported by quantum chemical calculations. These results suggest that range-aware BO can provide a practical and sample-efficient foundation for specification-driven design, particularly when design flexibility and solution diversity are important considerations.

</details>


### [235] [Beyond the Golden Teacher: Enhancing Graph Learning through LLM-GNN Co-teaching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11583)
*Zhuoyi Peng,Hanlin Gu,Lixin Fan,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes LLM-GNN Co-Teaching, a bidirectional framework where GNN and LLM exchange confident pseudo-labels without designating either as the golden teacher. A novel Round-based Pseudo-Label Preference Optimization (RPL-PO) mines supervision from cross-model contradiction-to-agreement transitions for DPO training, achieving substantial few-shot gains on TAG benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Few-shot graph learning on text-attributed graphs is challenging because GNNs fail on cold nodes (sparse topology) and LLMs fail on text-ambiguous nodes. Existing methods assume one model (GNN or LLM) is the golden teacher, but under sparse supervision neither is reliable, causing each model's blind spots to propagate to the other. The authors aim to eliminate the golden-teacher assumption entirely.

Method: LLM-GNN Co-Teaching: A bidirectional framework where GNN and LLM exchange only their most confident pseudo-labels via an architecture-specific small-loss criterion, with both updating every round. Supervision is mined from the training trajectory: when a node transitions from cross-model contradiction at round t to agreement at round t+1, the LLM's old (contradicting) and new (peer-endorsed) answers form a preference pair for DPO training, termed Round-based Pseudo-Label Preference Optimization (RPL-PO).

Result: On six benchmarks, LLM-GNN Co-Teaching consistently outperforms GNN-as-Judge and all prior methods, with 3-shot absolute gains of 7.86% on Cora and 7.73% on ogbn-arxiv. Gains persist in 5-shot settings and zero-shot cross-dataset transfer. Error-structure analysis confirms that removing the golden-teacher assumption significantly improves LLM graph learning on challenging samples.

Conclusion: Abandoning the golden-teacher assumption via bidirectional co-teaching with RPL-PO enables effective few-shot graph learning on TAGs, with both GNN and LLM improving each other without either being treated as infallible. The trajectory-based preference mining strategy provides a principled way to extract supervision from the co-teaching dynamics.

Abstract: Text-attributed graphs (TAGs) underlie real-world applications such as citation networks, social media, and e-commerce. Few-shot graph learning on TAGs is hard: with only a handful of labels per class and the rest of the graph unannotated, neither GNNs nor LLMs can learn well on their own. GNNs read topology and fail on cold nodes; LLMs read text and fail on text-ambiguous nodes. Existing LLM-GNN methods all follow the same recipe: designate one model as the golden teacher and use its outputs (e.g., features or pseudo-labels) to supervise the other. We argue this golden-teacher assumption breaks under sparse supervision: neither model is golden, and treating either as such transfers its blind spots into the student. We therefore ask: can we avoid designating either model as the golden teacher, and still perform effective graph learning? We answer with LLM-GNN Co-Teaching, a bidirectional co-teaching framework in which neither model is fixed as teacher. The GNN and LLM exchange their most confident pseudo-labels under an architecture-specific small-loss criterion, and both update every round. Supervision is then mined from the trajectory: whenever a node moves from cross-model contradiction at round t to cross-model agreement at round t+1, the LLM's two answers on the same input form a preference pair (old contradicting self < new peer-endorsed self) for DPO training. We call this Round-based Pseudo-Label Preference Optimization (RPL-PO). On six benchmarks, LLM-GNN Co-Teaching consistently outperforms GNN-as-Judge and all prior methods, with absolute 3-shot gains of 7.86% on Cora and 7.73% on ogbn-arxiv; improvements carry over to 5-shot and to zero-shot cross-dataset transfer. Error-structure analysis further shows that abandoning the golden-teacher assumption substantially improves the LLM's graph learning capability on challenging samples.

</details>


### [236] [Kuramoto Attention: Synchronizing Self-Attention on the Torus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11585)
*Joshua Nunley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Kuramoto attention, a self-attention layer where hidden states are angles on a torus, with updates governed by the Kuramoto synchronization dynamics. The attention matrix acts as a content-dependent coupling kernel. The approach bridges self-attention and phase synchronization, achieving competitive language modeling performance against RoPE+SwiGLU transformers on enwiki8.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a principled connection between self-attention mechanisms and phase synchronization dynamics by reformulating attention entirely on a compact manifold (the torus), showing that geometrically constrained representations can remain viable for language modeling while offering a new interpretability lens through synchronization.

Method: Kuramoto attention represents each token's hidden coordinate as an angle. Scores are computed via gated cosine similarity (a learned metric on the torus). The update rule is exactly the Kuramoto coupling term: attention-weighted sum of sin(θ_u - θ_t), i.e., the tangent component of the circular mean. Softmax weights solve an entropy-regularized phase-retrieval problem. Rotary position embeddings enter as position-dependent phase drifts. The framework generalizes to any compact group; the torus is the abelian case with closed-form solutions.

Result: On enwiki8 character-level language modeling at 1M parameters: 1.637±0.010 BPC vs. 1.616±0.004 for RoPE+SwiGLU transformer (within 0.02 BPC). At 5M parameters, median BPC is level (1.448 vs 1.452 over five seeds), with the transformer slightly ahead on mean (1.456 vs 1.468). Ablations identify critical components.

Conclusion: The constrained geometric structure is a viable language model at tested scales. The primary contribution is the structure itself and its synchronization interpretation, providing a compact bridge between self-attention and phase synchronization with potential for further theoretical and practical development.

Abstract: We introduce Kuramoto attention, a self-attention layer in which each hidden coordinate is an angle. The layer scores tokens by gated cosine similarity, attends over previous phase states, and updates each token by the tangent component of the attention-weighted circular mean. Because the values are the raw phase states, this update is exactly the Kuramoto coupling term $\sum_u A_{t,u}\sin(θ_u-θ_t)$, with the attention matrix acting as an adaptive, content-dependent coupling kernel. Equivalently, the gated score is a learned metric on the torus that selects which tokens couple, and the update pulls each token toward the circular mean of the tokens it selects, tightening their phase agreement. The same two ingredients, an invariant similarity score and an on-manifold mean, define such a layer on any compact group; the torus is the abelian case, where both are closed-form. The softmax weights solve an entropy-regularized phase-retrieval problem, and rotary position enters as a position-dependent phase drift in the score. On enwiki8 character-level language modeling, the layer trains as a functional language model whose bits-per-character stays close to a strong matched RoPE+SwiGLU transformer: within $0.02$ BPC at one million parameters ($1.637\pm0.010$ versus $1.616\pm0.004$) and level on the median at five million ($1.448$ versus $1.452$ over five seeds) with the transformer ahead on the mean ($1.468$ versus $1.456$). These experiments establish that the constrained geometric structure is a viable language model at this scale; the structure itself, and its synchronization reading, is the contribution. Ablations isolate the load-bearing components, and the result gives a compact bridge between self-attention and phase synchronization.

</details>


### [237] [Reinforcement Learning Disrupts Gradient-Based Adversarial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12251)
*Xinhai Zou,Chang Zhao,Alireza Aghabagherloo,Dave Singelée,Robin Degraeve,Bart Preneel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL-trained image classifiers exhibit inherent robustness against gradient-based adversarial attacks because RL training acts as an implicit regularizer that degrades gradient structure — producing unstable gradient directions and smaller magnitudes that cause PGD attacks to fail. Combining RL with adversarial training (RL-adv) yields a dual-layer defense that outperforms standard adversarially trained models across gradient-based, transfer-based, and query-based attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gradient-based adversarial attacks exploit gradient information to efficiently craft perturbations, posing a dominant threat to DNNs. The authors investigate whether RL-based training can fundamentally disrupt this gradient structure, providing a new angle on adversarial robustness beyond standard adversarial training.

Method: Image classifiers are trained using policy-gradient objectives with epsilon-greedy exploration. Robustness is evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-100 across multiple architectures. Mechanism analysis employs loss landscape visualization, static and dynamic gradient indicators, and predictive entropy. A hybrid approach (RL-adv) combining RL training with adversarial training is also evaluated against PGD, AutoAttack, transfer-based, and query-based attacks.

Result: RL-trained classifiers significantly disrupt gradient-based adversarial optimization. Mechanistic analysis reveals RL produces models with highly unstable gradient directions and reduced gradient magnitudes, making each PGD step unreliable. RL-adv achieves the highest robustness across all major attack types, outperforming standard supervised adversarial training (SL-adv) by a significant margin.

Conclusion: RL-induced gradient disruption is a complementary robustness mechanism. The dual-layer defense of RL-adv — gradient-level defense from RL plus boundary-level defense from adversarial training — motivates future work on hybrid SL-RL training schedules that combine SL's efficiency with RL's gradient-regularization properties.

Abstract: Gradient-based adversarial attacks remain a dominant threat to deep neural networks (DNNs), as they exploit gradient information to efficiently optimize adversarial perturbations. To address this, we investigate whether reinforcement learning (RL) training can disrupt the gradient structure used by attackers by training image classifiers with policy-gradient objectives and epsilon-greedy exploration. Through systematic experiments across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-100 with multiple architectures, we find that RL-trained classifiers significantly disrupt gradient-based adversarial optimization. To explain this, we conduct a comprehensive mechanism analysis using loss landscape visualization, static and dynamic gradient indicators, and predictive entropy. Our analysis reveals that RL acts as an implicit regularizer, producing models with highly unstable gradient directions and smaller gradient magnitudes. This combination makes each PGD step both unreliable in direction and limited in magnitude, causing gradient-based attacks to fail within practical iteration budgets. We further show that combining RL with adversarial training (RL-adv) provides a dual-layer defense operating at two complementary levels: RL degrades gradient information available to attackers (gradient-level defense), while adversarial training strengthens decision boundaries (boundary-level defense). RL-adv achieves the highest robustness across all major attack types evaluated, including gradient-based (PGD, AutoAttack), transfer-based, and query-based attacks, outperforming SL-adv by a significant margin. These findings identify RL-induced gradient disruption as a complementary robustness mechanism and motivate future research on hybrid SL-RL training schedules that combine SL's efficiency with RL's gradient-regularization properties.

</details>


### [238] [Physics-Distilled Neural Network enabled by Large Language Models for Manufacturing Process-Property Predictive Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11605)
*Ge Song,Kiarash Naghavi Khanghah,Anandkumar Patel,Rajiv Malhotra,Hongyi Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A knowledge distillation framework that injects LLM-extracted physics priors into a teacher model with Graph-Masked Attention, then distills to a lightweight student predictor (>6000 Hz inference), achieving robust high-accuracy predictions across five manufacturing processes even with scarce data and suboptimal priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manufacturing process-property prediction faces two key bottlenecks: (1) high experimental costs limit dataset sizes, and (2) complex black-box models lack interpretability. There is a need for a framework that achieves high predictive accuracy in data-scarce regimes while bridging theoretical physics knowledge with practical, real-time industrial deployment.

Method: The framework operates in three stages: (1) Analytical physics priors are extracted from scientific literature using Large Language Models. (2) These priors are integrated into a privileged teacher model employing a Graph-Masked Attention layer to capture complex physical dependencies among input variables (handling setpoint variables and mixed static/high-frequency temporal signatures). (3) The teacher's knowledge is distilled into a lightweight student predictor for efficient inference. Validation uses repeated K-fold cross-validation across five diverse manufacturing processes to ensure statistical reliability given small datasets.

Result: The framework consistently achieves high predictive accuracy across all five manufacturing domains. It demonstrates significant fault tolerance, maintaining robust performance even when LLM-derived analytical priors are suboptimal or incomplete. The distilled student predictor achieves inference frequencies exceeding 6000 Hz, enabling real-time edge deployment on standard industrial hardware.

Conclusion: The proposed knowledge distillation framework offers a scalable and effective solution for combining theoretical physics with real-time industrial monitoring in data-limited environments, successfully bridging the gap between scientific knowledge and practical deployment.

Abstract: Predicting process-property relationships in manufacturing is often challenged by high experimental costs and the limited interpretability of complex 'black-box' models. This paper proposes a novel knowledge distillation framework designed to achieve high-accuracy predictions in data-scarce scenarios. The framework integrates analytical physics priors, which are systematically extracted from scientific literature via Large Language Models, into a privileged teacher model. We employ a Graph-Masked Attention layer to capture the complex physical dependencies among input variables showing strict setpoints or a combination of static and high-frequency temporal signatures. This privileged knowledge is distilled into a lightweight student predictor for inference. The feasibility and robustness of the framework are evaluated through a comprehensive experiment across five diverse manufacturing processes. To ensure statistical reliability, given the small dataset sizes, a repeated K-fold cross-validation technique is employed to quantify model stability and generalization. Results indicate that the proposed framework consistently achieves high predictive accuracy across all evaluated domains. Most importantly, the architecture demonstrates significant fault tolerance by maintaining robust predictive performance even in scenarios where LLM-derived analytical priors are suboptimal or incomplete. Furthermore, the student predictor achieves an inference frequency exceeding 6000 Hz, which facilitates real-time edge deployment on standard industrial hardware. This work provides a scalable solution for bridging the gap between theoretical physics and real-time industrial monitoring in data-limited environments.

</details>


### [239] [Information-Theoretic Decomposition for Multimodal Interaction Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11614)
*Zequn Yang,Yake Wei,Haotian Ni,Zhihao Xu,Di Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies that multimodal interactions (redundant, unique, synergistic information) vary dynamically per sample, critiques existing paradigms' inability to capture all interaction types, and proposes DMIL — a variational decomposition framework that explicitly models sample-specific interactions, achieving superior performance across diverse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal learning paradigms fail to capture all three types of multimodal interactions (redundant, unique, synergistic) simultaneously, and critically neglect that these interactions are dynamic and sample-specific rather than static across a dataset. Modality ensemble methods miss synergy, while joint learning under-uses redundancy. No prior work systematically addresses learning sample-specific multimodal interactions in a principled, information-theoretic manner.

Method: The authors propose DMIL (Decomposition-based Multimodal Interaction Learning) with two key components: (1) a variational decomposition architecture that explicitly isolates redundant, unique, and synergistic interaction components from multimodal data using information-theoretic principles; and (2) a fine-tuning learning strategy that leverages these explicit interaction representations to achieve comprehensive, sample-adaptive interaction learning. The framework is model-agnostic and can be applied to various architectures.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse tasks and architectures show that DMIL consistently achieves superior performance compared to existing paradigms. The method demonstrates broad applicability and flexibility as a general interaction-centric multimodal learning framework.

Conclusion: The paper establishes that explicitly modeling sample-specific multimodal interactions is both critical and achievable. DMIL provides a flexible, broadly applicable interaction-centric paradigm that advances multimodal learning by adaptively capturing the full spectrum of redundant, unique, and synergistic information on a per-sample basis.

Abstract: Multimodal learning hinges on capturing redundant, unique, and synergistic information across modalities, which collectively constitute multimodal interactions. A critical yet underexplored challenge is that these implicit interactions vary dynamically across samples. In this work, we present the first systematic, information-theoretic analysis highlighting why learning these dynamic, sample-specific interactions is critical for effective multimodal learning. Our analysis further reveals deficits in conventional paradigms at learning these distinct interaction types: modality ensemble approaches struggle to capture synergy, while joint learning paradigms often under-utilize redundant information. This highlights the need for an approach that can adaptively learn from different interaction types on a per-sample basis. To this end, we propose Decomposition-based Multimodal Interaction Learning (DMIL), a novel paradigm that explicitly models and learns from sample-specific interactions. First, we design a variational decomposition architecture to isolate the constituent interaction components. Second, we employ a new learning strategy that leverages these explicit interaction components in a fine-tuning process to achieve comprehensive interaction learning. Extensive experiments across diverse tasks and architectures demonstrate that DMIL consistently achieves superior performance by adapting to holistic sample-specific interactions. Our framework is flexible and broadly applicable, establishing an interaction-centric paradigm for multimodal learning. The code is available at https://github.com/GeWu-Lab/DMIL.

</details>


### [240] [TimeRouter: Efficient and Adaptive Routing of Time-Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11625)
*Kanghui Ning,Yushan Jiang,Kashif Rasul,Anderson Schneider,Yuriy Nevmyvaka,Dongjin Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeRouter is a lightweight routing framework that dynamically selects the best pretrained time-series foundation model (TSFM) for a given forecasting task, using a learned routing head, selective gating, and ensemble fallback — achieving SOTA on GIFT-EVAL without costly LLM-based controllers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TSFMs have diverse inductive biases and no single model excels across all forecasting regimes. Existing agentic systems rely on LLM-based controllers for expert model selection, which incurs high inference overhead. This motivates an efficient, non-LLM routing mechanism that leverages empirical complementarity among TSFMs.

Method: TimeRouter integrates three components: (1) a learned discriminative routing head that predicts which TSFM expert to use based on input features; (2) a selective gating mechanism that abstains from routing when confidence is low; and (3) an ensemble fallback that combines multiple expert predictions when no single model is clearly preferred, all without requiring an LLM at inference time.

Result: TimeRouter achieves a state-of-the-art LB MASE of 0.6765 on the GIFT-EVAL leaderboard. Ablation studies demonstrate the importance of pool composition and selective gating in routing design.

Conclusion: TimeRouter provides a modular, lightweight routing layer that enables efficient expert selection in agentic time-series systems. Its strong empirical performance and design insights position it as a practical foundation for future systems built on TSFM pools.

Abstract: Time-series foundation models (TSFMs) are increasingly explored as predictive experts within emerging agentic time-series systems. However, TSFMs exhibit heterogeneous inductive biases, and no single model consistently dominates across forecasting regimes, making expert selection a critical challenge. Existing systems often delegate this decision to LLM-based controllers, incurring substantial inference overhead. We present TimeRouter, an efficient routing framework that leverages empirical complementarity across a pool of pretrained TSFMs through lightweight discriminative routing, selective gating, and ensemble fallback. Concretely, TimeRouter combines a learned routing head, a selective gate, and an ensemble fallback, enabling adaptive expert selection without invoking an LLM at inference time. TimeRouter achieves state-of-the-art performance on the GIFT-EVAL leaderboard, with an LB MASE of 0.6765. Beyond benchmark performance, our ablation studies provide empirical insights into TSFM routing design, highlighting the importance of pool composition and selective gating. Taken together, these results position TimeRouter as a modular and lightweight routing layer for future agentic time-series systems built upon foundation-model pools. Our code is available at https://github.com/UConn-DSIS/TimeRouter.

</details>


### [241] [When Context Returns: Toward Robust Internalization in On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11627)
*Xun Wang,Ruishuo Chen,Zhuoran Li,Yu Chen,Longbo Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: On-policy distillation can internalize privileged context into a student model, but reintroducing that context often harms performance — a phenomenon termed "context-induced degradation." The authors propose a consistency regularizer using stop-gradient and forward KL divergence to enforce "context removability," successfully mitigating degradation and improving no-context performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors identify a previously unstudied phenomenon: after on-policy distillation internalizes privileged context (e.g., system prompts) into a student model, reintroducing that same context at inference time degrades performance, even on correctly solved instances. This suggests current distillation methods fail to achieve true robustness — the model should remain stable whether or not the context is present, a property they call "context removability."

Method: The proposed method adds a lightweight consistency regularizer to the standard on-policy distillation objective. Specifically: (1) the student's no-context output is anchored via stop-gradient, and (2) a forward KL divergence penalty is applied to the context-conditioned output when it deviates from this anchor. This requires only one extra forward pass per training step. The approach is evaluated across 12 diverse configurations spanning multiple domains and model families.

Result: The consistency regularizer improves context-conditioned accuracy in the majority of settings, reduces context-induced harm in 11 out of 12 settings, and effectively eliminates response-length inflation. A mechanistic case study confirms that context removability operates at the representation level: hidden states remain nearly identical regardless of context presence.

Conclusion: Robust internalization of privileged context requires not only matching teacher behavior but also ensuring context removability — stable performance when context is reintroduced. The proposed stop-gradient + forward KL regularizer is a simple, effective solution that mitigates context-induced degradation and can even boost no-context performance, with representation-level evidence supporting its mechanism.

Abstract: Recent work has shown that on-policy distillation can internalize privileged context, such as system prompts or task hints, into a student model so that the context is no longer needed at inference time. Although this approach successfully improves the student's no-context performance, we identify an interesting and previously unstudied phenomenon: in many settings, reintroducing the original privileged context to the distilled student actually degrades its performance, even on instances it already solves correctly without context. We term this context-induced degradation and argue that robust internalization demands not only matching the teacher's context-conditioned behavior, but also remaining stable when the context is reintroduced, a property we call context removability. Motivated by this observation, we propose a lightweight consistency regularizer that first anchors the student's no-context output via stop-gradient, then penalizes the context-conditioned output for deviating from it via forward KL divergence. This simple addition requires only one extra forward pass per training step, yet it effectively mitigates context-induced degradation and, in many cases, even improves no-context performance. Across 12 configurations spanning diverse domains and model families, our method improves context-conditioned accuracy in the majority of settings, reduces context-induced harm in 11 out of 12 settings, and effectively eliminates response-length inflation. A mechanistic case study further confirms that context removability is achieved at the representation level, with hidden states remaining nearly identical regardless of whether the context is present.

</details>


### [242] [TAROT: Task-Adaptive Refinement of LLM-prior Graphs for Few-shot Tabular Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11640)
*Ruxue Shi,Yili Wang,Mengnan Du,Hangting Ye,Yi Chang,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TAROT is a GNN-based few-shot tabular learning framework that constructs a task-adaptive semantic graph by prompting LLMs to infer feature relationships, then refines the graph to prune LLM hallucinations and align with the downstream task, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing few-shot tabular learning methods have key limitations: traditional methods require expensive additional training on unlabeled/generated data; LLM-based methods raise privacy concerns when feeding raw tabular data into LLMs. Most importantly, both paradigms overlook semantic relationships between features, which provide crucial structural priors for modeling meaningful feature interactions in few-shot settings.

Method: TAROT consists of three components: (1) A Unified Semantic Tabular Node Encoder (USTNE) that encodes heterogeneous tabular data into unified node semantic representations; (2) LLM-prompted semantic graph construction using task descriptions and feature names to infer feature relationships; (3) Task-adaptive Semantic Graph Refinement that prunes hallucinated/spurious edges and adds missing task-related edges to align graph structure with the downstream objective. A GNN then performs message passing over the refined graph for prediction.

Result: Extensive experiments on various few-shot tabular learning benchmarks demonstrate that TAROT achieves superior performance, establishing it as a state-of-the-art approach in this domain.

Conclusion: Leveraging LLMs for semantic graph construction combined with task-adaptive graph refinement effectively addresses the limitations of prior few-shot tabular learning methods, with the refined semantic graph enabling GNNs to capture meaningful task-related feature dependencies for improved prediction.

Abstract: Few-shot tabular learning provides a cost-effective approach for real-world applications where annotation is costly and collecting sufficient samples for new tasks is difficult. Existing Traditional and LLM-based methods have demonstrated effectiveness in few-shot scenarios. However, traditional methods need additional training on unlabeled or generated data, which incur significant computational overhead. In addition, LLM-based methods that directly feed raw tabular data into LLMs raise privacy and compliance concerns. More importantly, both paradigms largely overlook the semantic relationships between features, which provide structural and semantic prior for constructing a semantic graph. Semantic graph is essential for modeling meaningful feature interactions in few-shot scenarios. In this paper, we propose TAROT, a GNN-based framework that encodes the structural and semantic prior by constructing and refining a task-adaptive semantic graph from this prior, thereby improving predictive performance in few-shot tabular learning. TAROT first encodes heterogeneous tabular data into unified node semantic representations via a Unified Semantic Tabular Node Encoder (USTNE). Then, it prompts LLMs to infer the semantic relationship between features based on the task description and feature names to construct a semantic graph. To mitigate structural noise introduced by the hallucination of LLMs, TAROT introduces Task-adaptive Semantic Graph Refinement that prunes spurious or task-unrelated edges and adds missing task-related ones, aligning the graph structure with the downstream objective. Finally, a GNN performs message passing over the refined graph to capture task-related semantic dependencies for prediction. Extensive experiments on various few-shot tabular learning benchmarks demonstrate the superior performance of TAROT, establishing it as a state-of-the-art approach in this domain.

</details>


### [243] [Tree-Structured Orthonormal Decomposition of the Aitchison Simplex](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11646)
*Daisuke Yamada,Qijun Zhang,Travis Pence,Barbara B. Bendlin,Federico Rey,Vikas Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolyILR introduces a canonical orthonormal decomposition of the Aitchison tangent space for compositional data that aligns with any tree topology, enabling multiscale, interpretable inference while preserving the intrinsic geometry of compositions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for compositional data with hierarchical structure either ignore the hierarchy, violate Aitchison geometry, are restricted to binary trees, or produce incomplete coordinate systems. There is a need for a general, geometry-preserving decomposition that exploits full branching structure.

Method: PolyILR defines a weighted local geometry at each internal node of an arbitrary tree, capturing full branching structure, then lifts these local geometries to a global orthonormal basis in the Aitchison tangent space, assigning each coordinate to a specific tree location.

Result: On microbiome and single-cell benchmarks, PolyILR yields stable, interpretable features and supports inference at multiscale tree resolution. A theoretical connection to softmax classifiers is also established.

Conclusion: PolyILR provides a principled, geometry-aware framework for compositional data analysis with hierarchical structure, offering both practical utility in biological benchmarks and theoretical links to probabilistic modeling.

Abstract: Compositional data -- vectors encoding relative proportions -- arise across scientific domains, including ecology, geochemistry, and genomics. The features in these data often come with known hierarchical structure (e.g., taxonomies, phylogenies, ontologies), yet existing methods either ignore this structure, discard the intrinsic Aitchison geometry, are designed for binary trees, or yield incomplete coordinate systems. We describe PolyILR, a canonical orthonormal decomposition of the Aitchison tangent space aligned with any tree topology. Our construction defines a weighted local geometry at each internal node capturing full branching structure, then lifts these to a global orthonormal basis where every coordinate corresponds to a specific tree location. On microbiome and single-cell benchmarks, PolyILR yields stable, interpretable features and enables inference at multiscale tree resolution. We also establish a novel theoretical connection to softmax classifiers, suggesting possible applications to probabilistic modeling.

</details>


### [244] [Structure-Preserving Neural Surrogates with Tractable Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11650)
*Handi Zhang,Adrienne M. Propp,Brooks Kinch,Houman Owhadi,Nathaniel Trask*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper constructs data-driven reduced-order models that serve as structure-preserving, real-time PDE surrogates by combining mixed finite element spaces (Raviart–Thomas/$dgP_0$) with Gaussian process regression. A transformer maps to structure-preserving $H(\mathrm{div})$–$L^2$ subspaces, and GP-based flux modeling yields a Dirichlet-to-Neumann map with closed-form posterior uncertainty. Training is posed as an optimal recovery problem with conservation-enforcing equality constraints, solved via a Schur-complement strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific ML enables fast PDE solutions but lacks the theoretical rigor of conventional simulators for verification and validation. The authors seek to bridge this gap by creating surrogates that are both real-time and structure-preserving, with rigorous uncertainty quantification grounded in conservation laws and exterior calculus.

Method: The method constructs structure-preserving $H(\mathrm{div})$–$L^2$ subspaces using Raviart–Thomas and $dgP_0$ elements, selected by a lightweight transformer.
