Reduced-order dynamics are learned by formulating a conservation law where a Gaussian process models flux relationships between volumes. Training is cast as an optimal recovery problem with equality constraints imposing conservation structure, solved efficiently via a Schur-complement strategy. The resulting model produces a Dirichlet-to-Neumann map with closed-form posterior uncertainty expressions, and RKHS posterior error bounds for linear functionals support UQ.

Result: The trained model yields real-time solutions with closed-form estimators for boundary fluxes given Dirichlet data. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the posterior distribution accurately serves as a surrogate for error estimation, and RKHS-based error bounds provide rigorous uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: The integration of mixed FEM subspaces with GP regression, framed via optimal recovery and enforced by conservation-based equality constraints, produces structure-preserving reduced-order models that are both computationally efficient and theoretically grounded. The framework supports real-time inference with rigorous UQ, making it suitable for verification and validation workflows.

Abstract: Recent advances in scientific machine learning provide a means of near-real-time solution to partial differential equations (PDEs), but lack the theoretical underpinnings of conventional simulators that support contemporary verification and validation. In this work, we construct data-driven reduced-order models that serve as structure-preserving, real-time surrogates. Remarkably, the exterior calculus that imposes physical conservation structure also exposes topological structure that we use to build a Gaussian process (GP) representation of uncertainty in state-flux relationships, ultimately yielding a Dirichlet-to-Neumann map for quantities of interest with closed-form expressions for posterior uncertainty. We specifically propose structure-preserving $H(\mathrm{div})$--$L^2$ subspaces of conventional Raviart--Thomas and $dgP_0$ elements prescribed by a lightweight transformer. Reduced-order dynamics consistent with this subspace are learned by posing a conservation law in which a GP describes the fluxes between volumes. This work hinges on a novel interface between mixed FEM spaces and GP regression; when training is posed as the optimal recovery problem (ORP), the resulting GP regression can be written as an optimization problem with equality constraints that impose a conservation structure, amenable to a fast Schur-complement training strategy. The trained model can then be solved in real time with closed-form estimators for boundary fluxes driven by prescribed Dirichlet data. The paper includes RKHS posterior error bounds for linear functionals to support uncertainty quantification, as well as numerical experiments demonstrating the accuracy of the posterior distribution as a surrogate for error estimation.

</details>


### [245] [DeepRHP: A Hybrid Variational Autoencoder for Designing Random Heteropolymers as Protein Mimics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11651)
*Shuni Li,Zhiyuan Ruan,Andy Shen,Ivan Jayapurna,Ting Xu,Haiyan Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeepRHP is a semi-supervised variational autoencoder that incorporates chemical features to guide the design of synthetic random heteropolymers (RHPs) for protein stabilization, validated on Aquaporin Z membrane protein stabilization in non-native environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic random heteropolymers (RHPs) can mimic protein-like behavior and function, but there is a lack of computational tools to efficiently guide their design. Bridging this gap is the core motivation.

Method: The authors develop DeepRHP, a modified variational autoencoder (VAE) under a semi-supervised framework. A classical VAE is augmented with an additional feature-based VAE, forcing the latent space to capture both critical chemical feature structures and individual RHP sequence patterns. This hybrid architecture allows flexible incorporation of any relevant chemical features.

Result: DeepRHP successfully suggests monomer compositions that stabilize membrane proteins (specifically Aquaporin Z) in non-native environments. The predictions show concordance with previously published experimental results, validating the model's effectiveness.

Conclusion: The strong alignment between model predictions and true RHP function demonstrates significant potential for hybrid autoencoder architectures in guiding RHP design for proteins and other biological compounds.

Abstract: Synthetic random heteropolymers (RHPs), consisting of a predefined set of monomers, offer an approach toward the design of protein-like materials. These RHPs, if designed appropriately, can mimic protein behavior and function. As such, there is a need for computational tools to efficiently guide RHP design. We bridge this gap by developing DeepRHP, a modified variational autoencoder (VAE) model under a semi-supervised framework. By equipping a classical VAE with an additional feature-based VAE, DeepRHP forces the latent space to capture structures of critical chemical features as well as individual RHP sequence patterns. In this sense, our method is versatile by allowing any relevant features to be incorporated in a hybrid manner. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DeepRHP by suggesting potential monomer compositions that stabilize membrane proteins (e.g. Aquaporin Z) in non-native environments and cross-validating our prediction with published results. The concordance between our model and true RHP function suggests strong potential in utilizing hybrid autoencoder architectures to guide RHP design for proteins and other biological compounds.

</details>


### [246] [IAPO: Input Attribution-Aware Policy Optimization for Tool Use in Small Multimodal Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11652)
*Yifan Yang,Zhen Zhang,Jiayi Tian,Liyan Tan,Zheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IAPO is an RL algorithm that improves multimodal SLM tool-calling by aligning the model's input attribution patterns with a teacher model, achieving ~3% average VQA accuracy gains over existing methods across six benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods for tool-calling rely on exact-match or format-based binary rewards, which are unsuitable for multimodal tasks where multiple valid tool-use trajectories exist and ground-truth annotations are unavailable. These sparse, brittle rewards also fail to guide the decision process, making learning especially difficult for small language models.

Method: Input Attribution-Aware Policy Optimization (IAPO), an RL algorithm that aligns the SLM's attribution distribution across input components (e.g., visual regions, text tokens) with that of a stronger teacher model, providing dense, process-oriented reward signals instead of binary outcome rewards.

Result: Experiments on Qwen2.5-VL-3B demonstrate an average 3% improvement in visual question answering accuracy across six test sets compared to prior visual tool-use methods, driven by better attention to the most relevant input evidence.

Conclusion: Aligning input attribution with a teacher model via RL provides effective, dense supervision for improving multimodal SLM tool-calling, overcoming the limitations of sparse binary rewards.

Abstract: This paper investigates reinforcement learning (RL) methods for improving tool-calling capabilities in multimodal small language model (SLM) agents. While existing works have explored various reward designs to improve agentic tool-calling ability, these approaches face inherent limitations for SLM training, especially under multimodal scenarios. First, many existing methods evaluate tool use correctness through exact matching against certain ground-truth or predefined formats. However, this assumption is often unsuitable for multimodal tasks, where multiple tool use paths may be valid and annotated tool trajectories are typically unavailable. Second, such sparse and brittle binary rewards provide little guidance on how to improve the underlying decision process, making them particularly difficult for multimodal SLM to learn from. To address these issues, we propose Input Attribution-Aware Policy Optimization (IAPO), an RL algorithm for improving tool use in multimodal SLM by aligning the model's attribution across input components with that of a stronger teacher. Experiments on Qwen2.5-VL-3B show that the proposed method improves visual question answering accuracy by an average of 3% across six test sets compared with existing visual tool use work, by helping the model attend to the most relevant input evidence.

</details>


### [247] [Sparse probes and murky physics: a case study of interpretability challenges in a foundation model for continuum dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11657)
*Katherine Rosenfeld,Maike Sonnewald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies mechanistic interpretability (sparse autoencoders) to a scientific foundation model for continuum dynamics (Walrus), using enstrophy as a physically grounded metric. Probing shear-flow simulations reveals piecewise consistent but intermittent feature usage that does not map cleanly onto standard physics decompositions. Systematic output-level discrepancies between the emulator and numerical simulations are partially linked to specific SAE feature activations, raising open questions about robustly evaluating scientific foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As generative AI emulators are increasingly deployed in physics domains with established theory and benchmarks, there is a pressing need to understand their internal mechanisms: does the model's internal behavior align with known physics, and can interpretability methods explain where and why the emulator succeeds or fails?

Method: The authors study Walrus (a cross-domain foundation model for continuum dynamics by Polymathic) using mechanistic interpretability. They apply a sparse autoencoder (SAE) to a selected internal layer, yielding over 20,000 features which they triage using enstrophy as a physically grounded metric. They focus on shear flow as a simple testbed, comparing SAE feature recruitment across multiple simulation parameter setups, and perform direct output-level comparisons between the numerical solver and the emulator.

Result: Across shear-flow setups, features exhibit piecewise consistency — subsets recur in similar roles — but this structure is intermittent and resists clean mapping onto standard physical decompositions. Direct comparisons reveal systematic discrepancies: the emulator produces fields where energy/structures are either too diffuse or too localized. Some of these output-level failures correlate with changes in specific SAE feature usage.

Conclusion: The work exposes open challenges for scientific foundation models: (1) how to robustly prioritize mechanistically meaningful features, (2) how to disentangle stable structure from artifacts of single-layer probing and SAE limitations, and (3) how to determine when "different" internal representations are genuinely informative versus merely effective for the task.

Abstract: Generative AI emulators are increasingly used in scientific domains where we already have strong theory, benchmarks, and physical intuition. This raises a central evaluation and interpretability question: when a foundation-style model can reproduce known continuum dynamics, what internal mechanism supports that behavior, is the internal behaviour consistent with known physics, and how does it relate to where the emulator succeeds or fails? We investigate a cross-domain foundation model for continuum dynamics, Walrus by Polymathic, using mechanistic interpretability guided by physical principles. We apply a sparse autoencoder (SAE) to probe a selected layer, and address the practical challenge of triaging a large feature set (over 20,000) using enstrophy as a physically grounded metric. As a deliberately simple testbed, we focus on shear flow and compare feature recruitment across multiple shear-flow setups, i.e. parameter values in the numerical simulation. Across setups we find evidence of piecewise consistency, with subsets of features recurring in similar roles, but this structure is intermittent and does not map cleanly onto standard physical decompositions. In parallel, direct comparisons between numerical simulation and the emulator reveal systematic output-level discrepancies, including regimes where energy/structures become too diffuse or too localized. We connect parts of these discrepancies to changes in specific SAE feature usage. Our work highlights open questions for scientific foundation models: how to robustly prioritize mechanistically meaningful features, how to separate stable structure from analysis artifacts (including single-layer and SAE limitations), and how to use established benchmarks to decide when "different" internal representations are genuinely informative rather than merely effective.

</details>


### [248] [Bergson: An Open Source Library for Data Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11660)
*Lucia Quirke,Louis Jaburi,David Johnston,William Z. Li,Gonçalo Paulo,Guillaume Martres,Girish Gupta,Stella Biderman,Nora Belrose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bergson is an open-source library for data attribution in large language models, providing scalable implementations of methods like MAGIC, SOURCE, and TrackStar, with support for on-disk gradient storage and multi-node distributed training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data attribution is promising for interpretability but suffers from high engineering effort at scale and a lack of open-source tooling, especially for cutting-edge methods. Bergson aims to accelerate research by filling this gap.

Method: Bergson provides an open-source library with native support for on-disk gradient stores, multi-node distributed training, researcher quality-of-life tools, and first open-source implementations of MAGIC, SOURCE, and TrackStar.

Result: The library delivers the first open-source implementations of three leading data attribution methods, enabling scalable attribution for very large language models and pre-training datasets.

Conclusion: Bergson lowers the barrier to entry for data attribution research at scale and is publicly available on GitHub under EleutherAI.

Abstract: Data attribution is a promising field in interpretability that aims to explain model behavior through the influence of its training data, with applications including debugging undesirable model behavior and training dataset curation. However, significant engineering effort is required to perform it at scale, and many cutting edge techniques lack open-source tooling and support. Bergson is an open source library that aims to enable faster progress in the field by providing a host of techniques that scale to very large language models and pre-training datasets. The library natively supports on-disk gradient stores and multi-node distributed training, and provides quality of life tools for researchers. Finally, we introduce the first open-source implementations of three leading data attribution methods: MAGIC, SOURCE, and TrackStar. The library is available at https://github.com/EleutherAI/bergson .

</details>


### [249] [Spectrally Regularized Latent Flow Matching for Turbulence Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11691)
*Khalid Rafiq,Aditya G. Nair*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent flow matching with spectrally regularized VAE dramatically improves dissipation-range fidelity in synthetic turbulence, raising retained spectral power from ~25% to ~94% (reconstruction) and ~20% to ~79% (generation), driven primarily by encoder-induced latent reorganization rather than decoder capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing latent diffusion and flow matching models for synthetic turbulence systematically under-represent dissipation-range amplitudes, limiting their utility for applications sensitive to small-scale dynamics. The authors identify that standard MSE-trained VAEs cause this failure mode and propose a targeted spectral regularization to address it.

Method: A latent flow matching framework with a spectrally regularized compression stage: the standard MSE-trained VAE is replaced with a zone-weighted log-spectral objective. The method is tested on a 256² DNS dataset at Re_f ≈ 2250. Diagnostic tools include encoder-decoder swap experiments and support-amplitude decomposition to isolate mechanistic contributions.

Result: The spectrally regularized VAE raises deep-dissipation retained spectral power from 25% to 94% (reconstruction) and 20% to 79% (unconditional generation). The MSE-trained latent space imposes a quality ceiling near DD bias −0.70 regardless of integrator or step count; the regularized space reaches DD bias −0.117 at just 20 function evaluations. Encoder-decoder swaps reveal the improvement is driven by encoder-induced latent reorganization. MSE models act as conservative suppression models, attenuating intermittent high-wavenumber structure. Both pipelines recover the second-order structure function and correct sign of S₃, indicating correct cascade direction without explicit supervision. A residual gap in S₃ magnitude remains.

Conclusion: Spectral regularization of the compression stage is an effective, targeted fix for dissipation-range under-representation in generative turbulence models. Phase-coherent triadic organization is identified as a complementary axis to amplitude fidelity for future work, given the residual S₃ magnitude gap.

Abstract: Latent diffusion and flow matching have emerged as leading approaches for synthetic turbulence generation, yet they systematically under-represent dissipation-range amplitudes. We introduce a latent flow matching framework with a spectrally regularized compression stage that directly targets this failure mode. On a 256^2 DNS dataset at Re_f \approx 2250, replacing an MSE-trained VAE with a zone-weighted log-spectral objective raises deep-dissipation retained spectral power from 25% to 94% in reconstruction and from 20% to 79% in unconditional generation. The improved latent representation also yields a substantially better sampling cost-fidelity tradeoff: the MSE-trained latent space imposes a fundamental quality ceiling near DD bias -0.70 that no integrator or step-count can overcome, while the spectrally regularized latent space reaches DD bias -0.117 at just 20 function evaluations. Mechanistically, encoder-decoder swap experiments show that the improvement is driven primarily by encoder-induced latent reorganization rather than decoder capacity, while a support-amplitude decomposition reveals that MSE-trained models behave as conservative suppression models, minimizing pointwise error by attenuating intermittent high-wavenumber structure. Both pipelines recover the second-order structure function and the correct sign of S_3, indicating the correct cascade direction without explicit supervision. A small residual gap in the magnitude of S_3 suggests that phase-coherent triadic organization remains a complementary axis to amplitude fidelity for future generative turbulence models.

</details>


### [250] [Noise-Aware Framework for Correcting Corrupted Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11695)
*Ha-Linh Nguyen,Hong-Anh Nguyen,Minh-Duc La,Phong Lam,Thu-Trang Nguyen,Son Nguyen,Hieu Dinh Vo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CANOLA is a noise-aware label correction framework that estimates dataset noise distribution, incorporates it into DNN training to down-weight unreliable signals, and performs cautious iterative soft label refinement, achieving 19–52% relative error reduction improvement over SOTA across six datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world datasets frequently contain corrupted labels that degrade ML/DL model performance. Existing methods either fail to explicitly model the underlying noise distribution or perform label correction too aggressively, leading to error propagation. A principled approach is needed that accounts for noise characteristics during training and corrects labels in a stable, controlled manner.

Method: CANOLA operates in two integrated stages: (1) Noise-aware learning: explicitly estimates the dataset's underlying noise distribution (noise transition matrix) and incorporates this into DNN training, enabling the model to down-weight unreliable supervision signals and focus on trustworthy patterns. (2) Cautious iterative soft label refinement: model predictions are blended with observed labels in a progressive, controlled fashion to avoid premature or erroneous updates, repairing the dataset gradually over multiple iterations.

Result: CANOLA consistently outperformed SOTA label correction methods across six diverse datasets under realistic noisy labeling scenarios, achieving 19%–52% relative improvement in error reduction. Downstream models trained on CANOLA-corrected data showed substantial performance gains—simple classifiers outperformed complex model-centric approaches by up to 67%.

Conclusion: Explicit noise distribution modeling combined with cautious iterative refinement provides a highly effective framework for label correction. CANOLA not only repairs corrupted datasets more accurately than prior methods but also enables simple downstream models to surpass complex model-centric approaches, highlighting the value of data quality over model complexity.

Abstract: High-quality labeled data is essential for training reliable ML/DL models. However, real-world datasets often contain a considerable proportion of corrupted labels, which can severely degrade model performance. To address this problem, we propose CANOLA, a novel framework for correcting corrupted labels through noise-aware learning and iterative label refinement. CANOLA explicitly estimates the underlying noise distribution of the dataset and incorporates this information into the training of a noise-aware Deep Neural Network. By incorporating noise characteristics during learning, CANOLA enables the model to down-weight unreliable supervision signals and focus on trustworthy patterns, thereby improving robustness and generalization. Label correction is performed via cautious, iterative soft label refinement, in which model predictions are blended with observed labels to prevent premature or erroneous updates. This progressive refinement allows the dataset to be repaired in a stable and controlled manner. We evaluate CANOLA on six widely used datasets under realistic noisy labeling scenarios. Experimental results show that CANOLA consistently outperforms SOTA label correction methods, achieving relative improvements ranging from 19% to 52% in error reduction. Moreover, models trained on datasets corrected by CANOLA obtain substantial downstream performance gains. Even simple classifiers trained on CANOLA's corrected data can outperform complex model-centric approaches by margins of up to 67%.

</details>


### [251] [A Data-Centric Framework for Detecting and Correcting Corrupted Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11699)
*Ha-Linh Nguyen,Hong-Anh Nguyen,Minh-Duc La,Thu-Trang Nguyen,Son Nguyen,Hieu Dinh Vo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Relabeler is a data-centric framework that detects noisy labels using local-global relationships and corrects them by estimating the most probable clean label from features and observed labels, achieving up to 58% higher correction precision and 6% downstream improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world datasets frequently contain noisy labels that significantly degrade model accuracy and reliability. Existing solutions often address detection or correction in isolation, motivating an integrated, end-to-end framework that jointly handles both tasks by exploiting structural relationships in the data.

Method: Relabeler operates in two stages: (1) corrupted label detection, which jointly leverages local and global relationships among data instances to identify suspicious samples; (2) label correction, which estimates the most probable clean label for each flagged instance by conditioning on both its input features and the observed noisy label.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, noise types, and noise rates show Relabeler consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with up to 58% improvement in label correction precision and up to 6% improvement in downstream task performance.

Conclusion: Relabeler provides an effective, unified data-centric solution for mitigating label noise, demonstrating that jointly modeling local-global instance relationships for detection and feature-label conditioning for correction yields substantial practical gains in both label quality and model performance.

Abstract: The performance of machine learning and deep learning models largely depends on the quality of the training data. However, the quality of the real-world datasets is often compromised by noisy labels, which can substantially degrade model accuracy and reliability. To address this challenge, we propose Relabeler, an end-to-end data-centric framework for detecting and correcting corrupted labels. For corrupted label detection, Relabeler jointly leverages both local and global relationships among data instances to identify potentially noisy samples. After detecting suspicious instances, Relabeler further performs label correction by estimating the most probable clean label for each instance based on both its input features and observed noisy label. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, noise types, and noise rates demonstrate that Relabeler consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 58% improvement in label correction precision and 6% improvement in downstream task performance.

</details>


### [252] [RLCSD: Reinforcement Learning with Contrastive On-Policy Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11709)
*Leyi Pan,Shuchang Tao,Yunpeng Zhai,Lingzhe Zhang,Zhaoyang Liu,Bolin Ding,Aiwei Liu,Lijie Wen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLCSD addresses "privilege-induced style drift" in on-policy self-distillation by using contrastive learning with both correct and wrong hints, focusing the training signal on task-bearing tokens rather than style tokens, and consistently outperforms GRPO and prior OPSD methods across reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In on-policy self-distillation (OPSD), a model learns from its own distribution under privileged context (verified solutions). The authors identify a pathology called "privilege-induced style drift": the learning signal concentrates on style tokens (e.g., brevity/directness) rather than task-bearing tokens, causing training instability and response length shrinkage. Existing OPSD methods fail to isolate the task-relevant portion of the teacher-student distributional gap.

Method: RLCSD (Reinforcement Learning with Contrastive on-policy Self-Distillation) contrasts the teacher-student distributional gap under a correct hint against that under a wrong hint. By subtracting the style shift induced by conditioning on any hint (regardless of correctness), the method suppresses style-dominated signals and yields a learning signal concentrated on task-bearing tokens. Experiments span Qwen3 (1.7B/4B/8B) and Olmo-3-7B-Think on math and logical reasoning tasks. The contrastive principle is shown to be general: it can plug into existing OPSD methods and extends to cross-model on-policy distillation.

Result: RLCSD consistently outperforms GRPO and prior OPSD methods across multiple model scales (1.7B–8B) and reasoning tasks (mathematical and logical). The contrastive principle plug-and-play improves existing OPSD methods, and its insight generalizes to cross-model on-policy distillation.

Conclusion: Privilege-induced style drift is a fundamental weakness in OPSD that can be effectively mitigated by contrastive self-distillation. RLCSD provides a robust, general-purpose solution that focuses reinforcement on task-relevant reasoning tokens, and the contrastive principle is broadly applicable beyond the specific OPSD setting.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) provides dense, token-level supervision for reasoning models by aligning a model's own distribution with the distribution it produces under privileged context, typically a verified solution. However, we show that the learning signal drawn from this distributional gap concentrates on style tokens rather than task-bearing ones, as the hinted model tends to produce more direct, shorter outputs. We term this pathology \emph{privilege-induced style drift}, which destabilizes training or causes response length to shrink. To address this, we propose \textbf{RLCSD} (Reinforcement Learning with Contrastive on-policy Self-Distillation), which mitigates this drift by contrasting the teacher-student gap under a correct hint against that under a wrong hint, suppressing the style shift that conditioning on a hint tends to induce regardless of correctness, and yielding a signal that is more concentrated on task-bearing tokens. Experiments on Qwen3 (1.7B/4B/8B) and Olmo-3-7B-Think across mathematical and logical reasoning show that RLCSD consistently outperforms GRPO and prior OPSD methods. We further show that the contrastive principle is general: it plugs into existing OPSD methods to improve them, and its underlying insight extends to the broader cross-model on-policy distillation setting.

</details>


### [253] [Capacity-Constrained Online Convex Optimization with Delayed Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11711)
*Alexander Ryabchenko,Idan Attias,Daniel M. Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies delayed online convex optimization (OCO) under a hard capacity constraint where only C pending rounds can be tracked. It introduces a semi-clairvoyant delay model, reduces the problem to a novel "delayed and weighted" OCO via randomized scheduling with importance weighting, and provides regret bounds for both first-order and bandit feedback under convex and strongly convex losses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard delayed online learning assumes the learner can track all pending rounds indefinitely, which is unrealistic when tracking resources (e.g., memory, compute) are finite. Feedback from untracked rounds is permanently lost. Prior constrained models either assumed full clairvoyance of delays or used overly simplistic delay models. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a more realistic semi-clairvoyant model and provide the first regret guarantees under a hard capacity constraint.

Method: The authors introduce a semi-clairvoyant model where delay expirations are observed online (not known at prediction time). The core technique is a reduction to a "delayed and weighted" OCO problem: a randomized scheduler decides which pending rounds to track, and importance-weighting corrects for the sampling bias. They propose and analyze Delayed-Weighted FTRL (Follow the Regularized Leader) and its bandit analogue, deriving regret bounds that capture the interplay between time-varying weights and delayed feedback. Capacity C = Ω(log T) suffices to recover standard unconstrained delayed OCO rates (up to log factors) for first-order feedback; bandit regret degrades gracefully as a function of (1 + σ_max/C).

Result: First regret guarantees for capacity-constrained OCO under both convex and strongly convex losses with first-order and bandit feedback. For first-order feedback, C = Ω(log T) recovers standard delayed OCO rates. For bandit feedback, regret scales with powers of (1 + σ_max/C), allowing sublinear regret even when C < σ_max. The semi-clairvoyant model successfully relaxes the unrealistic full-clairvoyance assumption of prior work.

Conclusion: The paper establishes the first comprehensive framework for delayed OCO under a hard tracking capacity constraint. The reduction to delayed-weighted OCO via randomized scheduling with importance weighting is a flexible and principled approach. The results demonstrate that sublinear regret is achievable even with severely limited tracking capacity, with graceful degradation as capacity decreases relative to peak pending observations.

Abstract: Online learning with delayed feedback typically assumes that the learner can track all pending rounds until their feedback arrives. In practice, tracking resources are finite, and feedback from untracked rounds is permanently lost. In this paper, we study delayed online convex optimization (OCO) under a hard capacity constraint, where at most $C$ pending rounds can be tracked at any time. To model delay information, we introduce a semi-clairvoyant model that refines the clairvoyant assumption from prior work: rather than requiring delays to be known at prediction time, the learner observes delay expirations online, consistent with the classical unconstrained delayed setting. Our approach proceeds via a reduction to a novel ``delayed and weighted'' OCO problem, using a scheduler that randomizes tracking decisions and importance-weights the resulting observations. For this base problem, we propose and analyze Delayed-Weighted FTRL and its bandit analogue, establishing regret bounds that explicitly characterize the interaction between time-varying weights and delayed feedback. Combining these base learners with our schedulers yields the first regret guarantees for capacity-constrained OCO under convex and strongly convex losses, for both first-order and bandit feedback. For first-order feedback, capacity $C = Ω(\log T)$ suffices to recover standard delayed OCO rates up to logarithmic factors. For bandit feedback, the regret rates are modulated by powers of $(1 + σ_{\text{max}}/C)$, where $σ_{\text{max}}$ is the maximum number of pending observations at any time. This allows the regret bound to degrade gracefully when $C < σ_{\text{max}}$, while remaining sublinear.

</details>


### [254] [ICA Lens: Interpreting Language Models Without Training Another Dictionary](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11722)
*Sida Liu,Feijiang Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ICALens introduces a practical ICA workflow for LLM interpretability, showing that ICA efficiently recovers interpretable directions competitive with sparse autoencoders without requiring per-layer dictionary training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse autoencoders are the standard for finding interpretable directions in LM representations but require training large overcomplete dictionaries, creating a bottleneck for rapid exploration. The authors hypothesize that many interpretable directions are already visible from activation geometry due to their non-Gaussian, token-selective nature.

Method: ICALens combines an optimized GPU-parallel FastICA pipeline with LLM-specific stability recipes and diagnostics. It applies ICA layer-wise to recover non-Gaussian directions. Evaluation uses SAEBench (sparse probing, targeted probe perturbation) across GPT-2 Small, Gemma 2 2B, and Qwen 3.5 2B Base.

Result: ICA is competitive with public SAEs on sparse probing and outperforms them on targeted probe perturbation under small-to-medium budgets. The method recovers compact, human-interpretable directions efficiently without gradient-based dictionary training.

Conclusion: ICA should not be viewed as a weak baseline but as an efficient, complementary first lens for exploring LM representations, offering a lightweight alternative to SAEs for initial interpretability analysis.

Abstract: Finding interpretable directions in language-model representations is critical for understanding and controlling model behavior. Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become the standard tool for this purpose, but using them as the default first lens often requires training, storing, and evaluating large overcomplete dictionaries. This bottleneck limits rapid exploration and raises a fundamental question: how much interpretable structure is already visible from activation geometry before training another neural dictionary? Our intuition is simple: many interpretable directions are selective on tokens, and these directions should look less Gaussian than random directions. We therefore revisit independent component analysis (ICA), a classical method for finding non-Gaussian directions, as a compact lens for language-model interpretability. We find that ICA has been underestimated for LLM interpretability, because prior uses often relied on off-the-shelf ICA implementations that are brittle on LLM activations and lacked systematic tools for inspecting and evaluating the recovered directions. To bridge these gaps, we introduce ICALens, the first practical workflow for stable, efficient, and auditable ICA analysis of LLM representations. It combines an optimized GPU-parallel FastICA pipeline with LLM-specific stability recipes and better fitting diagnostics, enabling efficient and reliable layer-wise analysis. Across GPT-2 Small, Gemma 2 2B, and Qwen 3.5 2B Base, ICALens efficiently recovers compact, human-interpretable directions without per-layer gradient-based dictionary training. On SAEBench, ICA is competitive with public SAEs in sparse probing and outperforms them in targeted probe perturbation under small-to-medium budgets. These results suggest that ICA should not be viewed as a weak baseline, but as an efficient and complementary first lens for exploring language-model representations.

</details>


### [255] [RCAP: Robust, Class-Aware, Probabilistic Dynamic Dataset Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11761)
*Atif Hassan,Swanand Khare,Jiaul H. Paik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RCAP is a dynamic dataset pruning algorithm that adaptively selects class-representative subsets during training using a closed-form class-wise sample fraction estimation and loss-prioritized sampling, achieving superior worst-group accuracy across balanced and imbalanced datasets with up to 8.69× speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic data pruning methods fail to maintain strong worst-group accuracy at high pruning rates, especially on class-imbalanced datasets. There is a need for a pruning algorithm that is robust across both balanced and imbalanced data distributions.

Method: RCAP uses a closed-form solution to estimate per-class sample inclusion fractions, adaptively adjusted each epoch via class-wise aggregated loss. It then applies an adaptive sampling strategy that prioritizes high-loss samples within each class to populate the training subset.

Result: RCAP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art pruning methods on six diverse datasets spanning balanced to highly imbalanced regimes, across five model architectures and three training paradigms (training from scratch, transfer learning, fine-tuning). At 10% data retention, it surpasses full-data training by >1% on imbalanced datasets and achieves an average 8.69× speedup.

Conclusion: RCAP provides a robust, class-aware solution to dynamic dataset pruning that preserves worst-group accuracy even under extreme pruning, making it particularly effective for imbalanced classification tasks where existing methods falter.

Abstract: Dynamic data pruning techniques aim to reduce computational cost while minimizing information loss by periodically selecting representative subsets of input data during model training. However, existing methods often struggle to maintain strong worst-group accuracy, particularly at high pruning rates, across balanced and imbalanced datasets. To address this challenge, we propose RCAP, a Robust, Class-Aware, Probabilistic dynamic dataset pruning algorithm for classification tasks. RCAP applies a closed-form solution to estimate the fraction of samples to be included in the training subset for each individual class. This fraction is adaptively adjusted in every epoch using class-wise aggregated loss. Thereafter, it employs an adaptive sampling strategy that prioritizes samples having high loss for populating the class-wise subsets. We evaluate RCAP on six diverse datasets ranging from class-balanced to highly imbalanced using five distinct models across three training paradigms: training from scratch, transfer learning, and fine-tuning. Our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art dataset pruning methods, achieving superior worst-group accuracy at all pruning rates. Remarkably, with only $10\%$ data, RCAP delivers $>1\%$ improvement in performance on class-imbalanced datasets compared to full data training while providing an average $8.69\times$ speedup. The code can be accessed at https://github.com/atif-hassan/RCAP-dynamic-dataset-pruning

</details>


### [256] [AI4Land: Scalable Deep Learning for Global High-Resolution Land Use Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11793)
*Amirpasha Mozaffari,Marina Castaño,Stefano Materia,Etienne Tourigny,Oscar Molina-Sedano,Jordi Varela-Agrelo,Dario Garcia-Gasulla,Miguel Castrillo Melguizo,Mario Acosta,Amanda Duarte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AI4Land is a data-driven U-Net framework that reconstructs high-resolution land use/land cover maps by integrating coarse scenario data with static geophysical features, and will later predict dynamic biophysical variables like leaf area index, aiming to reduce terrestrial carbon cycle uncertainties in climate models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty in the terrestrial carbon cycle remains a major constraint in climate projections, largely due to limitations in land surface representation and variability in Earth system models. There is a pressing need for high-resolution, physically consistent land surface reconstructions and projections that extend beyond the temporal coverage of direct Earth observations.

Method: A two-phase U-Net architecture framework: Phase 1 (current work) reconstructs annual land use and land cover by fusing coarse-resolution scenario data with static geophysical features. Phase 2 (planned) will use the high-resolution maps to predict dynamic biophysical variables, particularly leaf area index, at finer temporal scales. Models are trained on Earth observation data and deployed on MareNostrum5, leveraging GPU-accelerated HPC for global-scale pipelines. The output is a suite of open-source emulators for real-time coupling with digital twin platforms.

Result: The framework successfully learns to reproduce spatially explicit and physically consistent land surface patterns on MareNostrum5, demonstrating the viability of GPU-accelerated HPC infrastructure for global-scale climate AI pipelines. The resulting open-source emulators are designed for real-time integration with digital twin platforms such as those under the Destination Earth initiative.

Conclusion: By delivering realistic, evolving land surface conditions on demand, AI4Land aims to reduce critical uncertainties in land surface representation and improve the predictive power of next-generation climate simulations, particularly in the context of the terrestrial carbon cycle.

Abstract: Uncertainty in the terrestrial carbon cycle remains a major constraint in climate projections, partly driven by the uncertainties affecting the land surface representation and variability in Earth system models. To address this limitation, we present a data-driven framework AI4Land, for generating high-resolution historical reconstructions and future projections of key land surface variables. The framework follows a two-phase approach using a U-Net architecture. In the first phase, which is the focus of this work, it reconstructs annual land use and land cover by integrating coarse-resolution scenario data with static geophysical features. In a planned second phase, the resulting high-resolution maps will be used to predict dynamic biophysical variables, particularly leaf area index, at finer temporal scales. Trained on Earth observation data, the models learn to reproduce spatially explicit and physically consistent land surface patterns, extending temporal coverage to periods lacking direct observations. AI4Land was developed and trained on MareNostrum5, demonstrating how GPU-accelerated HPC infrastructure enables global-scale climate AI pipelines. The final product is a suite of open-source emulators designed for real-time coupling with digital twin platforms, such as those developed under the Destination Earth initiative. By delivering realistic and evolving land surface conditions on demand, this work aims to reduce critical uncertainties and improve the predictive power of next-generation climate simulations.

</details>


### [257] [Multimodal Ordinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Severity Using Structural MRI and Clinical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11794)
*Boris-Stephan Rauchmann,Jonathan Laib,Buse Ercik,Robert Perneczky,Sergio Altares-López*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multimodal ML framework combining T1-weighted MRI, demographics, and genetics with attention mechanisms and ordinal regression achieves superior Alzheimer's disease severity staging (QWK 0.549, adjacent-stage accuracy 0.970) compared to unimodal or non-ordinal approaches, with Grad-CAM++ and SHAP confirming clinically plausible model behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current clinical staging of Alzheimer's disease (e.g., CDR scale) is time-intensive and prone to variability. There is a need for accurate, scalable, automated tools that can reliably assess disease severity while providing interpretable predictions suitable for clinical decision support.

Method: An attention-enhanced multimodal framework was developed, integrating T1-weighted MRI with demographic and genetic features. Both unimodal (imaging-only, tabular-only) and multimodal architectures were compared, each tested with ordinal and non-ordinal prediction heads. Models were trained on ADNI, AIBL, and NIFD datasets using cohort-stratified, subject-level splits. A strictly held-out test set (subjects excluded from all training, validation, preprocessing, and tuning) prevented data leakage. Evaluation metrics included MAE, adjacent-stage accuracy, and Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK). Explainability was assessed via Grad-CAM++ and SHAP.

Result: The T1-MRI unimodal model slightly outperformed the tabular unimodal model (adjacent-stage accuracy 0.963 vs. QWK 0.433 vs. 0.444). The multimodal non-ordinal baseline achieved the lowest MAE (0.340), while the multimodal ordinal model achieved the highest adjacent-stage accuracy (0.970) and strongest clinical agreement (QWK 0.549). Explainability analyses showed anatomically and clinically plausible model behavior.

Conclusion: Ordinal formulations better capture the ordered structure of the CDR scale, yielding predictions more consistent with clinical staging. Attention-based multimodal learning with ordinal regression offers a robust, interpretable, and scalable approach for automated AD severity staging and AI-assisted clinical decision support.

Abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease (AD) require accurate and scalable tools for assessing disease severity, yet current clinical staging remains time-intensive and prone to variability. We propose an attention-enhanced multimodal machine learning framework with ordinal regression for automated and interpretable AD severity staging. The framework integrates T1-weighted MRI with demographic and genetic variables and compares unimodal and multimodal architectures using ordinal and non-ordinal prediction heads. Models were trained and validated using cohort-stratified splits derived from the ADNI, AIBL, and NIFD datasets. A strictly held-out test set was constructed using subjects excluded from all training, validation, preprocessing, and hyperparameter tuning procedures, with subject-level splitting employed throughout to prevent data leakage. Among unimodal approaches, the T1-weighted MRI model achieved slightly higher adjacent-stage accuracy (0.963) and agreement with clinical staging (QWK 0.444) than the tabular model (QWK 0.433). Integrating imaging, demographic, and genetic information improved overall performance. The multimodal non-ordinal baseline achieved the lowest prediction error (MAE 0.340), whereas the ordinal multimodal model achieved the highest adjacent-stage accuracy (0.970) and strongest agreement with clinical staging (QWK 0.549). These findings indicate that ordinal formulations better capture the ordered structure of the CDR scale and yield predictions more consistent with clinical staging. Explainability analyses using Grad CAM++ and SHAP demonstrated anatomically and clinically plausible model behavior, supporting transparent decision-making. Overall, attention-based multimodal learning with ordinal regression represents a robust, interpretable, and scalable approach for automated AD severity staging and AI-assisted clinical decision support.

</details>


### [258] [Space-sampled Value Decay: Forgetting Mechanisms for Non-stationary Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11797)
*Felix Störck,Fabian Hinder,Barbara Hammer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Space-sampled Value Decay (SSVD), an explicit forgetting mechanism for value-based deep RL to handle non-stationary environments (drift) without requiring task IDs or context information. It evaluates modifications of DQN and SAC, demonstrating both positive effects and limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rodents adapt to environmental drift without explicit change signals, inspiring forgetting-based models. Existing Non-stationary RL (NSRL) methods typically require partial perfect information (task IDs, context), which is unrealistic. There is a need for simple forgetting mechanisms that can handle drift under uncertainty in deep RL architectures.

Method: The authors develop Space-sampled Value Decay (SSVD), an explicit forgetting mechanism integrated into value-based deep RL architectures. The approach is applied as modifications to Deep Q-Networks (DQN) and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), and evaluated on non-stationary environments.

Result: SSVD demonstrates positive effects in adapting to non-stationary environments but also exhibits limitations in achieved returns. Results are discussed for both DQN and SAC variants under drift conditions.

Conclusion: Space-sampled Value Decay provides a simple yet effective forgetting mechanism for deep RL under non-stationarity without requiring drift-related information. It shows promise but has acknowledged limitations in return performance.

Abstract: Studies on rodents such as mice have shown the capabilities to adapt their behavior when dealing with changing parameters (``drift'') of the environment even if no information about change is provided (uncertainty) -- a behavior that can be modeled by forgetting mechanisms. Non-stationary Reinforcement Learning (NSRL) deals with adapting state-of-the-art RL methods to deal with changing environments: these however usually require (partially) perfect information about the drift such as ``task IDs'' or ``context''. To mitigate the effects of drift, this work develops \emph{Space-sampled Value Decay} as an explicit forgetting mechanism for value-based deep RL architectures as a simple yet effective approach. In particular we demonstrate and discuss positive effects but also limitations in achieved returns for modifications of Deep Q-networks (DQN) and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) when evaluated on non-stationary environments.

</details>


### [259] [From Uniform to Learned Graph Priors: Diffusion for Structure Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11831)
*Qi Shao,Hao Guo,Jiawen Chen,Duxin Chen,Wenwu Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diff-prior introduces a diffusion-based adaptive prior that calibrates edge posteriors in neural relational inference, replacing factorized uniform priors with a learnable denoising-style mechanism that yields more decisive and accurate interaction graph discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NRI methods use oversimplified, factorized graph priors (near-uniform) that treat edges as independent, creating a systemic misalignment with real-world relational structures. This produces diffuse and indecisive edge posteriors, limiting structural discovery reliability.

Method: Diff-prior reframes prior integration as a learnable denoising calibration using a diffusion model. Rather than generating graphs, it learns an adaptive structure prior that operates directly on the encoder's edge distribution before structural sampling, organizing scattered posteriors toward the true underlying structure. This provides a generic training paradigm over structured variables applicable across NRI-family architectures.

Result: Experiments on standard benchmarks show Diff-prior improves structure inference performance and produces more decisive edge posteriors across multiple NRI-family architectures.

Conclusion: A diffusion-parameterized adaptive prior that performs structured calibration effectively addresses the prior misalignment problem in NRI, enabling more reliable interaction graph discovery without being tied to a specific architecture.

Abstract: Neural relational inference (NRI) methods discover interaction graphs from trajectories through variational reasoning on discrete potential edges. However, these methods typically rely on oversimplified, factorized graph priors. Such priors, typically nearing uniform distributions, treat edges as independent entities. This systemic misalignment does not match the real-world systems and yields diffuse and indecisive edge posteriors limiting the reliability of structural discovery. To address this, we propose \textit{Diff-prior}, a diffusion-parameterized adaptive prior used to calibrate latent graph distribution rather than generate graphs. Our core insight is to reframe prior integration as a learnable denoising-style calibration that organizes scattered, uncertain edge posteriors into a more reliable overall structure which can be trained by the diffusion model. Diff-prior learns an adaptive structure prior that performs structured calibration on the edge posteriors during inference, guiding it towards a distribution closer to the underlying structure. The diff-prior operates before structural sampling and acts as a denoising calibrator directly on the encoder edge distribution, which provides a generic training paradigm over structured variables. Experiments on standard benchmarks validated our framework, and the results indicate that Diff-prior improves the performance of structure inference and generates more decisive edge posteriors across multiple NRI-family architectures. The code is available on https://github.com/Hardy158118/Diffprior.

</details>


### [260] [Flow Matching with In-Context Priors for Out-of-Distribution Brain Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11833)
*Sam Gijsen,Michał Łukomski,Marc-André Schulz,Kerstin Ritter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a per-timestep conditioned diffusion transformer that generates realistic whole-cortex fMRI brain dynamics for unseen cognitive tasks using compositional language descriptions and optional spatial priors, enabling zero-shot counterfactual neuroscience and in-silico experimental design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models for neural time series have been limited to categorical conditioning, preventing compositional generalization and zero-shot generation. The authors aim to overcome this by enabling generation of fMRI dynamics for unseen cognitive tasks, which would support counterfactual neuroscience — designing and evaluating novel experiments in silico before empirical validation.

Method: A diffusion transformer conditioned per-timestep with (1) compositional language embeddings describing cognitive tasks and (2) optional spatial priors injected in-context. The model is trained to generate whole-cortex fMRI time-series dynamics and evaluated on hundreds of held-out task conditions to assess generalization beyond the training manifold.

Result: Using language alone, the model recovers region-specific recruitment patterns and held-out spatial activation maps. When spatial priors are available, they complement the text pathway by improving generation quality in task-space regions where language-only conditioning degrades, while preserving compositional structure for counterfactual specification.

Conclusion: This is claimed as the first generative model of whole-cortex fMRI dynamics for unseen cognitive tasks, representing a step toward counterfactual neuroscience and data-driven experimental design by enabling zero-shot, compositionally specified brain activity generation.

Abstract: Flow matching and diffusion models enable conditional generation across domains ranging from images to proteins, with recent extensions to out-of-distribution contexts. Yet generative models of neural time series have largely remained restricted to categorical conditioning, precluding compositional and zero-shot generalization. In this work, we propose a per-timestep conditioned diffusion transformer for generating realistic fMRI brain dynamics during unseen cognitive tasks by injecting both compositional language and optional spatial priors in-context. Such zero-shot generation could enable counterfactual neuroscience by supporting in-silico design and evaluation of novel cognitive experiments before empirical validation. Leveraging this model, we evaluate across hundreds of held-out task conditions and characterize predictive performance in relation to the training manifold. From language alone, the model recovers region-specific recruitment across tasks and held-out spatial activation patterns. Spatial priors, when available, complement the text pathway by anchoring generation in regions of task space where language alone degrades, while retaining the compositional structure needed for counterfactual task specification. To our knowledge this is the first generative model of whole-cortex fMRI dynamics for unseen cognitive tasks, advancing counterfactual neuroscience and data-driven experimental design.

</details>


### [261] [TaskFusion: Continual Anomaly Detection for Heterogeneous Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11844)
*Dayananda Herurkar,Federico Raue,Joachim Folz,Jörn Hees,Andreas Dengel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A continual learning method for tabular anomaly detection that handles heterogeneous feature schemas, distribution shifts, and class imbalance via a shared-space AGF model, Taskfusion augmentation, and distilled replay with outlier exposure, achieving strong performance across 21 diverse datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world anomaly detection on tabular data often involves sequential data streams from diverse domains with heterogeneous features, distribution shifts, and severe class imbalance. Standard continual learning methods fail because they assume a fixed input space, leaving continual tabular anomaly detection largely underexplored.

Method: Three components: (1) AGF model — maps task-specific features into a shared space, aligns distributions to mitigate representation drift, and learns anomaly decision boundaries in that aligned space; (2) Taskfusion augmentation — combines boundary-aware intra-task interpolation and cross-task mixing to refine boundaries and transfer anomaly structure; (3) Tabular dataset distillation — compacts synthetic replay samples for memory efficiency, jointly used with augmented data under an outlier exposure objective to combat class imbalance.

Result: Evaluated on 21 heterogeneous tabular datasets across multiple domains. The proposed approach substantially outperforms sequential fine-tuning and other continual learning baselines in anomaly detection performance, while reducing catastrophic forgetting and maintaining stable detection across heterogeneous datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses the underexplored problem of continual anomaly detection in heterogeneous tabular data, demonstrating that combining shared-space alignment, boundary-refining augmentation, and distilled replay with outlier exposure yields robust, stable performance across diverse sequential tasks.

Abstract: Continual anomaly detection in tabular data is challenging and remains largely underexplored, particularly in settings with heterogeneous feature schemas, distribution shifts, and severe class imbalance. In many real-world applications, data arrive sequentially from diverse domains, rendering conventional continual learning methods ineffective due to their reliance on a fixed input space. We propose a continual learning (CL) method, which can overcome these challenges and continually learn from different tasks. Our method consists of three main parts: our AGF model, Taskfusion augmentation, and outlier exposure. The AGF-model maps task-specific features into a shared space, then aligns distributions to reduce representation drift, and learns anomaly decision boundaries in the aligned space. To improve stability, we introduce Taskfusion augmentation, combining boundary-aware interpolation within tasks to refine the model anomaly boundaries and cross-task mixing to transfer anomaly structure across datasets. To handle class imbalance and memory constraints, we employ tabular dataset distillation to store compact synthetic replay samples, which are jointly used with augmented data in an outlier exposure objective for robust anomaly detection. We evaluate the approach on 21 heterogeneous datasets across multiple domains. Results show that our approach substantially improves continual anomaly detection performance over sequential fine-tuning and other CL baselines while reducing catastrophic forgetting and maintaining stable detection across heterogeneous datasets.

</details>


### [262] [Fine-tuning Multi-modal LLMs with ART: Art-based Reinforcement Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11854)
*Michal Chudoba,Sergey Alyaev,Petra Galuscakova,Tomasz Wiktorski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ART (Art-based Reinforcement Training) is a PEFT method that fine-tunes frozen MLLMs by optimizing only the raw visual input pixels via gradient backpropagation, avoiding modifications to precompiled computational graphs. It achieves accuracy competitive with LoRA on math and tool-use benchmarks while generating task-relevant computational artworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PEFT methods like LoRA (adds weights between layers) and Soft Prompting (adds tokens to input) both require modifying precompiled computational graphs of LLMs, making them incompatible with high-throughput inference engines like vLLM. There is a need for a PEFT approach that works without altering the model's computational graph.

Method: ART injects task-specific information into a frozen Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) by treating the visual input as a learnable parameter. Gradients are backpropagated all the way into a plain pixel array, optimizing the raw visual input directly. This requires no changes to the model architecture or computational graph. The optimized visual input can be stylized as computational artworks relevant to the task.

Result: ART was validated on the open-source Qwen architecture at different scales across multiple textual benchmarks. It achieved accuracy competitive with LoRA on mathematics and structured-tool-use benchmarks.

Conclusion: ART provides a viable PEFT alternative that preserves compatibility with high-throughput, precompiled inference engines by shifting all learnable parameters into the visual input space of MLLMs, without sacrificing competitive performance.

Abstract: There are two main Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques for Large Language Models (LLMs). While Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) introduces additional weights between the LLM layers, Soft Prompting introduces additional fine-tuning-specific raw tokens to an LLM input. However, both require modification to the computational graphs of precompiled, preoptimized LLMs. As a result, neither is fully supported in high-throughput engines like vLLM. We propose fine-tuning with ART (Art-based Reinforcement Training). The method injects information into a frozen Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) by optimizing only its raw visual input, thus enabling the soft-token approach on pre-compiled computational graphs. It relies on backpropagation of gradients back into a plain pixel array and thus supports any fine-tuning objective. Moreover, the optimized visual input can be stylized as task-relevant computational artworks. The approach's effectiveness is confirmed for different sizes of a popular open Qwen architecture and for several textual benchmarks. Specifically, ART reaches accuracy competitive with LoRA across mathematics and structured-tool-use benchmarks.

</details>


### [263] [RePAIR: Predictive Self-Supervised Representation Learning in Chess](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11860)
*Christoph Koller,Johannes Fürnkranz,Timo Bertram*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RePAIR is a novel self-supervised architecture that combines MAE, JEPA, and BERT paradigms to learn compact representations of sequential data. Applied to chess, it masks latent state sequences and repairs them in a lower-dimensional embedding space, yielding semantically meaningful clusters, the ability to reason about piece movements without reinforcement learning, and intuitive game trajectory visualizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to develop a self-supervised representation learning method that can encode objects in sequential data (e.g., chess positions) into compact, semantically meaningful representations, without relying on costly reinforcement learning approaches. They seek to unify strengths from MAE (masking), JEPA (prediction in latent space), and BERT (bidirectional context) into a single architecture.

Method: RePAIR masks large portions of a sequence of latent states (akin to BERT/MAE), then applies a lightweight Predictor that repairs gaps in a lower-dimensional embedding space (akin to JEPA). The Encoder operates on sequential data — consecutive chess positions — and learns through iterative refinement of the masked representations.

Result: The Encoder produces latent spaces where meaningful chess concepts naturally cluster. The model can reconstruct masked board states, demonstrating reasoning about piece movements without reinforcement learning. Game path trajectories in the representation space enable quick, intuitive dissection of chess games.

Conclusion: RePAIR successfully synthesizes multiple self-supervised paradigms into a unified architecture that learns semantically rich representations of sequential data. In the chess domain, it yields interpretable latent structures, emergent reasoning capabilities, and practical tools for game analysis — all without reinforcement learning.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce Representation Prediction via Autoencoding using Iterative Refinement (RePAIR) - a novel self-supervised representation learning architecture that synthesizes Masked Autoencoders (MAE), Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA), and Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT). We demonstrate how it can be used to encode objects in sequential data like consecutive chess positions into compact yet meaningful representations. The basic principle of the architecture is to mask large portions of a sequence of latent states, similar to BERT and MAE. Then, we apply a lightweight Predictor to the latent representations that repairs gaps in the sequence in a lower-dimensional embedding space akin to JEPA. Our experiments in the domain of chess show that the Encoder refines the board representations such that meaningful chess concepts emerge clustered in the latent space. Furthermore, reconstructions of the masked board states show that the model is able to reason about the piece movements without relying on costly reinforcement learning methods. Lastly, we find that the resulting representation space allows for quick and intuitive dissections of chess games by observing the game path trajectories in this semantically rich space.

</details>


### [264] [MemNovo: Look Back at the Spectrum for Balanced De Novo Peptide Sequencing from Mass Spectrometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11868)
*Dongxin Lyu,Jingbo Zhou,Hongxin Xiang,Yuqiang Li,Jun Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Existing Transformer-based peptide sequencing decoders over-rely on generated sequence priors and under-utilize mass spectrum evidence, causing biologically plausible but unfaithful predictions. MemNovo, a training-free plug-and-play mechanism, uses a spectral memory bank with residual connections at inference to re-balance this, achieving up to 39.1% relative peptide precision improvement with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors identify a critical pathology in Transformer-based encoder-decoder models for de novo peptide sequencing: during auto-regressive inference, decoders progressively shift attention toward previously generated tokens and away from the input mass spectrum. This causes output peptides that are biologically plausible (due to strong language-model-like priors) but not chemically faithful to the experimental spectrum, undermining the core purpose of de novo sequencing.

Method: MemNovo introduces: (1) a persistent spectral memory bank that stores fine-grained spectral features extracted by the encoder; (2) at each decoding step, relevant spectral features are retrieved from this bank; and (3) an ultra-conservative residual connection injects these retrieved features directly into the final decoding layer, bypassing the information bottleneck of cross-attention. The mechanism is training-free, plug-and-play, and requires no architectural changes to the underlying model. Theoretical analysis using mutual information shows MemNovo restores the information flow between decoder states and the raw spectrum.

Result: On the Nine Species benchmark, MemNovo consistently improved both amino acid precision and peptide precision across two baselines (Casanovo and InstaNovo). Casanovo saw up to 39.1% relative improvement in peptide precision; InstaNovo achieved up to 3.9% relative improvement. These gains came with negligible computational overhead.

Conclusion: The identified over-reliance on sequence priors is a fundamental pathology in spectrum-to-peptide decoding, and the proposed spectral memory bank with conservative residual connections effectively addresses it without retraining. This highlights the importance of maintaining faithful spectral grounding throughout the decoding process in mass spectrometry-based de novo peptide sequencing.

Abstract: De novo peptide sequencing from tandem mass spectrometry is pivotal in proteomics, enabling identification of novel peptides without reference databases. While recent Transformer-based encoder-decoder models have achieved remarkable performance, we uncover a critical pathology in their inference dynamics. Through comprehensive feature scaling experiments, we demonstrate that existing auto-regressive peptide decoders tend to over-rely on generated-sequence priors while progressively under-utilizing fine-grained physical evidence from the input mass spectrum. This phenomenon leads to suboptimal results, where generated peptide sequences are biologically plausible yet not faithful to the input spectrum. To rectify this, we propose MemNovo, a training-free and plug-and-play mechanism that re-balances peptide and spectral contributions at inference time. MemNovo alleviates the information bottleneck by establishing a persistent spectral memory bank and injecting retrieved features directly into the final decoding stage via an ultra-conservative residual connection. Theoretical analysis confirms that this mechanism restores the mutual information between the decoder state and the raw spectrum. Extensive experiments on the Nine Species benchmark with two representative baselines, Casanovo and InstaNovo, demonstrate that MemNovo consistently improves both amino acid precision and peptide precision, achieving up to 39.1% relative improvement in peptide precision for Casanovo and up to 3.9% for InstaNovo, with negligible computational overhead.

</details>


### [265] [Beyond representational alignment with brain-guided language models for robust reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11893)
*Mingqing Xiao,Kai Du,Zhouchen Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that LLM internal representations partially align with human brain fMRI activity during deductive reasoning, and that brain signals can be used to directly enhance LLM reasoning performance—yielding up to 13% absolute accuracy gains across models ranging from 1.5B to 72B parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors identify a gap in current understanding: while LLMs and human higher-order cognition have been compared, it remains unclear whether LLMs align with neural signals from reasoning-specific brain regions and whether such signals can actively improve LLMs (beyond mere correlation). The dissociability of language and reasoning in the human brain motivates exploring reasoning-specific neural alignment and its utility for model enhancement.

Method: The authors employ a neural-predictivity metric to quantify alignment between LLM internal representations and task-fMRI activity in reasoning-related brain regions, both at aggregate and reasoning-type-specific levels. Building on this, they propose a brain-guided framework that steers LLM representations along directions derived from the joint structure of model and brain representations. This steering is applied via intervention at inference time and via fine-tuning during training, using task-evoked brain signals as a supervisory source orthogonal to traditional language-only training.

Result: LLMs explain substantial explainable variance in reasoning-related brain regions in aggregate, but predictivity is lower for specific reasoning types, indicating partial alignment with divergence. Brain-guided steering yields consistent reasoning gains across 10 LLMs (1.5B–72B parameters), transfers across reasoning types, and achieves up to 13% absolute accuracy improvement—benefits orthogonal to language-only supervision.

Conclusion: The work advances LLM-brain correspondences from mere correlation to causal guidance, establishing a brain-signal-driven pathway for building more robust and cognitively aligned AI systems.

Abstract: The correspondence between large language models (LLMs) and the neural mechanisms underlying human higher-order cognition remains insufficiently characterized. Given that language and reasoning in the human brain appear dissociable, an open question is whether LLMs align with neural signals from reasoning-related regions and whether such signals can improve them. Here, focusing on deductive reasoning, we show that LLM internal representations are not only partially aligned with task-fMRI activity but can also be directly enhanced by these signals. Using a neural-predictivity metric, we find that LLMs explain a substantial fraction of the explainable variance in reasoning-related regions at the aggregate level, whereas predictivity within specific reasoning types is lower, indicating both alignment and divergence. Building on this, we propose a brain-guided framework: we steer model representations along directions induced by the joint structure of model and brain representations, applying intervention at inference and fine-tuning during training. We demonstrate that task-evoked brain signals can directly enhance LLM reasoning, yielding gains orthogonal to language-only supervision across 10 LLMs (1.5B-72B), with transfer across reasoning types and up to 13\% absolute accuracy gain. Our results advance LLM-brain correspondences from correlation to guidance, establishing a brain-signal-driven pathway toward more robust and cognitively aligned AI.

</details>


### [266] [Categorical Prior Lock-in: Why In-Context Learning Fails for Structured Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11961)
*Antonio Pelusi,Stefano Braghin,Alberto Trombetta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ICL for structured tabular generation hits a hard ceiling on categorical distributions due to "categorical prior lock-in," where rare classes are entirely missed. LoRA fine-tuning overcomes this but introduces memorization risks and output instability, revealing an adaptability–privacy trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as conditional generators for structured data via in-context learning, but their behavior under distribution mismatch—especially with high-cardinality categorical features—is poorly understood. The authors aim to systematically characterize where and why ICL fails in this setting.

Method: The authors use high-cardinality tabular data as a controlled testbed. They evaluate two 7B-parameter open-weight LLMs, measuring ICL's ability to adapt to shifted categorical distributions as more in-context examples are provided. They compare ICL against LoRA fine-tuning, assessing both generation fidelity and memorization risk.

Result: ICL improves numerical fidelity with more examples but plateaus sharply on categorical generation—rare classes are never reproduced. This failure mode, termed "categorical prior lock-in," stems from the model's inability to override pre-training token priors. LoRA fixes the distributional gap but introduces measurable memorization and can destabilize structured outputs.

Conclusion: There exists a fundamental trade-off between adaptability and privacy in structured LLM generation: ICL preserves privacy but is structurally limited by locked-in categorical priors, while LoRA enables faithful distribution adaptation at the cost of memorization risk and potential output instability.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as conditional generators for structured data, relying on in-context learning (ICL) to adapt to new distributions without parameter updates. We investigate the limits of ICL for structured generation under distribution mismatch, using high-cardinality tabular data as a controlled test case, and identify a structural failure mode we term \textit{categorical prior lock-in}: the inability of ICL to update the model's prior over token distributions inherited from pre-training. Across two 7B-parameter open-weight models, ICL improves numerical fidelity with additional examples but exhibits a sharp ceiling on categorical distributions, failing to reproduce rare classes entirely. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) overcomes these limitations but introduces measurable memorization risk and, in some cases, destabilizes structured output generation, highlighting a fundamental trade-off between adaptability and privacy.

</details>


### [267] [HAMNO: A Hierarchical Adaptive Multi-scale Neural Operator with Physics-Informed Learning for Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11963)
*Mostafa Bamdad,Mohammad Sadegh Eshaghi,Timon Rabczuk*

Main category: cs.LG
