*Pietro Barbiero,Giovanni De Felice,Mateo Espinosa Zarlenga,Francesco Giannini,Filippo Bonchi,Mateja Jamnik,Giuseppe Marra,Ruggero Noris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the Standard Interpretable Model (SIM), a Lagrangian mechanics-based theory that deductively derives interpretability constraints from user-centric premises, unifies fragmented interpretability methods, and guides both model optimization and architecture design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability research lacks general theories for deductively designing methods, leading to a fragmented literature with inconsistent evaluation protocols. A principled theoretical framework is needed to unify and guide interpretability work.

Method: The SIM framework formalizes interpretability as a set of premises defining what interpretability means for a target user. From these premises, it derives interpretability symmetries and constraints using Lagrangian mechanics. The Lagrangian's minima represent optimal interpretable models, reachable either by optimizing opaque model parameters toward interpretability or by directly compiling constraints into interpretable architectures.

Result: The SIM empirically identifies and resolves limitations in traditional, concept-based, and mechanistic interpretability methods, reveals underexplored research directions, and informs the design of core programming interfaces for interpretability tools.

Conclusion: The SIM provides a unifying deductive theory for interpretability, offering both practical methodological guidance and pedagogical value. It has the potential to shift the scientific community's perspective on a historically fragmented discipline.

Abstract: As Artificial Intelligence models grow in complexity, interpretability has become an indispensable tool for understanding, debugging, and controlling their computations. However, interpretability lacks general theories to deductively design interpretable methods. This gap between theories and methods results in a fragmented literature and inconsistent evaluation protocols.
  To fill this gap, we introduce the Standard Interpretable Model (SIM), a general theory grounded in Lagrangian mechanics that enables the deductive design of interpretable methods. Specifically, the SIM summarises, in a set of premises, what interpretability is for a target user. From these premises, the SIM systematically derives interpretability symmetries and corresponding constraints, which shape the landscape of a Lagrangian whose minima correspond to optimal interpretable models. To reach the minima, one can either update the parameter values of an opaque model to make it more interpretable or compile constraints into an interpretable architecture.
  We empirically show that the SIM identifies and solves limitations of existing methods (including traditional, concept-based, and mechanistic interpretability), highlights underexplored research directions, and informs the design of core programming interfaces. Beyond being a research method, the deductive nature of the SIM offers pedagogical grounding for interpretability curricula and may shift the scientific community's perspective of a discipline that has long been fragmented.

</details>


### [291] [Harness In-Context Operator Learning with Chain of Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12318)
*Minghui Yang,Ling Guo,Liu Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CHOP introduces a framework that chains elementary transformations with a frozen In-Context Operator Network (ICON) to improve generalization to out-of-distribution operator tasks without fine-tuning, inspired by prompt engineering in LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators like ICON can learn operators from prompts without fine-tuning, but they still fail to generalize to out-of-distribution (OOD) operator tasks. The authors aim to harness a frozen ICON for OOD tasks without parameter updates, analogous to how prompt engineering improves LLM generalization.

Method: The CHOP framework constructs a chain of operators composed of explicit elementary transformations (closed-form, interpretable) and a frozen ICON model. The chain is designed so that the composite mapping solves OOD tasks that ICON alone cannot handle, without any parameter fine-tuning.

Result: Experiments on a scalar conservation law and a mean-field control problem demonstrate that CHOP reduces relative inference error compared to direct ICON evaluation. The operators in the chain remain interpretable and in closed form. Additionally, a chain constructed for one PDE family generalizes to a different PDE family, suggesting shared mechanisms across harness systems.

Conclusion: CHOP provides an effective, interpretable method for extending frozen neural operators to OOD tasks using operator chaining, with evidence of cross-domain generalization and shared underlying mechanisms.

Abstract: Neural operators approximate mappings between function spaces, but often generalize poorly to other operators and usually require fine-tuning or retraining. In-Context Operator Networks (ICON) addresses this issue by prompting the model with numerical context so that the model learns specific operators from prompts and adapt to different operators without fine-tuning. However, ICON may still fail to generalize to out-of-distribution (OOD) operator tasks. Inpired by the success of harness engineering of Large Language models (LLMs), we introduce Chain of Operators (CHOP), a framework that harness a frozen ICON to OOD operator tasks without updating its parameters. Specifically, CHOP constructs a chain of operators consisting of explicit elementary transformations and the frozen ICON. Experiments on a scalar conservation law and a mean-field control problem show that CHOP reduces relative inference error over direct ICON evaluation, while each operator in the chain remains interpretable and in closed form. A chain constructed on one PDE family further generalizes to a different family, indicating shared mechanisms across harness systems.

</details>


### [292] [Fourier Features Let Agents Learn High Precision Policies with Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12334)
*Balázs Gyenes,Emiliyan Gospodinov,Jan Frieling,Enrico Krohmer,Nicolas Schreiber,Xiaogang Jia,Niklas Freymuth,Gerhard Neumann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper hypothesizes that neural networks' spectral bias toward low-frequency functions limits point cloud-based manipulation policies. It proposes mapping point clouds from Cartesian to Fourier space to give encoders direct access to high-frequency features, and experimentally validates this simple yet effective approach across multiple benchmarks and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB-only robotic manipulation policies suffer from depth ambiguity and perspective scale issues. While point cloud-based policies provide stronger geometric priors, their performance is highly task-dependent. The authors attribute this inconsistency to the spectral bias of neural networks, which preferentially learn low-frequency functions, disadvantaging architectures conditioned on slow-varying Cartesian coordinates.

Method: The method maps 3D point cloud coordinates from Cartesian space into high-dimensional Fourier space using random Fourier feature encoding (inspired by Tancik et al., NeRF). Specifically, each point coordinate x ∈ ℝ³ is projected via sin(Bx) and cos(Bx), where B is a random Gaussian matrix, yielding high-frequency features that serve as input to the point cloud encoder. This encoding is applied as a preprocessing step before standard point cloud processing backbones (e.g., PointNet++, transformer-based encoders).

Result: Fourier features provide significant and consistent performance improvements across diverse encoder architectures, multiple challenging manipulation benchmarks (RoboCasa, ManiSkill3), and a real-robot setup. The benefits are robust across hyperparameter choices and enable policies to leverage fine geometric details more effectively than Cartesian features.

Conclusion: Fourier features serve as a simple, general-purpose tool for point cloud-based imitation learning in robotic manipulation, effectively mitigating the spectral bias problem and enhancing spatial reasoning without architectural changes or task-specific tuning.

Abstract: High-precision robotic manipulation requires fine-grained spatial reasoning that is often difficult to achieve with RGB-only policies due to depth ambiguity and perspective scale issues. Policies that leverage 3D information directly, such as those based on point clouds, offer a stronger geometric prior over purely image-based ones, yet their performance remains highly task-dependent. We hypothesize that this discrepancy may be due to the spectral bias of neural networks towards learning low frequency functions, which especially affects architectures conditioned on slow-moving Cartesian features. We thus propose to map point clouds from Cartesian space into high-dimensional Fourier space, effectively equipping the point cloud encoder with direct access to high-frequency features. We experimentally validate the use of Fourier features on challenging manipulation tasks from the RoboCasa and ManiSkill3 benchmarks and on a real robot setup. Despite their simplicity, we find that Fourier features provide significant benefits across diverse encoder architectures and benchmarks and are robust across hyperparameters. Our results indicate that Fourier features let policies leverage geometric details more effectively than Cartesian features, showing their potential as a general-purpose tool for point cloud-based imitation learning. We provide source code and videos on our project page: https://fourier-il.github.io/fourier-il

</details>


### [293] [Claw-SWE-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating OpenClaw-style Agent Harnesses on Coding Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12344)
*Mengyu Zheng,Kai Han,Boxun Li,Haiyang Xu,Yuchuan Tian,Wei He,Hang Zhou,Jianyuan Guo,Hailin Hu,Lin Ma,Chao Xu,Guohao Dai,Lixue Xia,Yunchao Wei,Yunhe Wang,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Claw-SWE-Bench introduces a multilingual benchmark and adapter protocol (350 instances across 8 languages) enabling fair comparison of heterogeneous coding agents like OpenClaw. Adapter design dramatically affects performance (19.1% → 73.4% Pass@1), and both harness choice and cost are elevated as first-class evaluation axes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose agents like OpenClaw cannot be fairly evaluated on SWE-bench because they don't natively satisfy Docker workspace, patch, and prediction contract requirements. There is a need for a standardized benchmark and adapter protocol that makes heterogeneous agent harnesses comparable under controlled settings, while also accounting for cost.

Method: The authors construct Claw-SWE-Bench (350 instances, 8 languages, 43 repositories) by curating from SWE-bench-Multilingual and SWE-bench-Verified-Mini with future-commit cleanup. They provide an adapter protocol enforcing fixed prompt, runtime budget, workspace contract, patch extraction, and evaluator. Claw-SWE-Bench Lite (80 instances) is subset-selected via a cost-aware, rank-aware procedure over 17 calibration columns. A sweep evaluates OpenClaw × 9 models and 5 claws × 2 models.

Result: Adapter design is critical: minimal direct-diff adapter yields 19.1% Pass@1 vs. 73.4% with the full adapter (same GLM 5.1 backbone). Model choice changes Pass@1 by 29.4 pp and harness choice by 27.4 pp. Systems with similar accuracy diverge substantially in total API cost.

Conclusion: Claw-SWE-Bench establishes harness design and cost accounting as first-class evaluation axes for SWE-style coding agents, alongside a full benchmark and a low-cost Lite subset for reproducible, fair comparison across heterogeneous agent systems.

Abstract: General-purpose agents such as OpenClaw are increasingly used as autonomous tool users, but their coding ability is difficult to measure under SWE-bench: a generic agent does not by itself satisfy the clean Docker workspace, patch, and prediction contract required for scoring. We introduce Claw-SWE-Bench, a multilingual SWE-bench-style benchmark and adapter protocol that makes heterogeneous agent harnesses, or claws, comparable under fair settings including a fixed prompt, runtime budget, workspace contract, patch extraction procedure, and evaluator. The full benchmark contains 350 GitHub issue-resolution instances across 8 languages and 43 repositories, drawn from SWE-bench-Multilingual and SWE-bench-Verified-Mini after future-commit cleanup. We also release Claw-SWE-Bench Lite for faster validation, which is an 80-instance subset selected by a cost-aware, rank-aware procedure over 17 calibration columns. On the full benchmark, OpenClaw with a minimal direct-diff adapter scores only $19.1\%$ Pass@1, whereas the full adapter reaches $73.4\%$ with the same GLM 5.1 backbone, showing that adapter design is essential for enabling OpenClaw-style harnesses to perform coding tasks effectively. Across an OpenClaw $\times$ nine-model sweep and a five-claw $\times$ two-model sweep, model choice changes Pass@1 by $29.4$ pp and harness choice by $27.4$ pp under fixed models; systems with similar accuracy can differ substantially in total API cost. Claw-SWE-Bench therefore treats harness and cost accounting as first-class axes of SWE-style coding-agent evaluation, providing both a full benchmark and a low-cost reference set for reproducible comparison. The data is available at https://github.com/opensquilla/claw-swe-bench and https://huggingface.co/datasets/TokenRhythm/Claw-SWE-Bench.

</details>


### [294] [Anatomy of Post-Training: Using Interpretability to Characterize Data and Shape the Learning Signal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12360)
*Leon Bergen,Usha Bhalla,Sidharth Baskaran,Max Loeffler,Raphael Sarfati,Dhruvil Gala,Ryan Panwar,Santiago Aranguri,Thomas Fel,Atticus Geiger,Matthew Kowal,Siddharth Boppana,Daniel Balsam,Owen Lewis,Jack Merullo,Thomas McGrath,Ekdeep Singh Lubana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a data-centric post-training pipeline that uses interpretability tools to inspect preference datasets before optimization, making latent concepts explicit so practitioners can audit, mitigate, or amplify specific model behaviors rather than relying on opaque scalar rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current post-training relies on optimizing scalar reward signals that obscure what the model actually learns from preference data. This opacity enables spurious correlations, over-stylization, and sycophancy. Practitioners lack visibility into which concepts drive preference decisions, making it hard to diagnose or control unwanted behaviors before training.

Method: The authors introduce a pipeline that applies interpretability protocols (e.g., sparse autoencoders, probing) to preference datasets to derive statistical hypotheses about latent concepts separating preferred from dispreferred responses. These concepts are surfaced for fine-grained human feedback, enabling feature-level or data-level interventions that shape reward signals directly. The framework unifies several existing interpretability-based training protocols under this data-centric, concept-aware paradigm.

Result: Empirically, the pipeline successfully diagnoses undesirable signals in existing preference data, mitigates off-target learning (e.g., sycophancy, stylistic artifacts), and amplifies desired properties such as safety safeguards and model personality traits.

Conclusion: Interpretability can transform post-training from a black-box reward optimization process into a transparent, auditable workflow where the learning signal itself is inspected and sculpted at the conceptual level, enabling more precise and accountable model alignment.

Abstract: Language-model post-training is the main stage at which model behavior is shaped, yet it still largely involves optimization of scalar rewards that summarize diverse desiderata. This abstraction gives practitioners little visibility into what their data actually teaches models, allowing spurious correlations to be learned by a model and inducing undesirable behaviors such as over-stylization and sycophancy. To address this problem, we ask: can we inspect a preference dataset before optimization and decide, at the level of concepts, which behaviors a model should be allowed to learn? Motivated by this, we introduce a data-centric post-training pipeline that uses interpretability protocols to develop statistical hypotheses for the latent concepts separating preferred from dispreferred generations, making them explicit for fine-grained user feedback. Building on this view, we unify several interpretability-based training protocols as ways of shaping rewards via feature or data interventions. Empirically, we show that our pipeline diagnoses undesirable signals in existing preference data, mitigates off-target learning, and can also help amplify or shape desired properties such as safeguards and model personality. More broadly, our results suggest that interpretability can turn post-training from optimizing opaque proxy rewards into a process of auditing and sculpting the learning signal itself.

</details>


### [295] [Latent World Recovery for Multimodal Learning with Missing Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12362)
*Hui Wang,Tianyu Ren,Joseph Butler,Christopher Baker,Karen Rafferty,Simon McDade*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LWR is a multimodal learning framework that handles missing modalities by aligning modality-specific embeddings in a shared latent space and fusing only available modalities, avoiding imputation. It achieves robust prediction on incomplete multi-omics tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In bioscience applications (e.g., multi-omics), heterogeneous modalities are often partially available at decision time. Existing methods either impute missing data (introducing errors) or require fixed modality sets, motivating an availability-aware approach that works directly from observed modalities.

Method: Latent World Recovery (LWR) operates on two principles: (1) modality-specific embeddings are aligned in a shared latent space via neighbor-based alignment, treating each modality as a partial perception of an underlying latent state; (2) a unified representation is formed by fusing only the embeddings of modalities actually present at training/inference time, using availability-aware fusion.

Result: LWR was evaluated on real-world incomplete multi-omics benchmarks and demonstrated effective performance on downstream tasks including cancer phenotype classification and survival prediction.

Conclusion: LWR provides a robust approach to multimodal learning under missing modalities by aligning latent representations and fusing available modalities, avoiding error propagation from explicit reconstruction while enabling flexible inference under partial observation.

Abstract: We study multimodal learning under missing modalities, with particular motivation from bioscience applications in which heterogeneous modalities are often only partially available when decisions need to be made. We propose Latent World Recovery (LWR), a framework built on two key ideas: (i) modality-specific embeddings from different modalities are aligned in a shared latent space, and (ii) a unified representation is constructed by fusing only the embeddings of the modalities that are actually available at both training and inference time. Rather than imputing missing modalities or requiring a fixed modality set, LWR treats each modality as a partial perception of an underlying latent state and performs availability-aware representation learning directly from the observed modalities. This combination of neighbor-based latent alignment and availability-aware modality fusion enables robust multimodal prediction under partial observation, while avoiding error propagation from explicit reconstruction of missing modalities. We evaluate the proposed framework on real-world incomplete multi-omics benchmarks and demonstrate that it provides an effective approach to downstream tasks such as cancer phenotype classification and survival prediction.

</details>


### [296] [On Subquadratic Architectures: From Applications to Principles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12364)
*Anamaria-Roberta Hartl,Levente Zólyomi,David Stap,Pieter-Jan Hoedt,Niklas Schmidinger,Lukas Hauzenberger,Sebastian Böck,Günter Klambauer,Sepp Hochreiter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper compares three subquadratic sequence model architectures (xLSTM, Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet) across code modeling and time-series tasks. xLSTM achieves the best overall performance, attributed to its gating mechanism enabling more flexible and stable memory correction and robust state tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers are dominant but costly due to quadratic attention complexity. While subquadratic architectures offer scalable alternatives, it is unclear which designs are most effective for complex sequence modeling tasks. The authors aim to systematically compare leading subquadratic approaches and explain performance differences through architectural analysis.

Method: The authors compare xLSTM, Mamba-2, and Gated DeltaNet across three challenging settings: (1) code-model pre-training, (2) distillation of code models from large language models, and (3) time-series foundation model pre-training. They develop a unified formulation of these architectures and analyze mechanisms of state tracking and memory dynamics, supplemented by controlled synthetic length-generalization experiments.

Result: xLSTM consistently delivers the strongest overall performance across all three task settings. The analysis reveals that xLSTM's gating scheme enables more flexible and stable memory correction compared to the other architectures. These findings are corroborated by synthetic length-generalization tasks, where xLSTM's advantages in state tracking and memory accumulation are confirmed.

Conclusion: xLSTM's performance gains on complex tasks stem fundamentally from robust state tracking and accumulation capabilities enabled by its gating mechanism, suggesting that gating-based memory correction is a key architectural principle for effective subquadratic sequence modeling.

Abstract: Transformers dominate modern sequence modeling, but their quadratic attention incurs substantial computational cost. Subquadratic architectures offer a scalable alternative. However, it remains unclear which designs yield the most effective sequence models. We compare three leading approaches: xLSTM, Mamba-2, and Gated DeltaNet. We evaluate these models on tasks with complex dependencies: (1) code-model pre-training, (2) distillation of code models from large language models, and (3) pre-training of time-series foundation models. Across these settings, xLSTM delivers the strongest overall performance. To explain xLSTM's advantage, we present a unified formulation and analyze the underlying architectural mechanisms, focusing on state tracking and memory dynamics. Our results show that xLSTM enables more flexible and stable memory correction via its gating scheme. We corroborate these findings on controlled synthetic length-generalization tasks. Overall, our findings indicate that xLSTM's gains on complex tasks stem from robust state tracking and accumulation.

</details>


### [297] [Breaking Entropy Bounds: Accelerating RL Training via MTP with Rejection Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12370)
*Yucheng Li,Huiqiang Jiang,Yang Xu,Jianxin Yang,Yi Zhang,Yizhong Cao,Yuhao Shen,Fan Zhou,Rui Men,Jianwei Zhang,An Yang,Bowen Yu,Bo Zheng,Fei Huang,Junyang Lin,Dayiheng Liu,Jingren Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bebop systematically studies Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) in LLM post-training for RL pipelines, revealing that MTP acceptance rates degrade due to entropy fluctuations during RL. The authors propose a novel end-to-end TV loss for MTP training and probabilistic rejection sampling, achieving up to 95% acceptance rates, ~10% improvement, up to 25% inference throughput gains, and up to 1.8x end-to-end acceleration in async RL training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rollout stage is the key bottleneck in RL training pipelines for LLMs. While Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) can accelerate rollouts via speculative decoding, acceptance rates degrade significantly during RL training, limiting speedup. There is a need for practical recipes to integrate MTP effectively into large-scale RL pipelines.

Method: (1) Analysis revealing MTP acceptance rate is bounded by model entropy fluctuation, showing a negative linear relationship with entropy rise in RL. (2) Probabilistic rejection sampling to alleviate entropy-induced disturbance (vs. greedy draft sampling). (3) A novel end-to-end TV (Total Variation) loss that directly optimizes multi-step rejection sampling acceptance rate, replacing suboptimal cross-entropy/KL objectives. (4) Testing of various online MTP training strategies, showing pre-RL MTP training with e2e TV loss and rejection sampling suffices without costly online updating.

Result: The proposed TV loss yields ~10% acceptance rate improvement, achieving up to 95% acceptance rates and up to 25% extra inference throughput gains across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and agentic tasks. Pre-RL MTP training maintains consistent acceptance rate and speedup throughout RL. Overall, up to 1.8x end-to-end acceleration in async RL training of Qwen3.5, Qwen3.6, and Qwen3.7 models.

Conclusion: MTP can be effectively integrated into RL post-training by addressing entropy-driven acceptance rate degradation. The combination of probabilistic rejection sampling and end-to-end TV loss optimization eliminates the need for costly online MTP updating while delivering substantial and consistent inference acceleration throughout RL training.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a key component in modern large language models, yet the rollout stage remains the key bottleneck in RL training pipelines. Although Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) offers a natural solution to accelerate rollouts through speculative decoding, many studies have observed that MTP acceptance rates degrade significantly during RL training, leading to limited speedup performance. To address this bottleneck, we present Bebop, a systematic study of MTP in LLM post-training, and offer practical recipes to integrate MTP into large-scale RL pipelines. First, we reveal that the MTP acceptance rate is fundamentally bounded by the fluctuation of model entropy, which demonstrates a clear negative linear relationship with the rise of entropy in the RL stage. Second, we show that probabilistic rejection sampling largely alleviates the disturbance introduced by entropy in RL compared to greedy draft sampling. We further identify that the conventional MTP training objectives (cross-entropy or KL) are suboptimal in such settings, and therefore we propose a novel end-to-end TV loss that directly optimizes multi-step rejection sampling acceptance rate, yielding ~10% acceptance rate improvements, achieving up to 95% acceptance rates and up to 25% extra inference throughput gains across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and agentic tasks. Third, we test various online MTP training strategies during RL and show that pre-RL MTP training with e2e TV loss and rejection sampling achieves a consistent acceptance rate and speedup throughout the entire RL, eliminating the need for costly online MTP updating. We provide extensive experiments and analysis that validate our findings. Experimental results show our method achieves up to 1.8x end-to-end acceleration in async RL training of Qwen3.5, Qwen3.6, and Qwen3.7 models.

</details>


### [298] [APPO: Agentic Procedural Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12384)
*Xucong Wang,Ziyu Ma,Yong Wang,Yuxiang Ji,Shidong Yang,Guanhua Chen,Pengkun Wang,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: APPO proposes fine-grained branching and credit assignment for agentic RL by selecting decision points via a Branching Score (combining token uncertainty and policy likelihood gains) and using procedure-level advantage scaling, achieving ~4-point gains across 13 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agentic RL methods assign credit at coarse heuristic units (tool-call boundaries, fixed workflows), failing to identify which intermediate decisions influence downstream outcomes. The authors' pilot analysis reveals that influential decision points are broadly distributed throughout sequences, not just at tool calls, and token entropy alone poorly predicts impact on final outcomes.

Method: APPO (Agentic Procedural Policy Optimization) introduces: (1) A Branching Score that combines token-level uncertainty with policy-induced likelihood gains of subsequent continuations to select fine-grained branching locations, enabling targeted exploration while filtering spurious high-entropy positions; (2) Procedure-level advantage scaling that better distributes credit across branched rollouts, moving beyond coarse interaction-unit attribution.

Result: APPO consistently improves strong agentic RL baselines by nearly 4 points across 13 benchmarks while maintaining efficient tool-call usage and behavior interpretability.

Conclusion: Shifting branching and credit assignment from coarse interaction units to fine-grained decision points significantly enhances agentic RL performance. The combination of Branching Score and procedure-level advantage scaling provides a more principled way to explore and assign credit in multi-turn agent trajectories.

Abstract: Recent advances in agentic Reinforcement Learning (RL) have substantially improved the multi-turn tool-use capabilities of large language model agents. However, most existing methods assign credit over coarse heuristic units, such as tool-call boundaries or fixed workflows, making it difficult to identify which intermediate decisions influence downstream outcomes. In this work, we study agentic RL from two perspectives: \textit{where to branch and how to assign credit after branching}. Our pilot analysis shows that influential decision points are broadly distributed throughout the generated sequence rather than concentrated at tool calls, while token entropy alone does not reliably reflect their impact on final outcomes. Motivated by these observations, we propose \textbf{Agentic Procedural Policy Optimization (APPO)}, which shifts branching and credit assignment from coarse interaction units to fine-grained decision points in the sequence. APPO selects branching locations using a Branching Score that combines token uncertainty with policy-induced likelihood gains of subsequent continuations, enabling more targeted exploration while filtering out spurious high-entropy positions. It further introduces procedure-level advantage scaling to better distribute credit across branched rollouts. Experiments on 13 benchmarks show that APPO consistently improves strong agentic RL baselines by nearly 4 points, while keeping efficient tool-calls and maintaining behavior interpretability.

</details>


### [299] [ATLAS: Active Theory Learning for Automated Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12386)
*Noémi Éltető,Nathaniel D. Daw,Kimberly L. Stachenfeld,Kevin J. Miller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ATLAS is an active learning framework that iteratively generates interpretable mechanistic models (Disentangled RNNs) and designs experiments to optimally discriminate between them, achieving 5-10x improvement in sample efficiency over random experimentation when recovering reinforcement learning agents from bandit task behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific discovery through mechanistic modeling is bottlenecked by the need to design maximally informative experiments. Automating this process—particularly the iterative loop of hypothesis generation and experimental design—could dramatically accelerate the discovery of interpretable behavioral models in cognitive science.

Method: ATLAS uses active learning with a diverse ensemble of Disentangled RNNs (sparse neural networks that serve as mechanistic hypotheses). It alternates between two phases: (1) generating candidate mechanistic hypotheses from existing data, and (2) designing novel experiments with temporal structure that optimally distinguish between competing hypotheses. The framework is tested on recovering reinforcement learning agents from behavior in bandit tasks.

Result: ATLAS-designed experiments exhibit qualitatively novel temporal structure tailored to the characteristics of underlying agents. Across behavioral, structural, and computational similarity metrics, ATLAS achieves a 5-10x improvement in sample efficiency compared to random experimentation, and performance is validated favorably against expert-designed experiments from the literature.

Conclusion: ATLAS demonstrates strong potential to accelerate the discovery of human-interpretable mechanistic insights, not only in cognitive science but also in other domains where scientific progress depends on uncovering mechanistic models through iterative experimentation.

Abstract: Advancing scientific understanding through mechanistic modeling requires posing the right experimental questions to yield maximally informative data. To automate this pursuit within cognitive science, we introduce ATLAS (Active Theory Learning for Automated Science), an active learning framework for the data-driven discovery of interpretable behavioral models. ATLAS iterates between generating mechanistic hypotheses--instantiated as a diverse ensemble of sparse neural networks (Disentangled RNNs)--and designing experiments that optimally distinguish between them. We test this approach on the problem of recovering reinforcement learning agents from their behavior in bandit tasks. ATLAS designs varied sequences of qualitatively novel experiments with temporal structure tailored to underlying agent characteristics. The models trained on these experiments are evaluated against a comprehensive set of metrics for mechanistic modeling that capture behavioral, structural, and computational similarity. ATLAS achieves a 5-10x improvement in sample efficiency across all metrics compared to random experimentation, and its performance is further validated against expert-designed experiments derived from literature. These in silico results showcase ATLAS's potential to accelerate human-interpretable insights in cognitive science and other domains where scientific inquiry relies on discovering mechanistic models.

</details>


### [300] [Redesign Mixture-of-Experts Routers with Manifold Power Iteration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12397)
*Songhao Wu,Ang Lv,Ruobing Xie,Yankai Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes aligning each router row in MoE models with the principal singular direction of its associated expert matrix, and introduces Manifold Power Iteration (MPI)—a "Power-then-Retract" paradigm—to achieve this alignment, demonstrating improved MoE model effectiveness across 1B–11B parameter scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In MoE models, router rows serve as expert proxies to compute token-expert affinity via dot products. However, there are currently no design principles to ensure that router rows effectively condense/encode the expert matrix into a representative vector, leading to suboptimal routing.

Method: The authors propose aligning each router row with the principal singular direction (first right singular vector from SVD) of its corresponding expert matrix, as this direction provides the most expressive mathematical summary. They introduce Manifold Power Iteration (MPI), which follows a "Power-then-Retract" paradigm: a power iteration step drives the router row toward the dominant singular direction, followed by a retraction step that enforces a norm constraint (e.g., projection onto a sphere) to ensure numerical efficiency and stability.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows MPI provably drives router rows to converge toward the principal singular directions of their associated experts. Empirical pretraining experiments on MoE models ranging from 1B to 11B parameters confirm that this alignment yields more effective MoE models compared to standard router designs.

Conclusion: Aligning router rows with the principal singular directions of expert matrices provides a principled design for MoE routers. The proposed MPI method offers a theoretically grounded and empirically validated approach to enforce this alignment, resulting in improved MoE model performance.

Abstract: Router is the cornerstone component to the Mixture-of-Experts models. Serving as expert proxies, the rows of the router matrix compute their similarity to the MoE inputs to determine which subset of experts is activated. Ideally, each router row is designed to encode the expert matrix into this representative vector, such that its dot-product with token can better reflect token-expert affinity. However, there exists no design principles to enforce this condensation. In this paper, we propose to align each router row with the principal singular direction of the associated expert, as this direction provides the most expressive mathematical description of a matrix. Based on this principle, we propose a router redesign with Manifold Power Iteration (MPI). Specifically, it introduces a "Power-then-Retract" paradigm, where a power iteration step is performed on the router weights, followed by a retraction to impose a norm constraint to ensure both efficiency and stability. Theoretically, we show that MPI drives router rows to converge toward the principal singular directions of associated experts. Empirically, we pretrain MoE model across scales from 1B to 11B parameters to confirm that this alignment facilitates more effective MoE models.

</details>
